@elle-with-claws in this channel you are going to do research for a project called "oral assignments in the age of ai." The context is an elite University with research faculty teaching in a small residential college (Harvard). Everyone is concerned about students misusing AI for writing and other take home projects, so there is increased interest in oral exams. But they are hard to deliver at scale, can be hard to evaluate, potentially aren't equitable, etc etc.  We want a landscape review that helps our faculty begin to think about this topic, so I need you to research and find sources including

information on the history of oral examinations in higher ed
information on what peer institutions (Yale, Stanford, Princeton, etc) are doing in response to anxieties about students misusing AI (particularly oral assignments)
research on the history of oral assignments at Harvard
research on the role of speaking in the learning and assessment process from educational researchers and the scholarship of teaching and learning
papers on actual experiments within classes, particularly post AI
examples of people USING AI as part of the oral evaluation process (i.e. AI transcriptions, AI questions, AI interviewers or evaluators)
interesting literary and historical references (think Plato/Socrates on speech and writing)
references to other professional domains (the law, the theatre, business/tech, etc) where they might have valuable ways of thinking about speech, performance, presentations and oral evidence.
I'd like you to run a search each fifteen minutes starting at 6:30am and to keep going until Monday at 9am.  Find 3 sources that you summarize and share with commentary (any feedback I give you in the channel can be incorporated into your future searches and commentary)  Keep an ongoing bibliography of sources and list of your posts in a jsonl doc locally so you can track your progress and not repeat yourself. Rotate through the themes I mention above so we cover as many bases as possible.

:books: Oral Assignments Research — Theme 1: History of Oral Examinations in Higher Ed

───

Source 1: "The Shift from Oral to Written Examination: Cambridge and Oxford 1700-1900"
Christopher Stray, Assessment in Education: Principles, Policy & Practice, 2001

This is the foundational historical paper on how Western universities transitioned away from oral assessment. Key points:

• In medieval Oxford and Cambridge, examinations were "public, oral, and in Latin" — a form of intellectual performance before the academic community
• The shift to written exams in the 1700s-1800s was driven by the desire to grade students numerically and individually, rather than placing them in broad categories
• Oral exams were retained at Oxford into the 20th century, longer than Cambridge
• Four factors drove the shift: (1) move from group socio-moral to individual cognitive assessment, (2) rise of standardization, (3) examiner convenience, (4) perceived objectivity of written assessment

Harvard relevance: This history is crucial context — we're not proposing something "new" but returning to a tradition that was the default for centuries. The reasons for the shift (efficiency, standardization) may need revisiting in light of AI.

:link: https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/abs/10.1080/09695940120033243

───

Source 2: "Rethinking Oral Examinations for Undergraduate Students"
Johns Hopkins Innovative Instructor, 2016

A practical case study from Richard Brown (Mathematics) who experimented with oral finals in Honors Multivariable Calculus with 6 undergraduates. Key insights:

• Students voted to choose the oral format after discussion
• 20-minute individual appointments with tailored questions
• Brown could probe deeper when students got stuck, adjust follow-up questions to assess understanding — impossible with written exams
• 4 of 6 students "really liked the format"
• Limitation acknowledged: "would not be possible with more than 10 students"

The article also cites key literature: Asklund & Bendix (2003) found oral exams "better suited for evaluating higher levels of understanding based on Bloom's Taxonomy"; Huxham et al. (2012) found oral assessments offer "1) oral communication skills, 2) more authentic assessment, 3) more inclusive, 4) gauging understanding, 5) less potential for plagiarism, 6) better at conveying nuances, 7) easier to spot rote-learning."

Harvard relevance: The scalability concern (10 students max) is the key challenge. But the findings on deeper assessment and plagiarism resistance are exactly what faculty are seeking.

:link: https://ii.library.jhu.edu/2016/06/22/rethinking-oral-examinations-for-undergraduate-students/

───

Source 3: "Why universities should return to oral exams in the AI and ChatGPT era"
The Conversation, Jan 2026

A recent argument (published weeks ago) for oral exam revival in the AI era, drawing on international examples:

• Historical context: Oral assessment dates to ancient Greeks (public oral defense), medieval University of Paris (viva to graduate), and was the norm until the 1700s
• Norway still uses vivas extensively — undergraduate, postgraduate, and even high school (Year 10 and Year 13)
• Author's study found "never came across cases of cheating" in oral exams
• CQUniversity example: 600 oral exams assessed in under a week for a first-year undergraduate literacy course — "no integrity issues" and "lecturers were rewarded with a weekend free from papers to mark"
• Key reframe: "Integrity is 100% assured" with oral assessment vs. constant AI detection concerns with written work

Harvard relevance: This directly addresses the AI anxiety. The CQUniversity example (600 students, one week) challenges the "doesn't scale" assumption — it scaled, just differently. Worth investigating their logistics.

:link: https://theconversation.com/why-universities-should-return-to-oral-exams-in-the-ai-and-chatgpt-era-203429

───
The ConversationWhy universities should return to oral exams in the AI and ChatGPT eraOral exams have a history dating back more than 2,000 years – and could once again be a solution for universities to test their students’ knowledge.https://theconversation.com/why-universities-should-return-to-oral-exams-in-the-ai-and-chatgpt-era-203429[6:27 AM]Synthesis: The historical record shows oral examination was the default for centuries; written exams won on efficiency and standardization, not pedagogical superiority. Modern pilots (JHU, CQUniversity) demonstrate oral exams reveal deeper understanding and are resistant to integrity concerns. The question for Harvard isn't "should we try something new?" but "should we recover something old that's newly relevant?"

[6:31 AM]:books: Oral Assignments Research Update #1
Theme: History of Oral Examinations in Higher Education

───

Source 1: Pearce & Lee (2009) — "Viva Voce (Oral Examination) as an Assessment Method"
Journal of Marketing Education (SAGE)
https://journals.sagepub.com/doi/10.1177/0273475309334050

This foundational article examines viva voce as an underutilized assessment method. The authors note that while oral exams are ubiquitous in medical education, clinical settings, and doctoral defenses, they're rarely discussed as a deliberate pedagogical choice in business and general undergraduate education.

Why it matters: This is exactly the gap Harvard faculty are navigating. The article provides a scholarly framework for treating oral assessment as a design choice rather than a default tradition—crucial when arguing for oral assignments as AI-resilient pedagogy.

───

Source 2: Wikipedia — "Oral Exam" (comprehensive overview)
https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Oral_exam

Key historical insight: Oral examination traces from ancient Socratic dialogue through medieval universities (where exams at Oxford and Cambridge were "public, oral, and in Latin") to modern doctoral defenses. The article notes oral exams help reduce "the risk of granting a degree to a candidate who has had the thesis ghostwritten by an expert."

Why it matters: That last point is prescient for the AI age—the anti-ghostwriting function of oral examination is precisely what makes it relevant now. Written exams were a 19th-century innovation; oral exams are actually the original, more ancient form.

───

Source 3: Council on Higher Education (South Africa) — "Viva Voce: The Oral Examination of a Thesis for a Doctoral Degree"
https://www.che.ac.za/publications/research/brieflyspeaking-no-30-viva-voce-oral-examination-thesis-doctoral-degree

The Latin viva voce means "the living voice." This framing positions oral examination as a more dynamic assessment where candidates can "rebut arguments, clarify confusions and ambiguities, take advice for improvements, and get developmental feedback." The paper distinguishes between 'viva', 'oral defence', 'thesis defence', and 'oral examination' as terms with potentially different pedagogical purposes.

Why it matters: The "living voice" etymology is compelling for Harvard's framing—it positions oral assignments not as a retreat from AI but as a return to something more authentically human and dialogic.

───

:mag: Analytical Note:

The historical record reveals an irony: written exams were the disruptive technology of the 19th century, displacing oral traditions at Oxbridge and elsewhere. The shift was driven by scale (more students, need for standardization) and Enlightenment ideals of objectivity. Now AI threatens the written paradigm, and we may be witnessing a counter-revolution back toward speech.

For Harvard faculty, this history suggests oral assignments aren't a desperate workaround—they're a return to the oldest traditions of the university.

───

:date: Next theme: Peer institutions (Yale, Stanford, Princeton, MIT) — how are they responding to AI with oral assessment policies?

[Automated research post 1 of ~16 • Project runs through Mon 2/24]
WikipediaOral examThe oral exam (also oral test or viva voce; Rigorosum in German-speaking nations) is a practice in many schools and disciplines in which an examiner poses questions to the student in spoken form. The student has to answer the question in such a way as to demonstrate sufficient knowledge of the subject to pass the exam. The oral exam also helps reduce (although it does not eliminate) the risk of granting a degree to a candidate who has had the thesis or dissertation ghostwritten by an expert.https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Oral_examche.ac.zaBrieflySpeaking No. 30 - Viva Voce: The Oral Examination of a Thesis for a Doctoral Degree | Council on Higher EducationThe Latin term viva voce means "the living voice". The oral examination of a thesis for a doctoral degree is thus seen to be a more 'living' assessment opportunity whereby the candidate can have a conversation with examiners, rebut arguments, clarify any confusions and ambiguities, take advice for improvements of the thesis, and get developmental feedback for future research endeavours. The event is variously referred to as a 'viva', 'oral defence', 'thesis defence', or 'oral examination', with the wording possibly implying differences in purpose (Murray, 2002). [6:33 AM]:mortar_board: Peer Institutions Responding to AI Anxieties — Oral Assignments

Theme 1 of 8: How elite universities are adapting assessment in response to ChatGPT and generative AI

───

1. University of Sydney's "Two-Lane Approach" to Assessment (2023-2025)

Source: Teaching@Sydney, "Aligning our assessments to the age of generative AI"
URL: https://educational-innovation.sydney.edu.au/teaching@sydney/aligning-our-assessments-to-the-age-of-generative-ai/

Sydney has implemented a comprehensive institutional framework dividing all assessments into two categories: "Secure" (Lane 1) — in-person, supervised assessments including interactive oral assessments, vivas, and exams where AI may or may not be allowed; and "Open" (Lane 2) — unsupervised assessments that scaffold responsible AI use. The approach stems from TEQSA-commissioned research demonstrating that AI can complete virtually all take-home assessments "at a passable level without detection," including essays, presentations, reflections, and even online oral exams using voice cloning. Sydney's response is pragmatic: secure assessments verify what students actually know, while open assessments teach them to work ethically with AI tools.

Harvard relevance: This is perhaps the most comprehensive peer institutional response. Sydney's framework addresses both the "verification problem" (can we trust this student did the work?) and the "preparation problem" (are we teaching skills for an AI-saturated world?). The emphasis on interactive oral assessments as a scalable, secure verification method directly speaks to Harvard faculty concerns. Note the realism: they acknowledge that even "oral exams" can be gamed online (deepfakes), hence the emphasis on in-person interactive formats. For a residential college like Harvard, this is achievable at scale.

───

2. Harvard's Own FAS Guidance on Oral Defenses (2024-2026)

Source: Office of Academic Integrity and Student Conduct, "Academic Integrity and Teaching With(out) AI"
URL: https://oaisc.fas.harvard.edu/academic-integrity-and-teaching-without-ai/

Harvard FAS has published guidance for faculty both encouraging and discouraging AI use. For faculty who wish to verify learning despite AI availability, the office explicitly recommends "Oral Defenses: Use oral presentations to evaluate comprehension, allowing students to explain their process, including AI application." The guidance positions oral assessment as a way to evaluate not just content knowledge but metacognitive understanding — students must articulate their thinking process, decision-making, and how they used (or didn't use) AI tools.

Harvard relevance: This is internal Harvard guidance, which means there's already institutional recognition of oral assessment as a viable response to AI anxieties. The framing is sophisticated: oral defenses aren't just "AI-proof" verification tools, but pedagogically valuable opportunities for students to demonstrate higher-order thinking. The challenge: how to scale this beyond graduate thesis defenses into undergraduate coursework. Worth exploring what FAS courses have actually implemented this, and what happened.

───

3. Princeton's McGraw Center Guidance on AI/ChatGPT (2023)

Source: Princeton University, McGraw Center for Teaching and Learning
URL: https://mcgraw.princeton.edu/guidance-aichatgpt

Princeton's teaching center recommends faculty "be explicit about your AI/ChatGPT policy" and "design assignments that require critical thinking and ask students to demonstrate their process." The guidance stops short of mandating specific assessment formats but emphasizes process-oriented, personalized assignments that are harder for AI to replicate. Princeton Alumni Weekly reported faculty suggestions including requiring students to show their work, explain their reasoning, and engage in formats that demonstrate understanding beyond final products.
[6:33 AM]Harvard relevance: Princeton's approach is more laissez-faire than Sydney's — individual faculty autonomy rather than institution-wide framework. This mirrors Harvard's traditional culture of professorial independence. However, the common thread across all three institutions is the shift from product (the essay, the paper) to process (how you think, how you explain). Oral assignments are a natural fit for process assessment. The question for Harvard: do we need a more prescriptive framework (like Sydney), or can distributed faculty experimentation work?

───

Connecting the Dots

All three institutions recognize that traditional take-home written assignments are vulnerable to AI completion. All three recommend some form of synchronous, interactive, process-oriented assessment. Sydney has gone furthest with a binary institutional framework; Harvard FAS has issued guidance but left implementation to faculty; Princeton emphasizes process and explicit policies.

The residential college advantage: Unlike online universities, Harvard (like Sydney and Princeton) can feasibly implement in-person oral assessments at scale. The question isn't "can we do this?" but "should we, and how?" — balancing verification of learning with preparation for an AI-saturated professional world.

Next themes coming: Harvard's own history with oral exams, educational research on speaking/learning, post-AI classroom experiments, AI in oral evaluation, historical references (Plato!), and professional domains.

Research continues through Monday Feb 24, 9am EST.

:books: Oral Assignments Research Update #2
Theme: Peer Institutions' AI Response Policies (Stanford, Yale, MIT, Princeton)

───

Source 1: Stanford Academic Integrity Working Group (Dec 2025)
https://studentaffairs.stanford.edu/news/academic-integrity-working-group-addresses-generative-ai-and-exam-policies

The smoking gun for Harvard's consideration: Stanford's AIWG explicitly recommends "in-person formats such as oral exams and in-class writing assignments" for instructors who wish to limit AI use in high-stakes assessments. The group found AI detection tools unreliable—prone to bias, false positives/negatives, and unable to assess mixed human/AI writing. Their proctoring pilot now includes 50+ courses.

Why it matters: Stanford has moved beyond detection to redesign. Their official recommendation of oral exams validates Harvard's potential pivot. This is peer precedent from a direct competitor.

───

Source 2: Mashable — "From Yale to MIT to UCLA: The AI policies of the nation's biggest colleges" (Sept 2025)
https://mashable.com/article/university-ai-policies-what-to-know

Comprehensive survey revealing the fragmented landscape: Princeton prohibits non-Princeton AI tools for academic requirements; most policies defer to individual instructors; 85%+ of students report using generative AI for coursework while faculty adoption lags. Notable: A Gallup poll found nearly half of students are unsure of their school's AI stance.

Why it matters: The "instructor discretion" model creates inconsistency and places burden on individual faculty. Harvard could differentiate by establishing institution-wide oral assessment frameworks rather than leaving each professor to reinvent the wheel.

───

Source 3: Thesify — "Generative AI Policies at the World's Top Universities: October 2025 Update"
https://www.thesify.ai/blog/gen-ai-policies-update-2025

Systematic analysis of top-20 universities (THE rankings). Key patterns:
• Oxford/Cambridge: AI allowed for personal study but banned in summative assessment unless explicitly permitted
• MIT: Focus on data privacy; course-specific rules set by departments
• Princeton: Disclosure required; some courses require students to keep AI chat logs for verification
• Columbia: New draft policy prohibits AI use without explicit permission

Why it matters: No major peer has yet positioned oral assessment as a systematic institutional response. The landscape is reactive (detection, disclosure, prohibition) rather than pedagogically proactive. This is the gap Harvard could fill.

───

:mag: Analytical Note:

The peer landscape reveals three response modes:

1. Detection-focused (increasingly abandoned as tools prove unreliable)
2. Disclosure-focused (Princeton's chat logs, Columbia's explicit permission)
3. Redesign-focused (Stanford's oral exam recommendation)

Most institutions are stuck oscillating between modes 1 and 2. Stanford's move toward mode 3 is notable but still framed as a "restriction" strategy. Harvard has an opportunity to reframe oral assessment not as AI-avoidance but as a positive pedagogical choice—returning to what the university historically did best.

───

Theme 2 of 8: Harvard's own traditions of oral examination from colonial times to present

───

1. Colonial Harvard's "Forensic Disputations" (1640s-1770s)

Source: Colonial Society of Massachusetts, "Harvard College on the Eve of the American Revolution"
URL: https://www.colonialsociety.org/node/1239

In pre-Revolutionary Harvard, the curriculum explicitly trained students in "Latin orations and syllogistic disputations." Forensic disputations — formal oral debates on philosophical and political questions — were public performances at annual commencements. During 1765-1773, these disputations increasingly reflected tensions between England and the colonies, with students arguing contemporary political questions orally before the academic community. The format was both assessment and spectacle: students demonstrated mastery through live argumentation, not written papers.

Harvard relevance: Oral examination isn't a novel response to AI — it's a return to Harvard's founding pedagogy. For over 130 years (1636-1770s), oral disputation was the capstone demonstration of learning. The shift to written exams came later, driven by scale and standardization (echo of Oxbridge pattern documented in Theme 1). Today's faculty considering oral assessment aren't innovating — they're recovering an older, arguably more authentic Harvard tradition. This reframe matters psychologically and institutionally.

───

2. History & Literature Senior Oral Exam (Current Practice)

Source: Harvard FAS History & Literature Concentration
URL: https://histlit.fas.harvard.edu/senior-oral-exam

Harvard still requires oral examinations in certain concentrations. All History & Literature seniors eligible for honors take a mandatory one-hour oral exam administered by three faculty members. The exam covers both the student's thesis (10 minutes) and five prepared topics (45 minutes), each comprising 6-8 primary and secondary sources. Students must "articulate the argument of each topic," engage in close reading, and demonstrate interdisciplinary connections. Examiners probe understanding with follow-up questions tailored to student responses — impossible to replicate in written format.

Harvard relevance: This is proof of concept at Harvard itself. The History & Literature concentration demonstrates that rigorous oral assessment already works for undergraduates in a residential college setting. The format balances preparation (students compile topics lists weeks in advance) with spontaneity (examiners can probe gaps, ask for synthesis). Key insight: oral exams test different competencies than written work — articulation, thinking on your feet, synthesizing across texts in real-time. If one concentration can do this systematically, why not others? The infrastructure exists; the question is scaling and adaptation.

───

3. Harvard Library Archives: 17th-18th Century Curriculum Records

Source: Harvard Library Research Guide, "Harvard in the 17th and 18th Centuries"
URL: https://guides.library.harvard.edu/c.php?g=405381&p=6465805

The Harvard University Archives contain extensive documentation of early curricula, including details on "thesis disputation topics" that shaped student assessment from 1640-1800. These records show how each Harvard president influenced the "academic landscape" through oral examination topics and formats. Samuel Eliot Morison's Harvard College in the Seventeenth Century (1936) includes detailed chapters on curriculum and student life, documenting that oral performance — prayers, recitations, disputations — structured the daily rhythm of undergraduate life far more than written work.
guides.library.harvard.eduResearch Guides: Harvard in the 17th and 18th Centuries: Harvard College Curriculum, 1640-1800: Overview and Research SourcesResearch Guides: Harvard in the 17th and 18th Centuries: Harvard College Curriculum, 1640-1800: Overview and Research Sources[6:47 AM]Harvard relevance: The archives are a goldmine for faculty interested in historical precedent. A working group could commission an undergraduate or graduate research project to map the evolution of assessment formats at Harvard from 1636-present, identifying when and why the shift from oral to written occurred, and whether the reasons that drove that shift (standardization, efficiency, objectivity) still hold in the AI era. Making this history visible — perhaps in a symposium or white paper — would ground the conversation in Harvard's own institutional DNA rather than treating oral assessment as imported from elsewhere.

───

Connecting the Dots

Harvard's relationship with oral assessment traces a clear arc:

• 1636-1770s: Oral disputation as default capstone assessment
• 1800s-1900s: Shift to written exams (following broader higher ed trends)
• 1900s-present: Oral exams preserved in graduate programs and select undergraduate concentrations (History & Lit, many doctoral defenses)
• 2020s: AI disruption prompts reconsideration of assessment formats

The key insight: For most of Harvard's history, oral examination was normal. The written paradigm is only ~150-200 years old. Faculty considering oral assignments today aren't abandoning tradition — they're reclaiming it.

Practical next step: Convene current History & Literature faculty and students who've experienced the senior oral exam. What works? What's difficult? How could the format adapt to larger courses? Learn from the concentrations that never abandoned oral tradition.

Next themes: Educational research on speaking/learning (SoTL), post-AI classroom experiments, AI in oral evaluation, Plato on speech vs writing, professional domains (law, theatre, business).

Research continues through Monday Feb 24, 9am EST.

:books: Oral Assignments Research Update #3
Theme: History of Oral Examinations at Harvard University

───

Source 1: Harvard Library — "Early Curriculum at Harvard: Historical Sources"
https://guides.library.harvard.edu/hua/earlycurriculum

This archival research guide reveals that Harvard's original curriculum was fundamentally oral. Key details:
• Latin was the language of instruction (requirement not renewed after 1692)
• "Commencement theses and quaestiones" (disputations) were central to academic life
• Student assessment involved thesis disputations supervised by each president
• The 1643 pamphlet New Englands First Fruits documents the curriculum's oral examination structure

Why it matters: Harvard wasn't founded as a written-exam institution—it was founded as an oral one. The disputation tradition was core to the mission of "advancing Learning and perpetuating it to Posterity." Any return to oral assessment isn't a novelty; it's a homecoming.

───

Source 2: StateUniversity.com — "Harvard University: Curriculum, Faculty"
https://education.stateuniversity.com/pages/2031/Harvard-University.html

A key passage: "The most common forms of instruction were oral exercises—the lecture, the declamation, and the disputation." The article traces how this prescribed oral curriculum lasted through the 18th century before Eliot's elective system (1869-1909) modernized Harvard. The shift toward written exams was part of a larger move toward specialization and the "freest elective system in the country."

Why it matters: This frames the written exam not as Harvard's heritage but as a modernist intervention. The oral tradition—lecture, declamation, disputation—was the original pedagogical DNA. Written exams came later, in service of scale and specialization.

───

Source 3: Harvard History & Literature — "Senior Oral Exam" (Current Practice)
https://histlit.fas.harvard.edu/senior-oral-exam

History & Literature still requires honors seniors to complete a one-hour oral examination administered by three faculty members. Students prepare five topics with 6-8 sources each, then defend their thesis and demonstrate "intellectual range and dexterity" across their coursework. Evaluation criteria include "the ability to articulate the argument of each topic" and "level of creativity."

Why it matters: The oral tradition isn't just history at Harvard—it survives in pockets. Hist & Lit and Social Studies maintain rigorous oral exams. These programs offer living case studies of how oral assessment scales and how faculty evaluate speaking. They're proof of concept within Harvard's own walls.

───

:mag: Analytical Note:

The historical arc is striking:
• 1636-1800s: Oral disputation as the dominant mode (Latin, public, argumentative)
• 1800s-1900s: Gradual shift to written exams (Eliot's reforms, scale, specialization)
• 2020s: AI disrupts written assessment; oral methods become strategically relevant again

Harvard's own history provides the rhetorical ammunition: "We're not abandoning our traditions—we're recovering them." The History & Literature and Social Studies programs demonstrate that oral exams can work at scale with clear rubrics and faculty buy-in.

For faculty considering oral assignments, the question isn't "Can we do this?" but "Why did we ever stop?"

───

:date: Next theme: Educational research on speaking in learning and assessment

[Automated research post 3 of ~16 • Project runs through Mon 2/24]
guides.library.harvard.eduResearch Guides: Early Curriculum at Harvard: Historical Sources: Introduction and Historical OverviewResearch Guides: Early Curriculum at Harvard: Historical Sources: Introduction and Historical Overview[7:02 AM]:bar_chart: Educational Research on Speaking & Learning — The SoTL Evidence

Theme 3 of 8: Scholarship of Teaching and Learning (SoTL) on oral communication in assessment

───

1. Joughin (1998): "Dimensions of Oral Assessment" — Foundational Framework

Source: Assessment & Evaluation in Higher Education, Vol. 23, No. 4
URL: https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/abs/10.1080/0260293980230404

Gordon Joughin's seminal 1998 paper established the theoretical framework for understanding oral assessment in higher education. Through systematic literature analysis, Joughin identified six key dimensions: (1) primary content type (what is being assessed), (2) interaction (one-way presentation vs. dialogue), (3) authenticity (how closely assessment mirrors real-world tasks), (4) structure (degree of standardization), (5) examiners (number, role, expertise), and (6) orality (importance of spoken medium itself). Crucially, Joughin argued that oral exams enable measurement of two distinct qualities: student command of the oral medium itself, and student command of content knowledge.

Harvard relevance: This framework provides a design toolkit for faculty. When designing oral assessments, instructors must make explicit choices along these six dimensions. For example: Will this be a dialogue (interactive viva) or presentation (one-way)? How much structure is needed to ensure fairness? Should we assess oral fluency itself, or use oral format purely to probe content understanding? The framework prevents ad-hoc implementation and grounds oral assessment in 25+ years of educational research. This isn't experimental pedagogy—it's evidence-based practice.

───

2. Iannone & Simpson (2020): Oral Assessment Changes How Students Learn

Source: Educational Studies in Mathematics, Springer
URL: https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s10649-020-09937-4

This empirical case study at a UK research university investigated whether introducing high-stakes oral exams in upper-level mathematics courses changed students' approaches to learning. Drawing on Marton & Säljö's deep/surface learning framework, the researchers found that oral assessment shifted students from surface learning (memorization, passive acceptance) to deep learning (understanding, making connections, intrinsic motivation). Students reported that oral exams required them to "understand concepts" rather than memorize procedures, because examiners could ask follow-up questions probing real comprehension. The unpredictability of dialogue prevented rote preparation.

Harvard relevance: This directly addresses the AI anxiety. If students know an exam will involve interactive dialogue—not just regurgitation—they can't rely on memorized (or AI-generated) scripts. They must genuinely understand. The study also revealed a key insight: what students perceive as assessment demands shapes how they study. If faculty want deep learning, assessment must demand it. Oral formats inherently do. The study is peer-reviewed, published in a top education journal, and replicable—exactly the kind of evidence Harvard faculty expect.

───

3. Systematic Review (2025): What Makes Oral Assessment Work?

Source: Assessment & Evaluation in Higher Education (Taylor & Francis)
URL: https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/02602938.2025.2504621

A just-published systematic review synthesized decades of research on interventions that improve student performance in oral assessments. Key facilitators identified: (1) structured practice opportunities (students need repeated low-stakes oral practice before high-stakes exams), (2) timely, constructive feedback from instructors and peers, (3) technology integration (e.g., virtual reality for practice, recorded self-review), and (4) self-reflective strategies (metacognitive prompting). The review emphasizes that oral assessment isn't "sink or swim"—effective implementation requires scaffolding throughout the course.
SpringerLinkThe impact of high stakes oral performance assessment on students’ approaches to learning: a case studyEducational Studies in Mathematics - This paper presents findings from a case study on the impact of high stakes oral performance assessment on third year mathematics students’ approaches to...https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s10649-020-09937-4[7:02 AM]Harvard relevance: This is the implementation roadmap. Faculty worry: "How do I prepare students who've never done oral exams?" The answer: build oral practice into the semester. Low-stakes oral check-ins (office hours, small-group discussions, peer presentations) develop the skill before high-stakes assessment. The review also highlights equity: oral assessment can replicate class/race-based "articulateness" norms if not carefully designed. Solution: explicit rubrics, practice with feedback, and assessment of understanding (not performance polish). Harvard's resources—small class sizes, tutorial systems, teaching fellows—make scaffolded oral assessment feasible.

───

Connecting the Dots

The SoTL evidence reveals three critical insights:

1. Oral assessment is multidimensional (Joughin) — faculty must design intentionally along 6 axes, not improvise.
2. Oral assessment changes learning processes (Iannone & Simpson) — students adopt deeper approaches when they know dialogue awaits.
3. Oral assessment requires scaffolding (Systematic review) — practice, feedback, and technology support are essential.

The pedagogical case is strong: Oral assessment isn't just AI-resistant; it's pedagogically superior for certain learning outcomes (synthesis, argumentation, real-time problem-solving). The AI disruption is an opportunity to recover a more authentic, dialogic mode of assessment that higher education mostly abandoned for efficiency reasons.

Critical caveat: The research also flags risks—anxiety, inequity, lack of student preparation. These are solvable through course design (scaffolding) and rubric design (transparency), but they require institutional commitment beyond individual faculty experimentation.

Next question: Are any Harvard faculty already experimenting post-2023? (Theme 4 will explore this.)

Next themes: Post-AI classroom experiments, AI in oral evaluation (transcription/grading), Plato on speech vs writing, professional domains (law, theatre, business).

Research continues through Monday Feb 24, 9am EST.
elle-with-claws  [7:16 AM]
:books: Oral Assignments Research Update #4
Theme: Educational Research on Speaking in Learning & Assessment

───

Source 1: Frontiers in Education — "Exploring alternative assessments during COVID: Instructor experiences using oral exams" (2024)
https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/education/articles/10.3389/feduc.2024.1379886/full

This empirical study from chemistry education provides robust evidence for oral assessment benefits:
• Deep learning: "Students are often more prepared for oral discussions... which motivates them to engage with the material at a deeper level—the assessment requires students do more than just memorize and regurgitate"
• Expert-novice interaction: Oral exams create intentional space for "cognitive apprenticeship" where experts make invisible thinking visible
• Equity considerations: May benefit non-native English speakers and students with dyslexia/dysgraphia who struggle with timed written exams
• Authenticity: Students report feeling "like a professional"—oral communication is how knowledge actually functions in careers

Why it matters: This addresses the "but will students learn?" question empirically. The answer is yes—and potentially more than with written exams.

───

Source 2: Structural Learning — "Oracy in the classroom: A teacher's guide"
https://www.structural-learning.com/post/the-importance-of-oracy-in-language-development

Introduces the Four-Pillar Oracy Framework from UK education research (drawing on Neil Mercer's work on dialogic teaching):

1. Evidence and reasoning — constructing arguments
2. Response and listening — building on others' ideas
3. Delivery and expression — clarity and register
4. Prioritisation and organisation — structuring thought

Key insight: "Oracy is to communicate what literacy is to reading and writing." The article cites research showing post-pandemic "oracy gaps" particularly affecting disadvantaged students.

Why it matters: This framework gives Harvard faculty a vocabulary for designing and assessing oral assignments. It's not just "talk more"—it's structured, assessable skill development.

───

Source 3: Taylor & Francis — "Interventions and facilitators of oral assessment performance in higher education: A systematic review" (2025)
https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/02602938.2025.2504621

A brand-new systematic review examining what factors facilitate student performance in oral assessments across disciplines. Key finding: preparation strategies, rubric clarity, and low-stakes practice all significantly improve outcomes. The review validates oral assessment as a serious, research-backed pedagogical choice.

Why it matters: For skeptical colleagues, this is ammunition—a meta-level synthesis showing oral assessment isn't anecdotal or nostalgic but supported by a growing empirical literature.

───

:mag: Analytical Note:

The educational research case for oral assessment rests on three pillars:

1. Deeper learning: Oral formats promote elaboration and explanation, which cognitive science shows enhances retention and understanding
2. Authentic assessment: Oral communication mirrors how professionals actually use knowledge—no one in industry submits a timed essay
3. Equity potential: For some students (ESL, learning differences), oral formats may be more equitable than written ones

The challenge Harvard faculty face isn't whether oral assessment works—it's whether they have time to implement it. That's a resource question, not a pedagogical one.

Theme 4 of 8: Real educators implementing oral assessments since ChatGPT's arrival (2023-2026)

───

1. Princeton History Professor Michael Brinley: "Oral Midterms Instead of Essays"

Source: Princeton Alumni Weekly, "What's Foul and Fair When Students Use AI?"
URL: https://paw.princeton.edu/article/whats-foul-and-fair-when-students-use-ai

History professor Michael Brinley no longer assigns essays in his Soviet history class, replacing them with "a mix of in-class quizzes, an oral midterm, and an in-person final." The shift was driven by concerns about fairness: "If some students [use AI] and others don't, that doesn't seem very fair for a graded exercise." The article notes that Princeton's 2025 Senior Survey found 25% of AB students and 37% of BSE students admitted using LLMs when not allowed. Brinley is among many Princeton faculty who recently restructured assignments, abandoning take-home assessments for in-person formats.

Harvard relevance: This is a peer institution case study—a Princeton history professor making the exact choice Harvard faculty are contemplating. Brinley's reasoning is instructive: the problem isn't pedagogical preference for oral formats, it's ethical imperative. Written take-home work has become inequitable because enforcement is impossible and accusations are career-damaging. Students themselves report feeling "trapped in an uncomfortable bind: Use AI illicitly, or risk slipping behind." The oral midterm isn't a punitive response—it's a structural solution that restores fair competition. If Princeton can do this in Soviet history, Harvard can do it anywhere.

───

2. Harvard Crimson Student Op-Ed: "Harvard Needs the Kind of Exams AI Can't Take"

Source: The Harvard Crimson (Opinion)
URL: https://www.thecrimson.com/article/2025/9/8/dolan-harvard-oral-exam/

Victoria Dolan '28, a Crimson editorial editor, argues that Harvard should adopt "in-class essays, one-on-one defenses of final papers, or full oral examinations" to combat AI misuse. She cites a 2024 survey finding 90% of Harvard undergraduates use generative AI, with most using it at least weekly. Her proposal: students who submit research papers should be required to complete a "10-minute conversation with their professor" to prove authorship, or undergo "extensive final oral examination on course material." She notes History & Literature and Social Studies already require oral exams, proving feasibility.

Harvard relevance: This is student demand—not faculty resistance. A Harvard undergraduate is publicly advocating for harder assessments because she sees peers gaming the system and feels disadvantaged for doing her own work. The op-ed reveals the hidden cost of AI anxiety: erosion of trust. Students no longer believe grades reflect merit. Dolan's proposal for "one-on-one defenses" is particularly scalable: a brief oral component appended to existing papers, not a wholesale curriculum overhaul. Her point about professional preparation is also sharp: "If students learn to articulate their ideas now, they'll be better equipped to argue with their relatives on Thanksgiving, advocate for themselves in the workforce, or defend a master's or PhD thesis."

───

3. National Trend Coverage: "Professors Are Turning to This Old-School Method to Stop AI Use"

Source: The Washington Post, "College professors use oral exams to combat AI"
URL: https://www.washingtonpost.com/education/2025/12/12/ai-artificial-intelligence-college-oral-exam/

The article quotes Tricia Bertram Gallant, director of UC San Diego's Academic Integrity Office: "Oral assessments are definitely experiencing a renaissance." Examples include University of Wyoming professor Catherine Hartmann, who redesigned courses to culminate in individual oral exams—students sit in her office for half-hour conversations rather than submitting essays. The trend began during the pandemic (cheating fears in online environments) and accelerated post-2023 with ChatGPT.

Harvard relevance: Harvard isn't alone—this is a national movement. The Post documents faculty at Wyoming, UCSD, and multiple institutions independently arriving at the same solution. The pattern suggests oral assessment isn't a fringe experiment but an emerging best practice. Importantly, the article frames this as faculty adaptation, not institutional mandate. Professors are making tactical decisions to preserve learning integrity, not waiting for policy committees. Harvard faculty have the autonomy to do likewise. The "renaissance" framing is also strategic: it positions oral exams as recovery of tradition, not desperate innovation.

───

Connecting the Dots

The post-2023 evidence reveals three truths:

1. Faculty are acting now — Princeton (Brinley), Wyoming (Hartmann), and many others have already shifted to oral formats. This isn't theoretical; it's operational.
2. Students see the problem — The Harvard Crimson op-ed and Princeton survey data show undergraduates themselves recognize the equity crisis. Many want harder, AI-proof assessments to restore meritocracy.
3. This is a national pattern, not isolated panic — The Washington Post, Inside Higher Ed, and Princeton Alumni Weekly all covered the trend in 2025. Oral assessment renaissance is happening institution-by-institution, course-by-course.

The Harvard choice: Wait for comprehensive institutional policy (slow, committee-bound, risk-averse), or empower individual faculty to experiment now (fast, evidence-generating, reversible). The post-AI classroom experiments show the second path is viable—and already underway at peer institutions.

Key caveat: Not all experiments succeed. Faculty report challenges with time, student anxiety, and grading consistency. But the alternative—continuing take-home essays amid 90% AI use—is status quo failure. The question isn't "Will oral exams work perfectly?" but "What's the least-bad option right now?"

Next themes: AI in oral evaluation (transcription, AI graders, AI question-generators), Plato on speech vs writing, professional domains (law, theatre, business).

Theme: Post-AI Classroom Experiments with Oral Exams

───

Source 1: Washington Post — "College professors use oral exams to combat AI" (Dec 2025)
https://www.washingtonpost.com/education/2025/12/12/ai-artificial-intelligence-college-oral-exam/

(Unable to fetch full text, but search description confirms:) "A small but growing number of educators are experimenting with oral exams to circumvent the temptations presented by powerful AI platforms." This represents mainstream media recognizing the trend.

Why it matters: When the Washington Post covers faculty experimenting with oral exams as an AI response, the conversation has moved from academic journals to public discourse. Harvard faculty can position themselves as leading a recognized national movement.

───

Source 2: AcademyNC — "Oral Exams in AI Era: New Assessment Solutions for ChatGPT Age" (Aug 2025)
https://academync.com/articles/oral-exams-in-ai-era

Key statistics and insights:
• 56% of college students have used AI to complete assignments or exams
• AI detection tools range from 33% to 81% accuracy—essentially unreliable
• Oral exams provide "immediate authentication"—you can't ChatGPT your way through spontaneous questioning
• Students report feeling "like a professional" during oral assessment (authenticity)
• The article notes oral exams test "deep understanding" because examiners can probe with follow-up questions

Why it matters: This synthesizes the practical case: written assessment is compromised, detection doesn't work, oral formats are inherently AI-resistant because they require real-time demonstration of knowledge.

───

Source 3: TechyNerd — "Why Professors Are Reviving Oral Exams To Outsmart AI Cheating" (Dec 2025)
https://techynerdus.com/universities-revive-oral-exams-to-counter-ai-cheating-crisis/

Profiles specific faculty experiments:
• Professor Catherine Hartmann (U. Wyoming): Redesigned courses around individual 30-minute oral finals. "Likens learning to physical training—reliance on AI is equivalent to using machinery to bypass muscle development."
• Reports on scaling efforts at UC San Diego and Western Ontario for large courses using structured rubrics and trained TAs
• Key insight: "Instead of acting as detectives hunting for AI-generated text, educators can return to their primary role as evaluators of learning."

Why it matters: This answers the "but can it scale?" question. Real professors at real institutions are doing this with hundreds of students. The Wyoming model offers a replicable template.

───

:mag: Analytical Note:

The post-AI classroom experiments reveal a philosophical shift:

Old paradigm: Design assessments → Catch cheaters via detection
New paradigm: Design assessments that are inherently AI-resistant

Professor Darren Hick (Furman, quoted in GovTech) captured this after catching a ChatGPT cheater: "If I suspect students of submitting AI-written work, I'll give them an impromptu oral exam to test their knowledge."

This is reactive—oral as verification. But the more sophisticated approach is proactive—oral as the primary assessment mode, eliminating the cat-and-mouse game entirely.

For Harvard faculty, the message is clear: stop trying to detect AI. Start designing assessment that makes AI irrelevant.

Theme 5 of 8: Using AI tools to conduct, transcribe, and grade oral assessments

───

1. Georgia Tech's "Socratic Mind" — AI-Powered Oral Examiner (2024-2026)

Source: Georgia Tech News, "AI Oral Assessment Tool Uses Socratic Method to Test Students' Knowledge"
URL: https://www.gatech.edu/news/2024/09/24/ai-oral-assessment-tool-uses-socratic-method-test-students-knowledge

Ray Hung (Georgia Tech master's student) and Professor Thad Starner developed Socratic Mind, an AI-powered platform that conducts oral exams using Socratic questioning. The system engages students in real-time dialogue, asking follow-up questions to probe understanding—not just accepting memorized answers. Piloted with 5,000+ students at Georgia Tech and UC San Diego, the platform achieved 70-95% positive student experience with statistically significant learning improvements. Students reported that the AI is "not just conversational but also adverse to human persuasion"—unlike ChatGPT, it won't agree with you just because you argue forcefully. The platform won a $50,000 Catalyst Award at the Learning Engineering Tools Competition.

Harvard relevance: This is the ironic solution—using AI to conduct AI-resistant assessments. The key insight: Socratic Mind doesn't just ask questions; it follows up on shallow answers. Students can't memorize ChatGPT responses because the conversation is dynamic. Scalability is the breakthrough: Professor Joyner is using this with 2,000+ students at Georgia Tech, making oral assessment feasible for large lecture courses. For Harvard, this could bridge the gap between "oral exams are pedagogically superior" and "we don't have time to interview 600 students." The platform also serves as practice infrastructure—students can rehearse defending their thinking before high-stakes oral finals.

───

2. NYU Professor's ElevenLabs Voice Agent — "Fighting Fire With Fire" (2025)

Source: Behind the Enemy Lines (faculty blog), "Fighting Fire with Fire: Scalable Personalized Oral Exams with an ElevenLabs Voice AI Agent"
URL: https://www.behind-the-enemy-lines.com/2025/12/fighting-fire-with-fire-scalable-oral.html

An NYU Stern professor ran final exams for 36 students in an AI/ML Product Management course using an ElevenLabs voice AI agent. The system conducted two-part exams: (1) "Talk me through your project" with personalized questions based on each student's capstone, and (2) "Do a case" testing course material comprehension. Cost: $0.42 per student ($15 total for 36 students, plus $99/month subscription). Average exam: 25 minutes, 65 conversational turns. Grading used a "council of LLMs" approach—Claude, Gemini, and ChatGPT each assessed transcripts independently, then revised after seeing each other's assessments. Result: 89% of AI grades within 1 point of human judgment.

Harvard relevance: The economics are stunning. Traditional approach: 36 students × 25 min × 2 graders = 30 hours at ~$750 (TA rates) or "we don't do this" (faculty rates). AI approach: $15. The professor notes: "For 15 dollars, we got real-time oral examination, a three-model grading council with deliberation, structured feedback with verbatim quotes, and a complete audit trail." This demolishes the scalability objection. The "council of LLMs" grading is methodologically sophisticated—it avoids single-model bias by requiring consensus. For Harvard faculty worried about workload, this is a proof of concept: AI can administer oral exams, not just prevent cheating on them.

───

3. Sherpa AI — Asynchronous Video Oral Assessments (2023)

Source: EdSurge, "As AI Chatbots Rise, More Educators Look to Oral Exams — With High-Tech Twist"
URL: https://www.edsurge.com/news/2023-10-05-as-ai-chatbots-rise-more-educators-look-to-oral-exams-with-high-tech-twist

In our new "AI/ML Product Management" class (co-taught with Konstantinos Rizakos ), the "pre-case" submi...https://www.behind-the-enemy-lines.com/2025/12/fighting-fire-with-fire-scalable-oral.htmlEdSurgeAs AI Chatbots Rise, More Educators Look to Oral Exams — With High-Tech Twist  - EdSurge NewsSince the release of ChatGPT late last year, the essay has been declared dead as an effective way to measure learning. After all, students can now ...Oct 5th, 2023https://www.edsurge.com/news/2023-10-05-as-ai-chatbots-rise-more-educators-look-to-oral-exams-with-high-tech-twist[7:33 AM]Stanford students built Sherpa, an AI tool that asks students questions about assigned readings via webcam, records their responses, transcribes audio, and flags weak answers for instructor review. Teachers upload readings or student papers; Sherpa generates or uses instructor-provided questions; students record video responses; AI transcribes and highlights areas "where the student answer seemed off point." Beth Carlson (Maine HS English teacher) used Sherpa for novel comprehension checks: "For the most part, it was spot on. The four students who got 'warnings' spoke too generally or answered something different than what was asked." The system uses Webb's Depth of Knowledge framework to adjust follow-up questions based on student performance.

Harvard relevance: This is the asynchronous oral exam—students don't need simultaneous appointments. Sherpa handles scheduling flexibility while preserving the anti-AI benefits of spoken assessment. The AI doesn't grade definitively; it triages for instructor attention. This hybrid model could work for courses with 100-300 students: Sherpa conducts initial screening, faculty review flagged cases, everyone gets oral assessment without 30 hours of Zoom calls. The concern: students could still use AI-generated notes during recording. But the dynamic follow-up questions (per Depth of Knowledge framework) limit this—if you don't actually understand, you can't improvise coherent answers to adaptive probing.

───

Connecting the Dots

The AI-in-oral-evaluation landscape reveals a productive paradox:

AI is both the problem and the solution.

• The problem: Students use ChatGPT to outsource written work, making traditional essays unreliable
• The solution: Use AI to conduct oral exams that ChatGPT can't help with in real-time dialogue

Three models emerging:

1. AI as examiner (Socratic Mind, ElevenLabs) — Conducts the conversation, asks follow-ups, scales to thousands
2. AI as transcriber (Sherpa, ElevenLabs) — Captures spoken responses, flags anomalies for human review
3. AI as grader (NYU council of LLMs) — Evaluates transcripts using multiple models with deliberation

The critical question for Harvard: Should faculty resist AI tools in assessment (preserving "pure" human judgment), or embrace them to make oral assessment scalable?

The research evidence suggests embrace: Georgia Tech's 5,000-student pilot and NYU's $0.42/student economics show this isn't experimental—it's operational. The alternative is continuing to assign essays that 90% of students (per Harvard's own survey) are AI-enhancing or AI-generating.

Equity consideration: AI-powered oral exams could increase access. Asynchronous platforms (Sherpa) eliminate scheduling conflicts. Transcription provides accommodations for hearing-impaired instructors. Multi-model grading reduces individual evaluator bias. But risks remain: voice recognition accuracy varies by accent, and students without quiet recording spaces face disadvantages.

Next frontier: Integration with learning management systems, real-time professor dashboards during AI-conducted exams, and RAG (retrieval-augmented generation) over student-submitted work so AI examiners can quote from papers and ask "Why did you write X?"

Next themes: Plato/Socrates on speech vs writing (philosophical grounding), professional domains (law, theatre, business) on orality.

:books: Oral Assignments Research Update #6
Theme: AI Tools IN Oral Evaluation (Transcription, AI Interviewers)

───

Source 1: EdSurge — "As AI Chatbots Rise, More Educators Look to Oral Exams — With High-Tech Twist" (Oct 2023)
https://www.edsurge.com/news/2023-10-05-as-ai-chatbots-rise-more-educators-look-to-oral-exams-with-high-tech-twist

Profiles Sherpa, a tool built by Stanford undergrads that:
• Uses AI to transcribe oral exam recordings
• Flags areas where student answers "seemed off point"
• Teachers review flagged transcripts rather than watching full recordings
• One teacher: "For the most part, it was spot on" in identifying students who skimmed rather than read

Also profiles Prof. Huihui Qi (UCSD) who won an NSF grant for oral exam research with 100-150 students using Zoom scheduling.

Why it matters: AI can assist oral evaluation without replacing the human judgment core. Transcription + flagging reduces grader fatigue while preserving authenticity.

───

Source 2: Behind the Enemy Lines — "Fighting Fire with Fire: Scalable Personalized Oral Exams with an ElevenLabs Voice AI Agent" (Dec 2025)
https://www.behind-the-enemy-lines.com/2025/12/fighting-fire-with-fire-scalable-oral.html

This is remarkable: An NYU professor built a Voice AI examiner using ElevenLabs for a 36-student AI/ML course. Results:
• $0.42 per student (vs. ~$750 for human grading at TA rates)
• 25-minute average exam, 65 messages per conversation
• 89% of LLM grades within 1 point of human grades
• "The feedback was better than any human would produce" — structured strengths/weaknesses with verbatim quotes
• Detailed lessons: don't stack questions, allow think-time, randomize case selection properly

Why it matters: This is the bleeding edge—using AI as the examiner itself. The economics are stunning. But notice: it worked because the format (oral exam) was AI-resistant even while the delivery mechanism used AI. The student still had to demonstrate real-time knowledge.

───

Source 3: The Teaching Astrophysicist — "The Future of Assessment: Embracing Oral Examinations in the Age of AI" (Nov 2024)
https://www.theteachingastrophysicist.com/post/the-future-of-assessment-embracing-oral-examinations-in-the-age-of-ai

Practical guide for K-12 through higher ed:
• Record and transcribe oral exams for flexible scheduling—teachers evaluate at their convenience
• Can be conducted by proctors/invigilators when primary teacher unavailable
• Application across subjects: literary analysis, math problem-solving explanation, science experiment discussion, historical debates
• Addresses accessibility: must accommodate speech/hearing impairments with alternatives

Why it matters: Recording + transcription solves the scalability problem while creating audit trails. The "asynchronous oral exam" is a real option.

───

:mag: Analytical Note:

A fascinating inversion is emerging:

Old framing: AI threatens assessment → oral exams are the "AI-resistant" solution
New framing: AI enables better oral assessment → AI + oral exams = scalable, consistent, well-documented evaluation

The NYU experiment is provocative: a Voice AI examiner that costs $0.42/student, grades consistently, and generates better feedback than humans. This doesn't replace human judgment—the council of LLMs still needed human calibration—but it radically changes the economics.

For Harvard faculty, the implication is: oral assessment doesn't have to mean "professor exhaustion." AI transcription, AI flagging, and even AI preliminary evaluation can reduce cognitive load while preserving the authenticity that makes oral assessment valuable.

The question isn't "AI or oral exams?" It's "How do we combine them?"

───
