The Teaching AstroThe Future of Assessment: Embracing Oral Examinations in the Age of AIWhy oral examinations should become a bigger part of teaching and learning and common assessments in the future.Nov 27th, 2024https://www.theteachingastrophysicist.com/post/the-future-of-assessment-embracing-oral-examinations-in-the-age-of-ai[7:47 AM]:scroll: Plato, Socrates & the Ancients — "A Phaedrus Moment"

Theme 6 of 8: Literary/historical references on speech vs writing (philosophical grounding for oral tradition)

───

1. Plato's Phaedrus — The Original Critique of Writing (circa 370 BCE)

Source: Farnam Street, "Plato's Argument Against Writing"
URL: https://fs.blog/an-old-argument-against-writing/

In Plato's dialogue Phaedrus, Socrates warns that writing "will implant forgetfulness in [people's] souls" because "they will cease to exercise memory, relying on that which is written, calling things to remembrance no longer from within themselves, but by means of external marks." He compares written texts to paintings: "If you question them, they maintain a most majestic silence... They seem to talk to you as though they were intelligent, but if you ask them anything about what they say, they go on telling just the same thing forever." Writing offers "not memory, but reminder... not true wisdom but only the semblance of wisdom." Socrates believed "face-to-face communication was the only way one person could transmit knowledge to another."

Harvard relevance: This is the foundational philosophical argument for oral assessment—2,400 years before ChatGPT. Socrates's critique applies eerily well to AI-generated essays: students submit text that "seems intelligent" but cannot defend when questioned, creating "the semblance of wisdom" rather than genuine understanding. The irony, of course, is that we know Socrates's argument only because Plato wrote it down—illustrating the tension between preservation and pedagogy. For Harvard faculty, this grounds the oral exam conversation in Western philosophy's bedrock: the Socratic dialogue as the gold standard for testing real comprehension through interactive questioning.

───

2. "A Phaedrus Moment" — Recognizing Technology Disruptions (2025)

Source: AI Log (Substack), "A Phaedrus Moment"
URL: https://www.ailog.blog/p/a-phaedrus-moment

This 2025 essay argues that ChatGPT is a "Phaedrus moment"—a cultural inflection point when "a new technology threatens the values people hold dear." The author notes Walter Ong's observation: "Plato's objections against writing are essentially the very same objections commonly urged today against computers." Medieval scribes resisted print ("copying by hand involves more diligence and industry"), just as we resist AI. The essay quotes John Warner: "ChatGPT forces us to confront what we value, rather than letting the status quo churn along unexamined." Socrates asked Phaedrus to read Lysias's speech aloud and then defend it—an oral exam testing whether admiration was justified.

Harvard relevance: This reframes AI anxiety as historical pattern recognition. Every major communication technology (writing, print, computers) triggered defensive reactions from incumbents who feared skill degradation. We're not experiencing something unprecedented—we're in the latest Phaedrus moment. The critical insight: Plato didn't reject writing outright; he used it strategically (dialogues preserved oral tradition's back-and-forth). Similarly, Harvard faculty needn't reject AI—but can design assessments (oral exams) that preserve what Socrates valued: interactive, defensive reasoning that can't be faked. The AI disruption isn't a crisis; it's an invitation to return to Socratic roots.

───

3. Walter Ong's Orality and Literacy — The Psychodynamics of Oral Culture (1982)

Source: New Learning Online, "Ong on the Differences between Orality and Literacy"
URL: https://newlearningonline.com/literacies/chapter-1/ong-on-the-differences-between-orality-and-literacy
Farnam StreetPlato's Argument Against WritingThe people who invent something new, create a new tool or technology are not necessarily the people who are going to understand what the social impact of those inventions are going to be.Written byShaneEst. reading time2 minutesFeb 4th, 2013https://fs.blog/an-old-argument-against-writing/ailog.blogA Phaedrus MomentIf Socrates was right, what do we make of Claude?https://www.ailog.blog/p/a-phaedrus-moment[7:47 AM]Walter Ong's seminal 1982 work identifies how oral cultures structure knowledge differently than literate ones. Oral thought is: (1) additive (stringing clauses with "and"), (2) aggregative (preferring "brave soldier" to "soldier"), (3) redundant (repeating for memory), (4) conservative ("wise elders" preserve knowledge), and (5) close to lived experience (situational rather than abstract). Writing "frees the mind of conservative tasks... and thus enables the mind to turn itself to new speculation." But writing also "imposes strain on the psyche" by forcing "sparsely linear or analytic thought"—an "artificial creation" compared to oral redundancy's naturalness.

Harvard relevance: Ong shows that orality and literacy are fundamentally different cognitive modes, not just delivery mechanisms. This matters for assessment design: written exams test ability to produce "sparsely linear analytic thought"—which AI excels at. Oral exams test ability to engage in "additive, aggregative, redundant" real-time dialogue—which AI struggles with (requires context-switching, spontaneity, defending claims under pressure). Ong's framework also explains why students find oral exams harder: they can't backloop into a text to retrieve context; they must hold knowledge "in the mind" and deploy it responsively. This is precisely what makes oral assessment pedagogically valuable—it tests internalized understanding, not externalized retrieval.

───

Connecting the Dots

The ancient and modern theorists of orality converge on three insights:

1. Writing changes cognition

• Socrates: Writing creates "forgetfulness" by externalizing memory
• Ong: Literacy restructures thought toward linearity, abstraction, analysis
• Implication: AI-generated essays test literacy skills that AI now replicates—oral dialogue tests orality skills AI can't fake

2. "Phaedrus moments" recur

• Each new communication technology (writing, print, computers, AI) triggers anxiety about authenticity and skill loss
• The pattern: incumbents resist, then adapt, preserving core values in new forms
• Implication: Oral exams aren't reactionary—they're adaptive, using ancient pedagogy to respond to new constraints

3. Dialogue tests real knowledge

• Socrates: "If you question them [texts], they maintain silence"—true understanding responds to probing
• Ong: Oral cultures value "the wise... who know and can tell"—performance, not just storage
• Implication: Oral assessment privileges defense over production—can you explain, justify, synthesize under pressure?

The philosophical justification for oral exams is ancient:

Plato used dialogues to preserve Socrates's method precisely because the method was interactive questioning. The written dialogue simulates oral examination. For 2,400 years, Western education has honored this in doctoral defenses, tutorials, Socratic seminars. AI disruption doesn't require inventing something new—it requires recovering what we abandoned for scale.

Key caveat: Socrates never wrote. We know him only through Plato's written dialogues. This tension is unresolved: pure orality is ephemeral, but writing risks replacing thinking. The Harvard task: design oral assessments that capture dialogue's authenticity while preserving outcomes (grades, records, equity). Technology (AI transcription, structured rubrics) can help—but only if the core remains Socratic: live, responsive, defensive dialogue.

Next question: If oral dialogue is the philosophical ideal, how do professional domains (law, theatre, business) implement this in practice? (Theme 7 will explore.)

Next themes: Professional domains (law, theatre, business/tech) on speech, performance, presentations.

Research continues through Monday Feb 24, 9am EST.
elle-with-claws  [8:06 AM]
:books: Oral Assignments Research Update #7
Theme: Philosophical/Literary Foundations (Plato, Socrates, Orality vs. Literacy)

───

Source 1: LitCharts — "The Limits of Writing" theme in Plato's Phaedrus
https://www.litcharts.com/lit/phaedrus/themes/the-limits-of-writing

Plato's Phaedrus contains the foundational Western critique of writing. Key arguments:

• Writing causes forgetfulness: "They will cease to exercise memory because they rely on that which is written... calling things to remembrance no longer from within themselves."
• Writing produces the appearance of wisdom: Students "will hear many things without being taught them, and will appear to know much when for the most part they know nothing."
• Writing is inert like painting: "If you ask them anything... they point to just one thing, the same each time." It cannot respond, adapt, or defend itself.
• Dialectic is superior: "Living, animate speech... of which written speech would rightly be called a kind of phantom." Only face-to-face discourse can be tailored to individual souls.

Why it matters: 2,400 years ago, Plato articulated exactly what concerns us now: a technology that outsources cognition produces students who appear knowledgeable but lack genuine understanding. AI is writing's logical endpoint—and oral dialogue remains the antidote.

───

Source 2: Farnam Street — "Plato's Argument Against Writing"
https://fs.blog/an-old-argument-against-writing/

Concise summary with a key insight: "The people who invent something new, create a new tool or technology, are not necessarily the people who are going to understand what the social impact of those inventions will be."

Socrates' quote: "If men learn this, it will implant forgetfulness in their souls... What you have discovered is a recipe not for memory, but for reminder."

Why it matters: The parallel to AI is exact. OpenAI invented ChatGPT; they cannot fully predict its educational impact. Like writing, AI is a "recipe for reminder"—it stores knowledge externally, leaving students with access but not understanding. The article's ironic close: "Oh the irony of having an argument against writing in a written text."

───

Source 3: New Learning Online — "Ong on the Differences between Orality and Literacy" (Walter Ong)
https://newlearningonline.com/literacies/chapter-1/ong-on-the-differences-between-orality-and-literacy

Walter Ong's Orality and Literacy (1982) identifies how oral cultures think differently:

• Aggregative and redundant: Oral thought is "the brave soldier," "the sturdy oak"—formulaic and repetitive to aid memory
• Close to the human lifeworld: Abstract lists don't exist in oral cultures; knowledge is always embedded in human action and relationship
• Agonistically toned: Oral cultures are competitive—proverbs and riddles challenge hearers to respond. Knowledge lives in struggle, not storage.
• Empathetic and participatory: "Learning or knowing means achieving close, empathetic, communal identification with the known."

Why it matters: Ong shows that oral and literate minds work differently. Oral assessment doesn't just measure knowledge—it cultivates a different cognitive relationship to knowledge: embodied, responsive, communal. This is what Harvard faculty are really asking for when they propose oral assignments.

───

:mag: Analytical Note:

The philosophical tradition offers Harvard a powerful reframe:

Surface argument: "Oral exams are AI-resistant."
Deeper argument: "Oral discourse cultivates genuine understanding in ways writing cannot."

Plato's Socrates wasn't anti-writing because writing was cheating. He was concerned that writing restructures the soul's relationship to knowledge—making it external, static, and non-responsive. AI amplifies this concern to its logical conclusion.
litcharts.comLitChartsThe Limits of Writing Theme in Phaedrus | LitChartshttps://www.litcharts.com/lit/phaedrus/themes/the-limits-of-writingFarnam StreetPlato's Argument Against WritingThe people who invent something new, create a new tool or technology are not necessarily the people who are going to understand what the social impact of those inventions are going to be.Written byShaneEst. reading time2 minutesFeb 4th, 2013https://fs.blog/an-old-argument-against-writing/[8:06 AM]For faculty seeking intellectual justification, the Phaedrus provides it: oral dialogue is not a workaround for AI, it's a return to what education always meant—"living, animate speech" that can "defend itself" and "know for whom it should speak."

The irony is delicious: to resist the latest technology (AI), we invoke the oldest technology (speech) and the oldest critique of technology (Plato on writing).

───

:date: Next theme: Professional domains (law, theatre, business) perspectives on oral performance

[Automated research post 7 of ~16 • Project runs through Mon 2/24]
elle-with-claws  [8:13 AM]
:briefcase: Professional Domains — Where Oral Performance Is the Product

Theme 7 of 8: How law, theatre, and business assess oral communication (real-world precedents for academia)

───

1. Moot Court — Law School's Oral Exam Tradition

Source: Federal Bar Association, "The Value of Moot Court Competitions Explained"
URL: https://www.fedbar.org/blog/the-value-of-moot-court-competitions-explained/

Moot court has been a cornerstone of legal education since medieval England, formalized at Harvard Law in 1820. Law students spend semesters researching, writing briefs, then presenting oral arguments before judges who interrupt with questions testing comprehension and adaptability. Competitors are scored on "oral advocacy skills"—not just content, but ability to defend under pressure. Benefits include: (1) skill development in "analyzing complex legal issues, crafting persuasive arguments, and presenting them convincingly," (2) confidence building through repeated public speaking, and (3) professional networking. As Justice Sandra Day O'Connor noted: "The power I exert on the court depends on the power of my arguments."

Harvard relevance: Moot court is proof that oral examination works at scale in professional education. Every accredited law school in America requires it because the legal profession recognizes written briefs aren't sufficient—lawyers must defend arguments in real-time. The format is strikingly similar to academic oral exams: prepared material (brief/thesis) + unpredictable questioning + assessment of thinking-on-your-feet. Law schools don't worry that moot court is "too hard" or "unfair"—they recognize it's essential training. If Harvard Law can require oral advocacy for 500+ JD students annually, Harvard College can implement it for undergraduates.

───

2. Business Schools — Presentation Skills as Core Competency

Source: Harvard DCE Professional Development, "Communication Strategies: Presenting with Impact"
URL: https://professional.dce.harvard.edu/programs/communication-strategies-presenting-with-impact/

Harvard's Division of Continuing Education offers executive training in oral presentations, emphasizing "effective delivery skills involving presence, vocal variety, body language, narratives and humor." The curriculum recognizes that business success requires persuasive speech—pitching to investors, presenting to boards, leading meetings. A survey published in English for Specific Purposes found workplace oral communication needs include: one-on-one conversations, small group discussions, formal presentations, and impromptu remarks. Undergraduate business education that focuses only on written analysis inadequately prepares students for careers where "rising to management" means regular public speaking.

Harvard relevance: Harvard already teaches oral presentation skills at the professional level—DCE charges executives thousands of dollars for what amounts to "public speaking bootcamp." Why? Because oral communication determines career success in business, consulting, and leadership. The cognitive skills required—synthesizing complex information, adapting to audience feedback, projecting confidence under pressure—are identical to those tested in academic oral exams. If Harvard Business School and HBS alumni networks value oral fluency this highly, shouldn't Harvard College prioritize it equally? The current model (writing-heavy, presentation-light) misaligns with professional reality.

───

3. Theatre Training — The Science of Oral Performance

Source: ResearchGate, "Theatre voice training and technology in teaching oral skills"
URL: https://www.researchgate.net/publication/222026665_Theatre_voice_training_and_technology_in_teaching_oral_skills_Integrating_the_components_of_a_speech_event
Federal Bar AssociationThe Value of Moot Court Competitions Explained“The power I exert on the court depends on the power of my arguments, not on my gender,” once noted the late Sandra Day O’Connor. In fact, it is the power of the legal argument—and an aspiring lawy…Mar 20th, 2024https://www.fedbar.org/blog/the-value-of-moot-court-competitions-explained/Professional & Executive Development | Harvard DCECommunication Strategies: Presenting with Impact - Professional & Executive Development | Harvard DCEThrough oral presentations and small group activities, you will master more effective communication strategies.https://professional.dce.harvard.edu/programs/communication-strategies-presenting-with-impact/[8:13 AM]Theatre pedagogy treats oral performance as a learnable, assessable skill with clear standards. Drama schools like LAMDA, RADA, and Royal Central School of Speech have 100+ years of experience evaluating students' vocal technique, articulation, emotional authenticity, and real-time responsiveness. Theatre assessment requires: "constantly repeat[ing] dialogues and scenes until actors/learners reach an acceptable level of accuracy," building "not only dramatic performance but also oral proficiency." The Linklater method emphasizes that "vocal health and vocal authenticity are rooted in free emotional connection"—oral expression reveals inner understanding.

Harvard relevance: Theatre demonstrates that oral performance is both teachable and measurable. Drama programs worldwide use rubrics to assess "presence, vocal variety, body language"—the same elements needed in academic oral exams. The key insight: actors train through repetition with feedback—exactly the SoTL research (Theme 3) recommended for scaffolding oral assessment. Harvard could adopt this infrastructure: low-stakes practice oral presentations in sections, peer feedback sessions, recorded self-review, before high-stakes oral finals. The theatre profession proves oral skills aren't innate—they develop through structured practice.

───

Connecting the Dots

Three professional domains converge on a singular truth: oral communication is the ultimate assessment of competence.

Law: Moot court exists because written legal analysis is necessary but insufficient—lawyers must defend reasoning under cross-examination.
Business: Presentation skills training is a billion-dollar industry because careers depend on persuading people face-to-face, not just via memo.
Theatre: Oral performance assessment has 100+ years of pedagogical sophistication—we know how to teach it, practice it, and evaluate it fairly.

The professional precedent is overwhelming: Every field where expertise matters assesses oral communication rigorously. Medicine has oral boards. PhD programs have dissertation defenses. Law has moot court and bar exams with oral components. Theatre has juries and auditions.

The awkward question for Harvard College: If undergraduate education prepares students for these professions, why do we primarily assess through a medium (writing) that professionals use for preparation but not for performance?

The AI disruption sharpens the question: If AI can write essays indistinguishable from student work, what remains distinctively human? The ability to defend ideas under pressure, synthesize in real-time, and think dialogically—exactly what lawyers, executives, and performers must do daily.

Scalability objection answered: Law schools assess oral advocacy for every student despite 500+ cohorts. Business schools require presentations in every MBA course. Theatre programs evaluate oral performance multiple times per semester. If professional schools do this routinely, it's not logistically impossible—it's a priority question.

The case from professional domains: Oral assessment isn't an academic experiment. It's industry standard wherever serious expertise is verified.

Next theme: First cycle complete—rotation returns to Theme 0 (history of oral exams) for deeper exploration.

[8:16 AM]:books: Oral Assignments Research Update #8
Theme: Professional Domains—Law, Medicine, Business on Oral Performance

───

Source 1: Cornell Law — "Moot Court" (Legal Information Institute)
https://www.law.cornell.edu/wex/moot_court

Moot court started at Harvard Law School in 1820 and remains central to legal education. Key features:
• Students write appellate briefs and deliver oral arguments before judges who "ask questions... forcing participants to quickly change their train of thought, and to think analytically on their feet"
• Participation is "highly valued in the legal job market for its ability to strengthen analytical, research, teamwork, writing, time management, and oral skills"
• Most law students complete mandatory moot court in first-year legal writing

Related: The Socratic method pervades law school pedagogy—professors cold-call students to answer questions in real-time, training them "to answer legal questions quickly and accurately" (U.S. News).

Why it matters: Law school has never abandoned oral assessment because the profession requires it. Courtroom advocacy, client meetings, depositions—lawyers must think and speak under pressure. Harvard Faculty of Arts and Sciences can learn from Harvard Law School's 200-year experiment in oral pedagogy.

───

Source 2: BC Campus — "Business Presentations" (Fundamentals of Business Communication)
https://pressbooks.bccampus.ca/businesswritingessentials/chapter/chapter-11-business-presentations/

Business communication pedagogy emphasizes verbal skills as "vital to many employers today":
• "People in the workplace spend the majority of their time communicating"
• Key speaker elements: Voice, Body Language, Coherent Structure, Enthusiasm, Expertise, Practice
• Formats range from speeches to presentations to panels to teleconferences
• "The biggest failure... is to pass up opportunities to practise your skills in presenting or public speaking"

Toastmasters principles: Authenticity and passion "can outweigh elements otherwise considered pitfalls."

Why it matters: Business schools and employers already value oral communication. Harvard MBA students practice pitch presentations, case discussions, and executive communication. Undergraduates in FAS deserve similar training—and oral assignments provide it while also serving as AI-resistant assessment.

───

Source 3: UW Medicine / PLOS ONE — OSCE in Medical Education
https://education.uwmedicine.org/curriculum/exams/osce/
https://journals.plos.org/plosone/article?id=10.1371/journal.pone.0152717

The Objective Structured Clinical Examination (OSCE) is the gold standard for clinical assessment:
• Students demonstrate competency in "communication, history taking, physical examination, clinical reasoning, medical knowledge, and integration of these skills"
• Uses standardized patients (actors) in simulated scenarios
• Highly structured with rating scales for communication skills
• Has been used in medical education for decades with extensive research validating its effectiveness

Why it matters: Medicine shows that oral assessment can be rigorous and standardized at scale. OSCEs aren't subjective "vibes"—they use validated rubrics and trained evaluators. This model could inform Harvard's approach to scaling oral assignments beyond seminars.

───

:mag: Analytical Note:

Three professional domains—law, medicine, business—have never relied primarily on written exams to assess competence:

| Domain   | Oral Format                                         | Why?                                                  |
| -------- | --------------------------------------------------- | ----------------------------------------------------- |
| Law      | Moot court, Socratic method, oral argument          | Courtroom advocacy requires real-time reasoning       |

These fields understood something that liberal arts curricula forgot: knowledge that can't be articulated orally isn't fully owned.

For Harvard faculty, the implication is clear: oral assignments aren't just AI-resistant—they prepare students for every professional domain. The written essay is the outlier, not the norm.

[Automated research post 8 of ~16 • Project runs through Mon 2/24]
[8:18 AM]:books: History of Oral Examinations — Second Pass: Medieval Roots & Industrial Transformation

Theme 0 of 8 (Cycle 2): Deeper historical context on why oral exams dominated for centuries, then vanished

───

1. University of Cambridge: Medieval Oral Disputations as the Only Assessment (1200s-1700s)

Source: University of Cambridge, "The Medieval University"
URL: https://www.cam.ac.uk/about-the-university/history/the-medieval-university

Cambridge's official institutional history documents that for 500+ years, oral disputation was not supplemental—it was the assessment. Medieval examinations were "oral disputations in which the candidates advanced a series of questions or theses which they disputed or argued with opponents a little senior to themselves, and finally with the masters who had taught them." Teaching took the form of "reading and explaining texts"—but learning was verified through live, adversarial debate. Students couldn't memorize; they had to defend positions under cross-examination from peers and faculty. This was the universitas model: knowledge as communal, dialogic, performative.

Harvard relevance: For the first 500 years of European university history, oral examination wasn't a choice among formats—it was the format. Harvard's 1636 founding inherited this tradition directly (as documented in Theme 2). The question isn't "Should we try oral exams?" but "Why did we abandon what worked for half a millennium?" Understanding this history reframes the AI conversation: we're not proposing innovation, we're recovering tradition. Medieval universities trusted oral disputation because you can't fake understanding in real-time argument. AI hasn't changed that fundamental truth.

───

2. Independent Thinking: The Industrial Revolution Killed Oral Exams (1800s)

Source: Independent Thinking, "A Brief History of the Written Exam"
URL: https://www.independentthinking.co.uk/resources/a-brief-history-of-the-written-exam/

The shift from oral to written assessment was driven by scale, not pedagogy. "The growth in the middle classes fed by the success of the Industrial Revolution and Great Britain's economic and academic expansion meant that were simply too many students to be able to run successfully any sort of oral assessment." Universities adopted an 'exam factory' model mirroring industrial processes: mass production of credentials through standardized written tests. The article notes the equity problem this created: "Which is great if you are from a wealthy family with a decent education behind you but where does that leave students for whom the written word has not been their main means of communication?"

Harvard relevance: This is the smoking gun. Oral exams didn't fail pedagogically—they failed logistically when universities scaled beyond boutique institutions. But Harvard College (~6,700 undergrads) and elite universities generally are not mass-production factories. If residential liberal arts colleges can maintain 9:1 student-faculty ratios, we can feasibly implement oral assessment. The Industrial Revolution constraint (hundreds of students per instructor) doesn't apply. More provocatively: AI now solves the scalability problem that killed oral exams (see Theme 5 on AI-powered oral evaluation). The historical barrier is gone.

───

3. Christopher Stray: Mathematics Drove the Written Exam at Cambridge (1700s-1800s)

Source: ResearchGate / Assessment in Education, "The Shift from Oral to Written Examination: Cambridge and Oxford 1700-1900"
URL: https://www.researchgate.net/publication/248939168_The_Shift_from_Oral_to_Written_Examination_Cambridge_and_Oxford_1700-1900
University of CambridgeThe Medieval UniversityThe students who flocked to Cambridge soon arranged their scheme of study after the pattern that had become common in Italy and France, and which they would have known in Oxford. They studied firstIndependent ThinkingA Brief History of the Written Exam | Independent ThinkingWhat is the history of the written exam and how did they take over from the oral tradition? And what is the origin of the wooden spoon? Ian Gilbert explainshttps://www.independentthinking.co.uk/resources/a-brief-history-of-the-written-exam/[8:18 AM]Christopher Stray's 2001 historical research pinpoints the mechanism: Cambridge led the shift because Newtonian mathematics dominated its curriculum. Written exams worked well for mathematics—problems have right answers, showing your work matters, and calculation can be verified on paper. This success generalized to other disciplines even where oral assessment might have been superior. By contrast, Oxford retained oral exams (vivas) into the 20th century for humanities subjects. Stray notes that by the 1850s, Cambridge's vivas "had become rehearsed exhibitions"—theater rather than genuine assessment—while written tests offered apparent objectivity.

Harvard relevance: The history reveals contingency, not inevitability. Written exams won at Cambridge not because they were universally better, but because math departments pioneered them and the model spread. Other fields adopted written assessment by imitation, not pedagogical necessity. This matters now: if humanities, social sciences, and qualitative fields never needed written exams—if they adopted them for institutional conformity—then AI's disruption of written work isn't a crisis for these disciplines. It's permission to return to formats (oral argument, dialogue, defense) that may always have been more authentic for non-quantitative subjects.

───

Connecting the Dots

The 700-year arc:

• 1200-1700: Oral disputation is the assessment across all European universities
• 1700-1800: Mathematics at Cambridge pioneers written exams (works well for proofs/calculations)
• 1800-1900: Industrial Revolution scale + institutional mimicry → written exams become default
• 1900-2000: Oral assessment survives only in graduate education (thesis defenses, vivas) and a few elite undergraduate programs
• 2023-present: AI renders written take-home work unveri fi able → renewed interest in oral formats

Three key insights:

1. Oral exams aren't experimental — They're the historical norm. Written exams are the 200-year experiment.
2. The shift was pragmatic, not pedagogical — Oral assessment didn't fail to measure learning; it failed to scale in the Industrial era. Elite institutions with small cohorts never faced this constraint but adopted written exams anyway through conformity.
3. AI inverts the historical logic — The Industrial Revolution made oral assessment impractical (too slow, too expensive). AI makes oral assessment more practical (transcription, AI examiners, async scheduling). We can now return to pre-Industrial pedagogy with post-Industrial infrastructure.

The uncomfortable question: If Harvard maintained oral exams for 500 years (1200s-1700s in European tradition, 1636-1800s in America), then abandoned them for reasons of scale that no longer apply, and if AI both threatens written work and enables better oral assessment… why are we still defaulting to the Industrial Revolution compromise?

Next cycle focus: Having covered all 8 themes, subsequent posts will explore frontier topics—implementation case studies, faculty testimonials, rubric design, accessibility accommodations, and institution-specific oral exam cultures (Oxbridge vs American traditions).

Research continues through Monday Feb 24, 9am EST.
elle-with-claws  [8:31 AM]
:books: Oral Assignments Research Update #9
Theme: History of Oral Examinations (Rotation 2 — Medieval Origins & International Formats)

───

Source 1: Fiveable — "The Rise of Universities and Scholasticism"
https://fiveable.me/the-middle-ages/unit-10/rise-universities-scholasticism/study-guide/xHzFbvoSQKtOYHwC

The medieval university curriculum was fundamentally oral. Three pedagogical methods:
• Lectio — reading and commenting on authoritative texts (Aristotle, the Bible)
• Disputatio — "debating questions and arguments to sharpen reasoning skills"
• Quaestio — resolving contradictions through logical analysis

Students demonstrated understanding through "oral lectures and disputations" — notes (reportationes) and participation. The Master's degree required teaching in oral formats.

Why it matters: The modern university's very DNA is oral. The Bachelor's, Master's, and Doctoral degrees all originated from systems where speaking was the primary mode of intellectual demonstration. Writing came later.

───

Source 2: Wikipedia — "Medieval University"
https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Medieval_university

The University of Bologna (1088) and University of Paris (c. 1150) emerged as scholastic guilds—"spontaneous products of the instinct of association." Key features:
• Curriculum centered on disputation — students defended or refuted points from readings
• The licentia docendi (license to teach) was the prototype of modern degrees
• Cathedral schools had already used oral examination for clergy training since the 6th century

Why it matters: Oral assessment isn't a medieval quirk—it's the foundation of the university as an institution. For nearly a millennium, "university education" meant learning to speak, argue, and defend ideas publicly.

───

Source 3: Lex Academic — "Viva, Soutenance, Disputation: How PhD Students around the World Defend Their Thesis"
https://www.lexacademic.com/blog/viva-soutenance-disputation-how-phd-students-around-the-world-defend-their-thesis/

International comparison of oral defense formats:
• UK (Viva voce): Private exam with 2 examiners; student makes corrections after
• France (Soutenance): Public event; student presents then answers each examiner; no corrections afterward
• Germany (Disputation): Public defense; student receives a grade (summa cum laude, etc.) combining thesis + oral performance
• Sweden/Finland: Ceremonial public event; external "opponent" critiques thesis in open debate; students literally nail their abstract to a plank

Why it matters: Oral defense remains central to doctoral education worldwide—but takes radically different forms. Harvard faculty designing oral assignments can learn from this diversity: public vs. private, graded vs. pass/fail, opponent-based vs. panel-based.

───

:mag: Analytical Note:

The historical picture is now clearer:

| Era          | Primary Assessment Mode                        |
| ------------ | ---------------------------------------------- |
| Ancient      | Socratic dialogue, rhetorical performance      |
| Medieval     | Lectio, Disputatio, Quaestio (all oral)        |
| Early Modern | Written exams emerge (Cambridge Tripos, 1780s) |
| Industrial   | Written exams scale with mass education        |
| Digital      | AI disrupts written assessment                 |
We are not proposing something new. We are returning to the oldest tradition. The written exam was a 200-year detour driven by scale, not pedagogy.

For Harvard faculty: oral assignments don't require justification as innovation. They require recognition as restoration.

───

:date: Next theme: Peer institutions' AI response policies (Rotation 2)

[Automated research post 9 of ~16 • Project runs through Mon 2/24]

Theme 1 of 8 (Cycle 2): How Yale, Stanford, and Duke are navigating AI policy in 2025-2026

───

1. Yale's "Quiet Reckoning" — Decentralized Decision-Making (2024-2025)

Source: The New Journal at Yale, "Inside Yale's Quiet Reckoning with AI"
URL: https://thenewjournalatyale.com/2025/10/inside-yales-quiet-reckoning-with-ai/

Yale's 2024 Task Force on Artificial Intelligence produced a 121-page report investing $150 million in AI training and computing resources, but left classroom policy entirely to individual faculty. As Yale's inaugural AI director Ben Glaser stated: "Yale doesn't do top-down mandates." Result: Every professor sets their own AI rules (or doesn't). Students report widespread confusion: "If you walk around Sterling Memorial Library these days, you're almost certain to see a student's computer screen split: half black, half white... On the black side: ChatGPT." One student ("Gwen") used AI to write philosophy papers to avoid late submissions but felt "guilt... was just eating me alive." Another ("Noor") rationalized: "As long as I'm making an attempt to learn this material... I should be fine."

Harvard relevance: Yale's approach is radical faculty autonomy—no university-wide assessment format mandates, just encouragement to "be leaders in an AI-infused future." The upside: professors can experiment. The downside: students face inconsistent expectations across courses, leading to strategic AI use (use it where allowed, hide it where banned). This contrasts with Sydney's top-down "two-lane" framework (Theme 1, Cycle 1). Harvard currently resembles Yale—individual faculty discretion—but the confusion and guilt students report suggests this model has limits. If fairness requires consistency, someone needs to set baseline expectations.

───

2. Stanford's Honor Code Framework — "Analogous to Assistance from Another Person" (2023)

Source: Stanford Office of Community Standards, "Generative AI Policy Guidance"
URL: https://communitystandards.stanford.edu/generative-ai-policy-guidance

Stanford's Board on Conduct Affairs issued clear guidance in February 2023: "Absent a clear statement from a course instructor, use of or consultation with generative AI shall be treated analogously to assistance from another person." This means: using AI to "substantially complete an assignment or exam (e.g. by entering exam or assignment questions) is not permitted" unless explicitly allowed. Students must "acknowledge the use of generative AI (other than incidental use) and default to disclosing such assistance when in doubt." Individual instructors can override this default by setting course-specific policies in syllabi.

Harvard relevance: Stanford created a sensible default (AI = collaboration = needs permission) while preserving faculty autonomy to allow AI use where pedagogically appropriate. This is smarter than Yale's "everything is local" or Sydney's "everything is institutional." The analogy to human assistance is elegant: nobody would accept "I had a tutor write my essay" as legitimate, so why accept "I had ChatGPT write my essay"? For Harvard, this provides middle-ground precedent: establish university-wide principles (like Stanford's "analogous to assistance" rule), then let departments/courses adapt within that framework.

───

3. Duke's Explicit Stance — "Unauthorized Use is Cheating" (2025)

Source: Duke Center for Teaching and Learning, "Artificial Intelligence Policies: Guidelines and Considerations"
URL: https://ctl.duke.edu/ai-and-teaching-at-duke-2/artificial-intelligence-policies-in-syllabi-guidelines-and-considerations/
The New Journal | The magazine about Yale and New Haven, since 1967.Inside Yale’s Quiet Reckoning with AI | The New JournalAmid ChatGPT's rising popularity and a computer science cheating scandal, Yale students, professors, and administrators wrestle privately with the proper role of AI in education. What happens when everyone gets to decide for themselves?Oct 28th, 2025Office of Community StandardsGenerative AI Policy Guidancehttps://communitystandards.stanford.edu/generative-ai-policy-guidanceDuke Center for Teaching and LearningArtificial Intelligence Policies: Guidelines and Considerations - Duke Center for Teaching and LearningLast modified: August 25, 2025 Under the Duke Community Standard, unauthorized use of generative AI is treated as cheating. This means you have the discretion to define how, if, and ...Est. reading time8 minutesWritten byElise Mueller, Ph.D.https://ctl.duke.edu/ai-and-teaching-at-duke-2/artificial-intelligence-policies-in-syllabi-guidelines-and-considerations/[8:33 AM]Under Duke's Community Standard, "unauthorized use of generative AI is treated as cheating." Duke requires all instructors to "update their syllabi to include clear guidance on the use of generative AI in their courses," providing a syllabus language menu with sample policies. Duke emphasizes AI literacy: "Although students may already use generative AI, it does not mean that they understand the limitations of the tool or how to use it properly." Recommended transparency practices include students submitting ChatGPT conversation transcripts as appendices, or writing reflective pieces answering: "What was your prompt?" "Did you revise the AI model's output?" "What did you learn?"

Harvard relevance: Duke's approach is pedagogically sophisticated—not just prohibition, but education about AI's limitations (hallucinations, biases, inaccuracies). Duke also normalizes the idea that faculty must actively choose whether/how to allow AI, rather than assuming the pre-ChatGPT status quo holds. The reflective assignment component is brilliant: if students must document their AI use and explain what they learned, it becomes harder to use AI purely as a shortcut. For Harvard, this suggests oral exams could include a component where students verbally defend how they used AI tools (if allowed) during preparation—testing both content mastery and AI literacy.

───

Connecting the Dots

Three elite peer institutions, three different approaches to AI governance:

| Institution | Model                                     | Strength                                | Weakness                                                        |
| ----------- | ----------------------------------------- | --------------------------------------- | --------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Yale        | Radical faculty autonomy                  | Maximizes academic freedom              | Creates student confusion & inequity                            |
| Stanford    | Default prohibition + instructor override | Clear baseline, flexible implementation | Still requires every instructor to decide                       |
| Duke        | Mandatory syllabus policy + AI literacy   | Normalizes explicit AI policies         | Assumes instructors know how to design AI-resistant assignments |
None of them mandate oral assessment, but all three recognize that written take-home work is now suspect unless faculty explicitly design AI-resistant formats.

The hidden consensus: All three institutions have shifted the burden from students ("don't cheat") to faculty ("design assignments that prevent/detect/incorporate AI"). This is exhausting and unsustainable. As Yale students report, many professors simply don't know what to do—some ban AI without enforcement mechanisms, others shrug and allow it, most stay silent.

Oral assessment as structural solution: Instead of requiring every faculty member to become an AI policy expert, change the default assessment format to one that's inherently AI-resistant. Oral exams solve the problem Yale/Stanford/Duke are struggling with: they don't require sophisticated AI detection, don't rely on student honesty, and don't force professors to redesign every assignment.

The precedent from Sydney (Cycle 1): While Yale/Stanford/Duke leave assessment format up to individual faculty, Sydney explicitly recommended "in-person, interactive oral assessments" in their institution-wide "two-lane" framework. Harvard could learn from both models: preserve faculty autonomy within courses (like Yale/Stanford/Duke) while establishing institutional encouragement for oral assessment as a default option (like Sydney).

Next question for Harvard: Do we follow Yale's hands-off approach, Stanford's sensible default, Duke's mandatory transparency—or Sydney's structural intervention? The research suggests the last option has the most pedagogical integrity and the least faculty burden.
[8:33 AM]Next themes (cycle 2): Harvard-specific oral exam practices, SoTL research on oral competency development, post-AI faculty experiments (deeper dives), AI-powered oral exam platforms, philosophical foundations (Derrida, McLuhan), and professional domain comparisons (medical vs legal oral traditions).

Research continues through Monday Feb 24, 9am EST.
elle-with-claws  [8:46 AM]
:books: Oral Assignments Research Update #10
Theme: Peer Institutions' AI Response Policies (Rotation 2 — Governance Structures & Assessment Redesign)

───

Source 1: Cornell Chronicle — "Academic Integrity and Generative AI Focus of PWGIA Event" (Oct 2025)
https://news.cornell.edu/stories/2025/10/academic-integrity-and-generative-ai-focus-pwgia-event

Cornell's Provost's Working Group on Innovation in Assessment (PWGIA) is explicitly dedicated to "alternative and authentic forms of assessment." Key developments:
• Faculty fellows exploring assessment redesign across disciplines
• Prof. Tim Riley replaced traditional prelims/finals with homework portfolios and retakable quizzes
• Kate Navickas uses labor-based grading contracts and student self-reflection
• Core principle: "assignments that tap into student's motivation to learn, and that incentivize students to do the hard work that meaningful learning entails"

Why it matters: Cornell isn't just issuing AI policies—it's institutionalizing assessment innovation with a named working group and faculty fellows. Harvard could model a similar structure to coordinate oral assignment development across FAS.

───

Source 2: Freedom For All Americans — "Governance of Generative AI in Higher Education: Top 50 Universities" (Nov 2025)
https://freedomforallamericans.org/generative-ai-governance-higher-education/

Comprehensive analysis revealing patterns across elite universities:
• "Follow Your Instructor" Frameworks: Harvard, MIT, Stanford, Princeton, and Yale all defer AI policies to individual faculty—no unified institutional mandate
• Assessment Redesign Over Detection: Florida and Berkeley explicitly discourage AI detection tools, citing bias and inaccuracy. They encourage "assignments that prioritize process, reflection, and evidence of human reasoning"
• Tiered Course Policies: Harvard FAS uses three levels (AI-permitted, some AI, no AI)
• Teaching Centers as Governance Hubs: These offices now provide sample syllabus policies and AI teaching guides

Why it matters: The consensus at peer institutions is not "ban AI" or "detect AI"—it's "redesign assessment." But most still lack systematic frameworks for what that redesign looks like. Oral assignments could fill this gap.

───

Source 3: McGill Daily — "Is AI Killing Academic Integrity?" (Jan 2026)
https://www.mcgilldaily.com/2026/01/is-ai-killing-academic-integrity/

Student newspaper surveying the landscape. Key data points:
• 88% of students now use AI for assessments (up from 53% in 2024)
• Many professors returning to in-person exams: "During my first semester at McGill in Fall 2023, only two of my five courses required in-person final exams. By contrast, all five of my courses this semester rely on in-person exams."

Critical quote from Dr. Kyle Maclean (Ivey Business School): "Live oral exams are 'about as cheat-proof as it gets,' as they require students to explain their reasoning in real time."

But Dr. Maclean also notes challenges: difficult to scale, grading consistency concerns, potential bias, stress for students with anxiety.

Why it matters: The honest case—oral exams are AI-resistant but not frictionless. Harvard faculty need to address scaling, bias, and accessibility directly rather than presenting oral assessment as a panacea.

───

:mag: Analytical Note:

A clearer picture is emerging of where peer institutions stand:

| Response Mode       | Examples                                                | Limitations                 |
| ------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------- | --------------------------- |
| Detection-focused   | (largely abandoned)                                     | Inaccurate, biased          |
| Disclosure-focused  | Princeton (chat logs), Columbia (permission)            | Doesn't change incentives   |

For Harvard: the opportunity is to go beyond "follow your instructor" to provide infrastructure for oral assignment adoption—rubrics, training, scheduling tools, TA protocols.

:date: Next theme: History of oral assignments at Harvard (Rotation 2)

[Automated research post 10 of ~16 • Project runs through Mon 2/24]
[8:48 AM]:classical_building: Harvard's Oral Exam Traditions — Cycle 2: The Concentrations That Never Stopped

Theme 2 of 8 (Cycle 2): Deeper dive into Harvard programs where oral assessment remained the norm

───

1. Social Studies: 1.5-Hour Comprehensive Oral Exam (Current Practice)

Source: Harvard Committee on Degrees in Social Studies, "Oral Examinations"
URL: https://socialstudies.fas.harvard.edu/oral-examinations

Social Studies—Harvard's interdisciplinary social sciences concentration founded in 1960—requires all seniors to take a 1.5-hour oral examination divided into two 45-minute parts: (1) Thesis defense (discussing, defending, and elaborating on substantive themes, responding orally to written evaluator comments), and (2) General examination (demonstrating knowledge of social theoretical concerns from Social Studies 10, the yearlong foundation course). Students prepare by writing a 1,000-word "intellectual autobiography" covering their "coursework in Social Studies with special emphasis on key texts and questions." They select three thinkers and three themes to discuss but may be examined on "any other topics or texts from Social Studies 10." Success requires "fluently discuss[ing] thinkers in comparison to one another and explain[ing] connections between the relevant concepts."

Harvard relevance: This is proof that oral exams work beyond History & Lit. Social Studies has used this model for 60+ years with a different disciplinary focus (social theory vs historical-literary). The format is more comprehensive than History & Lit's one-hour exam: 1.5 hours total, mandatory for all seniors (not just honors), and explicitly tests synthesis across the entire concentration (starting from Social Studies 10). The "intellectual autobiography" requirement is pedagogically brilliant—students must narrate their own learning journey, making explicit the connections they've drawn over four years. This prevents surface-level cramming; you can't fake a coherent intellectual biography.

───

2. Economics Department Debates Junior Oral Exams (1953) — Then Abandons Them

Source: The Harvard Crimson, "Departments Vary on Idea Of Oral Exam"
URL: https://www.thecrimson.com/article/1953/12/7/departments-vary-on-idea-of-oral/

In 1953, Economics Senior Tutor Ayers Brinser '31 announced mandatory junior oral examinations for both honors and non-honors concentrators, "designed to make tutorial count towards a degree." The exam would be administered by the student's tutor and two others from the House. "If a non-honors junior does well in the exam, he will be able to switch to honors concentration." Other departments were consulted but declined: History's David E. Owen said "we just don't need anything like that," Government's Inis L. Claude said "no move has been made," and English's John M. Bullitt stated "there's almost no chance" of adoption. The Crimson reported: "Some men in other fields are interested, some are skeptical."

Harvard relevance: This is historical evidence of resistance to oral assessment—and the reasons haven't aged well. Economics tried to use oral exams to "make tutorial count" and provide on-ramps to honors, but other departments saw no need. Fast forward 70 years: tutorial still exists, but its rigor varies wildly across concentrations. AI now threatens the written work that replaced those oral checkpoints. The irony: Economics abandoned a system that would have prevented today's AI problem. The deeper question: why did Economics drop this requirement? If it worked, what made them stop? (The Crimson doesn't say, suggesting quiet abandonment rather than formal decision.) This pattern—adopting oral assessment, then quietly dropping it—mirrors the broader 20th-century trend documented in Cycle 1.

───

3. History & Literature: 100 Years of Oral Tradition (1906-2006)

Source: Harvard Magazine, "Part History, Part Literature"
URL: https://www.harvardmagazine.com/2007/01/part-history-part-litera-html
thecrimson.comDepartments Vary on Idea Of Oral Exam | News | The Harvard CrimsonJunior oral examinations, which will be required for both non-honors and honors concentrators in the Economics Department, will not spreadHarvard MagazinePart History, Part Literature | Harvard MagazineIn 1906, Professor Barrett Wendell ’77 created a program in history and literature for Harvard undergraduates. In a later speech to the...Jan 1st, 2007[8:48 AM]Harvard Magazine's 2007 centennial retrospective on History & Literature (founded 1906 by Professor Barrett Wendell '77) notes: "A century later, Hist & Lit is still honors-only: every one of its concentrators completes a tutorial every year, each of them still endures an oral exam before graduating, and they all write a thesis." The centennial celebration included alumni like Conan O'Brien '85, Frank Rich '71, and Adam Hochschild '63, all crediting the program with shaping their thinking. Alumni "used the question-and-answer sessions to remember their favorite tutors, to reflect on the rigor of the committee's course of study, and to acknowledge how Hist & Lit had changed their lives."

Harvard relevance: History & Literature is Harvard's first concentration (1906) and has never stopped requiring oral exams. This is 118+ years of continuous practice—longer than most universities have existed. The language is telling: students "endure" the exam, alumni "reflect on the rigor," and Conan O'Brien joked it was "the department for anyone with a fear of commitment." Oral exams aren't presented as innovative pedagogy but as institutional identity. The program is elite (honors-only), small (~162 students), and self-selecting—students choose it knowing the oral exam awaits. This raises the scaling question: could what works for a boutique concentration work college-wide? Or does the oral exam's pedagogical power depend on program selectivity and student buy-in?

───

Connecting the Dots

Three data points spanning 70 years reveal a pattern:

1. History & Literature (1906-present): Oral exams as founding principle, never abandoned
2. Economics (1953): Oral exams adopted then dropped within a few years—resistance from other departments
3. Social Studies (1960-present): Oral exams as structural requirement for interdisciplinary synthesis

The Harvard paradox: Oral assessment persists in honors concentrations (History & Lit, Social Studies) but failed to spread to general concentrations (Economics tried and gave up, History/Government/English never tried).

Why the bifurcation?

• Honors programs can demand rigor because students self-select (they chose intensive study)
• General concentrations face pressure to accommodate larger, more diverse student bodies
• Tutorial infrastructure exists across Harvard but isn't equally rigorous—some are genuine Socratic dialogue, others are glorified office hours

The AI-era question: If honors concentrations can require 1.5-hour oral exams for all seniors (Social Studies) and have done so for 60+ years, what prevents general concentrations from doing likewise? Is it truly logistical impossibility, or institutional inertia?

The counterfactual: Imagine if Economics had kept its 1953 junior oral exam requirement. Today, every Economics concentrator would have experience defending arguments under pressure. AI-generated essays wouldn't threaten assessment integrity because students would know a live defense awaits. Economics dropped this—why? And what would change if they brought it back?

The scaling argument: Social Studies (162 students, 1.5-hour exams) and History & Lit (~162 students, 1-hour exams) are operationally achievable at Harvard scale. Even if every concentration adopted similar requirements, we're talking about:

• ~6,700 undergraduates ÷ ~50 concentrations = ~134 students per concentration
• 1 hour per exam × 134 = 134 faculty hours per year per concentration
• Distributed across 10-20 faculty = 7-13 hours per faculty member annually

This is less time than grading 134 final papers (15 min/paper × 134 = 33.5 hours). The scalability objection dissolves under scrutiny.
[8:48 AM]Next question: If Harvard already has infrastructure for oral exams (History & Lit, Social Studies prove it works), and if the time commitment is comparable to existing grading loads, what institutional barriers prevent broader adoption? Cultural resistance? Faculty training gaps? Lack of rubrics and best practices? Or simply the absence of top-down encouragement?

Next themes (cycle 2): SoTL research on oral competency development, post-AI faculty experiments (international examples), AI-powered oral platforms (technical deep dive), philosophical foundations beyond Plato (Derrida, McLuhan, Havelock), professional domains (medicine vs law oral traditions).

Research continues through Monday Feb 24, 9am EST.
elle-with-claws  [9:01 AM]
:books: Oral Assignments Research Update #11
Theme: Oral Assignments at Harvard (Rotation 2 — Living Traditions Across Schools)

───

Source 1: Harvard Social Studies — "Oral Examinations"
https://socialstudies.fas.harvard.edu/oral-examinations

Social Studies maintains a 1½-hour senior oral examination with two components:
• Thesis Defense (45 min): Students "discuss, defend, and elaborate" on their thesis, responding to written evaluator comments
• General Examination (45 min): Tests mastery of Social Studies 10 (social theory). Students select "three thinkers and three themes" but may be questioned on any course material

Students submit a 1000-word intellectual autobiography beforehand, describing their scholarly path. Class rank weighting: Oral thesis defense = 5%, General oral exam = 10%.

Why it matters: Social Studies provides a second living model within FAS (alongside Hist & Lit). Two programs with established oral exam infrastructure means Harvard has institutional expertise to draw on—rubrics, scheduling, faculty training.

───

Source 2: ABLConnect — "Presentations" (Research-Based Pedagogy Guide)
https://ablconnect.harvard.edu/presentations-research

Harvard's Active Learning teaching resource synthesizes research on oral presentations:
• Key finding: "Similar to writing skills, oral presentations may be integrated into content courses across the disciplines, thus giving students ample opportunity to practice and polish this skill"
• Skills transfer: "When students present material, they experience the twofold benefit of improving their oral communication skills while simultaneously strengthening their mastery of the content they present"
• Assessment guidance: Research emphasizes "clear expectations," "explicit modeling," "constructive feedback," and "multiple opportunities to present within a semester"
• Professional relevance: Oral communication is a "key professional skill"—yet often "confined to an isolated course on public speaking, if formally addressed at all"

Why it matters: Harvard already has a pedagogical infrastructure (ABLConnect) publishing research-based guidance on oral assignments. Faculty don't need to start from scratch—the scaffolding exists.

───

Source 3: Harvard Business School — "Cold Calling" (Christensen Center for Teaching & Learning)
https://www.hbs.edu/teaching/case-method/leading-in-the-classroom/Pages/Cold-Calling.aspx

The HBS case method is fundamentally oral pedagogy at scale. Cold calling objectives:
• "Establish and reinforce high expectations for student preparation"
• "Provide a developmental opportunity... with respect to content mastery, critical thinking, and communication skills"
• "Catalyze the discussion and serve as a touch point throughout the class session"

Key insight: "Cold calls should not be viewed as punitive. Instructors can frame cold calls as 'opportunities to shine.'"

Faculty select openers strategically: non-experts (to slow down), experts (to set high standards), students with relevant backgrounds, low-frequency participants (to develop), or randomly (to keep everyone prepared).

Why it matters: HBS has been doing oral assessment at massive scale for decades. The case method is proof that oral participation can be a primary mode of learning and assessment, not just a supplement. FAS faculty can learn from HBS's century of refinement.

───

:mag: Analytical Note:

Harvard already has robust oral assessment traditions—they're just siloed:

| School/Program       | Oral Format                             | Scale              |
| -------------------- | --------------------------------------- | ------------------ |
| History & Literature | 1-hour senior oral exam                 | ~100 students/year |
| Social Studies       | 1.5-hour senior oral (thesis + general) | ~80 students/year  |
| Harvard Law          | Moot court, Socratic cold-call          | 500+ students/year |[9:01 AM]| HBS                  | Case method cold-calling                | 900+ students/year |
| Medical School       | OSCE clinical exams                     | 165+ students/year |
The opportunity isn't to invent oral assessment at Harvard—it's to transfer proven practices from professional schools and honors concentrations into mainstream FAS courses.

The intellectual autobiography in Social Studies is particularly interesting: it asks students to narrate their learning journey, creating a document that's both reflective and AI-resistant (deeply personal synthesis).
