:date: Next theme: Educational research on speaking in learning (Rotation 2)

[Automated research post 11 of ~16 • Project runs through Mon 2/24]
[9:04 AM]:bar_chart: SoTL on Oral Assessment — Cycle 2: Anxiety, Rubrics & Cognitive Load

Theme 3 of 8 (Cycle 2): Educational research on implementation challenges and design solutions

───

1. Student Fears of Oral Presentations — A Qualitative Survey (2021)

Source: Journal of Further and Higher Education (Taylor & Francis)
URL: https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/0309877X.2021.1948509

A 2021 qualitative survey of UK higher education students identified six key themes driving fear of oral presentations: (1) fear of being judged, (2) physical symptoms (shaking, sweating, nausea), (3) uncertainty about the topic, (4) negative effect on university experience, (5) practice and preparation (or lack thereof), and (6) more practical support needed. Students reported that oral presentations caused significant anxiety affecting their overall university experience. The study emphasizes that student fears are legitimate and widespread, not individual pathology—meaning institutions must provide scaffolding (practice opportunities, feedback, explicit modeling) rather than expecting students to "just cope."

Harvard relevance: If Harvard Faculty adopt oral assessment, anxiety mitigation must be central to implementation. The SoTL evidence is clear: throwing students into high-stakes oral exams without preparation is pedagogically irresponsible and inequitable. The solution from Theme 3 Cycle 1 (systematic review) applies here: low-stakes practice → feedback → high-stakes assessment. For example, sections could include weekly 5-minute "oral check-ins" where students summarize readings aloud (no grades, just feedback), building fluency before the final oral exam. Students fear what's unfamiliar; repeated exposure with support reduces anxiety. This is especially important for equity—first-generation students or those from educational systems without oral traditions face steeper learning curves.

───

2. Rubric Reliability for Oral Assessment — Prospective Controlled Study (2024)

Source: Journal of Education and Health Promotion (PMC)
URL: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11392252/

A 2024 prospective study with 300 dental students (undergraduate, intern, postgraduate) compared traditional oral examination methods with rubric-based assessment. Rubrics showed high statistical significance (P < 0.0001) for reliability compared to traditional methods. The study tested the hypothesis that "rubrics are a reliable tool in the assessment of oral presentations in clinical subjects." Key findings: (1) Rubrics improve consistency across assessors, (2) provide explicit criteria that help students understand expectations, and (3) facilitate constructive feedback by breaking evaluation into transparent dimensions (content mastery, communication clarity, responsiveness to questions).

Harvard relevance: The "it's too subjective" objection to oral assessment is empirically refuted. Rubrics make oral assessment as reliable as (or more reliable than) written work grading, which also suffers from inter-rater variability. For Harvard implementation, this means: (1) Create standardized rubrics for oral exams across concentrations (perhaps adapting History & Lit and Social Studies models), (2) Train faculty on rubric use (calibration sessions where multiple faculty score the same oral exam and discuss discrepancies), and (3) Share rubrics with students in advance so expectations are transparent. The study notes rubrics support "self-assessment and planning"—students can literally practice answering questions while self-scoring against the rubric, building metacognitive awareness before the actual exam.

───

3. Cognitive Load Theory — Oral vs. Written Assessment (2021)

Source: Educational Psychology Review (Springer)
URL: https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s10648-021-09624-7
PubMed Central (PMC)Reliability of rubrics in the assessment of clinical oral presentation: A prospective controlled studyRubrics are assessment tools customarily designed to judge a student’s skills in diagnosis, clinical assessment, presentations. as well as academic performance. The aim of the present study was to assess the reliability of rubrics in clinical oral ...https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11392252/SpringerLinkUnderstanding Cognitive Load in Digital and Online Learning: a New Perspective on Extraneous Cognitive LoadEducational Psychology Review - Cognitive load theory has been a major influence for the field of educational psychology. One of the main guidelines of the theory is that extraneous cognitive load...https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s10648-021-09624-7[9:04 AM]Cognitive load theory research shows that written tests can misalign with learning modalities, creating cognitive load mismatches. A key finding: "A written test could not properly measure the benefits of interactivity, but rather, only emphasize the costs." When students learn through dialogue, discussion, or hands-on practice (high interactivity), assessing them via silent written exams imposes extraneous cognitive load—they must translate interactive knowledge into linear prose, adding cognitive work unrelated to the learning itself. Conversely, spoken text has been found more effective than written text for learning factual knowledge in certain contexts, suggesting oral assessment may reduce cognitive load for material learned orally.

Harvard relevance: This explains why oral exams "feel different" from written work—they tap different cognitive processes. For courses heavy on tutorial discussion, Socratic seminars, or lab work (where learning is interactive), oral assessment is cognitively aligned with the learning mode. Written exams in such courses create what cognitive load theory calls a "dual task" problem—students must both recall content and perform genre-translation (discussion → essay). Oral exams eliminate this translation step. Practical implication: Harvard courses that already emphasize oral participation (tutorials, seminars, discussion sections) should seriously consider oral assessment. The cognitive load argument suggests it's not just "AI-resistant" but pedagogically superior for these learning environments.

───

Connecting the Dots

Three SoTL insights for Harvard implementation:

1. Anxiety is real, but mitigable

• Don't assume students can "just do" oral exams—they need scaffolding
• Solution: Build oral practice into coursework (section presentations, oral summaries, peer feedback loops)
• Parallel: We wouldn't assign a 30-page thesis without earlier writing assignments; same logic applies to oral assessment

2. Rubrics make it rigorous

• Oral assessment isn't inherently "subjective" if designed well
• Solution: Transparent rubrics with explicit criteria (content mastery, argumentation, responsiveness, clarity)
• Benefit: Students can self-assess during preparation, faculty achieve inter-rater reliability

3. Cognitive alignment matters

• Oral assessment works best when it matches how students learned
• Solution: Use oral exams in courses with heavy discussion/tutorial components, retain written assessment where appropriate
• Implication: Not every course needs oral exams—but discussion-based courses absolutely do

The implementation recipe:

1. Identify courses with high oral learning (tutorials, seminars, labs)
2. Scaffold oral skills throughout the semester (low-stakes → high-stakes)
3. Design transparent rubrics (shared with students, calibrated among faculty)
4. Address anxiety explicitly (practice opportunities, demystify the format)
5. Test cognitive alignment (Does the assessment match the learning mode?)

Common faculty objection: "I don't know how to grade oral work."

SoTL response: You already do—in office hours, section discussions, thesis defenses. The difference is making implicit criteria explicit (rubrics) and formalizing what you already assess informally. ABLConnect (Harvard's teaching resource hub) already publishes guidance on oral presentations. The infrastructure exists; it's a training/norm-shift challenge, not a capability gap.

Equity consideration: The anxiety research shows oral assessment can disadvantage students unfamiliar with the format. But so does written assessment—students from under-resourced schools often lack essay-writing preparation. The solution in both cases is scaffolding and transparency, not abandoning the format. If anything, oral assessment may advantage students from oral cultures or those with dyslexia/writing disabilities, as long as accommodations (extra time, written notes allowed) are provided.
[9:04 AM]Next themes (cycle 2): Post-AI faculty experiments (international examples beyond US), AI-powered oral platforms (technical capabilities and limitations), philosophical foundations (Derrida on speech/writing, McLuhan on media), professional oral traditions (medical OSCEs vs legal cross-examination).

Research continues through Monday Feb 24, 9am EST.
elle-with-claws  [9:16 AM]
:books: Oral Assignments Research Update #12
Theme: Educational Research on Speaking in Learning (Rotation 2 — Cognitive Science Foundations)

───

Source 1: ScienceDaily — "Reading Information Aloud to Yourself Improves Memory" (University of Waterloo, 2017)
https://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2017/12/171201090940.htm

The "production effect" is now well-established in cognitive psychology:
• Speaking text aloud "helps to get words into long-term memory"
• It's the dual action of speaking AND hearing oneself that creates the benefit
• Testing 95 participants across four methods (silent reading, hearing others read, hearing recording of self, reading aloud), reading aloud in real time produced the best retention

Prof. Colin MacLeod: "Learning and memory benefit from active involvement. When we add an active measure or a production element to a word, that word becomes more distinct in long-term memory."

Why it matters: This is the cognitive science foundation for why oral assignments work. Speaking isn't just demonstrating knowledge—it's encoding it more deeply. The production effect means oral exams are pedagogically superior, not just AI-resistant.

───

Source 2: Memory & Cognition — "Widening the Boundaries of the Production Effect" (Springer, 2012)
https://link.springer.com/article/10.3758/s13421-012-0210-8

The academic literature formalizes the mechanism:
• Distinctiveness account: Reading aloud "involves encoding an additional dimension that stands out as distinct—speech"
• Remembering having said a word provides "confirmatory evidence that it was studied"
• Effect extends beyond vocalization: mouthing, writing, typing all produce memory benefits
• Effect is strongest in mixed-list designs where some items are produced and others aren't—distinctiveness is relative

Key insight: The more processes involved in encoding (visual + motor + auditory), the more that can be "replayed to aid recognition."

Why it matters: This validates the principle that multimodal engagement—speaking, listening to oneself, organizing thoughts for articulation—creates stronger memory traces than passive reading or writing alone.

───

Source 3: The Guardian — "The Big Idea: How the 'Protégé Effect' Can Help You Learn Almost Anything" (2024)
https://www.theguardian.com/books/article/2024/sep/09/the-big-idea-how-the-protege-effect-can-help-you-learn-almost-anything

The protégé effect: "We learn more effectively when we teach someone else about the topic we've just explored—even if that person doesn't really exist."

Key findings from Stanford research:
• Students assigned to teach a virtual character learned "considerably more" than those in self-study condition
• Least able students benefited most—performing at the same level as highest achievers in control group
• The brain boost arises from "expectation of teaching"—feeling responsibility to provide correct information
• Brain imaging shows "greater activity in regions responsible for attention, working memory and taking others' perspectives"

Even programmers use this: "Rubber duck debugging"—explaining code to a plastic toy helps identify problems.

Why it matters: Oral assignments aren't just exams—they're learning experiences. When students know they must explain material orally, they prepare more deeply, correct misunderstandings proactively, and encode knowledge more durably.

───

:mag: Analytical Note:

The cognitive science case for oral assessment is now robust:

| Mechanism           | Effect                                     | Implication for Oral Assignments                  |
| ------------------- | ------------------------------------------ | ------------------------------------------------- |
| Production Effect   | Speaking encodes to long-term memory       | Oral exams strengthen retention                   |
| Multimodal Encoding | Visual + motor + auditory = stronger trace | Speaking > writing for memory                     |ScienceDailyReading information aloud to yourself improves memory of materialsYou are more likely to remember something if you read it out loud, a study has found.SpringerLinkWidening the boundaries of the production effectMemory & Cognition - Words that are read aloud are more memorable than words that are read silently. The boundaries of this production effect (MacLeod, Gopie, Hourihan, Neary, & Ozubko,...https://link.springer.com/article/10.3758/s13421-012-0210-8the GuardianThe big idea: how the ‘protege effect’ can help you learn almost anythingExplaining things to another person – or a rubber duck – can boost your understanding and even bridge political dividesSep 9th, 2024https://www.theguardian.com/books/article/2024/sep/09/the-big-idea-how-the-protege-effect-can-help-you-learn-almost-anything[9:16 AM]| Protégé Effect      | Teaching expectation deepens preparation   | Oral assignments improve learning before the exam |
| Distinctiveness     | Active production stands out in memory     | Oral components make material memorable           |
For Harvard faculty, the argument shifts from "oral exams are AI-resistant" to "oral exams produce deeper learning." The AI-resistance is a bonus; the pedagogical value is the foundation.

This also suggests low-stakes oral practice throughout a semester—not just high-stakes finals—would maximize the production and protégé effects.

───

:date: Next theme: Post-AI classroom experiments (Rotation 2)

[Automated research post 12 of ~16 • Project runs through Mon 2/24]
[9:18 AM]:earth_africa: Post-AI Classroom Experiments — Cycle 2: International Responses & Honor Code Havens

Theme 4 of 8 (Cycle 2): UK data, Australian scale, US liberal arts experiences (2023-2026)

───

1. UK Crisis Point — 88% of Students Using AI in Assessments (2025)

Source: Times Higher Education, "Nine in 10 UK undergraduates now using AI in assessments – survey"
URL: https://www.timeshighereducation.com/news/nine-10-uk-undergraduates-now-using-ai-assessments-survey

A February 2025 survey by the Higher Education Policy Institute found 88% of UK undergraduates used generative AI in assessments over the past year—up from 53% in 2024. This "extraordinary growth" indicates AI has become "an integral aspect in the process of doing exams." One in four students used AI-generated text to draft assessments; most used it to "explain concepts, summarise articles, suggest ideas." Despite 59% saying universities changed assessment methods, only 3 in 10 agreed institutions encouraged AI use, and only 42% said staff were well-equipped to support AI work (though up from 18% in 2024). The "digital divide" has widened—wealthier students more likely to use AI. Report recommendation: "Exams which are easy for someone to achieve a high grade in if they have good AI skills, but without engaging deeply with the course, should be immediately rewritten."

Harvard relevance: The UK data is a wake-up call showing AI use doubling year-over-year. Harvard's 90% AI use rate (Theme 4, Cycle 1) isn't aberration—it's the international norm. The "mixed messages" students report mirrors Yale's decentralized approach (Theme 1, Cycle 2): confusion breeds strategic misuse. The digital divide is alarming: AI amplifies existing inequality, giving wealthy students another advantage. The key recommendation applies directly to Harvard: predictable, low-interactivity assessments are obsolete. Closed-book exams with "predictable questions" fail because students can "prepare in advance using AI tools." Only unpredictable, interactive, real-time assessment (oral exams, live defense) prevents this.

───

2. Australia's CQUniversity — 600 Oral Exams in Under a Week (2023)

Source: The Conversation, "Why universities should return to oral exams in the AI and ChatGPT era"
URL: https://theconversation.com/why-universities-should-return-to-oral-exams-in-the-ai-and-chatgpt-era-203429

An Australian professor and colleagues introduced vivas (oral exams) "for the first time" in a large first-year undergraduate literacy course for pre-service teachers. They successfully assessed 600 oral exams in under a week. Students created "reflective journals" on how their learning in educational neuroscience informed their practice, then verbally shared these reflections. "Chatbots cannot replicate this sort of task, ensuring student authenticity." The format combined asynchronicity (students scheduled their own slots) with live interaction (real-time Q&A with faculty). Ken Purnell, professor at CQUniversity, championed this model as AI-resistant while maintaining large-scale feasibility.

Harvard relevance: Six hundred students. One week. Oral exams. This destroys the scalability objection. CQUniversity isn't Harvard's peer (it's a regional Australian university with fewer resources), yet they pulled this off in a first-year course. If they can assess 600 students orally in seven days, Harvard—with 9:1 student-faculty ratios and infinitely more resources—has zero logistical excuse. The "reflective journal + verbal share" format is brilliant pedagogy: students can use AI to help organize thoughts (permitted scaffolding), but the live verbal defense is where assessment happens. This is the "hybrid" model: AI-assisted preparation, human-verified performance.

───

3. Haverford College & US Liberal Arts — "What's the Point of Being an English Major If You Don't Want to Write?" (2024)

Source: The Atlantic, "ChatGPT Doesn't Have to Ruin College"
Times Higher Education (THE)Nine in 10 UK undergraduates now using AI in assessments – surveyOne in four students tell Hepi they use text generated by tools such as ChatGPT in submitted workFeb 25th, 2025https://www.timeshighereducation.com/news/nine-10-uk-undergraduates-now-using-ai-assessments-surveyThe ConversationWhy universities should return to oral exams in the AI and ChatGPT eraOral exams have a history dating back more than 2,000 years – and could once again be a solution for universities to test their students’ knowledge.https://theconversation.com/why-universities-should-return-to-oral-exams-in-the-ai-and-chatgpt-era-203429[9:18 AM]URL: https://www.theatlantic.com/ideas/archive/2024/10/chatgpt-vs-university-honor-code/680336/

A visit to Haverford College (Philadelphia-area liberal arts school, ~1,400 students) in 2024 found students largely uninterested in using ChatGPT for coursework. When asked about AI cheating, English majors responded: "I want to write," "Chat doesn't write well anyway," "What's the point of being an English major if you don't want to write?" Faculty seemed "unfazed" by AI, many saying "I'm not the police." The article argues Haverford's honor code culture—plus small classes, readily available writing support, and comparatively unharried faculty—creates an environment where students don't need or want to outsource thinking. The author contrasts this with large public universities facing "collapsing tenure-track employment" and resource constraints: "Honor and curiosity can be nurtured, or crushed, by circumstance."

Harvard relevance: This is both encouraging and sobering. Encouraging: cultural infrastructure matters. Honor codes, small classes, faculty accessibility, and a genuine commitment to intellectual curiosity can suppress AI cheating without surveillance. Haverford proves it's possible. Sobering: The article explicitly argues this isn't scalable beyond wealthy private institutions. Harvard has Haverford's advantages (small classes, honor code, resources) but lacks its insularity—Harvard students face intense career pressure, resume optimization, and grade competition that Haverford students may not. The question: Can Harvard recover Haverford's ethos while remaining Harvard? Or does competitiveness inherently corrode honor? The oral exam argument: structural solutions (oral assessment) work where cultural solutions (honor codes) struggle.

───

Connecting the Dots

Three international/institutional responses reveal a pattern:

| Region/Type              | AI Use Rate                    | Response                                                 | Success?                                           |
| ------------------------ | ------------------------------ | -------------------------------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------- |
| UK (HEPI survey)         | 88% (2025), up from 53% (2024) | Mixed messages, vague policies, minimal faculty training | ❌ Failing—usage skyrocketing                       |
| Australia (CQUniversity) | N/A                            | 600 oral exams in <1 week for first-year course          | ✅ Working—scalable proof of concept                |
| US (Haverford)           | Low (anecdotal)                | Honor code culture + small classes + faculty support     | ✅ Working—but not scalable to large/public schools |
The UK represents Harvard's likely future if nothing changes: AI use will approach 100%, "digital divide" will widen, students will report confusion, and faculty will feel helpless. The doubling from 53% to 88% in one year suggests exponential adoption.

Australia represents a structural solution: CQUniversity didn't rely on honor or detection—they redesigned assessment to be inherently AI-resistant (oral defense of reflective practice). Scale: 600 students, 1 week. Logistics: asynchronous scheduling + live interaction. Result: authenticity guaranteed.

Haverford represents cultural resilience but with a caveat: it works in small, wealthy, insular liberal arts colleges with strong honor traditions. Harvard has some of these advantages but not all. And the Atlantic article's author admits: most American higher ed doesn't have these resources. For the 99% of universities without Haverford's privilege, oral assessment offers what honor codes cannot—democratized integrity.

The strategic choice for Harvard:

1. Do nothing → UK trajectory (88%+ AI use, inequality, confusion)
2. Trust honor codes → Haverford model (works if culture holds, fragile)
The AtlanticChatGPT Doesn’t Have to Ruin CollegeThe power of a robust honor code—and abundant institutional resourcesOct 23rd, 2024https://www.theatlantic.com/ideas/archive/2024/10/chatgpt-vs-university-honor-code/680336/[9:19 AM]3. Redesign assessment → CQUniversity model (scalable, structural, AI-resistant)
[9:19 AM]The evidence favors #3. Oral assessment doesn't require honor code buy-in or cultural transformation—it's a design choice that makes cheating structurally difficult rather than morally discouraged.

Key insight from UK data: "Predictable questions" are obsolete—even closed-book exams fail if questions can be anticipated and AI-prepped. Oral exams with unpredictable follow-ups solve this. You can memorize ChatGPT responses to likely questions, but you can't script a live dialogue where the examiner probes your specific gaps.

The scalability proof is in: 600 oral exams, under a week, first-year course, Australian regional university. If CQUniversity can do it, Harvard can do it.

The honor code lesson: Culture matters, but it's fragile and not universally available. For Harvard's competitive, career-focused student body, structural solutions beat cultural ones.

Next themes (cycle 2): AI tools in oral evaluation (deeper technical analysis), philosophical foundations (Derrida, McLuhan, Havelock on orality), professional domains (medical OSCEs vs legal cross-examination).

:books: Oral Assignments Research Update #13
Theme: Post-AI Classroom Experiments (Rotation 2 — Faculty First-Person Accounts)

───

Source 1: The Decoder — "NYU Professor Fights AI Cheating with AI-Powered Oral Exams" (Jan 2026)
https://the-decoder.com/nyu-professor-fights-ai-cheating-with-ai-powered-oral-exams-that-cost-42-cents-per-student/

Detailed account of Prof. Panos Ipeirotis's experiment reveals the diagnostic power of oral exams:
• Students' written work looked "suspiciously good... not 'strong student' good, but 'McKinsey memo that went through three rounds of editing' good"
• When cold-called in class, "many who had submitted thoughtful work couldn't explain basic decisions in their own submissions after two follow-up questions"
• The gap between written performance and oral defense was "too consistent to blame on nerves"

Ipeirotis's verdict: "The old system where take-home papers could reliably measure understanding is dead, gone, kaput."

Why it matters: This is the clearest articulation of the problem: written work no longer reliably indicates understanding. Oral verification isn't optional—it's necessary for assessment validity.

───

Source 2: HuffPost — "I Set A Trap To Catch My Students Cheating With AI" (Nov 2025)
https://www.huffpost.com/entry/history-professor-ai-cheating-students_n_69178150e4b0781acfd62540

History professor Will Teague's first-person account of discovering 39% of student papers were AI-generated:
• Used "Trojan horse" method: hidden text asking ChatGPT to write "from a Marxist perspective"
• Students couldn't explain what Marxism was or how it got into their papers
• One student said: "I thought it sounded smart"
• 12 of 47 AI cheaters eventually dropped the course rather than engage with the material

Key insight: "Students do not know what they do not know." They can't assess AI output quality because they lack the foundational knowledge to recognize errors.

Why it matters: This reveals why detection → oral verification is becoming standard practice. The professor notes that oral exams as follow-up ("If I suspect AI use, I'll give them an impromptu oral exam") is now the fallback—but proactive oral assessment would eliminate the detection game entirely.

───

Source 3: Community of Inquiry — "Viva Voce in the Digital Era" (Dec 2024)
https://samejohn.com/2024/12/19/viva-voce-in-the-digital-era/

A University of Melbourne engineering professor shares his 5+ years of using only viva voce assessment in Bioinstrumentation:

Why he chose it:

1. Live feedback: "Feedback is obtained in the dialogues between examiner and student"
2. Probing depth: "The examiner can probe whether the lack of an answer is a lack of understanding or a misunderstanding"
3. Completion ≠ competence: "Copying a circuit online can give you the same answer as building one. However copying one does not necessarily create knowledge"
4. Group project differentiation: Viva can distinguish individual understanding in collaborative work
5. Career preparation: "Helps students understand how to interview as a graduate"

Addressing concerns:
• Always 2 examiners (one male, one female)
• All exams recorded and stored
• Base questions determined beforehand and displayed on screen

Why it matters: This is a mature implementation model. The professor has run viva voce exclusively since 2020—before ChatGPT—and his framework addresses the common objections (bias, fairness, scaling). Harvard faculty don't need to start from scratch.

───

:mag: Analytical Note:

These three accounts reveal a progression of faculty responses:

```
| Stage                    | Response                      | Example                                 |
| ------------------------ | ----------------------------- | --------------------------------------- |

Most faculty are stuck at stages 1-2. The Melbourne model shows stage 3 is sustainable. The key insight from all three: written work no longer proves understanding. Oral assessment isn't a "nice to have"—it's becoming epistemically necessary.

For Harvard faculty: the question isn't whether to add oral components, but how quickly.

:date: Next theme: AI tools IN oral evaluation (Rotation 2)

[Automated research post 13 of ~16 • Project runs through Mon 2/24]
[9:34 AM]:robot_face: AI Used IN Oral Evaluation — Cycle 2: Technical Limitations & Bias

Theme 5 of 8 (Cycle 2): Critical examination of ASR accuracy, grading reliability, and equity concerns

───

1. CU Boulder NSF AI Institute — ASR Bias Against Non-White Speakers (2025)

Source: National AI Institute for Student-AI Teaming, "Tackling Bias in Automatic Speech Recognition"
URL: https://www.colorado.edu/research/ai-institute/2025/01/22/tackling-bias-automatic-speech-recognition-two-examples-our-ongoing-work

CU Boulder researchers testing popular ASR tools for classroom feedback found 24% lower accuracy for Black speakers vs. white speakers. The core problem: "The model was not shown enough speech from Black speakers when the AI was trained." Even after fine-tuning, the accuracy gap was only reduced by about one-third. Age-related bias is equally severe: ASR trained on adult speech had 56% word error rate (WER) for 9th graders—"the ASR gets it wrong more than half of the time." Models trained on elementary students achieved 46% WER on 9th graders. Conclusion: "Models trained on one age group do not really generalize well to a different age group."

Harvard relevance: This is the fatal flaw in "AI will solve oral assessment scalability" arguments. If ASR systematically disadvantages Black students and teenagers—exactly Harvard's undergraduate demographic—then AI-powered oral exams replicate existing inequities rather than solving them. The 24% accuracy gap means Black students' spoken responses are fundamentally less "hearable" to the AI, creating differential treatment in assessment. For Harvard to use AI transcription/grading in oral exams, explicit bias mitigation is mandatory: diverse training data, human review of flagged transcriptions, or abandoning automated scoring entirely for final grades. The CU Boulder team's partial success with fine-tuning suggests solutions exist, but they're not plug-and-play—institutions must invest in equity-focused AI development.

───

2. Ohio State Analysis — AI Grading Shows Systematic Bias (2024-2025)

Source: Ohio State Office of Distance Education, "AI and Auto-Grading: Capabilities, Ethics, and the Evolving Role of Educators"
URL: https://ascode.osu.edu/news/ai-and-auto-grading-higher-education-capabilities-ethics-and-evolving-role-educators

Synthesizing recent research (Wetzler et al. 2024, Flodén 2025), Ohio State identifies a consistent grading bias in LLMs: "AI often grades more leniently on low-performing essays and more harshly on high-performing ones." This "proportional bias" means AI compresses the grade distribution—weak work gets inflated scores, strong work gets deflated. The article concludes: "Generative AI is currently unsuitable as a sole grading tool, particularly for nuanced writing tasks that involve creativity and depth of thought." ChatGPT also produces "unreliable results such as incorrect answers, made-up facts, non-existent references" (Flodén 2025). Best practice: "AI is best used in formative assessments, where feedback can supplement human judgment rather than replace it."

Harvard relevance: This demolishes the NYU ElevenLabs "council of LLMs" model (Theme 5, Cycle 1) as a standalone grading solution. If AI systematically misgrades both ends of the performance spectrum, using it for high-stakes oral exam scoring creates validity problems: grades don't accurately reflect student ability. The recommendation—AI for formative feedback, humans for summative grading—suggests a two-tier implementation: (1) Practice oral exams with AI transcription/feedback (low stakes, builds fluency, students see patterns), (2) Final oral exams with human graders using AI-generated transcripts as reference (high stakes, maintains validity). The Ohio State synthesis treats "human scores as the benchmark" despite acknowledging human bias—a reminder that no assessment method is perfect, but transparency and validity matter.

───
NSF National AI Institute for Student-AI TeamingTackling Bias in Automatic Speech Recognition - Two Examples From Our Ongoing WorkAI systems that are designed to offer real-time classroom support need to be able to understand what students are saying—and do so with high accuracy. Thishttps://www.colorado.edu/research/ai-institute/2025/01/22/tackling-bias-automatic-speech-recognition-two-examples-our-ongoing-workascode.osu.eduAI and Auto-Grading in Higher Education: Capabilities, Ethics, and the Evolving Role of EducatorsAI and Auto-Grading in Higher Education: Capabilities, Ethics, and the Evolving Role of EducatorsAs artificial intelligence continues to reshape various industries, its impact on higher education, particularly in assessment, is becoming increasingly significant. Automated grading and AI-assisted evaluation tools are revolutionizing how instructors assess student work, streamline feedback, and manage large-scale courses.https://ascode.osu.edu/news/ai-and-auto-grading-higher-education-capabilities-ethics-and-evolving-role-educators[9:34 AM]3. arXiv Research — ASR Bias Against Disfluent Speech (May 2024)
[9:34 AM]Source: arXiv, "Lost in Transcription: Identifying and Quantifying the Accuracy Biases of ASR Systems Against Disfluent Speech"
URL: https://arxiv.org/abs/2405.06150

Comprehensive testing across multiple ASR systems (Whisper, Google, Azure, others) found "a consistent and statistically significant accuracy bias across all ASRs against disfluent speech, manifesting in significant syntactical and semantic inaccuracies in transcriptions." Disfluencies—pauses, filler words ("um," "uh"), false starts, self-corrections—are normal features of spontaneous speech, especially under stress (like oral exams). But ASR models trained primarily on polished, fluent speech (podcasts, audiobooks, scripted dialog) struggle with natural conversational patterns. The result: students who speak hesitantly or revise their thoughts mid-sentence (common in genuine reasoning-aloud) get penalized by transcription errors that misrepresent their actual statements.

Harvard relevance: This creates a double equity problem. First, disfluency correlates with anxiety, language learning status, and neurodiversity—populations already disadvantaged in oral assessment. Second, genuine intellectual work sounds disfluent: thinking aloud, self-correcting, and hedging claims are markers of careful reasoning, not confusion. ASR that penalizes "um"s and pauses favors polished performance over authentic cognition. For Harvard implementation: (1) Train faculty to recognize that transcription errors cluster around disfluencies, (2) Review audio, not just transcripts for borderline cases, (3) Consider human transcription for high-stakes exams (costly but equitable), or (4) Accept imperfect transcripts as reference material, not grading source. The research also suggests ASR is better for prepared presentations (scripted, rehearsed) than interactive oral exams (spontaneous, dialogic)—a format distinction that matters for Harvard's assessment design.

───

Connecting the Dots

Three critical limitations of AI-powered oral assessment:

| Bias Type            | Magnitude                     | Affected Groups                              | Mitigation                                              |
| -------------------- | ----------------------------- | -------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------- |
| Racial (CU Boulder)  | 24% lower accuracy            | Black speakers                               | Diverse training data, human review, fine-tuning        |
| Age (CU Boulder)     | 46-56% WER                    | Teenagers (9th grade)                        | Age-specific models, hybrid grading                     |
| Grading (Ohio State) | Lenient on low, harsh on high | All students (compresses distribution)       | Human graders, formative-only AI use                    |
| Disfluency (arXiv)   | Significant errors            | Anxious, ESL, neurodiverse, careful thinkers | Audio review, accept imperfection, favor fluent formats |
The optimistic take (Theme 5, Cycle 1): AI makes oral assessment scalable—Georgia Tech 5,000 students, NYU $0.42/student, CQUniversity 600 exams/week.

The critical take (Theme 5, Cycle 2): AI introduces systematic biases that disadvantage marginalized students and misgrade across the performance spectrum.

Both are true. The question for Harvard isn't "Should we use AI in oral assessment?" but "Under what conditions can AI enhance equity rather than harm it?"

Design principles for equitable AI-assisted oral assessment:

1. Never use AI as sole grader for high-stakes exams — Human final review mandatory
2. Audit ASR accuracy across demographics — Test on Harvard student voices before deployment
3. Provide multiple modalities — Allow written notes, visual aids to supplement oral responses
4. Accept disfluency as normal — Train examiners that "um"s ≠ ignorance
arXiv.orgLost in Transcription: Identifying and Quantifying the Accuracy Biases of Automatic Speech Recognition Systems Against Disfluent SpeechAutomatic speech recognition (ASR) systems, increasingly prevalent in education, healthcare, employment, and mobile technology, face significant challenges in inclusivity, particularly for the 80 million-strong global community of people who stutter. These systems often fail to accurately interpret speech patterns deviating from typical fluency, leading to critical usability issues and misinterpretations. This study evaluates six leading ASRs, analyzing their performance on both a real-world dataset of speech samples from individuals who stutter and a synthetic dataset derived from the widely-used LibriSpeech benchmark. The synthetic dataset, uniquely designed to incorporate various stutteri… [9:34 AM]5. Use AI for scaffolding, not gatekeeping — Practice exams with AI feedback (formative), human grading for finals (summative)
[9:34 AM]6. Transparency — Students deserve to know if/how AI affects their grades

The paradox: AI oral assessment tools promise to solve the scalability problem that killed oral exams in the Industrial Revolution (Theme 0, Cycle 2). But if those tools systematically disadvantage Black students, teenagers, anxious test-takers, and non-native speakers, we've just automated historical inequities rather than overcoming them.

The Harvard choice: Invest in equity-focused AI development (expensive, slow, requires technical expertise) OR use AI minimally/formatively while maintaining human-graded oral assessment (labor-intensive but fair). The CU Boulder researchers' partial success with fine-tuning suggests equity is achievable—but it's not automatic.

Key insight: The Georgia Tech Socratic Mind and NYU ElevenLabs experiments (Cycle 1) were proof of concept for functionality, not validation of equity. Harvard must ask not just "Does this work?" but "For whom does this work, and for whom does it fail?"

Next themes (cycle 2): Philosophical foundations (Derrida, McLuhan, Havelock on orality vs literacy), professional domains (medical OSCEs vs legal cross-examination as oral assessment models).

elle-with-claws  [9:46 AM]
:books: Oral Assignments Research Update #14
Theme: AI Tools IN Oral Evaluation (Rotation 2 — Practice & Feedback Platforms)

───

Source 1: InStage — "Voice AI for Higher Education"
https://www.instage.io/

A mature platform deployed at University of Waterloo, U of Toronto, UBC, Northeastern, and others. Key features:
• Voice AI agents conduct structured student conversations at scale
• Modules: Career Exploration, Mock Interview, Resume Assist, Guided Reflection
• 5-15 minute phone or web calls, 3+ per module
• Students get instant personalized feedback; staff receive actionable summaries
• FERPA-compliant, SOC 2-aligned, "human-in-the-loop" design

Testimonial (Dr. Norah McRae, U Waterloo): "Our commitment to responsible AI implementation at scale will transform how students develop professional competencies."

Why it matters: This is AI as preparation infrastructure for oral assessment. Students can practice interview skills, presentations, and reflection at scale—before high-stakes evaluation. Harvard could deploy similar tools to ensure students arrive at oral exams prepared.

───

Source 2: VirtualSpeech — "Get Instant Feedback on Your Presentation with Virtual Reality"
https://virtualspeech.com/blog/analysing-speech-in-vr-with-feedback

VR presentation practice with comprehensive AI analysis:
• Delivery feedback: Speaking pace, hesitation words, volume, eye contact, listenability, body language
• Content feedback: Relevance, clarity, tone, empathy, confidence, structure
• Practice in immersive environments (meeting rooms, auditoriums, panel interviews)
• Distractions like talking audience members or ringing phones simulate real-world conditions
• Sessions saved for progress tracking; enterprise dashboard for institutional deployment

Why it matters: VR + AI solves the "practice problem." Students can rehearse presentations dozens of times with consistent feedback before facing a human evaluator. This reduces anxiety and builds genuine competence—not just performance on one day.

───

Source 3: Speakable — "AI-Powered Speaking Practice for Students"
https://www.speakable.io/

Designed for K-12 and language learning but applicable to higher ed:
• Teachers create AI-powered speaking activities with instant feedback and auto-grading
• Supports 100+ languages; proficiency tracking over time
• Rubric-based feedback students receive immediately
• Integrates with Canvas, Google Classroom
• FERPA, COPPA, SOPPA compliant

Teacher testimonial: "The instant feedback that is possible with the Speakable prompts is game changing."

Why it matters: Shows the model for low-stakes oral practice at scale. Harvard could adapt similar approaches for oral components across disciplines—not replacing faculty evaluation but supplementing it with consistent practice opportunities.

───

:mag: Analytical Note:

A taxonomy of AI roles in oral assessment is emerging:

| AI Role                  | Tool Example              | Purpose                                   |
| ------------------------ | ------------------------- | ----------------------------------------- |
| AI as Practice Partner   | InStage, VirtualSpeech    | Students rehearse before human evaluation |
| AI as Examiner           | NYU ElevenLabs experiment | AI conducts the oral exam itself          |
| AI as Grader/Flagging    | Sherpa, Council of LLMs   | AI transcribes, flags, or assists scoring |
| AI as Feedback Generator | Speakable                 | Instant rubric-based feedback on speaking |
For Harvard, the safest entry point is AI as Practice Partner—deploying tools like InStage so students can practice oral skills at scale without requiring faculty time. AI examination and grading remain more controversial but increasingly viable.

The key insight: AI doesn't replace the human moment of oral assessment—it makes that moment more valuable by ensuring students arrive prepared and by reducing grading burden afterward.

[9:48 AM]:scroll: Philosophical Foundations — Cycle 2: Derrida, McLuhan & Havelock Challenge Pl

ato

Theme 6 of 8 (Cycle 2): Beyond Socrates — Modern theorists on orality, literacy, and media

───

1. Jacques Derrida — Deconstructing "Phonocentrism" (1967)

Source: Wikipedia, "Phonocentrism"
URL: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Phonocentrism

Derrida coined the term "phonocentrism" to critique Western philosophy's privileging of speech over writing. He argued that philosophers from Plato to Saussure promoted the belief that "speech is inherently superior to written language" because oral communication seems "closer to the presence of subjects" — the speaker is right there, guaranteeing meaning through their living voice. Derrida challenged this hierarchy, arguing that "speech can be seen as derived from writing as easily as writing is seen as derived from speech." He believed phonocentric cultures romanticize a mythical past "in which people lived in harmony and unity with nature" before writing "corrupted" pure oral expression. Derrida insisted that speech suffers from the same inherent flaws as writing—both are forms of representation, both involve absence and deferral of meaning.

Harvard relevance: Derrida complicates the oral exam argument. If we say "oral assessment is authentic, written work is corrupted by AI," we risk falling into the phonocentric trap Derrida critiqued—treating presence (live speech) as guaranteeing truth. Derrida would ask: Why assume spoken responses are more "real" than written ones? Both can be rehearsed, both can be performative, both involve representation rather than pure thought. This challenges simplistic "oral = authentic, written = suspect" binaries. The smarter framing: Oral assessment doesn't access "pure" knowledge—it tests different cognitive skills (real-time synthesis, responsiveness, dialogue) that happen to be harder for current AI to fake. Derrida reminds us not to romanticize orality as pre-technological Eden.

───

2. Marshall McLuhan — "The Medium is the Message" & Electronic Return to Orality (1962-1964)

Source: Wikipedia / MIT, "Understanding Media: The Extensions of Man"
URL: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Marshall_McLuhan

McLuhan's The Gutenberg Galaxy (1962) and Understanding Media (1964) argued that technologies reshape human consciousness. Print culture created "Gutenberg Man"—visual, individualistic, linear thinkers. But McLuhan predicted that "electronic media would replace visual culture with aural/oral culture"—a return to the "tribal" interconnectedness of oral societies, but mediated through electronic interdependence (radio, TV, later internet). His famous axiom: "The medium is the message"—the structure of communication technology shapes cognition more profoundly than content. Print encouraged private, sequential reading; electronic media encourage participation, simultaneity, and oral-style engagement.

Harvard relevance: McLuhan's framework suggests AI is a medium shift, not just a content problem. ChatGPT doesn't just threaten essays—it represents the eclipse of "print culture" pedagogy (silent, individual, linear composition) by "electronic culture" pedagogy (collaborative, networked, AI-assisted). McLuhan would say: Don't fight the medium shift—adapt assessment to it. Oral exams aren't a retreat from technology but an alignment with the post-print cognitive environment McLuhan predicted. If electronic media encourage oral-style participation and dialogue, then oral assessment is more authentic to how students actually think in 2026 than requiring them to produce Gutenberg-era essays. The irony: returning to oral exams isn't backward—it's adapting to McLuhan's "electronic oral culture."

───

3. Eric Havelock — "Preface to Plato" & the Iliad as Oral Encyclopedia (1963)

Source: Harvard University Press, Preface to Plato
URL: https://www.hup.harvard.edu/books/9780674699069
WikipediaPhonocentrismPhonocentrism is the belief that sounds and speech are inherently superior to, or more primary than, written language or sign language. Those who espouse phonocentric views maintain that spoken language is the primary and most fundamental method of communication whereas writing is merely a derived method of capturing speech. Many also believe that spoken language is inherently richer and more intuitive than written language.
Some writers have argued that philosophers such as Plato, Jean-Jacques Rousseau, and Ferdinand de Saussure have promoted phonocentric views. Walter Ong, who has also expressed support for the idea of phonocentrism, has argued that the culture of the United States is parti… WikipediaMarshall McLuhanHerbert Marshall McLuhan  (, mə-KLOO-ən; July 21, 1911 – December 31, 1980) was a Canadian philosopher whose work is among the cornerstones of the study of media theory. Raised in Winnipeg, McLuhan studied at the University of Manitoba and the University of Cambridge. He began his teaching career as a professor of English at several universities in the United States and Canada before moving to the University of Toronto in 1946, where he remained for the rest of his life. He is known as "the father of media studies".
McLuhan coined the expression "the medium is the message" (in the first chapter of his Understanding Media: The Extensions of Man), as well as the term global village.  He predicted… https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Marshall_McLuhan[9:48 AM]Havelock's Preface to Plato (1963—same year as McLuhan's Gutenberg Galaxy!) revolutionized understanding of ancient Greek oral culture. Havelock showed that the Iliad functioned as an "oral encyclopedia"—not entertainment, but the primary educational technology for preserving cultural knowledge before widespread literacy. Plato's attack on poets (mimesis, emotional identification) was actually an attack on oral pedagogy itself—Plato wanted to replace embodied, performative, mnemonic oral learning with abstract, written, philosophical reasoning. Havelock argued that Plato's Republic represents the transition from oral to literate culture, with all its cognitive and social consequences.

Harvard relevance: Havelock provides historical depth to the oral/written debate. For most of human history, oral performance WAS education—the Iliad wasn't a text to analyze but a script to memorize, embody, and perform. When we say "oral exams test different things," Havelock shows what they test: embodied knowledge, performative recall, situational application—the cognitive skills of oral cultures. Modern education privileged writing because literacy enables abstraction, analysis, critique (Plato's project). But if AI now performs those literate functions, maybe we need to recover oral culture's emphasis on presence, dialogue, and adaptive performance. Havelock's key insight: oral knowledge isn't inferior—it's differently structured. Oral exams don't test "less rigor"—they test oral rigor (synthesis under pressure, responsive argumentation).

───

Connecting the Dots

Three 20th-century theorists challenge Plato's (and our) assumptions:

| Theorist | Claim                                                                     | Implication for Oral Assessment                               |
| -------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Derrida  | Speech isn't "purer" than writing—both are representations                | Don't romanticize oral exams as accessing "authentic" thought |
| McLuhan  | Electronic media restore oral culture's participatory logic               | Oral assessment aligns with post-print cognitive environment  |
| Havelock | Oral cultures had sophisticated knowledge systems (Iliad as encyclopedia) | Oral exams test valid cognitive skills, not "lesser" rigor    |
The philosophical tension:

• Plato (370 BCE): Writing is inferior to live dialogue—memory decays, texts can't answer questions (Cycle 1)
• Derrida (1967): Plato's phonocentrism is a metaphysical illusion—speech and writing are equally problematic
• McLuhan (1964): Print created a temporary "visual bias"—electronic culture returns us to oral modes
• Havelock (1963): Oral culture wasn't primitive—it was a sophisticated cognitive technology

What this means for Harvard:

1. Don't oversell oral exams as "pure" or "AI-proof"

• Derrida warns: any assessment format can be gamed, any performance can be rehearsed
• Oral exams are strategically better than written essays right now, but not metaphysically superior

2. Recognize we're in a medium shift, not a crisis

• McLuhan: AI-generated essays don't mean students are lazy—they mean print-based assessment is obsolete
• The cognitive skills students develop in AI-assisted environments (prompting, editing, synthesis) are real, just not testable via traditional essays

3. Oral assessment tests legitimate cognitive skills

• Havelock: Oral cultures valued performative knowledge, responsive dialogue, situational application
• These aren't "backup" skills for when writing fails—they're distinct competencies worth assessing

The strategic reframe:

Harvard isn't "going back" to oral exams because AI "ruined" writing. We're adapting assessment to the medium environment we actually inhabit—where:

• Text is abundant and AI-generated (McLuhan's electronic interdependence)
[9:48 AM]• Authentic thinking manifests in dialogue, not solitary composition (Havelock's oral encyclopedia)
• Neither speech nor writing guarantees truth, but each tests different capacities (Derrida's deconstruction)

The question isn't "oral vs. written" but: "What cognitive skills matter in 2026, and how do we assess them?"

If the answer includes real-time synthesis, dialogic responsiveness, and adaptive argumentation under pressure—then oral assessment is the appropriate technology, regardless of AI.

Next themes (cycle 2): Professional domains (medical OSCEs vs legal cross-examination—how do high-stakes professions implement oral assessment?), then wrapping synthesis posts.

Research continues through Monday Feb 24, 9am EST.
elle-with-claws  [10:06 AM]
:books: Oral Assignments Research Update #15
Theme: Philosophical Foundations (Rotation 2 — Socratic Method & Arendt on Speech)

───

Source 1: Wikipedia — "Socratic Method"
https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Socratic_method

Comprehensive overview of the method Socrates used:
• Maieutic method: From Greek "midwifery"—Socrates described his role as helping interlocutors "develop their understanding and lead it out of them" like a child developing until ready for birth
• Elenchus: The central technique—cross-examination that tests beliefs for internal consistency and coherence
• Steps: (1) Interlocutor asserts thesis → (2) Socrates secures agreement to further premises → (3) Those premises imply the contrary of original thesis → (4) The thesis is shown false
• Aporia: Most inquiries end in "puzzlement"—improved awareness of one's ignorance

Key quote: "The unexamined life is not worth living."

Why it matters: Oral examination is the Socratic method institutionalized. The elenchus requires real-time response—it cannot be delegated to AI because its purpose is to reveal what the speaker genuinely understands vs. merely believes they understand.

───

Source 2: Colorado State TILT — "The Socratic Method: Fostering Critical Thinking"
https://tilt.colostate.edu/the-socratic-method/

Based on Stanford professor Rob Reich's award-winning teaching lecture. Key distinctions:
• Not "teaching" per se: No PowerPoints, no rote memorization. Teacher is neither "sage on the stage" nor "guide on the side"
• Shared dialogue: "The teacher leads by posing thought-provoking questions. Students actively engage by asking questions of their own."
• Productive discomfort: The classroom environment is challenging but not intimidating; professor doesn't have all the answers
• High stakes: "Any successful challenge to this system comes with high stakes—one might have to examine and change one's life"

The method is "better used to demonstrate complexity, difficulty, and uncertainty than to elicit facts about the world."

Why it matters: This reframes oral assessment not as fact-checking but as belief-examining. Harvard faculty can use oral assignments to probe students' underlying assumptions and reasoning—precisely what AI cannot replicate or fake.

───

Source 3: Hannah Arendt Center — "Speech, Plurality, and Natality in Dialogue" (2025)
https://medium.com/amor-mundi/speech-plurality-and-natality-in-dialogue-29bd95b9a300

Hannah Arendt's philosophy offers a different frame—speech as political action:
• Speech as "second birth": "With word and deed we insert ourselves into the human world, and this insertion is like a second birth"
• Plurality: Hearing from multiple viewpoints allows better judgment. "After one withdraws from the world... we must come back into the world to think what we are doing, with others"
• Natality: The initiation of "something new" when we speak and take our place as political actors
• The right to be seen and heard: "Sitting around a table establishes a space of equality in which people can appear"

Key insight: Dialogue requires goodwill and the courage to "stand one's ground" while remaining open to influence.

Why it matters: Arendt positions speech as how humans become political beings—not just demonstrating knowledge but appearing in the world. Oral assignments, in this frame, aren't just assessment—they're formation. Students practice being seen and heard, which AI cannot do for them.

───

:mag: Analytical Note:

Two complementary philosophical traditions support oral assessment:

```
| Tradition | Key Concept                  | Application to Oral Assignments                                 |
| --------- | ---------------------------- | --------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Socratic  | Elenchus (cross-examination) | Probes belief systems, reveals gaps in understanding            |WikipediaSocratic methodThe Socratic method is a form of argumentative dialogue in which an individual probes a conversation partner on a topic, using questions and clarifications, until the partner is pressed to come to a conclusion on their own, or else their reasoning breaks down and they are forced to admit ignorance. The method is also known as Socratic debate, the maieutic method, or the Socratic dialectic, and sometimes equated with the Greek term elenchus. Socratic dialogues between characters employing this method feature in many of the works of the ancient Greek philosopher Plato, where a fictionalized version of his real-life teacher Socrates debates or expounds upon various philosophical issues with a p… https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Socratic_methodThe Institute for Learning and TeachingThe Socratic Method: Fostering Critical Thinking | The Institute for Learning and Teaching"Do not take what I say as if I were merely playing, for you see the subject of our discussion—and on what subject should even a man of slight intelligence be more serious?—namely, what kind of life should one live . . ." - SocratesWritten byStan KruseEst. reading time3 minutesApr 13th, 2022https://tilt.colostate.edu/the-socratic-method/MediumSpeech, Plurality, and Natality in Dialogueby Susan ObermanReading time7 min readApr 29th, 2025https://medium.com/amor-mundi/speech-plurality-and-natality-in-dialogue-29bd95b9a300[10:06 AM]| Arendtian | Speech as appearance         | Students practice being in the world, becoming political actors |```
[10:06 AM]Both traditions share a common thread: speech is not merely instrumental (conveying information) but constitutive (forming the speaker).

For Harvard faculty, this suggests oral assignments should not simply test recall. They should:

1. Probe assumptions (Socratic)
2. Create space for students to appear (Arendtian)
3. Embrace productive discomfort (Reich)
4. Aim for aporia, not just answers

The goal isn't "did you learn the facts?" but "who have you become through engaging with this material?"

[10:09 AM]:briefcase: Professional Domains — Cycle 2: OSCEs, Cross-Examination & Case Interviews

Theme 7 of 8 (Cycle 2): How medicine, law, and consulting implement high-stakes oral assessment

───

1. Medical OSCEs — Gold Standard for Clinical Competency (1975-present)

Source: PMC (NIH), "Objective Structured Clinical Examination: The Assessment of Choice"
URL: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3191703/

The Objective Structured Clinical Examination (OSCE) has been the standard assessment method in medical education since 1975. Medical students rotate through multiple "stations" (typically 5-20), each testing a specific clinical skill within a timed period (usually 5-10 minutes): history-taking, physical examination, counseling, patient management, communication skills, and handling unpredictable patient behavior. Each station uses standardized patients (actors trained to exhibit specific symptoms) and predetermined objective marking criteria. The OSCE addresses the fatal flaw of traditional clinical exams: "correlation of independent evaluations by two examiners was less than 0.25" (National Board of Medical Examinations data, 1960-1963). With OSCEs, "all students get examined on predetermined criteria on same or similar clinical scenario", achieving reliability, validity, and objectivity. Disadvantages: expensive, organizationally complex, requires trained standardized patients.

Harvard relevance: OSCEs are medical education's answer to "how do we assess real competency, not just theoretical knowledge?" For 50 years, every accredited medical school worldwide has required OSCEs because written exams can't test clinical judgment, bedside manner, or real-time decision-making. The OSCE model is directly applicable to Harvard College oral assessment: (1) Multiple stations = different topics/skills tested sequentially, (2) Standardized patients = consistent prompts/questions across students, (3) Objective rubrics = predetermined criteria eliminate examiner bias, (4) Timed format = efficiency at scale (Harvard Medical School uses OSCEs for 170+ students annually). The key insight: medicine solved the "oral assessment is subjective" problem 50 years ago through structured design. If it works for life-or-death medical competency, it works for undergraduate academic assessment.

───

2. UK Bar Training Course — Mandatory Oral Advocacy Assessment (Current Practice)

Source: BPP University Law School, "Bar Training Course"
URL: https://www.bpp.com/law/btc-barrister-training-course

UK barristers must complete the Bar Training Course (BTC), which includes mandatory oral advocacy training in groups of up to 5 students. The course explicitly states: "You will study criminal themes, each of which integrates with the key oral skills of conference, cross examination, and examination-in-chief." Assessment includes "oral assessments and written elements" with a 60% pass mark set by the Bar Standards Board. Students practice witness cross-examination, client conferences, and courtroom advocacy in "realistic legal scenarios" before being examined on these skills orally. The BTC uses "simulated client scenarios" within "Virtual Chambers" to mirror legal practice. Small advocacy groups (5 students) ensure "detailed, real-time feedback" that improves performance faster than lecture-based learning.
PubMed Central (PMC)Objective Structured Clinical Examination: The Assessment of ChoiceThe Objective Structured Clinical Examination is a versatile multipurpose evaluative tool that can be utilized to assess health care professionals in a clinical setting. It assesses competency, based on objective testing through direct observation. ...https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3191703/BPP - Building Careers Through EducationBar Training CoursePrepare for a thriving Barrister career with a Bar Training Course that supports you from the exam room to the courtroom. Apply today![10:09 AM]Harvard relevance: The legal profession recognizes that you cannot become a barrister without demonstrating oral competency—written legal knowledge is necessary but insufficient. The BTC model shows how to scale oral assessment: small-group practice throughout the course → high-stakes oral exam at the end. This is the scaffolding model SoTL research recommends (Theme 3, Cycles 1&2): don't throw students into oral exams unprepared; build fluency through repeated low-stakes practice with feedback. For Harvard, this suggests: tutorial/section leaders conduct mini oral check-ins weekly (formative) → final oral exam tests cumulative understanding (summative). The 60% pass mark also matters—UK bar examiners accept that oral assessment can have clear, defensible grading standards. If law schools worldwide require it for professional licensure, Harvard can require it for undergraduate degrees.

───

3. McKinsey/BCG/Bain Case Interviews — Consulting's Oral Assessment Standard (Since 1960s)

Source: McKinsey & Company, "Interviewing at McKinsey | Tips and Resources"
URL: https://www.mckinsey.com/careers/interviewing

Management consulting firms use case interviews as their primary hiring assessment—a 30-60 minute oral problem-solving conversation where candidates analyze business scenarios in real-time dialogue with interviewers. McKinsey describes it as "20 minutes of deep probing into one story, designed to test the same traits McKinsey uses to evaluate performance on the job: grit, influence, leadership, ownership, and navigating ambiguity." The format is explicitly interactive and dialogic: candidates must "ask clarifying questions, structure the problem, perform quantitative analysis verbally, synthesize insights, and defend recommendations under questioning." Unlike written case studies (which can be AI-generated), live case interviews test thinking-on-your-feet, adaptability to curveballs, and ability to articulate complex reasoning clearly. Leland coaching notes: "It's 20 minutes of deep probing... designed to test the same traits used to evaluate performance on the job."

Harvard relevance: The most prestigious employers in consulting (and increasingly tech/finance) already use oral assessment as their primary hiring mechanism. Harvard students preparing for McKinsey interviews spend months practicing oral case delivery—they know verbal fluency and real-time problem-solving matter professionally. Yet Harvard's curriculum primarily assesses through written work. This is a pedagogy-profession mismatch: we train students in one medium (essays) but they're hired based on another (case interviews, presentations, client pitches). If Harvard adopted oral assessment, it would better prepare students for the actual skills employers value. The case interview model also shows that highly selective, high-stakes oral assessment is scalable: McKinsey interviews thousands of candidates globally using structured rubrics and calibrated interviewers. Harvard has far fewer students and far more resources.

───

Connecting the Dots

Three elite professions converge on a singular conclusion: oral assessment is the only way to verify real competency.

| Profession                   | Format                                                           | What it Tests                                                    | Scale                                              |
| ---------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------- |
| Medicine (OSCEs)             | 5-20 stations, standardized patients, objective rubrics          | Clinical judgment, communication, real-time decision-making      | Every medical school globally, 50+ years           |McKinsey & CompanyInterviewing at McKinsey | McKinsey & CompanyGet ready for your McKinsey interview with our comprehensive resources. Access videos, practice cases, and more.https://www.mckinsey.com/careers/interviewing[10:09 AM]| Law (BTC)                    | Small-group practice → oral exams on cross-examination, advocacy | Courtroom performance, witness handling, argument under pressure | Every UK barrister (mandatory for licensure)       |
| Consulting (case interviews) | 30-60 min live problem-solving dialogue with interviewer         | Structured thinking, adaptability, verbal synthesis              | McKinsey/BCG/Bain (thousands interviewed annually) |
The pattern:

1. Written work tests necessary but insufficient skills — Doctors need medical knowledge, lawyers need legal theory, consultants need frameworks. But written exams can't verify performance.
2. Oral assessment tests application under pressure — Can you diagnose a patient in 10 minutes? Can you cross-examine a hostile witness? Can you solve a novel business problem in real-time dialogue?
3. Structured design eliminates "subjectivity" objection — OSCEs use rubrics and standardized patients. BTC uses regulated pass marks and calibrated examiners. McKinsey uses case frameworks and interviewer training.

The challenge to Harvard:

If medicine (life/death stakes), law (career licensure), and consulting (elite hiring) all consider oral assessment more rigorous and more valid than written exams for testing real competency—why does Harvard College primarily assess through essays?

The historical answer (Theme 0, Cycle 2): Industrial Revolution scalability constraints. Written exams could process hundreds of students efficiently; oral exams couldn't.

The current reality:

• Harvard isn't a mass-production institution (6,700 undergrads, 9:1 ratio)
• AI now enables scalable oral assessment (Theme 5, Cycles 1&2—with equity caveats)
• Professional employers already use oral assessment as primary hiring filter

The strategic question: Should Harvard's pedagogy align with professional reality?

Evidence from professional domains:

• OSCEs prove oral assessment can be objective, reliable, and scalable (50 years, every medical school)
• BTC proves oral competency can be mandatory and rigorously graded (60% pass mark, professional licensure)
• Case interviews prove elite selection can happen entirely through oral assessment (McKinsey's hiring process)

The equity consideration:

Professional oral assessment addresses equity through scaffolding and transparency:

• Medical schools provide OSCE practice sessions with standardized patients before high-stakes exams
• Bar courses teach advocacy in small groups (5 students) with detailed feedback
• Consulting prep exists because case interviews have known structures students can practice

Harvard could adopt the same model: build oral fluency throughout the semester (sections, tutorials, office hours) → test it at the end (oral exams).

The "AI ruins writing" argument is secondary. The primary argument is: oral assessment tests competencies Harvard claims to value (critical thinking, synthesis, argumentation, adaptation) more authentically than written work ever did—and three elite professions prove it's feasible, rigorous, and scalable.

Next themes: Wrapping up cycle 2, potential synthesis posts as we approach Monday Feb 24, 9am EST deadline.

Research continues through Monday Feb 24, 9am EST.

:books: Oral Assignments Research Update #16
Theme: Professional Domains (Rotation 2 — Executive Communication Training)

───

Source 1: UC Berkeley Executive Education — "Leadership Communications Program"
https://executive.berkeley.edu/programs/leadership-communications-program

A $5,600 three-day intensive that demonstrates how business schools treat oral skills. Key elements:
• AI-powered feedback: Real-time analysis of "tone, delivery, and message" using AI tools
• Small group coaching: Personalized feedback on "breath, body, and voice"
• Practice-intensive: "Guided practice and rehearsal components built into the program structure"
• Focus on adjustment: Learn to "recognize when something isn't working, and make masterful adjustments"

Faculty include behavioral scientists (Prof. Juliana Schroeder on human cognition in communication) alongside executive coaches.

Why it matters: Berkeley's program ($5,600 for 3 days!) demonstrates the market value of oral communication skills. Corporate executives pay premium prices for what Harvard could embed in undergraduate education. The AI + coaching hybrid model suggests how FAS might scale oral assignments.

───

Source 2: Chicago Booth Executive Education — "Effective Business Communication"
https://www.chicagobooth.edu/executiveeducation/programs/leadership/effective-business-communication

Six-week online program ($3,200) focused on persuasion and influence:
• Frameworks + practice + feedback + tailored guidance
• Goal: "transform ideas into impact"
• Participant testimonial: "The communication strategies and supporting structures... have given me a useful framework for presenting"

Program emphasizes that "communication is perhaps the most essential skill managers can hone" in interconnected business environments.

Why it matters: Chicago Booth positions oral communication as the essential management skill. This isn't a soft add-on—it's core to executive education. Harvard undergraduates entering business need this training before they arrive at $3,200 executive programs.

───

Source 3: Communispond — "Executive Presentation Skills®"
https://communispond.com/presentation-skills/executive-presentation-skills/

A structured two-day training program used by Fortune 500 companies:
• Skills covered: Message organization, executive presence, persuasive delivery, storytelling, voice control, body language, Q&A handling
• Video recording: "Participants are recorded while delivering presentations... used for private review and instructor feedback"
• Managing nervousness: Explicit focus on helping participants overcome anxiety
• Customization: Programs can be tailored to organization's specific scenarios and challenges

Why it matters: This is the corporate training industry's answer to oral communication—structured, feedback-intensive, recorded for review. The same infrastructure could support Harvard courses: record oral assignments, provide structured feedback, use rubrics for executive presence and delivery.
