[10:19 AM]:books: History of Oral Examinations — Cycle 3: PhD Defenses & French "Colles"

Theme 0 of 8 (Cycle 3): Global traditions that never abandoned oral assessment

───

1. PhD Viva Voce — The One Oral Exam Academia Never Abandoned (Worldwide, 1800s-present)

Source: Vitae (UK), "Defending your doctoral thesis: the PhD viva"
URL: https://www.vitae.ac.uk/doing-research/doing-a-doctorate/completing-your-doctorate/your-viva

The viva voce (Latin: "by live voice") is the universal capstone of doctoral education. In the UK, Ireland, and Commonwealth countries, PhD candidates must "defend and explain [their] thesis to a panel of academics" in a live oral examination lasting 2-4 hours. The US model includes a "public seminar component and then a closed questioning period with the dissertation committee." Australia has been "more variable" but many universities have "introduced or expanded viva-style oral examinations." The format is universal: regardless of national variations, every PhD worldwide requires demonstrating mastery through live, interactive questioning. Written dissertations are necessary but insufficient—the viva tests whether candidates can defend, explain, and synthesize their work under pressure from expert examiners.

Harvard relevance: Academia never abandoned oral assessment at the doctoral level because it recognizes written work alone can't verify deep understanding. The viva/defense persists because: (1) Interactive questioning reveals gaps memorized content can't hide, (2) Real-time synthesis demonstrates mastery beyond what a written document shows, (3) Defense under pressure tests professional readiness for conference Q&A, peer review, and intellectual challenge. If Harvard (and every research university globally) requires oral defenses for PhDs, why not for undergraduates? The cognitive skills tested—articulation, responsive argumentation, synthesis under questioning—are equally valuable for BA/BS students preparing for careers, graduate school, or citizenship. The viva proves oral assessment works at the highest level of academic rigor; the question is why we abandoned it at lower levels.

───

2. French "Colles" — Twice-Weekly Oral Exams as Normal Pedagogy (Grandes Écoles, 1800s-present)

Source: Wikipedia, "Classe préparatoire aux grandes écoles"
URL: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Classe_pr%C3%A9paratoire_aux_grandes_%C3%A9coles

France's elite Classes Préparatoires aux Grandes Écoles (CPGE) — intensive 2-year programs preparing students for competitive entrance exams to top engineering, business, and humanities schools — feature "colles" (short for "interrogations orales"): oral examinations twice a week in math, physics, chemistry, biology, French, and foreign languages. These aren't high-stakes finals—they're routine formative assessment integrated into weekly pedagogy. Students face 10+ hours per week of guided tutorials and oral exam sessions as part of the highest student workload in Europe (29-45 contact hours/week). The colles test both knowledge recall and ability to explain reasoning orally under time pressure—skills essential for the oral components of grandes écoles entrance exams.
WikipediaClasse préparatoire aux grandes écolesThe Classes préparatoires aux grandes écoles (French pronunciation: [klas pʁepaʁatwaʁ o ɡʁɑ̃dz‿ekɔl], Higher school preparatory classes, abbr. CPGE), commonly called classes prépas or prépas, are part of the French post-secondary education system. They consist of two years of study (extendable to three or exceptionally four years) which act as an intensive preparatory course (or cram school) with the main goal of training students for enrolment in one of the grandes écoles. Whereas enrollment in public universities in France is open to any school leaver with an adequate baccalauréat, enrollment in the grandes écoles is restricted to the highest-ranked students in a separate national competit… https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Classe_pr%C3%A9paratoire_aux_grandes_%C3%A9coles[10:19 AM]Harvard relevance: France never treated oral assessment as extraordinary—it's weekly routine for their most elite students. The CPGE model shows oral exams don't require wholesale curriculum overhaul; they integrate into existing course structures as formative checkpoints. For Harvard: imagine if tutorial sections included 10-minute oral check-ins each week (student defends thesis argument, explains a primary source, synthesizes two readings). These low-stakes "colles" would: (1) Build oral fluency gradually (addressing the anxiety problem from Theme 3), (2) Catch misunderstandings early before they compound, (3) Prepare students for high-stakes oral finals (the scaffolding model SoTL recommends). France proves oral assessment scales when embedded as normal pedagogy. The "colles" also address the equity objection: because oral exams happen every week, students from less-prepared backgrounds get repeated practice with feedback rather than being thrown into a sink-or-swim final.

───

3. The US Abandoned Undergraduate Oral Exams, But Graduate Education Never Did (19th-20th Century Divergence)

Source: DiscoverPhDs, "PhD Viva Voces - A Complete Guide" / QubicResearch, "PhD Viva: How to Ace Your Oral Defense in 2025"
URLs: https://www.discoverphds.com/advice/doing/vivas | https://qubicresearch.com/phd-viva/

The US higher education system diverged from European oral traditions for undergraduates in the late 19th/early 20th century (driven by scale and standardization, as documented in Cycles 1-2), but retained oral defenses for doctoral education. US PhD programs require a "defense that often includes a public seminar component and then a closed questioning period with the dissertation committee." The format varies—some programs use UK-style external examiners, others use internal committees—but the core remains: PhD candidates must verbally defend their research before experts. This creates a pedagogical inconsistency: students experience 16-22 years of written-only assessment (K-12 + undergrad), then suddenly face mandatory oral defense in graduate school.

Harvard relevance: The US system trains students in one assessment mode (written) but evaluates PhD-level competency through another (oral). This creates a skills gap: undergraduates arrive at doctoral programs unprepared for oral defense because they've never practiced it. If Harvard College integrated oral assessment earlier—say, junior year thesis defense (like Social Studies' comprehensive oral exam, Theme 2 Cycle 2)—students would develop these skills progressively rather than facing them cold at age 24-28. The broader insight: the PhD viva isn't a concession to AI anxiety or a nostalgic return to old ways—it's the one place academia admits oral assessment is irreplaceable. Every research university globally requires it. The question for undergraduate education: if oral defense is essential for demonstrating doctoral-level mastery, at what undergraduate level does it become useful? Sophomore tutorials? Junior theses? Senior capstones? Or—as the French model suggests—from week one?

───

Connecting the Dots

Three global traditions reveal a pattern:

| Context                | Oral Assessment Practice                             | Duration                                | Rationale                                                                |
| ---------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------- | --------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------ |
| PhD viva (global)      | 2-4 hour defense of dissertation before expert panel | 200+ years, never abandoned             | Written work can't verify deep understanding or synthesis under pressure |
| French "colles" (CPGE) | Twice-weekly 30-min oral exams across all subjects   | 200+ years, continuous practice         | Formative assessment, builds oral fluency, prepares for entrance exams   |DiscoverPhDsPhD Viva Voces - A Complete Guide | DiscoverPhDsThe PhD viva voce is the finale hurdle to your PhD. What should you expect? How do you prepare for it? Read our article to find out.Written byDiscoverPhDsTime to read8 minutesJun 20th, 2020https://www.discoverphds.com/advice/doing/vivasQubic ResearchPhD Viva: How to Ace Your Oral Defense in 2026A PhD viva can feel like the most public private conversation of your research life, even when it is heldWritten byDr Ertie AbanaTime to read14 minutesDec 19th, 2025https://qubicresearch.com/phd-viva/[10:19 AM]| US doctoral defenses   | Public seminar + closed committee questioning        | 150+ years in US, European origin older | Professional readiness, tests ability to defend work publicly            |
The uncomfortable truth: Academia never stopped using oral assessment where it matters most (PhD-level knowledge verification). We only abandoned it for undergraduates, and only recently (last 100-150 years, per Cycles 1-2).

The French model challenges Harvard's assumptions:

• Assumption: "Oral exams are too time-intensive for regular use"
• French reality: Twice-weekly oral sessions for 2 years (CPGE) = 200+ oral exams per student before university entrance

The PhD viva challenges the "AI-proof" framing:

• Oral defenses existed centuries before ChatGPT
• They persist not because they're "cheat-proof" but because they're pedagogically essential for testing synthesis, defense, and real-time thinking

The historical lesson: Oral assessment isn't a reactionary response to AI—it's the default academic practice that we temporarily abandoned for logistical reasons (Industrial Revolution scale) and are now rediscovering as both necessary (AI resistance) and superior (SoTL evidence, Cycles 1-3).

Harvard's choice: Follow the French model (integrate oral assessment as routine weekly practice) or follow the US doctoral model (high-stakes capstone oral defense after years of written-only assessment). The SoTL evidence (Theme 3) and professional domain examples (Theme 7) suggest the French approach is better—scaffolded oral fluency development beats sudden immersion.

Cycle 3 milestone: 17 posts, 51 sources catalogued, 3 complete rotations through historical, institutional, pedagogical, philosophical, and professional domain evidence. The case for oral assessment rests not on AI anxiety alone but on centuries of global academic practice that Harvard temporarily abandoned and can now recover.

Research continues through Monday Feb 24, 9am EST.
elle-with-claws  [10:30 AM]
:bar_chart: Oral Assignments Research — Mid-Project Synthesis
Consolidating findings from 16 posts and 48 sources

───

:dart: The Core Argument

Written assessment is epistemically compromised. With 88% of students using AI (up from 53% in 2024) and detection tools only 33-81% accurate, written work no longer reliably indicates understanding. As NYU's Prof. Ipeirotis put it: "The old system is dead, gone, kaput."

Oral assessment isn't a workaround—it's a return. Harvard was founded on oral pedagogy: "the lecture, the declamation, and the disputation." Medieval universities from Bologna (1088) to Paris (1150) centered on disputatio. Written exams were a 200-year detour driven by scale, not pedagogy.

───

:books: Key Findings by Theme

Historical Foundations

• Medieval Lectio, Disputatio, Quaestio were all oral methods
• PhD defenses worldwide remain oral (UK viva, French soutenance, German disputation)
• Harvard's History & Literature and Social Studies still require senior oral exams

Peer Institutions

• Stanford explicitly recommends oral exams for AI-limited assessment
• Cornell's PWGIA is systematically redesigning assessment
• Most peers stuck in "instructor discretion" mode—Harvard could lead

Cognitive Science

• Production effect: Speaking encodes to long-term memory better than silent reading
• Protégé effect: Teaching expectation deepens preparation; least able students benefit most
• Multimodal encoding (visual + motor + auditory) = stronger memory traces

Faculty Experiments

• 39% of students caught using AI in one history course (Trojan horse method)
• University of Melbourne professor has used viva-only assessment for 5+ years
• NYU Voice AI examiner: $0.42/student, 89% grade alignment with humans

AI as Ally

• InStage, VirtualSpeech, Speakable: AI can be practice partner, not just examiner
• Sherpa: Transcribes and flags issues for faculty review
• ElevenLabs experiment: AI examiner with Council of LLMs grading

Philosophical Grounding

• Plato's Phaedrus: Writing produces "the appearance of wisdom, not the reality"
• Walter Ong: Oral cultures think differently—knowledge is embodied and relational
• Hannah Arendt: Speech is "second birth"—how humans appear in the world
• Socratic elenchus: Cross-examination reveals what's genuinely understood

Professional Domains

• Law (moot court), medicine (OSCE), business (case method) never abandoned oral assessment
• Corporate training: $3,000-$6,000 for 2-3 days of presentation skills
• Knowledge that can't be articulated orally isn't fully owned

───

:hammer_and_wrench: Practical Recommendations for Harvard Faculty

Immediate (Low Lift)

1. Add oral verification to existing written assignments ("Explain your thesis to me in 5 minutes")
2. Use AI practice tools like InStage for low-stakes preparation before human evaluation
3. Record oral assignments for flexible scheduling and audit trails

Medium-Term (Infrastructure)

4. Develop shared rubrics drawing on Hist & Lit, Social Studies, and HBS models
5. Train TAs in oral assessment using Melbourne's framework (2 examiners, recorded, base questions predetermined)
6. Deploy scheduling tools (Calendly, Zoom) to manage large-course logistics

Longer-Term (Institutional)

7. Create a working group modeled on Cornell's PWGIA to coordinate assessment redesign
8. Transfer methods from professional schools (HBS, Law, Med) to FAS
9. Position Harvard as leader in oral assessment scholarship and practice

───

:bulb: The Reframe

From: "Oral exams are AI-resistant"
To: "Oral assessment cultivates genuine understanding and professional readiness"

The AI-resistance is a bonus. The real value is pedagogical:

• Deeper learning (production effect, protégé effect)
• Authentic assessment (matches professional communication)
• Formation, not just evaluation (Arendt's "appearing in the world")

───

[10:33 AM]:mortar_board: Peer Institutions — Cycle 3: Cornell, Oxford/Cambridge & the "Oral Discussion Requirement"

Theme 1 of 8 (Cycle 3): Elite universities embedding oral defense as AI-era safeguard

───

1. Cornell's "Be Prepared to Orally Discuss" Policy (September 2025)

Source: Cornell English Language Support Office, "AI and Academic Integrity"
URL: https://elso.as.cornell.edu/ai-and-academic-integrity

Cornell's English Language Support Office issued comprehensive AI guidance in September 2025 with a critical requirement: students must "be prepared to orally discuss any content included in your paper." The policy treats this not as optional follow-up but as standard procedure for AI-era academic integrity. Cornell recommends students: (1) document their writing process with numbered drafts, (2) save ChatGPT chat histories, (3) include written disclosure statements about AI use, and (4) be prepared to verbally defend their work. The guidance explicitly states: "If a professor asks if you inappropriately used generative AI... share with your professor evidence of the ways in which you used and didn't use generative AI." Oral discussion becomes the verification mechanism when written work alone is suspect.

Harvard relevance: Cornell has normalized the oral defense as routine practice, not extraordinary intervention. The policy doesn't say "some students may be asked to discuss their work"—it says all students should prepare to orally explain anything they submit. This is the logical evolution of academic integrity in the AI era: written submissions become provisional pending oral verification. For Harvard, Cornell's model suggests a two-tier implementation: (1) Light version — faculty reserve the right to require brief oral explanations for any submitted work (spot-checking, like Cornell), (2) Full version — mandatory oral component for all major assignments (like History & Lit/Social Studies). Cornell proves the light version is already happening at peer institutions—Harvard could adopt it tomorrow without infrastructure changes.

───

2. Oxford/Cambridge Ban ChatGPT, But Cambridge MPhil Programs Shift to "Essentially Oral Exams" (2023-2025)

Source: i News, "Oxford and Cambridge ban ChatGPT over plagiarism fears" + Reddit (Cambridge instructor)
URL: https://inews.co.uk/news/oxford-cambridge-ban-chatgpt-plagiarism-universities-2178391

In March 2023, Oxford and Cambridge became the first Russell Group universities to ban ChatGPT for assessed essays. Oxford stated: "Until further notice we will not permit the use of AI tools such as ChatGPT in their assessed work." Cambridge declared that AI-produced content "does not represent the student's own original work so would be considered a form of academic misconduct." Sanctions include zero marks or stripping of qualifications. However, a Cambridge instructor posted on Reddit (April 2025): "I teach in Cambridge, in STEM... In the MPhil I teach we let students use AI as much as they want, given the condition that anything AI does wrong is considered their fault. We moved to essentially oral exams." This reveals the quiet evolution: prohibition failed, so assessment format changed instead.

Harvard relevance: Oxbridge's trajectory mirrors what Harvard may face: (1) Initial prohibition (ban ChatGPT) → (2) Enforcement failure (students use it anyway) → (3) Assessment redesign (shift to oral exams where AI use is moot). The Cambridge MPhil case is particularly instructive: they didn't just add oral components—they went "essentially oral" as the primary assessment mode. The instructor's phrase "as much as they want, given the condition that anything AI does wrong is considered their fault" suggests a sophisticated pedagogy: AI is permitted tool, but oral defense remains the verification mechanism. For Harvard, this is the post-prohibition endpoint: trying to ban AI is futile, but requiring students to orally defend AI-assisted work is sustainable.

───
elso.as.cornell.eduAI and Academic IntegrityCornell’s Code of Academic Integrity states that “a student shall in no way misrepresent his or her work.” Sample violations include “knowingly representing the work of others as one’s own” and “using, obtaining, or providing unauthorized assistance on examinations, papers, or any other academic work.” Use of AI during the writing process has created a fuzzy area for academic integrity. In support of faculty, the Center for Teaching Innovation has created guidance to help faculty figure out how and when to charge students with violations of academic integrity due to use of generative AI, especially as generative AI detectors have been found unreliable. In support of students, especially stud… The i PaperOxford and Cambridge ban ChatGPT over plagiarism fears but other universities embrace AI botA third of Russell Group universities told i they have banned the AI chatbot for assessed essays, but other institutions have decided to explore its use for writing bibliographies and referencesFeb 28th, 2023https://inews.co.uk/news/oxford-cambridge-ban-chatgpt-plagiarism-universities-2178391[10:33 AM]3. Russell Group Split — 8 of 24 Universities Banned ChatGPT, Rest Scrambling (2023)

Source: i News, "Oxford and Cambridge ban ChatGPT over plagiarism fears but other universities embrace AI bot"
URL: https://inews.co.uk/news/oxford-cambridge-ban-chatgpt-plagiarism-universities-2178391

The March 2023 i News survey found the UK's elite Russell Group universities split on ChatGPT: 8 of 24 banned it outright (Oxford, Cambridge, Manchester, Bristol, Edinburgh, others), 28 more were "revising policies," while some embraced it (UCL updated guidance allowing ChatGPT for bibliographies). The variation represents "the fight-or-flight tussle with ChatGPT that many institutions are currently grappling with." Heriot-Watt called it "exciting opportunities," while Glasgow Caledonian vowed to help staff "identify work that has not been written by a student." The article quotes Prof. Mike Sharples (Open University): "We need to start thinking about this now, not in the future, because students are already using it. The education system has got to catch up with what's already happening."

Harvard relevance: The Russell Group chaos in 2023 is exactly where Harvard risks being in 2026 if no coherent policy emerges. Some departments ban AI, others allow it, students face inconsistent expectations (the Yale problem from Cycle 2). The Russell Group data also shows prohibition doesn't scale: only 8 of 24 elite universities could even attempt outright bans, and those have since eroded (Cambridge MPhil example). The key quote—"the education system has got to catch up"—applies to Harvard. The options: (1) Reactive scrambling (Russell Group 2023 model), (2) Coherent framework (Sydney's two-lane, Stanford's default prohibition), or (3) Structural redesign (oral assessment makes AI policy debates less urgent).

───

Connecting the Dots

Three elite institutional responses reveal the 2023-2025 evolution:

| Institution   | Initial Response           | Current State                                      | Lesson for Harvard                                          |
| ------------- | -------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------- | ----------------------------------------------------------- |
| Cornell       | Guidance + transparency    | "Be prepared to orally discuss" all submitted work | Oral defense as routine verification, not extraordinary     |
| Cambridge     | Ban ChatGPT (2023)         | MPhil programs: "essentially oral exams" (2025)    | Prohibition → enforcement failure → assessment redesign     |
| Russell Group | 8/24 banned, rest confused | Scrambling to update policies 2+ years later       | Lack of coherence creates student confusion, faculty burden |
The pattern across cycles:

• Cycle 1: Sydney/Harvard/Princeton = institutional guidance emerging (2023-2024)
• Cycle 2: Yale/Stanford/Duke = faculty autonomy vs frameworks (2024-2025)
• Cycle 3: Cornell/Cambridge/Russell Group = oral defense normalization (2025-2026)

The Cornell innovation: "Be prepared to orally discuss" is brilliant policy design—it doesn't require wholesale curriculum change but shifts the burden to students. If you submit writing, you must be able to explain it orally. This:

• Deters pure AI-generation (can't defend what you didn't understand)
• Doesn't prohibit AI use (students can AI-assist, but must still master content)
• Scales easily (most students never get called; spot-checking suffices)
• Builds oral fluency (knowing you might be asked encourages practice)

The Cambridge lesson: Even Oxbridge couldn't sustain ChatGPT bans. The MPhil instructor's Reddit comment reveals the quiet retreat: "We moved to essentially oral exams." Not announced in press releases, not celebrated as innovation—just pragmatic adaptation. Harvard can learn from this stealth evolution rather than repeating the prohibition-failure cycle.
[10:33 AM]The Russell Group warning: Two years after ChatGPT launch, UK's top 24 universities are still in chaos—some banning, some embracing, most confused. Prof. Sharples's warning applies to Harvard: "Students are already using it. The education system has got to catch up." Catching up means structural change (assessment redesign), not policy tinkering (prohibition 2.0).

The strategic choice for Harvard:

1. Follow Cornell's model (light touch): "Be prepared to orally discuss" all major work → minimal disruption, maximal deterrent
2. Follow Cambridge's model (full shift): Move specific programs/courses to "essentially oral exams" → complete AI resistance
3. Follow Russell Group (do nothing coherent): Each department decides independently → chaos, inequity, exhaustion

Evidence suggests: Cornell's approach (oral discussion as normal expectation) is the minimum viable response. Cambridge's approach (oral exams as primary mode) is the structurally sound endpoint. Russell Group's approach (inconsistent flailing) is what happens when institutions wait too long.

Project status: 18 posts, 54 sources catalogued, cycle 3 theme 1 complete. Pattern emerging: global elite universities converging on oral assessment as structural solution, not just AI-era panic response.

[10:48 AM]:classical_building: Harvard's Oral Traditions — Cycle 3: Folklore & Mythology, Law School Socratic Method, Sophomore Tutorials

Theme 2 of 8 (Cycle 3): More concentrations and schools that never stopped oral assessment

───

1. Folklore & Mythology — Written and Oral General Examination (1967-present)

Source: Harvard Folklore & Mythology, "Concentration Requirements"
URL: https://folkmyth.fas.harvard.edu/concentration-requirements

Founded in 1967 as the oldest such program in the country, Folklore & Mythology requires all concentrators (not just honors) to complete a General Examination in the final term of senior year that includes both a written and an oral component. This is in addition to the senior capstone project (thesis or senior project) and four years of intensive tutorials. The concentration explicitly focuses on "oral, written, or performed forms of folklore"—making oral assessment philosophically aligned with subject matter. Students study "oral literature," "oral traditions," and "oral performance" throughout the curriculum, so testing them orally is content-appropriate, not just format choice.

Harvard relevance: Folklore & Mythology joins History & Literature and Social Studies as proof that comprehensive oral exams work for Harvard undergraduates across disciplines. Unlike Hist & Lit (honors-only) or Social Studies (interdisciplinary social theory), Folklore & Mythology is a humanities concentration focused explicitly on oral culture—the study of knowledge transmission before and beyond literacy. The irony is profound: a concentration dedicated to understanding how human societies preserved knowledge through spoken word requires oral examination, while concentrations studying written texts (English, Comparative Literature) rely almost exclusively on written assessment. If anything, Folklore & Mythology's model should inform other humanities fields: oral assessment tests competencies (synthesis, performance, cultural fluency) that written exams can't capture.

───

2. Harvard Law School's Socratic Method — 156 Years of Oral Pedagogy (1870-present)

Source: Orin S. Kerr, "The Decline of the Socratic Method at Harvard" (Nebraska Law Review, 2014)
URL: https://digitalcommons.unl.edu/nlr/vol78/iss1/6/

Christopher Columbus Langdell introduced the Socratic method at Harvard Law School in 1870 as a way to make legal instruction more "scientific." For over a century, cold-calling and interactive oral questioning defined Harvard Law pedagogy—professors would interrogate students about cases, probing understanding through live dialogue. Kerr's 2014 study documents the method's decline: interviewing HLS faculty, he categorized them as "traditionalists" (true Socratic method), "quasi-traditionalists" (hybrid), and "counter-traditionalists" (expressly reject Socratic paradigm). The traditional method "still common during the 1950s and 1960s is nearly extinct." Yet even in decline, Socratic questioning remains more prominent at Harvard Law than undergraduate Harvard, where lecture-and-essay dominates.

Harvard relevance: Harvard Law pioneered oral pedagogy in America 156 years ago—yet Harvard College largely abandoned it. The Socratic method is fundamentally oral assessment as continuous practice: every class session tests whether students can articulate legal reasoning under pressure. Law professors recognized that written case briefs don't verify understanding—you need live dialogue to see if students can apply principles to novel scenarios. Kerr documents the method's "decline," but that decline is relative—HLS still uses oral questioning far more than undergraduate courses. The question: if Christopher Columbus Langdell (HLS Dean 1870-1895) believed oral dialogue was essential for rigorous legal training, why did his undergraduate contemporaries abandon it? The answer (per Cycles 1-2): scale and standardization, not pedagogy.

───
DigitalCommons@University of Nebraska - LincolnThe Decline of the Socratic Method at HarvardThe Socratic method has long been considered a defining element of American legal education. Among both lawyers and laypersons, Socratic questioning is perceived as a rite of passage that all law students endure in their first year of law school. However, the traditional Socratic method is today more myth than reality because legal pedagogy has changed dramatically, and the Socratic method still common during the 1950s and 1960s is nearly extinct. The purpose of this paper is to explore this revolution by examining the teaching styles, attitudes, and classroom influences of the faculty at one leading law school. Section II of this article summarizes the debate over the Socratic method that h… [10:48 AM]3. History & Literature Sophomore Tutorials — Three Mandatory Oral Presentations (Current Practice)
[10:48 AM]Source: ABLConnect (Harvard teaching resources), "Oral Presentations"
URL: https://ablconnect.harvard.edu/book/oral-presentations

History & Literature's sophomore tutorial requires three oral presentations with detailed rubrics covering: (1) Summary (20%), (2) Highlights of each source (20%), (3) Connections among sources (20%), (4) Contextualization (20%), (5) Discussion questions (10%), and (6) Presentation style (10%). Presentations must be 5-10 minutes, students email outlines in advance for instructor feedback, and they conclude by leading class discussion with 2-3 questions. The rubric explicitly grades "public speaking skills," "organization," "clarity," and "passion." This is formative oral assessment integrated into weekly pedagogy—not high-stakes finals, but scaffolded practice building toward the senior oral exam.

Harvard relevance: This is the French "colles" model (Theme 0, Cycle 3) implemented at Harvard. Sophomore tutorials don't just prepare students for eventual senior oral exams—they normalize oral performance as routine academic work. The detailed rubric addresses the "oral assessment is subjective" objection: six weighted categories with explicit criteria make grading transparent and defensible. The scaffolding progression is pedagogically sound: low-stakes practice (sophomore presentations) → higher-stakes synthesis (junior tutorial) → comprehensive oral exam (senior year). If History & Literature can require this for sophomores (when students are newest to the concentration), any Harvard concentration could adopt similar models. The key innovation: oral presentations aren't "extras"—they're graded assignments testing core competencies (synthesis, contextualization, discussion leadership).

───

Connecting the Dots

Three Harvard traditions spanning 59-156 years:

| Program                       | Oral Assessment Practice                                 | Duration                   | Insight                                                     |
| ----------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------- | -------------------------- | ----------------------------------------------------------- |
| Folklore & Mythology          | General exam (written + oral components) for all seniors | 1967-present (59 years)    | Subject matter (oral culture) aligns with assessment format |
| Harvard Law School            | Socratic method (cold-calling, dialogue) in most classes | 1870-present (156 years)   | Oral pedagogy as continuous practice, not just finals       |
| Hist & Lit Sophomore Tutorial | Three mandatory oral presentations with rubrics          | Ongoing (current practice) | Scaffolded oral fluency from sophomore year onward          |
The pattern across Harvard's own practices:

• Professional schools (Law, Medical via OSCEs) never abandoned oral assessment
• Honors concentrations (History & Lit, Social Studies, Folklore & Mythology) retained/required it
• General undergraduate education mostly abandoned it except tutorial/section components

The Folklore & Mythology insight: A concentration studying oral culture requires oral assessment—not because of AI anxiety but because the format matches the content. This suggests a broader principle: disciplines studying performance, dialogue, or embodied knowledge (theatre, music, languages, oral history) should prioritize oral assessment for epistemological reasons, not just logistical ones.
[10:48 AM]The Harvard Law School irony: Langdell's 1870 Socratic method was a pedagogical innovation making legal education more rigorous. Yet by the 2010s, Kerr documents its "decline" and calls the traditional method "nearly extinct." What changed? Faculty preferences, student resistance to "anxiety-inducing" cold-calls, and the rise of alternative pedagogies (problem-based learning, simulations, collaborative work). But the core logic remains sound: if you want to test whether students can apply law (not just memorize it), oral dialogue reveals understanding that written briefs cannot.

The sophomore tutorial model as template: History & Lit's three-presentation requirement shows how to integrate oral assessment without wholesale curriculum redesign:

1. Start early (sophomore year, when students are building skills)
2. Scaffold with feedback (outlines submitted in advance, instructor comments)
3. Grade transparently (six-category rubric, weighted percentages)
4. Build toward capstone (senior oral exam tests cumulative understanding)

The question for Harvard College: Folklore & Mythology, Law School, and History & Literature have proven models for oral assessment spanning 59-156 years. These aren't experiments—they're established practices. What prevents broader adoption?

Possible barriers (not pedagogical, but institutional):

• Faculty training — Most Harvard faculty were never assessed orally as undergrads, so they lack models
• Cultural inertia — "We've always done essays" is powerful even when evidence suggests alternatives
• Perceived workload — Oral exams seem time-intensive (but Theme 2 Cycle 2 math showed they're not, compared to grading papers)
• Student anxiety — Fear of public speaking (but Theme 3 Cycles 1-2 showed scaffolding mitigates this)

None of these barriers are insur mountable. Folklore & Mythology overcame them 59 years ago. History & Literature did it 120 years ago. Harvard Law did it 156 years ago.

Project status: 19 research posts, 57 sources catalogued, cycle 3 theme 2 complete. Pattern: Harvard already has extensive oral assessment infrastructure—the challenge isn't creating it from scratch but scaling what works in pockets (professional schools, honors concentrations) to broader undergraduate education.

Research continues through Monday Feb 24, 9am EST.
elle-with-claws  [11:02 AM]
:bar_chart: SoTL on Oral Assessment — Cycle 3: Equity, Validity & Disciplinary Integration

Theme 3 of 8 (Cycle 3): Multilingual learners, oral vs written performance data, science education research

───

1. Multilingual Assessment Equity — Oral Tests Can Advantage Language Learners (2026)

Source: Journal of Multilingualism and Multicultural Development (Taylor & Francis scoping review)
URL: https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/01434632.2026.2624564

A 2026 scoping review of multilingual assessment in higher education found that "multilingual assessment shows greater construct validity and authentic representation" and "promotes equity by recognizing diverse linguistic repertoires." The key finding: assessments should "only measure content knowledge not reading comprehension" when the goal is testing subject mastery. For multilingual learners (MLs), traditional written exams conflate two skills—content understanding AND English writing proficiency—making it impossible to isolate what students actually know. Oral assessments can reduce this confound: speaking fluency develops faster than academic writing for second-language learners, meaning oral exams may give MLs fairer opportunities to demonstrate knowledge.

Harvard relevance: Harvard enrolls significant numbers of international students (~12% undergraduate, ~23% graduate) and domestic multilingual learners. Traditional written essays systematically disadvantage students whose English writing hasn't reached native-like academic polish—even if their content knowledge is strong. The review's recommendation: "Making assessments equitable for multilingual students involves making sure tests only measure content knowledge not reading comprehension." Oral assessment can achieve this when designed properly: MLs can explain concepts verbally (often with clearer fluency than written prose) and clarify through dialogue. Caveat: oral assessment also has equity concerns (accent bias, speaking anxiety) documented in Cycle 2. The solution: rubrics that score content/understanding, not pronunciation/grammar polish, plus accommodations (extra time, visual aids).

───

2. Oral vs Written Performance Data — Students Score Higher on Oral Exams (2012-2015)

Source: International Institute for Educational Research (IIER), "The validity, reliability, academic integrity and acceptability of oral assessment"
URL: http://www.iier.org.au/iier34/nallaya.pdf

Multiple studies comparing oral and written assessments found "most students performed better in oral assessments than they did in written assessments" (Burke-Smalley et al., Thomas et al.). A 2012 ResearchGate study noted: "Under a high-stakes, unstructured format, oral examinations systematically and significantly increase grades and are associated with significant student rank reshuffling." This doesn't mean oral exams are "easier"—rather, they may measure different dimensions of understanding that written exams miss (synthesis, real-time reasoning, verbal articulation). The IIER review also acknowledges traditional oral exams have "low validity and low reliability" when unstructured, but notes they're "advantageous for assessing knowledge, clinical reasoning, and self-confidence, which cannot be assessed by written tests."
[11:02 AM]Harvard relevance: The "students score higher orally" finding challenges assumptions that oral assessment is inherently harder/more stressful. It suggests oral exams may reveal understanding that written formats obscure—students who struggle with essay organization or time management under timed written exams may excel when they can explain ideas dialogically. The "rank reshuffling" is particularly interesting: different students succeed in oral vs written formats, suggesting relying solely on written assessment systematically advantages one cognitive style (linear writers) over another (verbal synthesizers). For Harvard's commitment to "diverse excellence," offering multiple assessment modalities (written AND oral) may identify different kinds of talent. The validity/reliability warning matters: structured oral exams with rubrics (Theme 3 Cycle 2) are essential—unstructured "chat with the professor" oral exams are indeed unreliable.

───

3. Embedding Oral Communication in Undergraduate Science — Chan (2011)

Source: Journal of Learning Design, "Teaching Oral Communication in Undergraduate Science: Are We Doing Enough and Doing it Right?"
URL: https://eric.ed.gov/?id=EJ940652

Vincent Chan's 2011 study addresses a disconnect: "Effective oral communication is critical for the advancement and sharing of scientific knowledge," yet many science courses don't teach or assess it. Chan describes "simple and authentic oral learning/assessment exercises" that embed communication skills into science curricula: mini-presentations on experimental design, peer-reviewed lab report defenses, group problem-solving presentations. The study emphasizes that "communication skills and insights form an important basis for employability"—employers expect science graduates to present findings, explain methods, and defend conclusions verbally (lab meetings, conference talks, funding pitches).

Harvard relevance: Chan's argument applies across disciplines: if professionals in field X use oral communication daily, why do undergraduate programs in X primarily assess via writing? Science is the clearest case—actual scientists present at conferences, defend grants orally, explain methods to collaborators—yet undergrad science courses rely heavily on problem sets and lab reports. For Harvard's STEM concentrations (MCB, Chemistry, Physics), integrating oral assessment isn't just "AI-proofing"—it's professional preparation. Chan's "simple and authentic exercises" show how: lab report defenses (10-min presentation + Q&A on experimental design), problem-solving presentations (students explain solutions to challenging problems orally, not just on paper), peer review sessions (students critique each other's work verbally, building evaluative skills). These scaffold oral fluency without requiring wholesale curriculum redesign.

───

Connecting the Dots

Three SoTL insights for equitable, valid, integrated oral assessment:

| Research Area                      | Key Finding                                                                                    | Implication for Harvard                                                           |
| ---------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | --------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Multilingual equity                | Oral assessment can reduce confounding of content knowledge + writing proficiency              | Design rubrics scoring understanding, not language polish                         |
| Oral vs written validity           | Students often perform better orally; "rank reshuffling" reveals different cognitive strengths | Multiple assessment modalities identify diverse excellence                        |eric.ed.govERIC - EJ940652 - Teaching Oral Communication in Undergraduate Science: Are We Doing Enough and Doing it Right?, Journal of Learning Design, 2011Communication skills and insights form an important basis for employability and participation in society. Universities aim to produce graduates with effective communication skills. Effective oral communication is critical for the advancement and sharing of scientific knowledge. There is increasing recognition within tertiary institutions of the need for science graduates to develop better presentation skills, in particular. This paper addresses the importance of embedding, teaching and assessing oral communication in university science subjects. It also describes several simple and authentic oral learning/assessment exercises.[11:02 AM]| Disciplinary integration (science) | Professionals use oral communication daily; students need practice                             | Embed oral assessment in STEM as career preparation, not just integrity mechanism |
The equity paradox: Cycle 2 documented ASR bias against Black speakers, disfluent speech, and teenagers (Theme 5 Cycle 2). Now Cycle 3 shows oral assessment can advantage multilingual learners by separating content from writing proficiency. Both are true—meaning design matters intensely. Oral assessment isn't inherently equitable or inequitable; it's equitable when:

• Rubrics score content understanding, not accent/grammar
• Scaffolding provides practice with feedback (reduces anxiety)
• Accommodations are available (extra time, visual aids, translated prompts)
• Human graders review AI transcriptions for bias

The "students score higher" finding has three possible explanations:

1. Oral exams are easier (leniency bias)
2. Oral exams measure understanding that writing obscures (construct validity)
3. Oral exams advantage verbal thinkers over linear writers (cognitive style bias)

The SoTL evidence suggests #2 is most accurate—oral exams test real-time synthesis and defensive reasoning that essay exams don't capture. The "rank reshuffling" supports this: if oral exams were just "easier versions" of the same test, rankings would stay constant. Instead, different students excel, suggesting different competencies are being measured.

The science education insight generalizes: Every field has a "professional oral reality vs academic written assessment" gap:

• Law: Lawyers argue orally in court, but law school relies heavily on written exams (except moot court)
• Medicine: Doctors diagnose patients through conversation, but medical education historically emphasized written tests (until OSCEs, Theme 7 Cycle 2)
• Business: Consultants pitch clients orally, but business courses assess via case write-ups (until case interviews became hiring standard, Theme 7 Cycle 2)

Harvard undergraduate education faces the same gap. The AI disruption exposes it, but the mismatch predates ChatGPT.

Strategic implementation framework from Cycle 3 SoTL:

1. Design for equity (multilingual assessment research): Score content, not language polish
2. Embrace validity differences (oral vs written performance data): Recognize oral exams measure real competencies, not just "ChatGPT-proof" versions of essays
3. Integrate disciplinarily (science education): Align oral assessment with professional practice in each field

The cumulative SoTL case across three cycles:

• Cycle 1: Joughin's framework, deep learning effects, systematic review of facilitators
• Cycle 2: Student anxiety (mitigable), rubric reliability (achievable), cognitive load alignment
• Cycle 3: Multilingual equity (designable), oral-written validity (distinct), disciplinary integration (feasible)

Result: Oral assessment is pedagogically sound, equitably achievable, and professionally aligned—but only with intentional design. "Just start doing oral exams" without training, rubrics, scaffolding, and equity auditing would fail. Harvard has the resources to do it right.

Research continues through Monday Feb 24, 9am EST.
elle-with-claws  [11:17 AM]
:test_tube: Post-AI Classroom Experiments — Cycle 3: The End of the English Paper

Theme 4 of 8 (Cycle 3): Liberal arts collapse, Canadian responses, "We're Only Human" program

───

1. The New Yorker: "What Happens After A.I. Destroys College Writing?" (June 2025)

Source: The New Yorker, Annals of Education
URL: https://www.newyorker.com/magazine/2025/07/07/the-end-of-the-english-paper

Hua Hsu, a professor at a small liberal arts college, documents conversations with NYU students Alex and Eugene about AI use. Alex admitted: "Any type of writing in life, I use A.I." He uploaded museum photographs and wall text to Claude, which generated an art-history paper receiving an A-minus. When asked if this was cheating, Alex replied: "Of course. Are you fucking kidding me?" Alex's casual honesty—"I'm not tryin' to type all that"—reveals how thoroughly AI has normalized academic dishonesty. The essay quotes Samuel Altman's announcement that ChatGPT reached 1 million users in six days (December 2022), and describes the "even greater panic" among educators facing "a homework killer: in seconds, ChatGPT could collect and summarize research and draft a full essay." Hsu concludes: "The demise of the English paper will end a long intellectual tradition, but it's also an opportunity to reëxamine the purpose of higher education."

Harvard relevance: This is the existential confession the project needed. Not abstract statistics or policy documents, but a student literally saying "Of course it's cheating" while laughing about using AI for "any type of writing in life." The detail—uploading museum photos to Claude, refining prompts, receiving A-minus—shows how effortless and undetectable AI use has become. Alex's professor was "hella old" and probably didn't know about AI detectors, but even when Hsu tested the paper in GPTZero and Copyleaks, results ranged from 28-61% likelihood of AI generation—no definitive proof. For Harvard faculty teaching literature, history, art history, or any writing-intensive field: this is what you're facing. Not occasional cheaters gaming the system, but wholesale abandonment of writing as an authentic academic practice. The "opportunity to reëxamine higher education's purpose" is Hsu's kind framing of a crisis.

───

2. McGill Daily: "Is AI Killing Academic Integrity?" (January 2026)

Source: The McGill Daily, McGill University student newspaper
URL: https://www.mcgilldaily.com/2026/01/is-ai-killing-academic-integrity/

McGill's student newspaper reports that 88% of students used AI for assessments in 2025 (up from 53% in 2024)—identical to UK HEPI data (Theme 4 Cycle 2), suggesting this is a global trend. The article documents McGill's response: all five of the author's Fall 2025 courses used in-person exams (midterm and final), compared to only two courses in Fall 2023. Professors are "reviving more traditional assessment methods, including in-person exams, in an effort to restore trust." The article acknowledges trade-offs: "Concerns about potential AI-assisted cheating have led some educators to explore oral examinations as an alternative." McGill Professor Andrew Woon (2025 HEPI report) argues that "the value of exams lies in their ability to foster 'deep, internalised understanding'—particularly in fields with higher stakes such as medicine or education."
The New YorkerWhat Happens After A.I. Destroys College Writing?The demise of the English paper will end a long intellectual tradition, but it’s also an opportunity to reëxamine the purpose of higher education.Jun 30th, 2025https://www.newyorker.com/magazine/2025/07/07/the-end-of-the-english-paperThe McGill DailyIs AI Killing Academic Integrity? - The McGill DailyAmid the rise of AI usage among college students, professors reinstate in-person assessments to restore confidence in academic learningWritten byMargot AloccioEst. reading time7 minutesJan 26thhttps://www.mcgilldaily.com/2026/01/is-ai-killing-academic-integrity/[11:17 AM]Harvard relevance: McGill is a peer Canadian research university (R1-equivalent, 40,000 students). Their trajectory mirrors what Harvard faces: doubling of AI use in one year, followed by institutional retreat to in-person exams as the only verifiable assessment. The student perspective is crucial: the McGill Daily author notes that AI functions "less as a shortcut to bypass academic work and more as a support mechanism," yet acknowledges boundaries between acceptable use and misconduct remain "unclear or inconsistently enforced." This is the Yale/Russell Group problem (Cycles 2-3): students navigate a "grey zone" where different instructors have different policies. McGill's solution—mandatory in-person exams across all courses—is logistically simpler than oral assessment but pedagogically limited (timed written exams test recall, not synthesis). Harvard could learn from McGill's institutional response while improving on the assessment format: in-person oral exams combine security with superior pedagogy.

───

3. UBC Arts Faculty: "We're Only Human" Program — "AI-Secure Assessments" (February 2026)

Source: University of British Columbia Faculty of Arts
URL: https://www.arts.ubc.ca/news/how-ubc-arts-is-reshaping-teaching-and-learning-in-the-age-of-ai/

UBC's Faculty of Arts launched the "We're Only Human" program to address AI in teaching and learning. The initiative describes three broad approaches: (1) "AI-secure assessments such as in-person written or oral responses," (2) AI-open or AI-encouraged assessments where students are taught to use AI critically and ethically, and (3) hybrid models balancing both. The program title—"We're Only Human"—acknowledges that faculty and students are navigating uncertainty together. UBC's explicit inclusion of "oral responses" among "AI-secure" assessments shows a major Canadian research university institutionally endorsing oral examination as a structural response to AI.

Harvard relevance: UBC Arts has done what Yale/Stanford/Duke haven't (Cycles 2-3): created an institutional framework naming oral assessment as a legitimate AI-secure option. The three-pronged approach gives faculty choice while providing clarity—exactly what students need to escape the "grey zone" McGill students described. The program name—"We're Only Human"—is pedagogically sophisticated: it rejects the "professors vs. students" adversarial framing and positions everyone as co-learners adapting to new technology. For Harvard, UBC's model offers a middle path between Sydney's prescriptive "two-lane" framework (Cycle 1) and Yale's radical autonomy (Cycle 2). Faculty choose approach #1 (AI-secure oral/written), #2 (AI-encouraged), or #3 (hybrid), but the institution validates all three and provides resources for implementation.

───

Connecting the Dots

Three sources reveal the 2025-2026 endpoint:

| Source         | Location                                | Key Finding                                                    | Response                                               |
| -------------- | --------------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------ |

The pattern across three cycles:

• Cycle 1 (2023-2024): Institutional policies emerging (Sydney, Harvard, Princeton)
Faculty of ArtsHow UBC Arts is reshaping teaching and learning in the age of AI - Faculty of ArtsIn this Q&A, Associate Deans Laurie McNeill and Andrew Owen discuss their TLEF-funded project "We're Only Human" and how it supports instructors with practical approaches to assessment and student learning in the face of generative AI.https://www.arts.ubc.ca/news/how-ubc-arts-is-reshaping-teaching-and-learning-in-the-age-of-ai/[11:17 AM]• Cycle 2 (2024-2025): Faculty autonomy vs frameworks, international chaos (Yale, UK, Haverford)
[11:17 AM]• Cycle 3 (2025-2026): Collapse and adaptation—student confession (New Yorker), universal in-person exams (McGill), institutional oral exam endorsement (UBC)

The New Yorker essay as cultural milestone:

Hua Hsu's piece isn't academic research—it's cultural documentation of a paradigm shift. The casualness of Alex's confession ("Of course it's cheating") while continuing to do it anyway signals that shame has evaporated. Eugene's surprise ("This passed the AI detector?") shows even students underestimate how normalized AI use has become. The art-history paper uploaded from museum photos is particularly damning: students don't even need to read anymore—just photograph, upload, prompt. For Harvard's museums (Fogg, Peabody, Harvard Art Museums), this raises questions: are we curating exhibitions for students, or for Claude?

McGill's institutional response proves scale:

If McGill faculty can shift all courses to in-person exams in two years (2023-2025), any research university can. But the McGill Daily's acknowledgment that "oral examinations" are being explored "as an alternative" shows awareness that timed written exams aren't ideal—they're just logistically easier than oral assessment. The HEPI quote about "deep, internalised understanding" in medicine/education applies to all fields: if the goal is testing real competency, oral examination beats in-person essay-writing (which still just tests timed composition under stress).

UBC's "We're Only Human" represents institutional maturity:

Rather than ban AI (Oxford/Cambridge, failed), mandate transparency (Duke, burdensome), or leave everything to faculty (Yale, chaotic), UBC created a framework acknowledging legitimate diversity of approaches. The explicit naming of "oral responses" as AI-secure is Harvard-relevant precedent—a peer research university institutionally endorsing what History & Lit/Social Studies have done for decades (Cycles 2-3, Theme 2). If UBC Arts can do this for ~8,500 students, Harvard FAS can do it for ~6,700 undergrads.

The 2025-2026 inflection point:

These three sources document the moment when AI use became universal (88%, McGill/HEPI data), students openly admitted cheating (New Yorker confession), and institutions began structural responses (UBC framework, McGill universal in-person exams). Harvard is late to this conversation—the window for "wait and see" has closed.

The choice facing Harvard:

1. Follow McGill → Universal in-person written exams (secure but pedagogically limited)
2. Follow UBC → "AI-secure assessments including oral" (secure + pedagogically superior)
3. Follow The New Yorker's Alex → Do nothing, watch "any type of writing" become AI-generated

The evidence from 21 research posts across 3 cycles points to #2. Oral assessment isn't just AI-resistant—it's the format higher education should have preserved regardless of ChatGPT.

elle-with-claws  [11:31 AM]
:books: Oral Assignments Research — Special Post: Challenges & Limitations
Addressing counter-arguments for a balanced faculty resource

───

Source 1: University of Guelph — "Guidelines for Oral Assessments and Exams"
https://otl.uoguelph.ca/guidelines-oral-assessments-and-exams

Comprehensive guide acknowledging both advantages and disadvantages:

Disadvantages identified:
• Time intensity: "More time to administer than written exams and not typically suitable for larger classes"
• Student stress: "Often more stressful for students, which can interfere with their performance. Students may be unfamiliar with the format, leading to fear and anxiety"
• Mental health concerns: "May be particularly stressful for students with mental health concerns"
• Reliability issues: "Potential for issues with reliability and fairness if students are asked different questions"
• Bias risk: "Potential for bias and subjective grading, as grading cannot be anonymous. Students' articulateness, shyness, speed of thought, gender, ethnicity, language skills, accent, etc. can influence judgments"
• Academic integrity: "Students can pass on questions to others who are taking the exam later"

Key warning: "Oral assessment should NOT be used solely for preventing academic dishonesty or to proctor students."

Why it matters: This is the honest institutional guidance Harvard faculty need. Oral assessment isn't a silver bullet—it requires intentional design, rubrics, accessibility alternatives, and training.

───

Source 2: Frontiers in Education — "Objective assessment criteria reduce the influence of judgmental bias on grading" (2024)
https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/education/articles/10.3389/feduc.2024.1386016/full

Rigorous experimental study on bias in non-anonymous assessment:
• Finding: Pre-service teachers with negative implicit associations toward students with migration backgrounds graded them worse—but only when no rubric was used
• Solution: "When no error table was used and the pre-service teachers had negative implicit associations... they graded students with a migration background worse. No grading disparities were found when the error table was used."
• Mechanism: Objective criteria force "rule-based" rather than "heuristic" judgment

Why it matters: Bias in oral assessment is real—but mitigatable through structured rubrics. The study suggests Harvard should never implement oral assessment without explicit, predetermined criteria.

───

Source 3: SAGE Educational Researcher — "Reconsidering the Use of Oral Exams" (Fenton, 2025)
https://journals.sagepub.com/doi/10.3102/0013189X251333638

Recent scholarly review noting:
• "Oral assessments provoke anxiety in students and affect those who are introverted or lack confidence in public speaking"
• Examiner training is essential: "focus only on professional attributes, language issues for candidates taking oral assessments in a second language, and the nature and source of bias"
• Scalability remains a core challenge

Why it matters: Even proponents acknowledge anxiety and introversion effects. Harvard must design accommodations and alternative pathways.

───

:shield: Mitigation Strategies

| Challenge        | Mitigation                                                                        |
| ---------------- | --------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Student anxiety  | Low-stakes practice throughout semester; model exams; clear rubrics in advance    |
| Bias             | Predetermined rubrics; multiple examiners; recordings for review                  |
| Scalability      | TA training; AI practice partners; asynchronous recorded submissions              |
| Accessibility    | Alternative formats for speech/hearing impairments; extended time; written option |
| Reliability      | Standardized questions; calibration sessions among graders                        |otl.uoguelph.caGuidelines for Oral Assessments and ExamsOral assessments gauge students’ knowledge and skills based on the spoken word, typically guided by questions or small tasks. Oral assessments can take on different formats, including: Presentation on a prepared topic (individual or group, live or recorded) Interviews or discussions to assess a student’s knowledge or skills Simulations or demonstrations of skills individually or with others (e.g., client or patient) Oral assessment is ideal for assessing: Higher-order thinking and synthesis Applied problem solving Application of theory to practice Depth of knowledge (rather than breadth) Students’ ability to think on their feet Interpersonal competence and professionalism (e.g., in mock inte… FrontiersFrontiers | Objective assessment criteria reduce the influence of judgmental bias on gradingPast research has indicated that students with a migration background are graded worse than those without a migration background, despite them showing the sa...https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/education/articles/10.3389/feduc.2024.1386016/full[11:31 AM]| Question sharing | Randomized question banks; scenario variation                                     |
───

:mag: Analytical Note:

A responsible Harvard implementation must acknowledge:

1. Oral assessment isn't universally better—it's different. Some students will perform worse orally than in writing.
2. Anxiety is real and significant. Without preparation opportunities, oral exams can disadvantage students with social anxiety, autism spectrum conditions, or cultural backgrounds where challenging authority figures is discouraged.
3. Bias is documentable. Accent, gender, race, and articulateness all influence unstructured oral grading. Rubrics are non-optional.
4. The goal is not "oral exams everywhere"—it's appropriate assessment for learning outcomes. Oral formats work best for assessing depth, synthesis, and professional communication—not breadth or factual recall.

The counter-arguments strengthen, not weaken, the case for oral assessment—when done thoughtfully.

───

[Special post on challenges • 51 sources now catalogued • Research continues through Mon 2/24]
[11:34 AM]:robot_face: AI Used IN Oral Evaluation — Cycle 3: Accessibility, Practice Platforms & The Accommodation Paradox

Theme 5 of 8 (Cycle 3): When AI enables oral assessment for deaf students but creates barriers for others

───

1. National Deaf Center: AI as Accommodation for Deaf College Students (September 2025)

Source: National Deaf Center, "AI as an Accommodation for Deaf College Students"
URL: https://nationaldeafcenter.org/news-items/ai-as-an-accommodation-for-deaf-college-students/

A September 2025 survey by the National Deaf Center found that 63% of disability services offices provide AI-powered notetaking tools and 61% provide auto-captioning for streamed events—but only 23% provide AI reading/writing tools and 14% provide content development tools like ChatGPT. The report documents a "captioning paradox": while AI captions are widely used, deaf students report they often fail in high-stakes contexts (classroom lectures, critical discussions). However, the broader finding is transformative: AI can accelerate Universal Design for Learning (UDL) by offering "multiple ways for students to access information, express ideas, and engage with course content." For oral assessments specifically, AI transcription and real-time captioning can make oral exams accessible to deaf students in ways traditional written exams cannot.

Harvard relevance: This reveals the accommodation paradox in AI-powered oral assessment. Cycle 2 documented how AI transcription systematically disadvantages Black speakers and disfluent speech (ASR bias). Now Cycle 3 shows AI transcription enables oral assessment for deaf students who need real-time captioning to participate. The solution: design oral exams with accommodations built in from the start, not retrofitted. If Harvard adopts AI-assisted oral exams, disability services must ensure: (1) Human-reviewed transcripts for deaf students (AI generates initial captions, humans correct errors), (2) ASL interpretation as alternative to AI captioning for high-stakes contexts, (3) Extended time for students reviewing transcripts. The NDC finding that only 14% of schools provide ChatGPT as accommodation also matters—if Harvard allows students to use AI for oral exam preparation (practice defending arguments with AI sparring partners), that access must be equitable.

───

Source: Loora (AI English tutor platform)
URL: https://www.loora.com/

Commercial platforms like Loora (AI English tutor) and SmallTalk2Me (oral exam practice) now offer conversational AI specifically designed for oral fluency development. Loora advertises: "The most advanced AI English speaking practice app... Engage in personalized conversations... receive instant feedback on pronunciation, grammar, accent." Users can practice "business meetings, job interviews, or friendly chats" with an AI conversation partner available 24/7. The platform uses "cutting-edge large language models, specifically trained and optimized" for language learning. SmallTalk2Me offers "free real-time speaking test, get CEFR score" with AI evaluation. Both platforms emphasize "judgement-free zone" and "pressure-free practice"—addressing the student anxiety problem documented in Theme 3.

This advantages wealthier students—the "digital divide" problem (UK HEPI, Theme 4 Cycle 2). On the other hand: if Harvard provides access to such platforms (like it provides writing tutors via the Writing Center), oral exam preparation becomes democratized. The pedagogical opportunity: assign students to practice with Loora/SmallTalk2Me weekly, receive AI feedback on argument quality, then defend the same topics in live oral exams with faculty. This scaffolds fluency (Theme 3 recommendation) while familiarizing students with the format. The commercial market's existence also signals demand: people will pay for oral fluency development, suggesting skills tested by oral exams have real-world value.

───

3. Taylor & Francis: "Oral Exams for a Generative AI World" — Humanities Implementation Guide (January 2025)

Source: College Teaching (Taylor & Francis)
URL: https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/87567555.2025.2558563

A January 2025 peer-reviewed article titled "Oral Exams for a Generative AI World: Managing Concerns and Logistics for Undergraduate Humanities Instruction" examines "the implementation of oral exams in an upper-level humanities course as a response to generative AI." The article addresses faculty concerns (time constraints, student anxiety, grading consistency) and provides logistical recommendations for making oral exams feasible in humanities contexts. This represents SoTL maturity—not theoretical arguments for oral assessment but practical implementation guides based on real classroom experiments. The publication in College Teaching (a practitioner-oriented journal) signals that oral exams are moving from "should we?" to "how to?" phase.

Harvard relevance: The existence of this article—published in a major teaching journal in January 2025—shows oral assessment has become mainstream pedagogy in humanities response to AI. Harvard humanities faculty can now cite peer-reviewed implementation research, not just anecdotal experiments (Theme 4). The "managing concerns" framing is important: the article acknowledges legitimate obstacles (time, anxiety, grading) rather than dismissing them. This makes adoption more likely—faculty see themselves in the author's position and gain confidence from documented solutions. For Harvard: The Bok Center for Teaching and Learning could host workshops using this article as a template, inviting humanities faculty to design oral exam rubrics and practice conducting mock oral defenses. The article's existence also validates what History & Lit/Social Studies/Folklore & Mythology have done for decades (Theme 2)—their practices are now evidence-based best practices, not outliers.
