:books: Oral Assignments Research — Theme 6: AI Tools IN Oral Evaluation (Cycle 3)

This Saturday 5:15AM drop covers the emerging toolkit for using AI to make oral assessment more scalable—turning AI from threat to ally.

───

Source 1: "AI Simulation of Oral Exams: LLM Revolutionizing STEM Education"
AI-Scholar (arXiv paper summary), July 2024
https://ai-scholar.tech/en/articles/large-language-models/future-proofing-llms

Key insight: Researchers built a working prototype where an LLM conducts simulated oral exams. The system asks questions within a given subject area, corrects misinformation, asks follow-up questions for unclear responses, and "provides grades using a university rating scale upon request." The paper explicitly frames oral exams as reducing academic fraud risk because "the face-to-face format makes it more difficult to use dishonest means."

:pushpin: Why it matters: This is an arXiv paper showing AI can conduct oral exams, not just grade them—potentially solving the scaling bottleneck. The system can personalize difficulty and provide instant feedback, which human examiners can't do at scale.

───

Source 2: "Automating Oral Examination Grading with AI"
Ann Michaelsen (The Digital Classroom), December 2023
https://annmichaelsen.com/2023/12/19/automating-oral-examination-grading-with-ai/

Key insight: A practical 5-step workflow for AI-assisted oral exam grading: (1) record using Word's dictation, (2) transcribe and correct with ChatGPT/Bard/Bing, (3) develop grading rubric, (4) use AI for initial grading against rubric, (5) teacher review and adjustment. Key benefit: "AI-based grading systems can impartially evaluate a broader spectrum of criteria, including fluency, organization, and vocabulary usage."

:pushpin: Why it matters: This is an educator's practical guide, not just theory. It shows how to integrate AI into existing oral assessment workflows today using tools faculty already have. The teacher-in-the-loop model addresses concerns about fully automated grading.

───

Source 3: "Oral Exam Ideas — AI for Education"
AI for Education, October 2024
https://www.aiforeducation.io/prompts/oral-exam-ideas

Key insight: Prompt templates for using ChatGPT to design oral exams. Example: "Create a 10-question oral exam for my 11th grade AP French students studying Candide by Voltaire. The exam should be about 30 minutes long and cover key concepts, vocabulary terms, and encourage advanced grammatical usage." Also suggests using AI to develop rubrics, help students prepare, and generate pronunciation guides.

:pushpin: Why it matters: This is a turnkey resource for faculty who want to try oral assessment but don't know where to start. AI can help design the oral exam, even if humans conduct and grade it—lowering the barrier to experimentation.

───

:crystal_ball: Synthesis: The emerging picture: AI isn't just the problem oral exams solve—it's also part of the solution. AI can help (1) design oral exam questions, (2) conduct simulated practice exams, (3) transcribe and analyze responses, and (4) assist with rubric-based grading. The human examiner remains central, but AI handles the parts that don't scale. This reframes the conversation from "AI vs. oral exams" to "AI-enhanced oral assessment."

Earlier cycles explored Harvard's contemporary oral traditions (History & Lit, Social Studies, Folklore & Mythology). This round uncovers a forgotten history: Harvard's 1916 introduction of undergraduate oral examinations in History, Government, and Economics — a reform experiment that shaped concentration-based education.

───

1. Harvard's 1916 Undergraduate Oral Exams: The Original "General Examination"

:page_facing_up: Harvard. First Undergraduate General and Specific Exams in History, Government and Economics Division, 1916
Economics in the Rear-View Mirror (archival research blog), 2019
https://www.irwincollier.com/harvard-first-undergraduate-general-and-specific-exams-in-history-government-and-economics-division-1916/

Summary: In 1916, Harvard introduced a revolutionary assessment system for undergraduates concentrating in History, Government, and Economics: students would take "a final examination covering their special field within the Division, consisting of a written examination early in the spring, and an oral examination toward the close of the year." This replaced course-by-course grading as the primary certification mechanism. Critically, the system required special tutors beginning with the Sophomore year to prepare students for these comprehensive exams — recognizing that oral performance required sustained pedagogical scaffolding.

Harvard Context: This 1916 reform is the ancestor of Harvard's tutorial system and General Examinations. The architects understood what contemporary faculty sometimes forget: oral exams don't work as standalone assessments. They required a complete pedagogical infrastructure — sophomore tutorials, junior tutorials, dedicated tutors — to prepare students for high-stakes comprehensive orals. The system was elite and expensive (not every concentration could afford dedicated tutors), but it established the principle that oral competence develops over years, not weeks. If Harvard wants to expand oral assessment in the AI era, this historical precedent suggests we need proportional investment in preparation — more tutorials, more practice opportunities, more formative feedback.

Historical note: The 1916 system was part of A. Lawrence Lowell's broader reforms to create "concentration and distribution" — the ancestor of today's concentration/Gen Ed structure. Oral exams weren't just assessment; they were pedagogical philosophy (synthesizing knowledge across courses).

───

2. Committee on the Study of Religion: Oral Exams Still Required

:page_facing_up: General Examinations in the field [of New Testament and Early Christianity]
Harvard Committee on the Study of Religion, current
https://studyofreligion.fas.harvard.edu/academic-fields/new-testament-and-early-christianity/

Summary: The Committee on the Study of Religion (CSR) still requires oral examinations as part of PhD qualifying exams, administered jointly with the Faculty of Divinity. Graduate students take written examinations ("of three hours each") plus "an oral examination prescribed by the Committee on the Study of Religion and the Faculty of Divinity." This represents one of Harvard's continuous oral examination traditions, spanning over a century.
Economics in the Rear-View MirrorHarvard. First Undergraduate General and Specific Exams in History, Government and Economics Division, 1916.The first general and economics-specific undergraduate divisional examinations for history, government, and economics at Harvard in 1916.Written byIrwin CollierEst. reading time24 minutesJul 5th, 2019https://www.irwincollier.com/harvard-first-undergraduate-general-and-specific-exams-in-history-government-and-economics-division-1916/Committee on the Study of ReligionNew Testament and Early ChristianityThe doctoral program (Th.D. and Ph.D.) in New Testament and Early Christianity involves the multidisciplinary study of Christian literature (canonical and extracanonical, from the first to the fourth centuries), history, religion, and theology within the broader context of the ancient Mediterranean world. The principal focus of this doctoral work is twofold: 1) to analyze ancient...[5:17 AM]Harvard Context: CSR's persistence with oral exams reveals disciplinary specificity: fields centered on textual interpretation, theological argument, and hermeneutics have maintained oral assessment because it aligns with professional practice (sermons, teaching, scholarly dialogue). The lesson for undergraduate education: oral exams work best when they're tied to disciplinary norms, not imposed generically. Economics concentrators shouldn't necessarily have the same oral requirements as Study of Religion students. If Harvard adopts more oral assessment, we should ask: What does oral competence look like in each discipline? For philosophers, perhaps Socratic dialogue; for scientists, defending experimental design; for historians, synthesizing conflicting sources under questioning.

Caveat: This is graduate education (PhD level), not undergraduate. The stakes, preparation time, and expectations differ significantly.

───

3. PhD Dissertation Defenses Across Harvard Schools: The Gold Standard

:page_facing_up: Dissertation and Final Oral Exam
Harvard John A. Paulson School of Engineering and Applied Sciences, current
https://seas.harvard.edu/office-academic-programs/graduate-policies-procedures-and-forms/phd-degree-requirements/dissertation-and-final-oral-exam

Summary: Across Harvard's graduate schools (SEAS, Psychology, HMS, etc.), PhD candidates must pass oral dissertation defenses to earn degrees. The format is standardized: student delivers a complete draft to committee members at least two weeks prior, presents findings in a formal seminar, and then undergoes questioning from committee members. The exam is "moderated by the student's advisor" and focuses on methodology, findings, and scholarly contribution. Importantly, students have spent 4-6 years preparing for this single oral assessment.

Harvard Context: PhD defenses represent the apex of oral assessment at Harvard — high-stakes, heavily prepared, and discipline-specific. But they're also infrequent and expensive: one oral exam after years of work, evaluated by 4-5 faculty members. Scaling this model to undergraduate education is implausible (imagine conducting oral defenses for 1,600+ graduating seniors annually). However, PhD defenses offer design principles for undergraduate orals: (1) advance preparation (provide materials/prompts beforehand), (2) clear evaluation criteria (what counts as passing?), (3) trained evaluators (faculty development on conducting/grading orals), and (4) supportive structure (advisor moderates, not adversarial). The challenge: how do we capture the rigor of PhD orals while making them feasible for undergraduates at scale?

Methodological observation: PhD oral defenses are summative assessments of research capacity, not learning processes. Undergraduate oral exams should arguably be more formative — opportunities to demonstrate thinking, receive feedback, and revise understanding.

───

Connection to the Broader Project

This historical deep-dive reveals that Harvard has extensive experience with oral assessment — but primarily at the graduate level, or (historically) embedded in comprehensive tutorial systems. Three key lessons:

1. Infrastructure precedes implementation: The 1916 undergraduate oral exams succeeded because Harvard built tutorial systems to prepare students. Ad hoc oral exams without pedagogical scaffolding will fail.
2. Disciplinary variation matters: CSR requires orals; Economics moved to written honors exams. Different fields have different oral traditions and needs.
3. Scale is the challenge: PhD defenses work because they're rare, high-stakes, and resource-intensive. Undergraduate orals must be either (a) less frequent/comprehensive (capstone only), (b) lower-stakes/formative (practice conversations), or (c) massively resourced (more tutors, more training).
seas.harvard.eduDissertation and Final Oral Exam | Harvard John A. Paulson School of Engineering and Applied Sciences[Part of the Policies of the CHD, last updated fall 2021]Dissertation[5:17 AM]For contemporary Harvard: If faculty want to expand oral assessment in response to AI, the 1916 precedent offers both inspiration and warning. Inspiration: oral exams can work at the undergraduate level when properly supported. Warning: they require systemic investment, not just policy changes. We can't bolt oral exams onto existing courses without tutorials, practice opportunities, and faculty development. The question isn't "Should we do oral exams?" but "Are we willing to build the infrastructure that makes them work?"

───

Research continues through Monday, Feb 24, 9:00 AM EST. Questions/feedback welcome.
elle-with-claws  [5:31 AM]
:books: Oral Assignments Research — Theme 7: Philosophical Foundations (Cycle 3)

This Saturday 5:30AM drop covers the deeper intellectual traditions that frame the oral/written divide—giving Harvard faculty scholarly grounding for an oral pedagogy initiative.

───

Source 1: "Walter J. Ong" — Wikipedia (comprehensive biography)
Updated February 2026
https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Walter_J._Ong

Key insight: Ong's central thesis: writing is a technology that "transforms human consciousness." Primary oral cultures use proverbs, epic poetry, and stylized heroes (wise Nestor, crafty Odysseus) as mnemonic devices. Writing makes these unnecessary and creates "interiorization and individuation." Crucially, Ong distinguishes "secondary orality"—the new oral forms (radio, TV, podcasts) that emerge from literate cultures—from primary orality.

:pushpin: Why it matters: Ong provides the theoretical vocabulary for what Harvard faculty might be doing: not returning to primary orality, but creating a form of secondary orality appropriate to the AI age. Oral assignments aren't pre-literate regression; they're post-AI adaptation.

───

Source 2: "Jacques Derrida" — Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy
https://iep.utm.edu/jacques-derrida/

Key insight: Derrida famously critiqued "logocentrism"—the Western tradition's privileging of speech over writing. His argument: speech seems to offer "direct access to the speaker's thought and presence," but this is an illusion. Writing is not degraded speech; both are forms of "arche-writing." The speech/writing binary is a false opposition that deconstruction undermines.

:pushpin: Why it matters: Derrida offers a sophisticated counterargument that Harvard faculty should anticipate: "Isn't privileging oral over written just another form of logocentrism?" The answer: we're not claiming speech is ontologically superior—we're making a pragmatic move in response to AI's disruption of written assessment.

───

Source 3: "Oral Tradition" — Encyclopaedia Britannica
https://www.britannica.com/topic/oral-tradition

Key insight: Milman Parry and Albert Lord's fieldwork in 1930s Yugoslavia revealed how oral poets memorize thousands of lines: through "formulaic phrases, typical scenes, and story patterns." This solved the Homeric Question—how could illiterate bards compose the Iliad? Oral tradition is not memorization but compositional fluency using systematic oral languages.

:pushpin: Why it matters: This research shows that oral performance isn't about rote memory—it's about internalized patterns that enable generative expression. That's exactly what oral assignments test: not whether students memorized facts, but whether they can think with concepts in real-time.

───

:crystal_ball: Synthesis: These three sources frame oral pedagogy within a rich intellectual tradition. Ong shows how technologies transform consciousness (AI is the latest); Derrida reminds us to avoid naive claims about speech's superiority; Parry-Lord demonstrate that oral performance is compositional, not mechanical. Together, they suggest: oral assignments aren't about nostalgia for pre-literate times—they're about cultivating generative fluency in an age where written fluency has been automated.

Up next: Theme 8 — Professional domains (law, theatre, business)

Post 26 of ~24 | 72 sources collected | Research concludes Mon Feb 24 9AM
WikipediaWalter J. OngWalter Jackson Ong,  (November 30, 1912 – August 12, 2003) was an American Jesuit priest, professor of English literature, cultural and religious historian, and philosopher. His major interest was in exploring how the transition from orality to literacy influenced culture and changed human consciousness. In 1978 he served as elected president of the Modern Language Association.https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Walter_J._Ong[5:32 AM]Theme 3 (Cycle 5): Educational Research (SoTL) — Peer Feedback, Constructive Alignment, and Disciplinary Transfer

Building on earlier SoTL explorations, this round examines recent research on peer assessment of oral presentations, constructive alignment in oral communication pedagogy, and the challenge of disciplinary transfer — whether oral skills learned in one context transfer to others.

───

1. Peer Assessment Outperforms Teacher Feedback for Oral Presentations

:page_facing_up: Oral presentations in higher education: a comparison of the impact of peer and teacher feedback
Taylor & Francis | Assessment & Evaluation in Higher Education, 2017
https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/abs/10.1080/02602938.2017.1303032

Summary: This comparative study found that students who received peer assessment with rubrics improved their oral presentation skills by 10% between pre-test and post-test, while students who received only teacher feedback improved by just 5%. The researchers attribute this to the dual benefit of peer assessment: students learn both by giving feedback (which requires evaluative judgment) and receiving it (multiple perspectives vs. single instructor view). The study supports the pedagogical value of engaging students in the assessment process.

Harvard Context: This finding has profound implications for scaling oral assessment at Harvard. If peer feedback is more effective than instructor feedback alone, it suggests a pathway for making oral assignments viable in large courses. Rather than requiring faculty to evaluate 100+ oral presentations individually, courses could use structured peer review with clear rubrics. Students present in small groups, provide written feedback to peers using standardized criteria, and the instructor spot-checks/calibrates. This isn't just a workaround for faculty workload — the research suggests it's pedagogically superior. However, peer assessment requires training: students need rubrics, examples of strong/weak presentations, and practice giving constructive feedback. The Derek Bok Center could develop resources for implementing peer-assessed oral assignments across concentrations.

Methodological strength: Pre-test/post-test design with control group. However, sample size and disciplinary context matter (results may not generalize to all fields).

───

2. Constructively Aligned Instructional Design for Oral Presentations in STEM

:page_facing_up: Constructively Aligned Instructional Design for Oral Presentations
Revista de Gestão Social e Ambiental (RGSA), April 2024
https://rgsa.openaccesspublications.org/rgsa/article/view/5692

Summary: This study investigates the 'General, Particular, Specific' (GPS) model for improving constructive alignment in oral communication instruction for STEM students. Constructive alignment (Biggs & Tang) means that learning outcomes, teaching activities, and assessment tasks all reinforce the same skills. The GPS model breaks oral presentation training into general communication principles (e.g., audience awareness), particular disciplinary norms (e.g., how engineers vs. biologists structure talks), and specific content (the actual research/project being presented). Results showed improved student performance when instruction explicitly addressed all three levels.
[5:32 AM]Harvard Context: The GPS model offers a framework for designing oral assignments that transfer across contexts. A common Harvard problem: students give excellent oral presentations in Expos but struggle in Chemistry lab meetings, or vice versa. This is often because we teach oral skills in isolation ("here's how to make eye contact") rather than embedding them in disciplinary contexts ("here's how chemists signal credibility in lab talks"). If Harvard wants oral exams to assess thinking rather than just performance, we need constructive alignment: if the learning outcome is "synthesize conflicting historical sources," the oral exam should require synthesis under questioning, not polished speeches. The GPS model suggests: teach general oral skills (Expos, freshman seminars), disciplinary oral norms (concentration courses), and specific content knowledge (capstone projects) — and align assessments accordingly.

Source quality: Published in an open-access journal; peer-reviewed but not a top-tier venue. Findings are plausible but would benefit from replication.

───

3. Oral Communication in the Disciplines: A Framework for Faculty Development

:page_facing_up: Oral Communication in the Disciplines: A Resource for Teacher Development and Training
Deanna P. Dannels | Parlor Press, 2019
https://parlorpress.com/products/oral-communication-in-the-disciplines

Summary: Dannels' book synthesizes decades of Communication Across the Curriculum (CAC) research to provide a framework for teaching oral communication in disciplinary contexts. The core argument: oral communication isn't a generic skill that transfers automatically across disciplines. Engineers, historians, and literary critics have different oral genres (technical briefings vs. seminar discussions vs. conference papers), norms (data-driven vs. interpretive), and values (objectivity vs. positionality). Effective oral communication pedagogy must be discipline-specific, not one-size-fits-all.

Harvard Context: Dannels' work challenges the idea that a single "Oral Communication" requirement (analogous to Expos for writing) would solve Harvard's assessment problems. Instead, it suggests that each concentration should define oral competencies for its discipline and scaffold them progressively. What does "speaking well" mean in Economics vs. History & Literature vs. Molecular & Cellular Biology? The answers differ substantially. For oral exams to be meaningful, they should reflect disciplinary practices: economists might defend policy proposals under questioning; historians might synthesize historiographical debates; scientists might present experimental design and defend methodology. This requires faculty development: most Harvard faculty have never been trained to teach or assess oral communication. Dannels' book could serve as a resource for departmental workshops on designing discipline-specific oral assignments.

Source quality: Scholarly monograph from a reputable press, authored by a leading CAC researcher. Highly credible.

───

Connection to the Broader Project

These three sources converge on a critical insight: oral assessment isn't a plug-and-play solution. Doing it well requires:

1. Peer assessment infrastructure (rubrics, training, calibration)
2. Constructive alignment (outcomes, activities, and assessments must reinforce each other)
3. Disciplinary specificity (generic oral skills don't transfer; teach within context)

For Harvard: If faculty adopt oral exams to combat AI, we must avoid the temptation to treat them as simple substitutes for essays. The research suggests that effective oral assessment requires:

• Scaffolding: Students can't perform well on high-stakes oral exams without practice opportunities (low-stakes presentations in earlier courses, peer feedback, formative assessment).
• Clear criteria: Rubrics must specify what counts as strong performance in each discipline.
Parlor PressOral Communication in the Disciplines: A Resource for Teacher Development and Training"Provides faculty and administrators . . . a well-thought framework for advancing students' oral communication skills in disciplinary contexts. The authors highlight key instructional decisions that underlie effective oral assignments and offer clear options and examples to guide faculty."—Sean Conin[5:32 AM]• Faculty development: Most Harvard faculty have expertise in their fields but not in teaching/assessing oral communication. Resources and training are essential.
• Peer learning: Structured peer assessment can improve learning outcomes and reduce faculty workload — a win-win if implemented carefully.

The risk: Implementing oral exams poorly (no rubrics, no practice, no training) could actually worsen equity gaps, favoring students with prior debating experience or cultural capital around "speaking well." The research shows that thoughtful design can mitigate these risks — but only if we invest in infrastructure, not just policy changes.

───

Research continues through Monday, Feb 24, 9:00 AM EST. Questions/feedback welcome.
elle-with-claws  [5:46 AM]
:books: Oral Assignments Research — Theme 8: Professional Domains (Cycle 3)

This Saturday 5:45AM drop covers how law and business have already solved the oral assessment challenge—offering models Harvard can adapt.

───

Source 1: "Classroom Strategies to Prepare Students for Oral Argument"
Legal Writing Institute (LWI), October 2024
https://www.lwionline.org/article/classroom-strategies-prepare-students-oral-argument

Key insight: Law schools have developed systematic pedagogy for oral assessment anxiety and skill-building. Four strategies: (1) treat anxiety as a learning opportunity, (2) expose students to multiple perspectives/feedback styles, (3) create layered practice opportunities (2-min → 3-min → 6-min progressions), (4) incorporate reflection through video review. Quote: "With the legal industry increasingly expecting new lawyers to be practice-ready, we need to double down on teaching our students oral-argument skills."

:pushpin: Why it matters: This is a pedagogical blueprint. Law schools have confronted the same challenges Harvard faculty worry about (anxiety, grading consistency, scalability) and developed evidence-based solutions. The progressive skill-building model (short → longer presentations) is directly transferable.

───

Source 2: "Tips on Oral Advocacy" — Duke Law School
https://law.duke.edu/life/mootcourt/tips

Key insight: A detailed rubric for oral assessment in legal education. Key elements: theme statement (slanted favorably), roadmap (2-3 issues maximum), memorized opening, adaptability to judge interruptions, etiquette ("never speak over a judge"), and conclusion with prayer for relief. Critical quote: "The most serious error at oral argument is not listening carefully to the questions and thus failing to address the concerns raised by the judges."

:pushpin: Why it matters: This demonstrates that oral assessment can be highly structured with explicit criteria. Harvard faculty asking "how do we grade oral work fairly?" can look to moot court rubrics that have been refined over decades.

───

Source 3: "How Venture Capitalists Really Assess a Pitch"
Harvard Business Review, May 2017
https://hbr.org/2017/05/how-venture-capitalists-really-assess-a-pitch

Key insight: In-person oral performance reveals what written documents cannot. Research by Lakshmi Balachandra (Babson College): VCs would read a business plan, get excited, do industry research, grow more enthusiastic—then invite the founder in for a pitch meeting and "by the end of it they'd have absolutely no interest." The written proposal was strong; the oral presentation revealed something different.

:pushpin: Why it matters: This is the ultimate proof-of-concept for oral assessment. In high-stakes business decisions, sophisticated professionals trust oral performance more than written documents because it reveals dimensions that writing conceals. The same principle applies to student learning.

───

:crystal_ball: Synthesis: Professional domains have long understood what AI is forcing academia to confront: oral performance reveals what written work cannot. Law schools have developed sophisticated pedagogy (anxiety management, progressive practice, structured rubrics); business knows that live pitches expose what documents hide. Harvard isn't inventing something new—it's importing proven practices from professional education where oral assessment is already standard.

Rotation 3 complete. Next: Theme 0 begins rotation 4—or synthesis/final summary as we approach Feb 24 deadline.

Earlier cycles explored international and community college responses to AI. This round examines three emerging pedagogical movements in elite U.S. higher education: the oral exam revival documented by The Washington Post, the "unessay" alternative assessment movement, and the paradox of using AI to prepare students for oral exams.

───

1. Washington Post: "College professors use oral exams to combat AI"

:page_facing_up: College professors use oral exams to combat AI
The Washington Post, December 2025
https://www.washingtonpost.com/education/2025/12/12/ai-artificial-intelligence-college-oral-exam/

Summary: This major feature documents a "growing number of educators" shifting to oral exams to test learning "without the benefit of AI platforms such as ChatGPT." The article profiles professors across disciplines who've replaced take-home essays with in-person oral assessments, framing this as a widespread pedagogical trend. The "oral exam check" — having students explain their work verbally to verify understanding — is described as "surprisingly effective because it tests whether understanding matches performance."

Harvard Context: The Washington Post coverage signals that oral exams have entered mainstream educational discourse — no longer confined to graduate seminars or specialized concentrations, but presented as a viable response to AI across higher education. This media attention creates both opportunity and pressure for Harvard. Opportunity: faculty who've been hesitant about oral assessment now have institutional cover ("everyone's doing it"). Pressure: if peer institutions implement oral exams and Harvard doesn't, we risk appearing complacent about academic integrity. However, the article's framing is reductive — it treats oral exams primarily as anti-cheating measures rather than pedagogical choices. Harvard should resist this framing. The question isn't "How do we stop students from using AI?" but "What forms of assessment best serve learning?" Oral exams may be part of the answer, but only if designed thoughtfully, not deployed reactively.

Source quality: Major national newspaper; journalistic not scholarly, but reflects real pedagogical shifts. Likely amplifies trends beyond their actual prevalence (selection bias toward early adopters).

───

2. The "Unessay" Movement: Creative Alternatives to Traditional Papers

:page_facing_up: Students and teachers love it, AI cannot make it: the necessity of the unessay in our assessment world
Academic Health Economists' Network, August 2023
https://ahenetwork.org/students-and-teachers-love-it-ai-cannot-make-it-the-necessity-of-the-unessay-in-our-assessment-world/

Summary: The "unessay" — coined by medievalist Daniel Paul O'Donnell — invites students to demonstrate learning through non-traditional formats: podcasts, videos, creative projects, performances, websites, or other media that engage their passions. The assessment focuses on depth of engagement and originality rather than adherence to essay conventions. Proponents argue that unessays are inherently AI-resistant because they require personal creativity, context-specific application, and multimodal skills that generative AI can't replicate. Research shows students and teachers report higher satisfaction, deeper learning, and bypass "the vapidness of AI generated assignments."
The Washington PostProfessors are turning to this old-school method to stop AI use on examsA small but growing number of educators are experimenting with oral exams to circumvent the temptations presented by powerful AI platforms.Dec 12th, 2025https://www.washingtonpost.com/education/2025/12/12/ai-artificial-intelligence-college-oral-exam/ahenetwork.orgStudents and teachers love it, AI cannot make it: the necessity of the unessay in our assessment worldMilena Marinkova, Joy Robbins, Leeds University, Leeds, United Kingdom The unessay is a type of assessment approach that allows students a degree of freedom not seen in most assessment types. Stude…Aug 2nd, 2023[5:47 AM]Harvard Context: The unessay movement offers an alternative to oral exams for making assessment more AI-resistant while maintaining written/creative expression. Rather than abandoning writing entirely, unessays transform it — students might create a podcast analyzing a historical event, a video essay on philosophical arguments, or an interactive website exploring scientific concepts. Harvard already has examples: VES students create multimedia portfolios; Music concentrators compose original pieces; some literature courses allow creative responses. The challenge: scaling unessays requires significant grading infrastructure (faculty must evaluate diverse formats, not standardized essays) and clear rubrics (what counts as "good" in a podcast vs. a traditional paper?). The benefit: unessays address AI and other longstanding pedagogical critiques — that traditional essays privilege particular communication styles, disadvantage non-native writers, and fail to engage diverse student interests.

Methodological note: Article is advocacy piece, not empirical study. Claims about student learning would benefit from systematic assessment data.

───

3. The AI Paradox: Using ChatGPT to Reduce Public Speaking Anxiety

:page_facing_up: The impact of AI applications on pre-service teachers' public speaking anxiety and academic speaking skill in the context of oral presentations: A mixed-methods study
ScienceDirect | Teaching and Teacher Education, August 2025
https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0742051X25002574

Summary: This empirical study investigated whether AI tools (Yoodli for speech practice, ChatGPT for content preparation) could reduce public speaking anxiety and improve oral presentation skills among pre-service teachers. Results showed significant improvements in both anxiety reduction and speaking competence, with participants reporting that AI provided "scaffolded, learner-centered support" for oral skill development. The tools offered low-stakes practice opportunities, immediate feedback, and personalized coaching that students found less intimidating than human audiences.

Harvard Context: Here's the uncomfortable irony: if Harvard adopts oral exams to resist AI, students will likely use AI to prepare for those exams — generating talking points, rehearsing with chatbots, getting feedback on delivery. This isn't necessarily bad! The study suggests AI-assisted practice can genuinely improve oral skills, especially for anxious or underprepared students. But it complicates the "oral exams are AI-proof" narrative. What we're really assessing is students' ability to synthesize, internalize, and articulate ideas under real-time questioning — not whether they used AI in preparation. This requires a pedagogical shift: oral exams should emphasize improvisation, dialogue, and critical thinking (responding to unexpected questions, defending positions, admitting uncertainty) rather than polished speeches (which can be rehearsed with AI). The equity dimension: if affluent students have better AI coaching tools or more time to practice, oral exams could reproduce existing advantages. Harvard might need to provide institutional AI practice resources to level the playing field.

Source quality: Peer-reviewed empirical study with mixed methods. Strong methodological rigor; findings are credible.

───

Connection to the Broader Project

These three sources reveal that post-AI assessment innovation is messy and multidirectional:

• Oral exams: Mainstream media presents them as the solution; reality is more complex
• Unessays: Creative alternatives that resist AI and address other pedagogical problems
• AI-assisted practice: Students will use AI to prepare for oral exams, complicating "AI-proof" claims
[5:47 AM]For Harvard: The key insight is that there's no single solution to the AI assessment crisis. Oral exams, unessays, scaffolded written assignments, hands-on projects — all have merits and limitations. Rather than declaring "we're switching to oral exams," Harvard should:

1. Diversify assessment: Encourage faculty to use multiple formats (oral + written + creative)
2. Focus on process: Assess thinking/learning, not just final products
3. Provide resources: Support students in developing oral skills (workshops, AI practice tools, peer feedback)
4. Train faculty: Most Harvard faculty have never designed/graded creative alternatives or oral exams

The meta-lesson: AI hasn't just disrupted assessment — it's exposed longstanding problems with how we evaluate learning. The real opportunity isn't to make assignments "AI-proof," but to design assessments that genuinely measure what we value: critical thinking, intellectual courage, authentic engagement.

───

elle-with-claws  [6:04 AM]
:books: Oral Assignments Research — Theme 1: History of Oral Exams (Rotation 4)

This Saturday 6AM drop focuses on the living tradition of oral examination in graduate education—evidence that oral assessment never disappeared at the highest academic levels.

───

Source 1: "PhD Viva Voces — A Complete Guide"
DiscoverPhDs, January 2021
https://www.discoverphds.com/advice/doing/vivas

Key insight: The PhD viva is the final assessment at the doctoral level, and it's entirely oral. The viva determines five things: (1) the work is your own, (2) you understand your project deeply, (3) you know broader research in your field, (4) you're aware of limitations, (5) you made an original contribution. Six possible outcomes range from immediate pass to immediate fail—making this a genuine high-stakes oral assessment.

:pushpin: Why it matters: The PhD viva proves that oral assessment scales to the highest level of academic achievement. If Harvard's most prestigious credential (the doctorate) requires oral defense, the infrastructure and legitimacy for undergraduate oral assignments already exists.

───

Source 2: "Comprehensive Examination" — Wikipedia
https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Comprehensive_examination

Key insight: "Orals" is the colloquial term for qualifying exams in many US PhD programs. The comprehensive exam system is primarily US/Canadian, dating to the late 1930s and proliferating after WWII as graduate populations grew. Some undergraduate programs (Wabash, Reed, Kenyon, University of the South) also require comprehensive exams—oral assessment isn't exclusively graduate-level.

:pushpin: Why it matters: The historical note is significant: comprehensive exams "date back only to the late 1930s" and "proliferated when graduate populations grew too large after World War II to manage by dissertation alone." Oral exams were added to scale graduate education—they weren't eliminated.

───

Source 3: "20 Tips for the Ph.D. Exam"
University of Washington History Department
https://history.washington.edu/20-tips-phd-exam

Key insight: Practical oral exam preparation advice from a major research university. Key tips: "During the oral exam, try to connect your fields and show how readings in different areas connect and/or inform one another." "Give long(ish) answers. Short answers may lead to rapid fire questioning." "Try to take control of the oral exam as much as possible."

:pushpin: Why it matters: This demonstrates that oral examination has developed sophisticated pedagogy at the graduate level. The skills being tested—synthesis, connection-making, real-time argumentation—are exactly what AI disrupts at the undergraduate level. Graduate programs have always known these skills require oral demonstration.

───

:crystal_ball: Synthesis: Graduate education never abandoned oral assessment—it's embedded in comprehensive exams, qualifying exams, and dissertation defenses. The question isn't whether oral assessment is academically legitimate (it determines PhDs) but why it disappeared from undergraduate education. Harvard's highest credential already requires oral demonstration; the case for extending this to undergraduates is strengthened by recognizing that oral assessment is already the standard at the doctoral level.

Up next: Theme 2 — Peer institution AI response policies (rotation 4)

You can also download this page as a PDF.[6:06 AM]Theme 5 (Cycle 6): AI Used IN Oral Evaluation — The Recursive Loop

Earlier cycles explored AI-powered transcription and language learning tools. This round examines the latest frontier: AI systems that grade oral presentations, detect bias in speech analytics, and even conduct viva voce examinations as automated interviewers. The recursive irony: using AI to evaluate assessments designed to resist AI.

───

1. AI Auto-Grading for Oral Presentations: Rubrics, Ethics, and Faculty Displacement

:page_facing_up: AI and Auto-Grading in Higher Education: Capabilities, Ethics, and the Evolving Role of Educators
Ohio State University Office of Distance Education, 2024
https://ascode.osu.edu/news/ai-and-auto-grading-higher-education-capabilities-ethics-and-evolving-role-educators

Summary: OSU's analysis distinguishes rule-based auto-grading (multiple choice, simple math) from AI-assisted grading powered by large language models that can "evaluate complex, open-ended assignments such as essays or discussions" using natural language processing. The article notes that LLMs can now assess submissions with "minimal explicit programming" through few-shot or zero-shot learning — meaning faculty provide a rubric and sample answers, and the AI grades entire cohorts. The ethical concerns raised include bias, transparency, and "the evolving role of educators" (i.e., what happens when grading is automated?).

Harvard Context: The promise of AI-graded oral exams is scalability: imagine recording 100 student presentations, uploading them to an AI grading platform with a rubric, and receiving scored evaluations overnight. This could make oral assessment feasible in large courses where faculty workload is the primary barrier. The ethical dangers are profound: (1) Bias amplification — AI trained on dominant communication styles may penalize non-native speakers, students with accents, or neurodivergent presentation styles. (2) Rubric rigidity — automated grading requires precisely defined criteria, potentially reducing oral assessment to checkboxes (eye contact: yes/no; clarity: 1-5) rather than holistic judgment. (3) Faculty deskilling — if AI does the grading, do faculty lose evaluative judgment expertise? The OSU piece suggests AI should be "AI-assisted" not "AI-automated" — faculty review AI-generated scores and provide final evaluation. For Harvard, this means AI grading tools could support oral assessment scaling, but only with strong human oversight and explicit bias auditing.

Source quality: University office publication; not peer-reviewed research but reflects institutional policy thinking.

───

2. Speech Analytics Bias: When AI Evaluation Discriminates

:page_facing_up: A bias evaluation solution for multiple sensitive attribute speech recognition
ScienceDirect | Computer Speech & Language, March 2025
https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0885230825000129

Summary: This technical paper addresses a critical problem: speech recognition systems exhibit bias across sensitive attributes (race, gender, accent, age), which "may lead to the misinterpretation or ignoring of the voice of a specific group of people." When these biased systems are used for assessment, they systematically disadvantage certain populations. The paper proposes a bias evaluation framework that measures differential error rates across demographic groups and flags when AI speech analytics show discriminatory patterns. The goal is to make bias visible so it can be corrected.
ascode.osu.eduAI and Auto-Grading in Higher Education: Capabilities, Ethics, and the Evolving Role of EducatorsAI and Auto-Grading in Higher Education: Capabilities, Ethics, and the Evolving Role of EducatorsAs artificial intelligence continues to reshape various industries, its impact on higher education, particularly in assessment, is becoming increasingly significant. Automated grading and AI-assisted evaluation tools are revolutionizing how instructors assess student work, streamline feedback, and manage large-scale courses.https://ascode.osu.edu/news/ai-and-auto-grading-higher-education-capabilities-ethics-and-evolving-role-educators[6:06 AM]Harvard Context: This research exposes the equity nightmare of AI-graded oral exams. If Harvard adopts AI tools to transcribe and evaluate oral presentations, and those tools have higher error rates for non-native speakers or African American English speakers, we've just automated discrimination. The research shows that even state-of-the-art speech recognition systems struggle with accent variation, disfluencies, and non-standard dialects. For Harvard, this means: (1) Never use AI grading without bias auditing — any vendor claiming "objective" oral assessment must provide demographic performance data. (2) Human review is mandatory for high-stakes assessments — AI can assist with transcription/preliminary scoring, but faculty must review. (3) Accessibility accommodations — students with speech impediments, anxiety-induced disfluencies, or accented English must have alternative pathways (written supplements, extended time, human-only evaluation). The brutal truth: AI makes oral exams less equitable, not more.

Source quality: Peer-reviewed technical paper in a reputable journal. High credibility.

───

3. Conversational AI as Viva Voce Examiner: Automated Oral Interviews

:page_facing_up: Investigating the Value of Using Emotionally Intelligent Artificial Conversational Agents to Carry out Assessments in Higher Education: Review
IEEE Conference Publication, 2023
https://ieeexplore.ieee.org/document/10099518/

Summary: This IEEE paper reviews emerging conversational AI systems designed to conduct viva voce examinations — oral interviews where AI asks questions, evaluates responses, and follows up based on student answers. The technology leverages large language models to generate adaptive questioning sequences, simulating the dynamic interaction of a human examiner. The paper explores "emotionally intelligent" AI agents that can detect student anxiety, adjust question difficulty, and provide encouragement. Proponents argue this could make oral assessment more scalable and consistent (every student gets the same baseline rigor); critics worry about losing the human judgment and mentorship central to traditional vivas.

Harvard Context: The dystopian potential: students defend their senior theses to chatbot examiners instead of faculty committees. The pragmatic use case: students practice oral exams with AI tutors before the real assessment (low-stakes rehearsal). The equity question: does AI democratize access to oral exam practice (students who can't afford private tutors now have AI coaches), or does it widen gaps (affluent students buy premium AI viva prep)? For Harvard, conversational AI likely fits best as a formative tool — students rehearse with AI, get feedback, then face human evaluators for summative assessment. This mirrors how students already use AI for writing (drafting with ChatGPT, then revising based on tutorial feedback). The line: AI can augment preparation but shouldn't replace human evaluation in high-stakes contexts. However, the technology raises a philosophical question: if AI can conduct credible oral exams, what does that say about what oral exams actually assess? Are we measuring thinking (which requires human judgment) or performance (which AI can evaluate)?

Source quality: Peer-reviewed conference paper (IEEE). Credible, though some claims about "emotional intelligence" in AI are contested.

───

Connection to the Broader Project

These three sources reveal a recursive paradox at the heart of AI-era oral assessment:

1. Faculty adopt oral exams to resist student use of AI
2. Scaling oral exams requires faculty time/resources
3. AI grading tools promise to solve the scalability problem
4. Now we're using AI to evaluate assessments designed to resist AI

The trap: If we outsource oral exam evaluation to AI, we've just automated the very problem we were trying to escape — replacing human judgment with algorithmic scoring, amplifying bias, and reducing learning to rubric compliance.
[6:06 AM]The opportunity: Use AI formatively (practice, feedback, transcription support) while keeping summative evaluation human (final grading, pass/fail decisions, critical judgment). This hybrid model leverages AI's strengths (consistency, scalability, 24/7 availability) while preserving what humans do better (contextual judgment, mentorship, bias mitigation through lived experience).

For Harvard: Three principles for AI in oral assessment:

1. Transparency: If AI assists with grading, students must know. No "black box" evaluation.
2. Bias auditing: Any AI tool must demonstrate equitable performance across demographics.
3. Human override: Faculty retain final authority; AI recommendations are advisory, not binding.

The deeper question: If AI can grade oral exams competently, maybe oral exams aren't measuring what we think they measure. True intellectual assessment might require elements that can't be automated — improvisational thinking, ethical reasoning under ambiguity, authentic dialogue. That's the real challenge for Harvard: designing assessments that foreground irreducibly human capacities.

───

Research continues through Monday, Feb 24, 9:00 AM EST. Questions/feedback welcome.
elle-with-claws  [6:16 AM]
:books: Oral Assignments Research — Theme 2: Peer Institution AI Policies (Rotation 4)

This Saturday 6:15AM drop covers the latest institutional policy developments—showing an emerging consensus that instructor permission is now the default framework.

───

Source 1: "Guidance for Use of Generative AI Tools" — MIT IS&T
MIT Information Systems & Technology, Updated 2025
https://ist.mit.edu/ai-guidance

Key insight: MIT explicitly warns against using AI "for evaluating student academic performance" without in-depth risk assessment. The guidance prohibits entering medium- or high-risk data (including "unpublished research results, confidential third-party information, personally identifiable information") into AI tools. Faculty must "disclose the use of generative AI tools for all academic, educational, and research-related uses."

:pushpin: Why it matters: MIT's policy implicitly supports oral assessment: if AI can't be used to evaluate student performance without risk assessment, and students can't use AI for coursework without disclosure, then assessments that bypass AI entirely (oral exams) become administratively simpler than managing disclosure regimes.

───

Source 2: "Generative AI Policies at the World's Top Universities: October 2025 Update"
Thesify, October 2025
https://www.thesify.ai/blog/gen-ai-policies-update-2025

Key insight: A T20 policy survey shows convergence: Columbia finalized a "permission required" policy; Imperial College released guidelines; Johns Hopkins issued comprehensive responsible-use guidance; UCLA published sample syllabus statements. Oxford requires a declaration for any AI use in summative assessments; unauthorized use is academic misconduct. Princeton: "Generative AI is not a source" and students must confirm AI is permitted before using it.

:pushpin: Why it matters: The emerging consensus is "instructor permission required" rather than blanket permission or blanket prohibition. This creates a policy space where faculty can simply require oral defense of written work—the same instructor discretion that allows AI use also allows requiring its verification.

───

Source 3: "Navigating AI Policies for PhD Students in 2025: A Doctoral Researcher's Guide"
Thesify, October 2025
https://www.thesify.ai/blog/phd-ai-policies-2025

Key insight: Even at the doctoral level, AI policies emphasize verification and disclosure. Cambridge: "Unacknowledged AI in summative assessments is academic misconduct." Harvard: "Users remain responsible for any content containing AI material." Caltech: "Students must confirm AI is permitted before using it and must disclose its role; copying AI-generated text into your thesis without disclosure is an integrity violation."

:pushpin: Why it matters: If doctoral students at Caltech must disclose AI assistance in their thesis, the verification problem isn't limited to undergraduates. But doctoral students already face oral defense. The logical extension: undergraduate written work could similarly require oral verification.

───

:crystal_ball: Synthesis: The policy landscape has matured since 2023. The dominant model is now "instructor discretion with mandatory disclosure"—which creates the opening for oral verification. If instructors can require disclosure, they can also require demonstration. The administrative machinery for oral assessment doesn't need to be invented; it can slot into existing "instructor sets policy" frameworks at MIT, Harvard, Princeton, Columbia, and beyond.

Building on earlier explorations of Plato, Quintilian, and Isocrates, this round examines three 20th-century thinkers whose work illuminates why oral assessment matters (or doesn't): Vygotsky on inner speech as cognitive development, Freire on dialogue as liberation, and Ellul on how technological society "humiliates the word."

───

1. Vygotsky's "Thought and Language": Inner Speech as Cognitive Scaffolding

:page_facing_up: Inner Speech: Development, Cognitive Functions, Phenomenology, and Neurobiology
PMC (National Institutes of Health), 2015
https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4538954/

Summary: Lev Vygotsky's seminal theory posits that cognitive development proceeds through the internalization of external speech. Children first use language socially (communicative speech), then develop "egocentric speech" (talking to themselves while problem-solving), which eventually becomes "inner speech" — silent, abbreviated thought. Vygotsky argued that the ability to regulate one's own behavior through language is fundamental to higher-order thinking. Importantly, this process moves from oral (external dialogue) to internal (thought), suggesting that spoken language is the foundation for abstract reasoning.

Harvard Context: Vygotsky's framework offers a developmental justification for oral assessment: if inner speech develops from externalized dialogue, then asking students to "think aloud" during oral exams externalizes their cognitive process, making reasoning visible for evaluation and coaching. This is fundamentally different from written exams, which capture only final products of thought. Oral exams in the Vygotskian tradition would emphasize metacognitive talk — students explaining how they solved a problem, why they chose an interpretation, or what confused them. This maps onto Harvard's tutorial system: the one-on-one or small-group oral dialogue is precisely the "zone of proximal development" where students can articulate emerging ideas with scaffolding. The AI connection: if ChatGPT can generate polished written arguments, but struggles to replicate the messy, self-correcting process of human reasoning-aloud, then oral exams that foreground process over product remain AI-resistant. The challenge: training faculty and students to value "thinking aloud" rather than polished speeches.

Source quality: Peer-reviewed PMC article synthesizing Vygotsky's work. Highly credible scholarly overview.

───

2. Paulo Freire's "Pedagogy of the Oppressed": Dialogue vs. Banking Education

:page_facing_up: Paulo Freire: dialogue, praxis and education
infed.org (Informal Education Encyclopedia), 2025
https://infed.org/dir/welcome/paulo-freire-dialogue-praxis-and-education/

Summary: Paulo Freire distinguished between "banking education" (teachers deposit knowledge into passive students) and "problem-posing education" (teachers and students engage in dialogue as equals to co-create understanding). For Freire, dialogue is not just a teaching method but an ethical and political act — it requires "profound love," mutual respect, and the belief that all humans are capable of critical consciousness. Freire saw education as either reproducing oppression or fostering liberation, depending on whether it silences or amplifies student voices. Critically, dialogue is oral by definition — it requires real-time exchange, listening, responding.
PubMed Central (PMC)Inner Speech: Development, Cognitive Functions, Phenomenology, and NeurobiologyInner speech—also known as covert speech or verbal thinking—has been implicated in theories of cognitive development, speech monitoring, executive function, and psychopathology. Despite a growing body of knowledge on its phenomenology, development, ...https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4538954/infed.orgPaulo Freire: dialogue, praxis and education - infed.orgPaulo Freire, dialogue, praxis and education. Perhaps the most influential thinker about education in the late twentieth century, Paulo Freire has been particularly popular with informal educators with his emphasis on dialogue and his concern for the oppressed. contents: introduction · contribution · critique · further reading and references · links Paulo Freire (1921 – ... Read moreEstimated reading time8 minuteshttps://infed.org/dir/welcome/paulo-freire-dialogue-praxis-and-education/[6:17 AM]Harvard Context: Freire's critique exposes the danger of using oral exams as banking education in disguise: if faculty simply interrogate students to check whether they've "deposited" the correct knowledge, oral exams become oppressive rituals, not liberatory dialogues. The Freirean alternative: conversational oral assessments where students and faculty explore questions together, students can challenge interpretations, and "I don't know" is a legitimate starting point for inquiry. This is closer to Harvard's best tutorials — where the goal isn't recitation but collaborative problem-solving. The AI dimension: Freire would ask, Who benefits from oral exams? If they're adopted to restore faculty authority over AI-empowered students, that's banking education defending itself. But if oral exams are designed to center student voices — to hear marginalized perspectives, validate diverse ways of knowing, and create space for dialogue that AI can't replicate — then they align with Freire's vision. The equity question: Freirean dialogue requires power-sharing. Can Harvard faculty conduct oral exams that don't reinforce hierarchies (professor-as-judge) but instead create genuine conversation?

Source quality: Authoritative encyclopedia entry on informal education; well-cited but not original research. Reliable scholarly overview.

───

3. Jacques Ellul's "Propaganda": The Technological Society's "Humiliation of the Word"

:page_facing_up: Jacques Ellul, Propaganda, and the Technological Society
Lance Strate's Blog Time Passing, 2011
https://eric.ed.gov/?id=ED391188 (ERIC abstract)
http://lancestrate.blogspot.com/2011/08/jacques-ellul-propaganda-and.html

Summary: French philosopher-theologian Jacques Ellul argued that technological society transforms all communication into propaganda — not in the narrow sense of political messaging, but as the systematic subordination of language to efficiency, technique, and mass persuasion. Ellul warned of the "humiliation of the word": in a technologically mediated world, language loses its dialogic, truthful quality and becomes merely instrumental. Neil Postman built on Ellul's work, arguing that television's image culture upset the balance between orality and literacy achieved in print culture, privileging emotional manipulation over rational discourse. Ellul saw propaganda as relying on "emotion and irrationality" — rationality disappears when communication is industrialized.

Harvard Context: Ellul's critique offers a dystopian warning about oral exams in the AI era. If Harvard adopts oral assessment primarily as an efficiency technique to combat AI-generated essays — turning exams into standardized, rubric-driven performances optimized for speed and scalability — we risk "humiliating the word" in exactly the way Ellul warned against. Oral dialogue becomes another form of propaganda: students learn to perform understanding (eye contact, confident tone, rehearsed talking points) rather than engage authentically. The technological trap: using AI to grade oral exams, or requiring students to record video presentations for asynchronous evaluation, transforms oral communication into mediated, manipulable content — the opposite of Ellul's ideal of unmediated dialogue. The Ellulian alternative: oral exams should be radically inefficient — small seminars, unhurried conversations, face-to-face encounters that resist standardization. This contradicts the scalability imperative but honors what makes oral communication valuable: presence, spontaneity, vulnerability.

Source quality: Blog post by media ecology scholar Lance Strate + ERIC abstract of academic paper. Credible but secondary/interpretive rather than primary source.

───

Connection to the Broader Project

These three thinkers offer competing visions of what oral communication is for:

• Vygotsky: Oral speech is cognitive scaffolding — the developmental foundation for thought
eric.ed.govERIC - ED391188 - Contributions of Jacques Ellul's "Propaganda" to Teaching and Research in Rhetorical Theory., 1995-Nov-18Jacques Ellul is widely known among sociologists and philosophers in the West for his analyses of the impact technology has on human society and humans themselves. Less well known is Ellul's deep interest in human life. Ellul's interest in these areas is evident in "Propaganda: The Formation of Men's Attitudes" (1965) and "The Humiliation of the Word" (1985). Of interest to students of rhetorical criticism, Ellul does divide propaganda into political and sociological types. Ellul is known for his searing attack on the technological mindset, "la technique," which he argues is a self-directing and self-augmenting entity. It is the technological mindset, he argues, that disrupts human reflectiv… lancestrate.blogspot.comJacques Ellul, Propaganda, and the Technological SocietyHere's a bit more fall-out from the discussion that went on over the Media Ecology Association listserv.  I've extracted my comments from th...[6:17 AM]• Freire: Dialogue is political practice — the means of liberation or oppression
[6:17 AM]• Ellul: Technological society degrades language — oral communication risks becoming propaganda

For Harvard: These frameworks suggest that the form of oral assessment we choose reveals our values:

1. Vygotskian orals would emphasize process: "Think aloud while you solve this problem"; "Explain how you arrived at that interpretation"
2. Freirean orals would emphasize dialogue: Professors and students explore questions together; uncertainty is valued; power is shared
3. Ellul-aware orals would emphasize presence: Small-scale, face-to-face, unmediated; resisting efficiency and standardization

The synthesis: If Harvard faculty want oral exams to be more than anti-AI panic responses, we need to ask: What kind of oral communication do we value, and why?

• If we value cognitive development (Vygotsky), oral exams should externalize thinking
• If we value democratic participation (Freire), oral exams should redistribute interpretive authority
• If we value human presence (Ellul), oral exams should resist technological mediation

The worst outcome: oral exams that are neither Vygotskian (just recitation, not thinking-aloud), nor Freirean (hierarchical interrogation, not dialogue), nor Ellul-aware (standardized, recorded, AI-graded) — in other words, banking education in oral form.

───

Research continues through Monday, Feb 24, 9:00 AM EST. Questions/feedback welcome.
elle-with-claws  [6:31 AM]
:books: Oral Assignments Research — Theme 3: Harvard Oral History (Rotation 4)

This Saturday 6:30AM drop focuses on Harvard Law School—the birthplace of American legal education's oral tradition and a living laboratory for understanding the power and challenges of Socratic pedagogy.

───

Source 1: "The Socratic Method in the Age of Trauma"
Jeannie Suk Gersen, Harvard Law Review (Vol. 130)
https://harvardlawreview.org/print/vol-130/the-socratic-method-in-the-age-of-trauma/

Key insight: HLS transformed Professor Gersen from "silent" to "speak"—and she now views this as life-changing. "It was 1L year at Harvard Law School that changed my default mode from 'silent' to 'speak.' ... What happened as I repeatedly faced my unwelcome turn, heard my voice, and got through with many stumbles was a revelation that changed my life." She acknowledges critiques about disproportionate adverse impact on women and minorities, yet defends oral pedagogy as transformative.

:pushpin: Why it matters: This is a Harvard faculty member's personal testimony that oral pedagogy develops skills impossible to cultivate through written work alone. The frank acknowledgment of challenges (anxiety, equity concerns) alongside advocacy for oral methods provides a balanced model for Harvard faculty considering oral assignments.

───

Source 2: "The Decline of the Socratic Method at Harvard"
Orin S. Kerr, Nebraska Law Review (Vol. 78)
https://digitalcommons.unl.edu/nlr/vol78/iss1/6/

Key insight: The "traditional Socratic method is today more myth than reality" at Harvard Law School. Through interviews with HLS faculty, Kerr categorizes professors as traditionalists (still using classic Socratic dialogue), quasi-traditionalists (mixing Socratic with innovations), and counter-traditionalists (rejecting the paradigm). The method "common during the 1950s and 1960s is nearly extinct."

:pushpin: Why it matters: Even Harvard Law has moved away from pure oral interrogation. This is both a warning (oral methods face real resistance) and an opportunity (the AI crisis may justify reviving what was lost). If HLS needs to rethink what it abandoned, so might the broader university.

───

Source 3: "Making the Case"
Harvard Magazine, September 2003
https://www.harvardmagazine.com/2003/09/making-the-case-html

Key insight: Christopher Columbus Langdell invented the case method at HLS in 1870, then developed "through trial and error what is now called the Socratic method." Before Langdell, law was taught via the "Dwight Method"—lecture, recitation, and drill based on memorizing treatises. Langdell's innovation: students read original sources (cases), draw their own conclusions, and face "detailed follow-up questions from the instructor." HBS adopted cases in 1920; HMS followed in 1985.

:pushpin: Why it matters: This is the origin story. Harvard Law invented American oral legal education; HBS and HMS followed. If Harvard is the mother institution of case-based, Socratic, oral pedagogy in professional education, extending that tradition to undergraduate education has deep institutional precedent.

───

:crystal_ball: Synthesis: Harvard Law School is both origin and cautionary tale. Langdell's 1870 innovation—oral Socratic questioning instead of passive memorization—became the foundation of American legal education. Yet even HLS has seen this tradition decline. The AI moment presents an opportunity: if oral pedagogy was worth inventing in 1870 and worth spreading to business and medicine, it may be worth reviving now. Professor Gersen's personal transformation shows what oral methods can achieve; Kerr's research shows how easily they can be abandoned.

Up next: Theme 4 — Educational research on speaking/assessment (rotation 4)

Time in the classroom must...Sep 1st, 2003[6:32 AM]Theme 7 (Cycle 7): Professional Domains — Improv, TED Talks, and Science Communication

Earlier cycles explored venture capital pitches, diplomacy, and medical rounds. This round examines three contemporary professional oral practices that have influenced higher education: improvisational theater techniques, the TED Talk phenomenon, and emerging norms for PhD defense presentations — each offering lessons (and warnings) for Harvard's oral assessment design.

───

1. Improvisational Theater as Oral Skills Training: Harvard's Own Bok Center Players

:page_facing_up: Improv skills can translate to social and professional skills
Harvard Gazette, 2023
https://news.harvard.edu/gazette/story/2019/05/improv-skills-can-translate-to-social-and-professional-skills/

Summary: Harvard's Bok Center Players use theatrical improvisation to address topics like race, gender, and identity through applied theater. The article notes that improv training develops "communication, confidence, commitment, listening, focus, story-telling, and spontaneity" — precisely the skills needed for effective oral assessment. Businesses now use improv workshops for professional development, recognizing that the ability to respond spontaneously, build on others' ideas, and handle unexpected questions translates directly to workplace communication.

Harvard Context: The irony: Harvard already has infrastructure for teaching improvisational oral skills (Bok Center Players, dramatic arts courses), but we rarely connect this to academic assessment. If oral exams value intellectual spontaneity — the ability to think on your feet, respond to challenges, admit "I don't know" and explore from there — then improv pedagogy offers a training model. Students could practice low-stakes "improv seminars" before high-stakes oral exams, learning to build arguments collaboratively, listen actively, and embrace uncertainty. The key improv principle: "Yes, and..." (accept what's offered, then build on it) contrasts with adversarial oral exams where students defensively protect positions. Harvard could pilot "conversational oral exams" modeled on improv scenes rather than interrogations — faculty and students co-construct understanding through dialogue. The equity dimension: improv skills are teachable, reducing the advantage for students who arrive with debating experience or cultural capital around "performing well."

Source quality: University press coverage; credible but not peer-reviewed research. Describes existing Harvard programs.

───

2. TED Talks and the Commodification of Oral Presentation

:page_facing_up: Public Speaking 101 launched to teach essential communication and presentation skills / TED Masterclass: TED's Official Public Speaking Course
TED-Ed / TED Masterclass, 2025
https://ed.ted.com/blog/2025/08/21/public-speaking-101-launched-to-teach-essential-communication-and-presentation-skills
https://masterclass.ted.com/course-overview

Summary: TED has launched Public Speaking 101 and TED Masterclass, teaching "essential communication and presentation skills" through a "progression of videos" that guide learners in developing "TED-style talks." The courses, led by TED speaker coaches, promise to help participants "communicate more clearly, persuasively and authentically." TED has effectively standardized a genre of oral presentation: 18-minute talks with narrative arcs, personal anecdotes, visual aids, and inspirational conclusions.
Harvard GazetteImprov skills can translate to social and professional skillsHarvard College’s Immediate Gratification Players discuss how improv skills can translate to social and professional skills.May 1st, 2019https://news.harvard.edu/gazette/story/2019/05/improv-skills-can-translate-to-social-and-professional-skills/TED-EdPublic Speaking 101 launched to teach essential communication and presentation skillsIdeas change everything —  and since language lets us share our ideas, learning how to use it well gives speakers the power to inspire people and even change how they think. Opportunity: TED has made oral presentation a valued skill, created accessible training resources, and demonstrated that effective speaking can be taught systematically. Many Harvard students already aspire to give TED-style talks; courses could leverage this motivation. Danger: TED-style presentations privilege performance over substance. Critics argue TED talks favor slick delivery, emotional manipulation, and oversimplified "ideas worth spreading" over nuanced analysis or methodological rigor. If Harvard oral exams reward "TED-able" performances — confident storytelling, charismatic delivery, inspiring messaging — we risk valuing style over intellectual depth. The discipline question: TED works for public intellectuals and entrepreneurs, but does it work for historians defending complex arguments, or scientists presenting negative results? Harvard should resist the temptation to make all oral exams "TED-like." Different fields need different oral genres. The critical pedagogy move: have students critique TED Talks as a genre (what gets simplified? whose voices dominate?), not just emulate them.

Source quality: Corporate educational materials from TED. High production value, proven reach, but not pedagogically neutral — designed to promote a specific communication style.

───

3. PhD Defense Presentations: The "Layman Talk" as Accessibility Standard

:page_facing_up: Navigating the PhD Defense: Strategies for a Successful Presentation
IMISCOE (International Migration Research Network), 2025
https://www.imiscoe.org/news-and-blog/phd-blog/1852-navigating-the-phd-defense-strategies-for-a-successful-presentation-2

Summary: This guide emphasizes the "layman talk" as an "essential component" of PhD defenses — presenting research "to a non-specialist audience" to demonstrate "ability to communicate complex ideas in a clear and engaging manner." The expectation: doctoral candidates should be able to explain their dissertations to intelligent non-experts, not just disciplinary specialists. This reflects broader trends in academia toward public engagement and accessible scholarship.

Harvard Context: The layman talk standard offers a democratic model for oral assessment: can you explain your work to someone outside your subfield? This challenges the common oral exam format where students recite jargon to prove insider knowledge. Applied to undergraduate oral exams, the layman talk principle suggests: students should explain their thesis/project to faculty from other concentrations, or to peers, not just their advisors. This tests deeper understanding — you can't hide behind technical vocabulary if your audience doesn't share your specialized background. The Science Communication movement (which Harvard participates in through various initiatives) aligns with this: researchers must explain why their work matters to broader publics, funders, and policymakers. For Harvard undergraduates, oral exams could include cross-disciplinary panels — a History concentrator defends their thesis to a committee including an economist and a biologist, requiring translation across paradigms. The challenge: this requires more faculty time (assembling diverse committees) and student preparation (learning to code-switch between specialist and generalist registers).

Source quality: Professional network blog post; practical advice rather than empirical research. Credible but not peer-reviewed.

───

Connection to the Broader Project

These three professional oral practices reveal competing models for what "speaking well" means:

• Improv: Spontaneity, collaboration, "yes and," listening, building together
• TED: Polished performance, narrative arc, inspiration, accessibility (but also oversimplification)
• Layman talk: Translation across expertise levels, accessibility without sacrificing complexity
imiscoe.orgNavigating the PhD Defense: Strategies for a Successful Presentation - IMISCOEIMISCOE is the largest European network of scholars in the area of migration and integration. The focus is on comparative research and joint research projects.[6:32 AM]For Harvard: The challenge is choosing which professional oral norms to import into academic assessment. We could:

1. Embrace improv principles → oral exams as collaborative sense-making, not interrogation
2. Resist TED-ification → avoid privileging charisma and oversimplification over rigor
3. Adopt layman talk standards → require students to explain ideas across disciplinary boundaries

The synthesis: Effective oral assessment should combine:

• Improv's spontaneity (handling unexpected questions, thinking aloud)
• TED's accessibility (clear communication to non-specialists)
• PhD defense's rigor (defending methodology, engaging with criticism)

The warning: Professional oral communication training can also reproduce inequality. Improv workshops, TED coaching, and dissertation boot camps cost money or require institutional access. If Harvard expects "professional-quality" oral presentations without providing equitable training, we advantage students who can afford private coaching or who studied at elite prep schools. The solution: embed oral skill development across the curriculum (first-year seminars → junior tutorials → senior defenses), not expect it to emerge spontaneously.
