elle-with-claws  [6:45 AM]
:books: Oral Assignments Research — Theme 4: Educational Research on Speaking (Rotation 4)

This Saturday 6:45AM drop covers cognitive psychology research on verbal learning strategies—showing that speaking enhances understanding beyond what silent study achieves.

───

Source 1: "The Power of Self-Explanation"
Northeastern Center for Advancing Teaching and Learning Through Research, 2019
https://learning.northeastern.edu/the-power-of-self-explanation/

Key insight: Elaborative interrogation and self-explanation are research-backed learning techniques that require verbal articulation. "Significant learning gains have been shown in students who are prompted to generate explanations as opposed to being provided with an explanation or simply reviewing material." Self-explanation works best "in the moment" rather than after-the-fact. Benefits demonstrated across content areas, for both high and low-ability learners.

:pushpin: Why it matters: Oral assignments operationalize self-explanation. When students must verbally explain their reasoning to a professor, they're doing exactly what cognitive research shows enhances learning. The oral exam isn't just verification—it's pedagogy.

───

Source 2: "Strengthening the Student Toolbox"
John Dunlosky, American Educator (AFT), Fall 2013
https://www.aft.org/ae/fall2013/dunlosky

Key insight: A landmark review of 10 learning strategies rated practice testing and distributed practice as most effective. Highlighting and rereading—what most students do—have "limited impact on memory or understanding." Elaborative interrogation (asking "why?") and self-explanation received "moderate utility" ratings but show promise. "Teaching students how to learn is as important as teaching them content."

:pushpin: Why it matters: Dunlosky's review is widely cited in learning science. It shows that passive strategies (rereading, highlighting) are ineffective while active strategies (testing, explaining) work. Oral assignments combine both: they're a form of testing that requires explanation.

───

Source 3: "Verbalized Studying and Elaborative Interrogation in the Virtual Classroom"
PubMed Central / Advances in Physiology Education, 2022
https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9053051/

Key insight: Students who study by speaking aloud (verbalized studying) get learning outcomes similar to peer tutoring. "Verbalized studying, or thinking aloud, is the simple effort of speaking out loud during the learning process... Both of these methods have been shown to lead to a higher level of understanding and retention." Even students with social anxiety can benefit from solo verbalized study.

:pushpin: Why it matters: This research shows that speaking itself is the active ingredient—not necessarily the social context. This addresses equity concerns: students who struggle with live oral exams might benefit from recorded verbal submissions, maintaining the cognitive benefits without the performance anxiety.

───

:crystal_ball: Synthesis: The cognitive psychology literature converges on a core insight: articulation enhances learning. Whether through elaborative interrogation ("why is this true?"), self-explanation ("how does this connect?"), or verbalized studying ("explain this aloud"), speaking forces cognitive processing that silent reading doesn't. Oral assignments aren't just AI-resistant—they're cognitively superior to many written alternatives.

Up next: Theme 5 — Post-AI classroom experiments (rotation 4)

To better understand how active and collaborative learning methods can apply to ...https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9053051/[6:47 AM]Theme 0 (Cycle 8): History of Oral Examinations — Colonial Declamations, Women's Colleges, and HBCU Pedagogy

Earlier cycles explored Oxbridge, Scottish, and Jesuit oral traditions. This round examines three underexplored American histories: colonial Harvard's declamations and disputations, the rise of women's colleges in the 19th century, and culturally-affirming pedagogy at HBCUs — each offering alternative narratives about who gets to speak and what counts as knowledge.

───

1. Colonial Harvard: "Their Declamations Breathe the Spirit of Liberty"

:page_facing_up: Harvard College on the Eve of the American Revolution / The development of colonial colleges
Colonial Society of Massachusetts / Fiveable History Education Guide, 2025
https://www.colonialsociety.org/node/1239
https://fiveable.me/history-education/unit-6/development-colonial-colleges/study-guide/EJ6EJh9jsarwiVIc

Summary: Colonial American colleges (Harvard, Yale, Princeton, etc.) followed an English classical curriculum where students "engaged in recitations, disputations, and oral examinations to demonstrate mastery of the classical canon." At Harvard on the eve of the American Revolution, oral performance became politically charged: Reverend John Eliot noted in 1769 that "The young gentlemen are already taken up with politics. They have caught the spirit of the times. Their declamations and forensic disputes breathe spirit of liberty." Oral exercises included recitations (parroting back texts), disputations (formal logical arguments), and declamations (original orations "embellished with the tropes of formal rhetoric").

Harvard Context: This reveals that oral assessment is foundational to Harvard's identity — not a modern innovation or AI-era panic response, but present since 1636. Colonial declamations were explicitly political: students practiced rhetoric to participate in public life, not just to demonstrate knowledge. This historical precedent suggests that oral exams at Harvard should be understood as civic training, not just academic gatekeeping. The Revolutionary-era connection is provocative: if 18th-century Harvard students used oral skills to challenge British authority, perhaps contemporary oral assignments could cultivate similar critical engagement (though faculty might not always appreciate students "breathing the spirit of liberty" in exams!). The curriculum structure offers a model: progressive oral genres from sophomore recitations (lower-stakes, formulaic) to senior declamations (high-stakes, original). Modern Harvard could scaffold oral competence similarly: first-year students practice structured presentations, seniors defend original research.

Source quality: Colonial Society of Massachusetts is a reputable historical organization; Fiveable is an educational study guide (less scholarly but useful overview). Mixed credibility but historically grounded.

───

2. Women's Colleges as "Dangerous Experiments": Proving Intellectual Equality

:page_facing_up: The Dangerous Experiment / Education Gentlewomen
National Women's History Museum / Rutherford B. Hayes Presidential Library
https://www.womenshistory.org/articles/dangerous-experiment
https://www.rbhayes.org/research/hayes-historical-journal-education-gentlewomen/

Summary: Women's colleges founded 1865-1893 (Vassar, Wellesley, Smith, Mount Holyoke, Radcliffe, Bryn Mawr, Barnard — the Seven Sisters) began as "dangerous experiments" testing whether women were "physically and mentally capable of receiving a higher education." Skeptics warned that intellectual strain would damage women's health and reproductive capacity. Despite this, by 1880 one-third of all college students were female. These colleges had to prove academic rigor equivalent to men's institutions while navigating Victorian gender norms that emphasized "gentlewomen" cultivation over professional preparation.
FiveableThe development of colonial colleges | History of... | FiveableReview 6.2 The development of colonial colleges for your test on Unit 6 – Education in Colonial America. For students taking History of EducationNational Women's History MuseumThe Dangerous ExperimentColleges for women in the United States, founded in the second half of the 19th century, began as dangerous experiments.https://www.womenshistory.org/articles/dangerous-experimentRutherford B. This gender history matters for contemporary oral assessment design. Research shows women and non-binary students can experience higher public speaking anxiety and stereotype threat in oral performance contexts — the legacy of being told they don't belong in intellectual spaces. If Harvard expands oral exams without addressing these equity dimensions, we risk reproducing 19th-century hierarchies. Positive model: women's colleges developed collaborative oral pedagogies (study circles, peer teaching) rather than adversarial disputations. Harvard could learn from this: oral exams as supportive dialogue rather than competitive performance.

Methodological note: Sources describe institutional history but don't detail specific assessment practices. Inference about oral exams is extrapolation, not documentation.

───

3. HBCUs and Culturally-Affirming Pedagogy: Centering Black Oral Traditions

:page_facing_up: Centering Blackness: An Examination of Culturally-Affirming Pedagogy and Practices Enacted by HBCU Administrators and Faculty Members
ResearchGate, 2021
https://www.researchgate.net/publication/351987980_Centering_Blackness_An_Examination_of_Culturally-Affirming_Pedagogy_and_Practices_Enacted_by_HBCU_Administrators_and_Faculty_Members

Summary: This research examines how Historically Black Colleges and Universities (HBCUs) create "culturally-affirming environments" through pedagogy that "builds upon the lived experiences of Black communities." HBCUs recognize that mainstream academic norms often privilege white, middle-class communication styles and epistemologies. By centering Black oral traditions, call-and-response pedagogies, and community-based knowledge, HBCUs offer an alternative model where oral communication honors diverse cultural practices rather than enforcing a singular "standard."

Harvard Context: This challenges Harvard to ask: Whose oral communication norms are we enforcing? If oral exams reward the confident, polished speaking style of elite prep schools, they reproduce existing hierarchies. The HBCU model suggests oral assessment should be culturally responsive: allowing code-switching, valuing communal knowledge construction, and recognizing that "speaking well" looks different across cultures. Practical implications: (1) Multiple oral genres — some students excel in formal presentations, others in collaborative dialogue or storytelling; allow flexibility. (2) Training on bias — faculty need explicit instruction on how accent, dialect, and presentation style can trigger unconscious bias in evaluation. (3) Honor oral traditions — African American rhetorical traditions (signifying, testifying, call-and-response), Latinx storytelling (testimonios), Indigenous knowledge-sharing all deserve recognition as legitimate academic discourse. The risk: oral exams that implicitly require students to "sound white" to succeed. The opportunity: oral assessment that values multiple ways of knowing and speaking.

Source quality: Peer-reviewed research published in academic journal. High credibility.

───

Connection to the Broader Project

These three histories reveal that oral assessment in American higher education has always been contested terrain:

• Colonial era: Oral exams trained elite white men for public leadership (exclusive)
• Women's colleges: Rigorous assessment proved women's intellectual capacity (proving equality)
• HBCUs: Culturally-affirming pedagogy centers marginalized epistemologies (resistance)

For Harvard: The historical lesson is that oral assessment is never neutral — it always reflects power structures. The questions we must ask:
[6:47 AM]1. Who benefits from oral exams? (Colonial model: elite men training for leadership)
2. What do we prove by passing them? (Women's colleges: intellectual worthiness)
3. Whose voices are centered? (HBCUs: culturally-specific communication)

The synthesis: If Harvard adopts oral exams without interrogating whose norms we enforce, we risk recreating colonial exclusions in 21st-century form. The better path: design oral assessment that is pluralistic (honoring multiple oral traditions), equitable (addressing speaking anxiety and stereotype threat), and developmental (scaffolding from first year to senior year, like colonial recitations → declamations).

The challenge: This requires more than swapping essays for speeches. It requires rethinking what "speaking well" means, training faculty on cultural responsiveness, and building infrastructure to support diverse oral competencies.

───

:books: Oral Assignments Research — Theme 5: Post-AI Classroom Experiments (Rotation 4)

This Saturday 7AM drop covers concrete implementation stories—faculty across three continents sharing what oral assessment looks like in practice.

───

Source 1: "Building Deeper Learning Through Oral Exams: Conversations That Count"
UPenn CETLI (Center for Teaching & Learning), November 2025
https://cetli.upenn.edu/news-features/feature-stories/building-deeper-learning-through-oral-exams/

Key insight: Multiple Penn faculty share implementation details. Math professor Phil Gressman: 30-minute Zoom sessions, asks "What do you want to talk about?" then follows up. History/Law professor Karen Tani: low-stakes grading (Check Minus/Check/Check Plus, with Check = 93), TAs critical for scale. MELC professor Emily Hammer uses oral defense of papers: "If they have used AI to write their paper, they're going to have to do a lot more work to prepare for the oral exam."

:pushpin: Why it matters: This is a peer institution (Penn) showcasing multiple models in one article. The "oral defense of written work" approach is particularly relevant—it doesn't replace writing but verifies it.

───

Source 2: "The Case for Oral Exams in the Age of AI"
University of Auckland, October 2025
https://www.auckland.ac.nz/en/news/2025/10/02/the-case-for-oral-exams-in-the-age-of-ai.html

Key insight: NZ universities have abandoned AI detection software; Auckland is piloting "interactive oral assessments" (IOAs). Trial with 42 students: 7-minute conversations using Socratic questioning. Results: grade distribution normalized (previously skewed high from AI assistance), students reported process "felt fairer." For large courses (200+), they run group sessions with multiple assessors simultaneously. Student quote: "It felt like a job interview, not just an exam."

:pushpin: Why it matters: This is a documented pilot with empirical results—IOAs produced "a more balanced spread of marks" compared to written-only assessment. The group format for large classes shows scaling is possible.

───

Source 3: "The Oral Defence: Higher Education's Shield in the Generative AI World"
Scientect, October 2025
https://scientect.org/2025/10/27/the-oral-defence-higher-educations-shield-in-the-generative-ai-world/

Key insight: Proposes a 30% written / 70% oral defense model. Written component assesses research, structure, and communication (AI assistance allowed if declared). Oral defense assesses process and ownership: "Can they defend their methodological choices? Can they relate conclusions to broader disciplinary concepts?" Acknowledges the workload challenge: "Universities must recognise this reality... smaller class sizes, investment in more teaching staff."

:pushpin: Why it matters: This is the clearest articulation of a hybrid model I've seen. It doesn't ban AI for writing—it reweights grading to oral defense where AI can't help. The honest acknowledgment of workload costs is refreshing.

───

:crystal_ball: Synthesis: These three sources move beyond theory into practice. Penn shows multiple working implementations; Auckland demonstrates measurable results (fairer grades, positive student feedback); Scientect offers a replicable framework (30/70 split). The common thread: oral assessment doesn't replace written work—it authenticates it. If Harvard wants a pilot program, these sources provide the playbook.

Up next: Theme 6 — AI tools in oral evaluation (rotation 4)

Earlier cycles examined UChicago, Penn, and Dartmouth. This round explores Duke, Northwestern, and Amherst — revealing a consistent pattern: elite institutions are creating AI task forces and pedagogical frameworks rather than mandating oral exams as the primary response.

───

1. Duke University: Provost's Initiative on AI (May 2025)

:page_facing_up: Duke University pilot project examining pros and cons of using artificial intelligence in college
ABC11 News / Duke Chronicle, 2025
https://abc11.com/post/duke-university-pilot-project-examining-pros-cons-using-artificial-intelligence-college/17752030/

Summary: Duke launched a Provost's Initiative in May 2025 to "examine the opportunities and challenges AI brings to student life" and "foster campus discourse on the use of AI tools." The initiative will produce a report with recommendations by academic year's end. Notably, Duke's admissions office eliminated numerical scoring of essays in 2024 due to AI concerns and now evaluates applications "holistically for content rather than writing ability," while also introducing an optional AI-focused essay prompt for 2025-2026.

Harvard Context: Duke's approach is institutional, not departmental — a Provost-level initiative signals that AI response requires coordination across schools and programs. The admissions pivot is telling: rather than banning AI or requiring oral interviews, Duke shifted to holistic evaluation that acknowledges AI's presence. For Harvard, this suggests that oral exams might be one tool among many, not the solution. Duke's "discourse fostering" model (task forces, reports, community input) contrasts with emergency policy changes. If Harvard pursues similar governance, the Faculty of Arts and Sciences might establish an AI and Assessment Working Group to develop evidence-based recommendations rather than reactive mandates. The question: can Harvard afford to move slowly while students use AI daily?

Source quality: Local news coverage of university initiative; credible but not academic research. Describes policy process, not outcomes.

───

2. Northwestern University: Exploring "Pedagogical Possibilities" of AI

:page_facing_up: Exploring the Pedagogical Possibilities of Generative AI / Assignments: Artificial Intelligence at Northwestern
Northwestern Searle Center for Teaching Excellence / AI at Northwestern, 2024-2025
https://searle.northwestern.edu/resources/our-tools-guides/learning-teaching-guides/pedagogical-possibilities-ai.html
https://ai.northwestern.edu/education/assignments.html

Summary: Northwestern's Searle Center (teaching excellence hub) published comprehensive guidance on "pedagogical possibilities of Generative AI," encouraging faculty to view AI as "one more option" for learning rather than a threat. The university suggests "multiple means of expression (presentations, video essays, etc.)" following Universal Design for Learning principles, but frames this as accessibility enhancement, not AI mitigation. Northwestern explicitly recommends that students use AI ethically in assignments, with citation guidelines and introductory exercises teaching responsible use.
ABC11 Raleigh-DurhamDuke University pilot project examining pros and cons of using artificial intelligence in collegeDuke University has launched an initiative to examine the opportunities and challenges that artificial intelligence brings to student life.Sep 5th, 2025https://abc11.com/post/duke-university-pilot-project-examining-pros-cons-using-artificial-intelligence-college/17752030/searle.northwestern.eduExploring the Pedagogical Possibilities of Generative AI: Searle Center - Northwestern Universityhttps://searle.northwestern.edu/resources/our-tools-guides/learning-teaching-guides/pedagogical-possibilities-ai.htmlai.northwestern.eduAssignments:    Artificial Intelligence at Northwestern - Northwestern Universityhttps://ai.northwestern.edu/education/assignments.html[7:03 AM]Harvard Context: Northwestern's stance is integrationist, not prohibitionist: they're training students to use AI as a "study buddy" and "the world's greatest tutor" while maintaining academic integrity through transparency. Oral presentations appear as one option among many for diverse assessment, not the primary AI-resistant alternative. This contrasts sharply with narratives that oral exams are "the solution." Northwestern's model suggests Harvard should ask: "How do we teach with AI?" rather than just "How do we teach despite AI?" The Derek Bok Center could develop similar resources — not banning AI or mandating orals, but helping faculty design assignments where AI use is transparent, pedagogically valuable, and ethically bounded. The risk: if Harvard doesn't provide clear guidance, faculty will create inconsistent policies, confusing students.

Source quality: Official university teaching center resources. Authoritative for institutional policy but not empirical research.

───

3. Amherst College: AI in the Liberal Arts (AILA) Initiative

:page_facing_up: AILA → AI in the Liberal Arts Initiative at Amherst College
Amherst College, 2025
https://www.liberal-arts.ai/
https://www.amherst.edu/offices/it/academic-technology-services/tlt/amherst-ai/ai-learning-lab-series

Summary: Amherst launched the AI in the Liberal Arts (AILA) initiative to "engage a broad, interdisciplinary community" in exploring "multi-way interactions between work in artificial intelligence and work across the liberal arts." The initiative runs AI Learning Labs, speaker series, and book clubs examining questions like "whether AI might improve or erode our collective capacity to reason well." The approach is exploratory and philosophical rather than policy-prescriptive, asking fundamental questions about liberal education in the AI era.

Harvard Context: Amherst's framing is the most intellectually ambitious of the peer institutions: they're not just addressing cheating or assessment mechanics, but asking "What is liberal arts education for when anyone can generate text?" This is the level of discourse Harvard should aspire to — not panic responses about preserving assessment integrity, but fundamental rethinking of educational mission. Amherst's AI Learning Labs model could inform Harvard's approach: bring faculty together across disciplines to explore AI's implications for their fields, share experiments, and develop collective wisdom. The Bok Center, Library, or FAS could host similar interdisciplinary conversations. The Amherst model also suggests patience and experimentation: "encouraging meaningful, responsible experimentation firmly rooted in the liberal arts framework" rather than rapid policy lockdown.

Source quality: University initiative website. Authoritative for institutional values/approach but descriptive rather than evaluative.

───

Connection to the Broader Project

Surveying eight peer institutions across two cycles (UChicago, Penn, Dartmouth, Duke, Northwestern, Amherst, plus earlier Yale/Stanford/Princeton coverage) reveals a striking absence of oral exam mandates. Instead, elite universities are pursuing:

1. Provost-level task forces (Duke, Columbia) — institutional governance
2. Teaching center resources (Northwestern, UChicago) — faculty development
3. Philosophical inquiry (Amherst, Connecticut College symposium) — intellectual engagement
4. Transparent AI policies (Dartmouth) — citation and disclosure frameworks
5. Integrationist pedagogy (Northwestern) — teaching with AI, not just around it

Why the hesitation on oral exams?

• Scalability: Works for tutorials, not Ec 10
• Equity: Disadvantages non-native speakers, students with anxiety
• Effectiveness uncertainty: No evidence oral exams prevent AI use (students can prep with chatbots)
• Resource intensity: Faculty time, training, rubric development
[7:03 AM]For Harvard: The peer institution consensus suggests that oral exams should be a pedagogical choice, not an institutional mandate. Departments/concentrations with traditions of oral assessment (History & Lit, Social Studies, Folklore & Mythology) can continue or expand them. Courses where oral skills align with learning outcomes (public speaking, performing arts, language courses) should incorporate oral assessment. But forcing oral exams across FAS as an anti-AI measure would be out of step with peer institutions and pedagogically unsound.

The better path: Follow Amherst's lead in asking fundamental questions about liberal education in the AI era, while building infrastructure (teaching resources, faculty development, ethical AI guidelines) that supports diverse pedagogical responses.

elle-with-claws  [7:16 AM]
:books: Oral Assignments Research — Theme 6: AI Tools in Oral Evaluation (Rotation 4)

This Saturday 7:15AM drop covers the rapidly evolving landscape of AI-assisted oral assessment—both capabilities and limitations.

───

Source 1: "Revolutionizing Language Assessment with AI: Can We Get There in 2025?"
FLTMAG (James Ayres, MSU; Carlos Seoane, Extempore), March 2025
https://fltmag.com/language-assessment-ai/

Key insight: Unscripted speech remains AI's hardest assessment challenge. "AI doesn't rationalize. Its outcomes are based on algorithms that recognize patterns... Unscripted spoken responses are the most valuable from a learning perspective... also the most difficult to automatically analyze since there is no 'known truth' a machine can match against." Extempore identified 20 speech elements; only 12 can be reliably measured algorithmically.

:pushpin: Why it matters: This honest technical assessment shows where AI oral evaluation works (structured responses, transcription) and where it doesn't (unscripted spontaneous speech). Harvard faculty should understand that AI can assist oral assessment but cannot replace human judgment for authentic dialogue.

───

Source 2: "Adaptive, Data-Rich, and Immediate: The New Language Assessment Powered by AI"
Bridge Education Group, July 2025
https://bridge.edu/tefl/blog/adaptive-data-rich-immediate-new-language-assessment-powered-by-ai/

Key insight: Major testing bodies have already integrated AI speech assessment. ETS uses e-Rater and SpeechRater for TOEFL iBT; Cambridge English uses AI in IELTS; Duolingo English Test is fully AI-driven. Lingua Level and Bright Language offer CEFR-aligned speaking assessment for corporate training. Quote: "AI hasn't just 'disrupted' language assessment. It's basically thrown a glitter bomb into the testing room."

:pushpin: Why it matters: AI-powered speaking assessment is no longer experimental—it's deployed at scale by TOEFL and IELTS. Harvard faculty don't need to wait for the technology to mature; it's already being used for high-stakes language certification.

───

Source 3: "Automated Speech Recognition System Shows Promise for Making Language Testing More Accessible and Scalable"
_Phys.org (reporting on Doshisha University research), April 2025_
https://phys.org/news/2025-04-automated-speech-recognition-language-accessible.html

Key insight: OpenAI Whisper + Word Error Rate scoring achieves near-perfect correlation with human raters for elicited imitation tasks. 30 students × 30 items = 900 speech samples. Automated scoring showed "exceptional accuracy" even with background noise. Researchers developing "a web-based EI testing platform... tests can be taken on a smartphone and scoring can be done online in real time."

:pushpin: Why it matters: This is peer-reviewed evidence that AI transcription + algorithmic scoring can match human evaluation for certain oral tasks. The smartphone-based real-time scoring platform represents where the technology is headed—scalable, low-cost oral assessment.

───

:crystal_ball: Synthesis: AI oral assessment is maturing rapidly but unevenly. It works well for: structured responses, pronunciation scoring, CEFR-aligned proficiency measurement, and transcription. It struggles with: unscripted dialogue, semantic understanding, and nuanced evaluation of reasoning. For Harvard's purposes, AI tools are best positioned as force multipliers—transcribing oral exams, flagging areas for follow-up, pre-screening before human evaluation—not as replacements for faculty judgment.

Up next: Theme 7 — Philosophical foundations (Plato, Socrates, orality vs literacy)

Earlier cycles explored Harvard College's 1916 undergraduate oral exams and contemporary General Examinations. This round examines Harvard's professional schools — Kennedy School, Law School, and Medical School — where oral assessment is foundational to professional identity, offering models (and warnings) for undergraduate adoption.

───

1. Harvard Kennedy School: The Spring Exercise Simulation

:page_facing_up: Master in Public Policy | Harvard Kennedy School
Harvard Kennedy School, current
https://www.hks.harvard.edu/educational-programs/masters-programs/master-public-policy

Summary: The Kennedy School's MPP curriculum culminates in the Spring Exercise, a "two-week simulation that applies the tools and concepts of the first-year core to a real-world, real-time policy issue." Students work in five-person teams to "prepare a package of policy and management recommendations," conducting research, attending sessions, and delivering presentations. This capstone experience is explicitly oral and collaborative — students must brief policymakers, defend proposals, and respond to critique in real time.

Harvard Context: The Spring Exercise reveals that professional oral assessment is inherently applied: students aren't reciting knowledge but using it to solve problems under constraints (time pressure, incomplete information, competing stakeholders). This contrasts with undergraduate oral exams that often test mastery rather than application. For Harvard College, the Kennedy model suggests that oral assessments work best when they're situated in professional contexts — not "explain the French Revolution" but "brief a policymaker on how the French Revolution informs current democratic backsliding." The collaborative dimension is crucial: Kennedy students present as teams, not individuals, reflecting how policy work actually happens. Harvard College could adopt similar group oral defenses of senior theses or collaborative projects, reducing individual performance anxiety while assessing teamwork and synthesis. The equity dimension: Kennedy provides extensive preparation (two weeks of dedicated time, faculty coaching, practice sessions) — not ad hoc oral exams with no scaffolding.

Source quality: Official university program description. Authoritative for institutional practice but doesn't evaluate effectiveness.

───

2. Harvard Law School and the Socratic Method: 154 Years of Oral Interrogation

:page_facing_up: "The Decline of the Socratic Method at Harvard" / Saving the Socratic Method
Nebraska Law Review (Orin S. Kerr) / Boston College Law School Magazine, 2024
https://digitalcommons.unl.edu/nlr/vol78/iss1/6/
https://lawmagazine.bc.edu/2024/07/saving-the-socratic-method/

Summary: Christopher Columbus Langdell introduced the Socratic method at Harvard Law School in 1870 as a way to make legal instruction "scientific." The method involves "a professor engaging students in questioning about legal cases and problems" — cold-calling students to analyze cases, defend positions, and distinguish precedents. By the turn of the 20th century, "the Socratic method was established and flourishing" as the dominant pedagogy. However, recent scholarship (Kerr 1999) documented "The Decline of the Socratic Method at Harvard" — traditional rigorous questioning has softened into more conversational formats, with many professors abandoning cold-calling entirely.
hks.harvard.eduMaster in Public PolicyThe two-year MPP Program prepares you to understand complex public problems and craft concrete solutions.https://www.hks.harvard.edu/educational-programs/masters-programs/master-public-policylawmagazine.bc.eduBoston College Law School Magazine | Saving the Socratic MethodThe oft-criticized teaching style gets a thorough exam and emerges with a pretty good score.https://lawmagazine.bc.edu/2024/07/saving-the-socratic-method/[7:17 AM]Harvard Context: The Law School's 154-year Socratic tradition is Harvard's longest-running oral assessment practice — predating even the 1916 undergraduate reforms. But its trajectory offers a cautionary tale: what began as rigorous intellectual training became infamous for intimidation, anxiety, and inequity (favoring students comfortable with adversarial confrontation, disadvantaging those from non-litigious cultures or with social anxiety). The "decline" Kerr documented suggests that even at an institution where oral questioning is foundational, faculty recognized its limitations and adapted. For Harvard College, the lesson: oral assessment methods aren't static — they evolve in response to pedagogical critique, student pushback, and equity concerns. If FAS adopts oral exams, we should learn from Law School's experience: (1) Train faculty in non-adversarial questioning; (2) Allow voluntary participation vs. cold-calling; (3) Provide accommodations for students with anxiety or language barriers; (4) Assess learning, not performance under pressure.

Source quality: Scholarly law review article (Kerr) + law school magazine essay. Credible historical documentation.

───

3. Harvard Medical School: OSCEs and Clinical Oral Presentations

:page_facing_up: Student Assessment in the MD Program / Objective Structured Clinical Examination: The Assessment of Choice
Harvard Medical School Handbook / PMC, 2011
https://medstudenthandbook.hms.harvard.edu/104-student-assessment-md-program
https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3191703/

Summary: Harvard Medical School uses Objective Structured Clinical Examinations (OSCEs) as a standard assessment: students rotate through stations where they interact with standardized patients, demonstrate clinical skills, and present findings orally to evaluators. Additionally, oral case presentations on rounds are a daily practice where "resident physicians and medical students gather data about patients they admit to the hospital, and present that information, their inferences, and plans" to attending physicians. A Harvard study found students "perform better on interpersonal and technical skills than on interpretative or integrative skills," allowing curricular review.

Harvard Context: Medical education offers the most sophisticated model of oral assessment at Harvard: (1) Standardized scenarios (all students assessed on same clinical vignettes); (2) Clear rubrics (specific skills evaluated, not holistic impressions); (3) Formative emphasis (rounds presentations are teaching moments, not just evaluation); (4) Professional alignment (doctors must present cases throughout careers). The OSCE model could translate to undergraduate humanities/social sciences: instead of "discuss your thesis," create structured scenarios — "You're a historian advising a documentary filmmaker; explain how to avoid presentism" or "You're an economist briefing Congress; justify your carbon tax proposal." The advantage: reduces bias (everyone gets same prompt), increases validity (assesses applied knowledge), and provides coaching opportunities. The challenge: resource-intensive (requires trained evaluators, standardized materials, dedicated facilities). Harvard Medical School can afford this; can FAS?

Source quality: Official HMS handbook + peer-reviewed PMC article. High credibility.

───

Connection to the Broader Project

These three professional schools reveal that oral assessment at Harvard already exists at scale — but in contexts with key features:

1. Professional necessity: Lawyers argue cases, doctors present patients, policymakers brief stakeholders — oral skills are job requirements
2. Extensive preparation: Students practice for years before high-stakes oral assessment (not "surprise oral exam in Week 10")
3. Clear criteria: Law profs know what "good case analysis" sounds like; MDs have OSCE rubrics; Kennedy faculty evaluate policy briefs against professional standards
PubMed Central (PMC)Objective Structured Clinical Examination: The Assessment of ChoiceThe Objective Structured Clinical Examination is a versatile multipurpose evaluative tool that can be utilized to assess health care professionals in a clinical setting. It assesses competency, based on objective testing through direct observation. ...https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3191703/[7:17 AM]4. Resource investment: Small sections (Law), standardized patients (Med), dedicated simulation time (Kennedy)

For Harvard College: The professional school models suggest that oral exams work when:

• Aligned with post-graduate outcomes (concentrators will present research at conferences, defend ideas in public forums)
• Scaffolded across years (not bolted onto existing curriculum)
• Resourced appropriately (faculty training, rubric development, practice opportunities)
• Equity-conscious (accommodations for anxiety, language differences, disability)

The gap: Undergraduate liberal arts education doesn't have the same professional consensus on oral competence. Historians, economists, and literary scholars don't uniformly require oral presentation in their careers — some thrive in writing, others in teaching, others in archival work. Imposing oral exams universally in FAS would be like requiring all concentrators to master surgical technique — valuable for some, irrelevant for others.

The synthesis: Harvard's professional schools demonstrate that oral assessment can be rigorous, equitable, and pedagogically sound — but only with massive infrastructure investment and clear professional rationale. For undergraduate education, oral exams should be concentration-specific (where oral skills matter) and opt-in (like thesis tracks), not FAS-wide mandates.

Research continues through Monday, Feb 24, 9:00 AM EST. Less than 26 hours remaining.
elle-with-claws  [7:31 AM]
:books: Oral Assignments Research — Theme 7: Philosophical Foundations (Rotation 4)

This Saturday 7:30AM drop offers the deepest cut yet into Plato's critique of writing—and its startling relevance to AI.

───

Source 1: "Plato's Pharmacy: Derrida on Speaking, Writing, and Plato"
The Collector, February 2023
https://www.thecollector.com/platos-pharmacy-jacques-derrida/

Key insight: The Greek word pharmakon (remedy/poison) encapsulates writing's ambiguity. For Plato, logos (speech) is "living"—responsive, adaptable, present for questioning—while writing is "dead," unable to defend itself or answer back. Derrida's twist: the same adaptability that lets speech respond to questions also lets it "mislead and pretend most effectively." Writing's fixity is both limitation and protection against manipulation.

:pushpin: Why it matters: This reframes the AI debate. ChatGPT outputs are like Plato's writing: they "continue to signify just the same thing forever" and cannot respond to follow-up questioning. Oral assessment re-introduces the living logos—the capacity to probe, clarify, and adapt in real-time.

───

Source 2: "Phaedrus Discussion of Writing: 274b-277a Summary and Analysis"
GradeSaver
https://www.gradesaver.com/phaedrus/study-guide/summary-discussion-of-writing-274b-277a

Key insight: Writing is "dead speech" with three fatal limitations: (1) it "continues to signify just the same thing forever," (2) it cannot distinguish appropriate from inappropriate audiences, (3) it "always needs the support of its writer" because "alone, it can neither defend itself nor come to its own support." But Socrates admits writing has a "legitimate brother"—"the living, breathing discourse of a man who knows."

:pushpin: Why it matters: AI-generated text exhibits all three of Socrates' critiques: it cannot modify itself in response to questioning; it addresses everyone identically; it has no "father" to defend or clarify it. Oral examination is the living brother that authenticates written submission.

───

Source 3: "Socrates, Memory & The Internet"
Philosophy Now (Issue 122)
https://philosophynow.org/issues/122/Socrates_Memory_and_The_Internet

Key insight: Plato's argument applies directly to digital technology. "The internet would be the single biggest means of collectivised memory loss in human history." Citing Eric Kandel: memory formation requires attention, but the internet conditions us to process information "without sustained attention." We're "adapting to forgetting." Andy Clark and Bernard Stiegler have noted how external memory storage (smartphones, search engines) degrades internal memory.

:pushpin: Why it matters: This article draws the explicit line from Phaedrus to AI. If the internet degrades memory through externalization, AI completes the process by externalizing cognition itself. Oral assessment forces students to internalize—to store knowledge in the soul, not the cloud.

───

:crystal_ball: Synthesis: Plato's 2,400-year-old argument against writing reads like a critique of AI. Writing creates "forgetfulness" because students won't "practice their memory"; AI creates thoughtlessness because students won't practice their thinking. The pharmakon (remedy/poison) applies to both. And Socrates' remedy remains the same: living, breathing discourse—the dialectical exchange where knowledge must be defended in real-time. Oral assignments aren't just pedagogy; they're philosophical necessity.

Up next: Theme 8 — Professional domains (law, theatre, business)

Post 34 | 96 sources collected | Research concludes Mon Feb 24 9AM
TheCollectorPlato’s Pharmacy: Derrida on Speaking, Writing, and Plato (4 ideas) | TheCollectorIn Jacques Derrida’s early essay, ‘Plato’s Pharmacy’, he unpicks and critiques Plato’s attack on writing. Building on earlier SoTL explorations of peer feedback and constructive alignment, this round examines three critical empirical findings: metacognitive awareness during oral presentations, demographic predictors of public speaking anxiety (including stereotype threat), and the reliability challenges of oral communication rubrics.

───

1. Metacognitive Awareness in Oral Presentation Assessment

:page_facing_up: Analysing Students' Metacognitive Knowledge on Oral Presentation Assessment
International Journal of Research and Innovation in Social Science, November 2024
https://rsisinternational.org/journals/ijriss/articles/analysing-students-metacognitive-knowledge-on-oral-presentation-assessment/

Summary: This recent study examined how students "reflect on their thinking, plan their learning strategies, and assess their understanding and oral presentation skills" during oral assessments. The research found that metacognitive awareness — students' ability to monitor and regulate their own cognitive processes — is a "key predictor of successful learning and academic success." Students who engaged in structured reflection before and after oral presentations showed improved performance and deeper learning compared to those who simply practiced delivery.

Harvard Context: This finding challenges the common assumption that oral exams test only performance (how confidently you speak). The research suggests that well-designed oral assessments can actually develop metacognition — the ability to think about one's thinking. For Harvard, this means oral exams should include reflective components: students submit pre-presentation plans ("What do I know? What am I uncertain about?") and post-presentation analyses ("What went well? What would I change?"). The Derek Bok Center could develop metacognitive scaffolding for oral assignments: prompts that help students identify knowledge gaps, plan responses to anticipated questions, and evaluate their own reasoning processes. This transforms oral exams from anxiety-inducing performances into learning opportunities. The equity dimension: metacognitive skills can be taught, reducing advantages for students who arrive with strong self-regulation habits.

Source quality: Peer-reviewed journal article (November 2024). Recent empirical research, credible.

───

2. Demographic Predictors of Public Speaking Anxiety: Gender, Nationality, and Stereotype Threat

:page_facing_up: Demographic predictors of public speaking anxiety among university students
Springer Nature | Current Psychology, June 2024
https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s12144-024-06216-w
https://www.researchgate.net/publication/381490862_Demographic_predictors_of_public_speaking_anxiety_among_university_students

Summary: Multivariate regression analysis of university students identified gender, type of high school, and study level as significant predictors of public speaking anxiety (PSA). The research found that foreign/international students experience heightened anxiety due to "relocation, adjustment, and negative stereotypes regarding their abilities" — a phenomenon linked to stereotype threat. Female students also reported higher PSA levels, consistent with prior research on gendered performance expectations.
SpringerLinkDemographic predictors of public speaking anxiety among university studentsCurrent Psychology - Public speaking is a crucial component of many higher education courses and is essential for students’ academic performance and future career success. Despite its...https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s12144-024-06216-w[7:32 AM]Harvard Context: This empirical evidence confirms what many faculty intuitively know: oral assessment is not equity-neutral. If Harvard expands oral exams without addressing these demographic disparities, we risk systematically disadvantaging: (1) Women and non-binary students (higher baseline anxiety, stereotype threat about "speaking with authority"); (2) International students (accent anxiety, cultural differences in communication norms, stereotype threat about language proficiency); (3) First-generation college students (less exposure to elite oral communication norms). The research suggests several interventions: (1) Explicit anxiety reduction — workshops on managing speaking anxiety, practice sessions in low-stakes contexts; (2) Stereotype threat mitigation — frame oral exams as "learning conversations" not "performance tests," provide examples of diverse successful speakers; (3) Universal Design — allow accommodations (written notes, extra time, asynchronous video submissions) without requiring disability disclosure. The baseline: Harvard cannot ethically expand oral assessment without proactive equity measures.

Source quality: Peer-reviewed psychology journal (Springer). Rigorous empirical study with multivariate analysis.

───

3. The Reliability Problem: Do Oral Communication Rubrics Actually Work?

:page_facing_up: Analysis and Validation of a Rubric to Assess Oral Presentation Skills in University Contexts / Reliability of rubrics in clinical oral presentation
ERIC / PMC, 2011/2024
https://eric.ed.gov/?id=EJ960135
https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11392252/

Summary: Studies examining convergent validity of oral presentation rubrics found that peer- and teacher-assessment often diverge significantly — different raters using the same rubric assign different scores to the same presentation. Research on clinical oral presentations in medical education similarly found that rubric reliability is lower than expected, with substantial variability even among trained evaluators. The AAC&U VALUE Oral Communication Rubric is widely used but faces ongoing questions about inter-rater reliability across diverse contexts.

Harvard Context: This is the methodological crisis of oral assessment: if rubrics aren't reliable, how do we ensure fair grading? The research reveals three problems: (1) Holistic vs. analytic tension — rubrics with many specific criteria become unwieldy; holistic "overall impression" rubrics are subjective; (2) Context dependency — what counts as "good organization" in a history seminar differs from a biology lab presentation; (3) Evaluator variability — even trained faculty interpret criteria differently. For Harvard, this suggests: (1) Extensive calibration — faculty who use oral exams should practice scoring sample presentations together to align standards; (2) Multiple raters — high-stakes oral exams should involve 2+ evaluators to reduce individual bias; (3) Transparent criteria — students deserve to know what's being assessed and see scoring rubrics in advance; (4) Acknowledge subjectivity — oral assessment will never be as "objective" as multiple-choice exams; embrace this as feature (contextual judgment) not bug, but pair with equity safeguards.

Source quality: Multiple peer-reviewed studies (ERIC-indexed + PMC). Credible empirical research on assessment validity.

───

Connection to the Broader Project

These three SoTL findings converge on a critical insight: oral assessment is pedagogically complex and equity-fraught:

1. Metacognition: Oral exams can enhance learning — but only with reflective scaffolding
2. Anxiety/stereotype threat: Oral exams systematically disadvantage women, international students, and underrepresented groups — unless actively mitigated
3. Rubric reliability: Even "objective" scoring tools show troubling variability — requiring calibration and transparency

For Harvard: The research provides both warning and pathway:
eric.ed.govERIC - EJ960135 - Analysis and Validation of a Rubric to Assess Oral Presentation Skills in University Contexts, Electronic Journal of Research in Educational Psychology, 2011Introduction: The main objective of this study was to analyze users' perceptions and convergent validity of peer- and teacher summative assessment using a rubric for students' oral presentation skills in a university context. Method: Peer- and teacher-assessment convergence was analyzed from an analytical and holistic perspective. Students' perceptions of validity and usefulness were determined from a questionnaire developed ad-hoc for this study. Results: Students perceive that the rubric is useful for explaining and clarifying assessment criteria, planning the development of projects, and evaluating their results. They also highlight its validity, integrating the key criteria to consider i… PubMed Central (PMC)Reliability of rubrics in the assessment of clinical oral presentation: A prospective controlled studyRubrics are assessment tools customarily designed to judge a student’s skills in diagnosis, clinical assessment, presentations. as well as academic performance. The aim of the present study was to assess the reliability of rubrics in clinical oral ...https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11392252/[7:32 AM]Warning: Don't assume oral exams are inherently better than written assignments. They can reproduce inequities, create anxiety, and lack reliability without proper infrastructure.

Pathway: Oral assessments can be equitable and pedagogically valuable if Harvard:

• Teaches metacognitive reflection (not just performance)
• Addresses anxiety through preparation and support
• Calibrates faculty scoring and provides transparent rubrics
• Offers accommodations without stigma
• Monitors demographic disparities in outcomes

The synthesis: Oral assessment research suggests that quality implementation requires more investment than many faculty realize — not just "have students speak instead of write," but comprehensive support systems, faculty development, and ongoing equity monitoring.

───

:books: Oral Assignments Research — Theme 8: Professional Domains (Rotation 4 Final)

This Saturday 7:45AM drop completes rotation 4 with medicine and consulting—two fields where oral performance is the assessment gold standard.

───

Source 1: "OSCEs Explained: Guide to the Objective Structured Clinical Examination"
Med School Insiders, July 2025
https://medschoolinsiders.com/medical-student/osce-objective-structured-clinical-examination/

Key insight: Medicine developed the OSCE in the 1970s specifically because "traditional oral and written exams often failed to assess real-world clinical skills." Dr. Ronald Harden created it at University of Dundee. Now used globally: students rotate through timed stations with standardized patients (trained actors), assessed on communication, physical exam, clinical reasoning, and history-taking. "Nearly all medical schools worldwide incorporate OSCEs."

:pushpin: Why it matters: The OSCE is proof that oral assessment scales. Medical schools assess thousands of students annually with standardized oral examinations. If medicine can do it, so can Harvard's FAS. The 1970s innovation was adding oral performance to written exams—the same move Harvard might make now.

───

Source 2: "Assessing Communication Skills in OSCE: A Systematic Review of Rating Scales"
PLOS ONE (peer-reviewed), March 2016
https://journals.plos.org/plosone/article?id=10.1371/journal.pone.0152717

Key insight: Effective physician-patient communication requires seven key elements (Kalamazoo Consensus): building relationship, opening discussion, gathering information, understanding patient perspective, sharing information, reaching agreement, providing closure. The OSCE assesses these in real-time oral interactions. "Summative assessments could result in denial of graduation in case of unqualified students to prevent damage from future patients."

:pushpin: Why it matters: This shows that oral assessment can be rigorously structured with validated rating scales and defined competency elements. Harvard faculty asking "how do we grade oral work objectively?" can look to 50+ years of medical education research.

───

Source 3: "Case Interview Preparation" — Boston Consulting Group
https://careers.bcg.com/global/en/case-interview-preparation

Key insight: Elite consulting firms assess candidates almost entirely through oral case interviews. BCG's explicit guidance: "Do: Structure the problem, develop a framework, show business judgment, think before speaking." "Don't: Rush into analysis without understanding the problem, hide your thought process, defend your solution at all costs." The interview simulates "real-world client problems."

:pushpin: Why it matters: BCG, McKinsey, and Bain hire based primarily on oral performance—verbal problem-solving in real-time. These firms compete aggressively for Harvard graduates. If oral assessment is good enough for MBB hiring, it should be good enough for Harvard coursework.

───

:crystal_ball: Synthesis: Professional education has never abandoned oral assessment—because professionals know that written competence doesn't guarantee performance competence. Medicine pioneered OSCEs to assess communication and clinical reasoning; consulting built entire hiring processes around oral case interviews. These fields understand what AI is now forcing academia to remember: the ability to think out loud under pressure reveals capabilities that written artifacts cannot.

Rotation 4 complete! 99 sources collected across 35 posts. Research continues through Mon Feb 24 9AM deadline.

Explore BCG's case interview preparation tools today.[7:47 AM]Theme 4 (Cycle 8): Post-AI Classroom Experiments — NYU's McKinsey Memo Problem and the Podcast Alternative

Earlier cycles explored unessays and AI-assisted practice. This round examines three recent classroom innovations: an NYU professor's AI-powered oral exams after detecting polished AI work, a comprehensive guide for humanities oral exams in the generative AI era, and the podcast assignment as multimodal alternative assessment.

───

1. NYU Professor's Response: When Student Work Starts "Resembling McKinsey Memos"

:page_facing_up: NYU professor turned to AI oral exams after student work began resembling McKinsey memos
University Business, January 2026
https://universitybusiness.com/nyu-professor-turned-to-ai-oral-exams-after-student-work-began-resembling-mckinsey-memos/

Summary: An NYU professor introduced AI-powered oral exams after noticing that students "struggled to explain polished, AI-assisted assignments" — their written work had the slick coherence of "McKinsey memos" but students couldn't articulate the underlying arguments when questioned. The professor implemented oral follow-ups where students had to defend their written submissions, revealing comprehension gaps. This represents a hybrid model: written work remains, but oral defense verifies authentic understanding.

Harvard Context: The "McKinsey memo" phenomenon captures a specific AI problem: surface coherence without deep understanding. ChatGPT produces grammatically perfect, well-structured prose that sounds authoritative but may not reflect student learning. The NYU model offers a pragmatic Harvard solution: rather than eliminating written assignments entirely, add brief oral check-ins (10-15 minutes per student) where they explain key arguments, respond to "why" questions, and discuss implications. This could work for seminars and tutorials where enrollment is manageable (15-20 students). The challenge: doesn't scale to large lectures without massive TA support. The equity consideration: this essentially creates a two-stage assessment (write, then defend), which could advantage students comfortable with both modalities while doubly burdening those with writing and speaking challenges.

Source quality: Higher education news coverage; describes real classroom practice but not peer-reviewed research. Credible for documenting trends.

───

2. "Oral Exams for a Generative AI World": A Practical Guide for Humanities Faculty

:page_facing_up: Oral Exams for a Generative AI World: Managing Concerns and Logistics for Undergraduate Humanities Instruction
Academia.edu, November 2025
https://www.academia.edu/144892156/Oral_Exams_for_a_Generative_AI_World_Managing_Concerns_and_Logistics_for_Undergraduate_Humanities_Instruction

Summary: This recent paper directly addresses common faculty concerns about oral exams — student anxiety, fairness, and instructor time management — through "evidence-based strategies and careful implementation." The guide provides specific protocols for scheduling, question design, rubric creation, and accommodations. It frames oral exams not as punishment for AI use but as pedagogical opportunity to develop speaking skills students will need beyond graduation.
University BusinessNYU professor turned to AI oral exams after student work began resembling McKinsey memosAn NYU professor introduced AI-powered oral exams after students struggled to explain polished, AI-assisted assignments. The move reflects how colleges are rethinking assessment in the generative AI era.Written byBusiness InsiderEst. reading time1 minuteJan 5thacademia.eduOral Exams for a Generative AI World: Managing Concerns and Logistics for Undergraduate Humanities InstructionThe emergence of generative artificial intelligence has forced humanities educators to reconsider traditional assessment methods, particularly take-home writing assignments vulnerable to content produced by generative artificial intelligence. This[7:47 AM]Harvard Context: This is exactly the kind of practical resource Harvard faculty need if FAS decides to expand oral assessment. The Derek Bok Center could commission a similar guide customized for Harvard's context (concentration-specific examples, tutorial system integration, equity considerations). Key insights for Harvard: (1) Structured preparation reduces anxiety — provide sample questions, practice sessions, clear rubrics in advance; (2) Time management — 15-minute individual orals for a 20-student seminar = 5 hours total, manageable if spread across two weeks; (3) Fairness protocols — multiple examiners, standardized question pools, transparent criteria; (4) Accessibility — allow written notes, extra time, or alternative formats for documented disabilities. The paper's existence signals that faculty are actively experimenting with oral exams in humanities, creating collective knowledge that Harvard can learn from rather than reinventing from scratch.

Source quality: Academic paper on Academia.edu; likely pre-publication or working paper. Not peer-reviewed but represents practitioner scholarship.

───

3. Podcast Assignments: Oral Storytelling as Alternative Assessment

:page_facing_up: Listen up! Student-produced Podcasts are an Authentic Way to Assess Learning / A Guide to Alternative Assessments
University of Michigan Learning & Teaching Consulting / York University Teaching Commons, 2023/2025
https://sites.lsa.umich.edu/learningteachingconsulting/2023/11/16/listen-up-student-produced-podcasts-are-an-authentic-way-to-assess-learning-and-we-can-help/
https://www.yorku.ca/teachingcommons/wp-content/uploads/sites/993/2025/07/A-guide-to-alternative-assessment.pdf

Summary: Multiple teaching centers now promote student-produced podcasts as alternative to traditional research papers: students "formulate a script and set of questions to address during the podcast in a way that ensures the dialogue flows smoothly," combining research, writing (the script), and oral communication. Podcasts allow students to "showcase their learning" through audio storytelling and dramatization, making complex ideas accessible to non-specialist audiences. Universities provide LinkedIn Learning access for students to learn podcasting technical skills.

Harvard Context: Podcast assignments offer a multimodal alternative that addresses AI concerns while developing valuable communication skills. Unlike traditional oral exams (high-stakes, synchronous, anxiety-inducing), podcasts are: (1) Asynchronous — students record on their own schedule, reducing performance anxiety; (2) Editable — students can revise, unlike live presentations; (3) Public-facing — podcasts train students to communicate with broader audiences, not just professors; (4) Portfolio-building — students can share podcasts with employers or graduate programs. For Harvard, podcasts work especially well in fields where public scholarship matters: History students create podcast episodes on historical events, Government students analyze policy debates, Literature students discuss novels. The AI dimension: while students could use ChatGPT to draft scripts, the oral delivery, voice, storytelling choices, and editing process reveal engagement. The infrastructure need: Harvard would need to provide technical training (Library workshops?) and platforms for hosting/sharing podcasts.

Source quality: University teaching center resources. Authoritative for institutional best practices but not empirical research.

───

Connection to the Broader Project

These three experiments reveal diverse responses to the AI assessment crisis:

1. Hybrid verification (NYU): Keep writing, add oral defense to verify understanding
2. Comprehensive substitution (Humanities guide): Replace written exams with oral entirely, with careful scaffolding
3. Multimodal transformation (Podcasts): Create new assessment genres that combine oral, written, and technical skills
[7:47 AM]For Harvard: The diversity suggests there's no one-size-fits-all solution. Different courses, concentrations, and learning goals require different approaches:

• Seminars/tutorials → NYU hybrid model (write + defend) or full oral exams (with preparation)
• Large lectures → Podcast alternatives or video presentations (scalable through peer review)
• Thesis/capstone → Oral defense + podcast/public presentation (both scholarly rigor and public engagement)

The meta-lesson: Faculty experimentation is happening in real time across higher education. Harvard doesn't need to invent oral assessment from scratch — we can learn from peers' successes and failures, adapt proven models, and contribute our own innovations to the collective knowledge base.

The warning: All three approaches require infrastructure investment — time for oral exams, rubrics and training for fairness, technical support for podcasts. Adopting oral alternatives without resources will fail.

───
