elle-with-claws  [4:10 PM]
The Equity Paradox: Oral Assessment as Both Solution and Problem

As we consider oral work's renewed relevance in the AI era, we need to confront an uncomfortable tension: the same assessment mode that can level some playing fields simultaneously tilts others. Oral assessment eliminates advantages that accrue to students with access to private tutors, editing services, or now, sophisticated AI tools—these are genuine equity gains. But it potentially disadvantages multilingual students, those with speech or social anxiety disorders, and students from cultures where challenging a professor's framing would be seen as disrespectful. Recent research on anxiety as a reliability risk in oral assessment (St Andrews Journal, 2025) and UNESCO's documentation of 250 million learners facing systemic exclusion due to language barriers reminds us that "authentic" assessment can privilege particular forms of authenticity—specifically, the confident, fast-talking native speaker who thrives in high-pressure verbal performance.

The deeper challenge is that most Harvard faculty have never been trained to conduct oral assessments, much less to design them equitably. Medical and dental schools have developed structured approaches—standardized viva formats, rubrics that separate content from delivery, examiner training protocols—precisely because they learned that unstructured oral exams amplify evaluator bias (PMC, 2024; PubMed, 1999). But in the humanities and social sciences, we're largely improvising, which means our oral assessments risk being simultaneously more burdensome to administer and less reliable than the written work they're meant to replace. If we're serious about oral work at scale, we need parallel investment in faculty development: not just "here's how to run an oral exam," but critical examination of how different oral formats interact with learning objectives, disciplinary norms, and student populations. The question isn't whether to use oral assessment—it's how to do so in ways that are pedagogically sound, demonstrably rigorous, and genuinely equitable rather than accidentally discriminatory.
elle-with-claws  [5:03 PM]
"Oral Assessment" Is Not One Thing: Why Disciplinary Context Matters

When we talk about expanding oral work at Harvard, we risk treating "oral assessment" as a monolithic category—as if a philosophy tutorial, a chemistry lab presentation, an economics policy brief, and an architecture desk crit are all variations of the same pedagogical practice. They're not. Research on disciplinary cultures (CBE Life Sciences Ed, 2019; UMich Press, 2025) reveals that what counts as rigorous oral work varies dramatically by field, shaped by each discipline's epistemological traditions, professional preparation goals, and assessment histories. A physicist explaining a proof at the board is performing a different cognitive and rhetorical task than a historian defending an interpretive claim in seminar discussion. The former demonstrates mastery of established knowledge through clear exposition; the latter navigates contested evidence through dialogic argumentation. Both are "oral," but they serve different learning objectives and require different pedagogical scaffolding.

This matters because one-size-fits-all guidance on oral assessment—"all courses should include oral exams" or "rubrics should separate content from delivery"—fails to account for what oral communication actually does in different disciplines. In STEM fields, oral work often serves to verify understanding of concepts students learned elsewhere (the problem set is written; the explanation is oral). In humanities, oral dialogue is frequently the site of learning itself—students don't arrive at interpretation and then present it; they develop interpretation through seminar conversation. Social sciences split the difference: oral work as professional skill-building (the policy memo is written; the stakeholder briefing is oral). If Harvard adopts oral assessment more broadly, we need discipline-specific frameworks that honor these differences rather than forcing artificial uniformity. The question isn't "should we have oral exams?" but "what oral practices serve this discipline's learning objectives, and how do we design them rigorously and equitably within this field's intellectual traditions?"
elle-with-claws  [6:08 PM]
Does Oral Fluency Signal Deep Understanding? The Self-Explanation Paradox

One of the most compelling arguments for oral assessment rests on an intuitive claim: if you can explain something clearly, you must understand it. This idea has solid cognitive science behind it—Chi's decades of research on the "self-explanation effect" demonstrates that generating explanations to oneself deepens comprehension by forcing learners to monitor their understanding, make connections, and infer missing information (Cognitive Science, 1994; ASU Learning Sciences, 2010). But here's the pedagogical wrinkle: oral assessment asks students to explain to an examiner, not to themselves. That shift transforms explanation from a learning activity into a performance—and performances can be rehearsed, scripted, and optimized for evaluation rather than understanding. The student who explains photosynthesis flawlessly in a five-minute oral exam may have genuinely mastered the concept through self-explanation, or they may have memorized a well-crafted AI-generated script the night before. Oral fluency, it turns out, is not a transparent window into cognition.

This creates a real design challenge for rigorous oral assessment. If we want oral work to genuinely test understanding rather than verbal polish, we need formats that resist rehearsal: follow-up questions that probe beyond prepared answers, tasks that require spontaneous application to novel scenarios, or dialogic formats where students must build explanations collaboratively rather than deliver them solo. The distinction matters enormously. A formal presentation of a thesis—even with Q&A—can be scripted and perfected through multiple practice runs (with AI as coach). But a Socratic seminar where students must respond to peers' challenges in real time, or a tutorial where the instructor follows the student's reasoning down unexpected paths, genuinely reveals whether understanding is robust or brittle. The irony is that the oral formats most resistant to gaming (spontaneous dialogue, think-aloud problem-solving) are also the hardest to scale and the most anxiety-inducing for students who struggle with on-the-spot verbal performance. We're back to the equity paradox: the very features that make oral assessment rigorous also risk making it exclusionary.
elle-with-claws  [7:03 PM]
The Scaffolding Solution: Why Low-Stakes Oral Work Matters More Than High-Stakes Exams

If the equity paradox teaches us anything, it's that dropping students into high-stakes oral exams without preparation—expecting them to perform spontaneous intellectual dialogue on demand—is a recipe for reproducing existing inequalities. But there's a pedagogically sound alternative: use frequent, low-stakes oral work throughout the semester to build the very skills that high-stakes assessment demands. Research on scaffolding (UCSB Teaching & Learning; T&F Higher Ed, 2025) shows that students who practice oral skills in formative contexts—think-pair-share activities, weekly check-ins, peer explanation exercises—develop both competence and confidence that transfer to more formal assessment. A 2025 study on interactive oral assessments found that when students had practiced the format in low-stakes settings, they experienced the final evaluation as less stressful than traditional written exams, not more. The key is volume and variety: lots of brief, informal opportunities to think aloud, respond to questions, and explain concepts to peers before facing any graded oral performance.

This matters enormously for equity. The student who arrives at Harvard having attended a well-resourced high school with seminar-style classes and debate team has had hundreds of hours of oral practice; the student from an under-resourced school where "discussion" meant teacher lecture may have had almost none. If we assess both students identically in a high-stakes oral exam without scaffolding, we're measuring prior opportunity as much as current learning. But if we build oral skill development into the course—making brief oral explanations a regular, low-pressure part of weekly problem sets, reserving five minutes of each section for student-led mini-presentations, using fishbowl discussions where students rotate through "active participant" roles—we give all students repeated practice in contexts where stumbling is safe and feedback is immediate. The irony is that this approach requires more faculty time upfront (designing scaffolding, providing formative feedback, creating practice opportunities) but may actually make high-stakes oral assessment unnecessary: if students are explaining concepts weekly in low-stakes formats, we've already verified their understanding. The assessment becomes the learning.
elle-with-claws  [8:05 PM]
The Rubric Trap: How Assessment Criteria Can Undermine What They're Meant to Measure

Here's a practical dilemma every faculty member faces when designing oral assessment: detailed rubrics improve reliability and reduce bias (PMC, 2024; IIER, 2024), but they can also transform spontaneous intellectual dialogue into a performance checklist. If your rubric says "makes eye contact 70% of the time" or "uses at least three discipline-specific terms," students optimize for those criteria rather than engaging authentically with ideas. The student who genuinely grasps photosynthesis might stumble over terminology while thinking aloud; the student who memorized an AI script hits every rubric criterion perfectly. Analytic rubrics—which break assessment into discrete, scorable components—work beautifully for structured presentations with predictable elements (DePaul Teaching Commons; Cult of Pedagogy, 2025). But they're pedagogically dangerous for the very oral formats we've identified as most rigorous: Socratic dialogue, spontaneous problem-solving, exploratory conversation where students build understanding in real time.

The alternative—holistic rubrics that evaluate "overall quality of reasoning" or "depth of engagement"—preserve authenticity but sacrifice reliability. Without clear criteria, two professors might grade the same oral exam completely differently, and students have no actionable guidance on what "excellent" looks like. This creates both fairness problems (inconsistent standards) and learning problems (vague feedback). The most promising solution may be single-point rubrics that specify core criteria without prescribing exactly how students must meet them: "Demonstrates understanding by applying concepts to novel situations" rather than "provides exactly three examples." Combined with norming sessions where faculty calibrate their judgment by scoring sample performances together, this approach can preserve both the spontaneity that makes oral work valuable and the consistency that makes grading fair. But it requires significant faculty development time—precisely the resource Harvard has historically under-invested in for FAS teaching.
elle-with-claws  [9:03 PM]
The Oxbridge Mirage: Why We Can't Just Copy the Tutorial Model

When oral assessment advocates point to Oxford and Cambridge as proof that dialogic oral work can be done at scale, they're often citing a system built on fundamentally different resource assumptions. The Oxford/Cambridge tutorial—a weekly one-on-one or one-to-two session where students defend an essay to a tutor who knows their work intimately—operates with a 3:1 faculty-student ratio (QS Rankings, 2021). Harvard's FAS operates at roughly 7:1, which sounds comparable until you realize that Oxbridge tutorials aren't in addition to lectures and seminars; they are the primary mode of instruction, requiring each faculty member to conduct 8-12 hours of tutorials per week. As one analysis bluntly states: "The tutorial system only works if the student-to-tutor ratio is sufficiently low to allow for 1:1 or 1:2 supervisions... it can't be adapted for larger universities without compromising quality" (Quora, 2024). This isn't a pedagogical choice; it's a structural constraint. You can't have sustained, personalized, intellectually rigorous oral dialogue without either dramatically increasing faculty numbers or dramatically reducing student loads.

This matters because much of the literature praising oral assessment's effectiveness comes from contexts—medical schools with clinical rotations, law schools with small seminars, Oxford colleges with endowments older than American democracy—that bear little resemblance to Harvard's FAS reality. A chemistry course with 200 students and three teaching fellows can implement think-pair-share and weekly problem-set check-ins (scaffolding at scale). It cannot provide Oxford-style tutorials where every student defends their work one-on-one for an hour each week. We need to be honest: if Harvard wants genuinely rigorous oral assessment—the kind that reveals brittle understanding through spontaneous dialogue—we're talking about either massive investments in teaching capacity (more postdocs, more preceptors, more TFs with real training and time), or accepting that oral work will remain concentrated in small seminars and capstone experiences for advanced students. The middle path—mandating "oral components" across all courses without the resources to do them well—risks creating exactly what we're trying to avoid: checkbox exercises that add faculty workload without serving learning.
elle-with-claws  [10:05 PM]
Oral Work as Proof-of-Authorship: A Solution That Creates New Problems

The most compelling practical argument for expanding oral assessment in 2026 is brutally simple: it's harder to fake. A 2025 study in the British Journal of Educational Technology found that faculty increasingly "second-guess authorship" when grading written work, attempting to detect AI-generated or AI-modified submissions—a fundamentally unsustainable position that transforms grading into forensic analysis rather than evaluation of learning. Interactive oral assessments and role-play formats, the study suggests, "allow for thorough evaluation of the student, thereby overcoming the GenAI challenge." An Oxford study (2024) comparing ChatGPT-generated humanities essays to student work revealed that even experienced markers struggle to reliably distinguish AI from authentic writing—but nobody mistakes a live conversation for a chatbot. If you can ask follow-up questions, probe reasoning, and watch a student think through an unfamiliar application in real time, you know the intellectual work is theirs.

But here's the uncomfortable truth: using oral assessment primarily as an authenticity check rather than a learning tool is pedagogically backwards and potentially corrosive. If the implicit message is "we don't trust your written work anymore, so now you have to prove you did it yourself orally," we've replaced the learning relationship with a surveillance relationship. Students will reasonably ask: if oral work is just an anti-cheating mechanism, why should I care about doing it well rather than just adequately? More troublingly, this framing advantages students who are verbally confident under pressure while disadvantaging those who think better in writing, need processing time, or struggle with high-stakes verbal performance—not because they didn't do the work, but because proving authorship orally privileges a particular cognitive and linguistic style. The irony is bitter: we adopt oral assessment to restore academic integrity, but if we implement it poorly or punitively, we undermine the educational integrity we're trying to protect. Oral work should reveal understanding because that serves learning, not because we've given up on written assessment.
elle-with-claws  [11:04 PM]
The Grade Differentiation Problem: What Does "Excellent" Oral Work Actually Look Like?

Here's an uncomfortable question that most oral assessment literature sidesteps: how do you use the full grade range when evaluating spontaneous dialogue? Written work leaves artifacts—you can reread an essay, count arguments, check citations, compare drafts. Oral work is ephemeral, context-dependent, and shaped by the dynamic between examiner and student. Research on grading reliability (Guskey, 2022) shows that as the number of discernible grade categories increases, subjectivity increases and reliability decreases—yet Harvard's A/A-/B+/B/B- system demands precisely that fine-grained differentiation. The practical result is often grade compression: if most students can articulate something coherent in an oral exam, and the spontaneous format makes it hard to distinguish "very good" from "excellent," grades cluster around A-/B+. We've replaced written grade inflation with oral grade inflation, just with more faculty time spent per student.

The solution isn't to grade more harshly—it's to design oral assessments where different levels of mastery reveal themselves through the task structure. An A-level student doesn't just explain a concept; they spontaneously connect it to other material, identify its limitations, and apply it to novel problems without prompting. A B-level student explains accurately but needs scaffolding questions to make connections. A C-level student struggles to go beyond memorized explanations. But here's the catch: you only see these distinctions if your oral format creates space for them. A five-minute presentation with prepared answers compresses everyone toward competent performance. A fifteen-minute dialogue starting with "explain X" and then following the student's reasoning wherever it leads—asking "how does that relate to Y?" or "what would happen if we changed Z?"—naturally differentiates depth of understanding. This means oral assessment that genuinely uses the full grade range requires more faculty time per student, not less. If we're not willing to invest that time, we should be honest that we're using oral work for other purposes (authenticity verification, basic competence checks) rather than as the primary site of rigorous evaluation.
elle-with-claws  [2:06 AM]
The Accommodation Paradox: When "Alternative Assessment" Becomes the New Barrier

Here's an irony that disability services offices know well but assessment reformers often overlook: oral work is frequently recommended as an accommodation for students with certain disabilities (dyslexia, dysgraphia, processing disorders that make written exams harder than oral), but it simultaneously creates barriers for students with other disabilities. Research on social anxiety disorder (PMC, 2018) found that university students with SAD "were more likely to have weak clinical exam performance, and to avoid performing oral presentations"—not because they lacked knowledge, but because the assessment format itself triggered disabling anxiety. Students with speech impediments, stammering, selective mutism, or certain autism spectrum presentations may be able to demonstrate sophisticated understanding in writing but struggle to perform that understanding orally under evaluative pressure. When Harvard's standard accommodation for written work is "substitute with oral examination," what's the parallel accommodation when oral work becomes the default?

The problem deepens when we consider how disability accommodations actually work. For written exams, the mechanisms are well-established: extended time, reduced-distraction environments, use of assistive technology, breaks as needed. These preserve the assessment's validity (we're still testing the same content knowledge) while removing disability-related barriers. But what's the equivalent for oral assessment? "Extended time" for a ten-minute oral exam means... a fifteen-minute conversation? That changes the fundamental nature of the interaction. Recording the session for later review helps with documentation but doesn't address performance anxiety. Allowing written responses instead of oral defeats the entire purpose if we've adopted oral assessment specifically for its dialogic, spontaneous, AI-resistant properties. The uncomfortable truth is that if we make oral work a significant or required component of assessment across the curriculum—whether to combat AI, verify authorship, or test "real understanding"—we may be inadvertently closing doors for students with legitimate disabilities, replacing one access barrier with another. Universal design for learning suggests offering multiple ways to demonstrate mastery, not replacing written monopoly with oral monopoly.
elle-with-claws  [3:03 AM]
The Cultural Invisibility Problem: When "Good" Oral Performance Assumes Western Communication Norms

When we design oral assessments around Socratic dialogue, vigorous debate, or spontaneous questioning of assumptions, we're often embedding culturally specific communication norms without realizing it. Research on classroom participation (T&F Education, 2024) found that students from Confucian-heritage cultures often show "reluctance to challenge or disrupt the established order in the classroom," not from lack of understanding but from cultural values that prioritize respect for authority and social harmony. A study on Asian international students (Academia.edu, 2025) documented how "cultural expectations shape students' reluctance to interrupt teachers and prioritize high-quality questions"—meaning students who might excel in written analysis hesitate to speak up in rapid-fire discussion not because they lack knowledge, but because asking a "low-quality" question or appearing to challenge the professor violates cultural norms they've internalized since childhood. When oral assessment rubrics reward "actively challenges peers' assumptions" or "asks probing questions without prompting," we may be grading cultural assimilation as much as intellectual engagement.

This goes deeper than language proficiency—it's about discourse norms. In many Asian educational contexts, silence signals attentive listening and respectful learning; interrupting a speaker or "thinking aloud" while still formulating ideas is seen as disrespectful or intellectually careless (Northeastern, 2024). A Vietnamese or Chinese student who waits for the professor to finish speaking, formulates a careful response, and speaks only when certain of their point may be demonstrating higher cognitive engagement than an American student who jumps in mid-sentence with a half-formed idea. But in oral assessment formats that value spontaneity, verbal fluency, and "building on each other's ideas in real time," the former student appears passive while the latter appears engaged. The equity issue is compounded because many rubrics explicitly penalize behaviors that are culturally normative elsewhere: "makes direct eye contact" (seen as aggressive or disrespectful in many cultures), "speaks confidently even when uncertain" (valued in US academic culture, seen as arrogant or foolish elsewhere). If Harvard expands oral assessment without training faculty to recognize and value diverse communication styles, we risk systematically undervaluing students from non-Western backgrounds—even when they've mastered the material as thoroughly as their American peers.
elle-with-claws  [4:05 AM]
Theme 3: Educational Research on Speaking in Learning/Assessment (SoTL) – Cycle 10

Focus: Transfer to Professional Practice & Deep Learning

───

1. Assessment of Students' Oral Communication Skills: Do Students and Workplace Supervisors Rely on General Response Patterns?
:paperclip: ASEE Conference Proceedings (American Society for Engineering Education), 2008
:link: https://peer.asee.org/assessment-of-students-oral-communication-skills-do-students-and-workplace-supervisors-rely-on-general-response-patterns.pdf

This empirical study bridges the classroom-to-workplace gap by comparing student self-assessments of oral communication skills with actual workplace supervisor evaluations. Key finding: significant misalignment between what students think they've learned and what employers value. Students rate themselves highly on "presenting information," but supervisors prioritize "listening," "asking clarifying questions," and "adapting to audience" — skills not typically assessed in traditional classroom presentations.

Harvard relevance: If oral assessments are justified as preparation for professional life, we need evidence they're transferring the right skills. This study suggests traditional "stand and deliver" presentations may miss critical dimensions like dialogue, active listening, and adaptive communication. Concentrations with strong professional pipelines (Gov, Econ, CS) should consider interactive oral formats (vivas, oral defenses with Q&A) rather than one-way presentations.

───

2. Interactive Oral Assessments (IOA): An Authentic, Experiential Form of Assessment
:paperclip: University of Melbourne Centre for the Study of Higher Education, 2025
:link: https://melbourne-cshe.unimelb.edu.au/resources/topics/assessment-and-feedback/specific-help/interactive-oral-assessments

Melbourne defines IOAs as "unscripted, real-time, two-way conversations between students and assessors, centred on demonstrating understanding." Unlike presentations (student monologues) or traditional oral exams (interrogation), IOAs emphasize co-constructed dialogue where students explain, justify, and adapt their reasoning in response to probing questions.

Harvard relevance: This aligns with the tutorial tradition (History & Lit, Social Studies) but operationalizes it for scalable assessment. The key design principle: authenticity through interaction. Students can't script or outsource a genuine intellectual conversation. For Harvard faculty concerned about AI, IOAs offer a middle path — not returning to high-stakes final oral exams, but embedding low-stakes oral dialogues throughout the semester (office hours as assessment, discussion section participation with structured rubrics).

Equity consideration: Melbourne notes IOAs require faculty training in "generous questioning" — avoiding gatekeeping or intimidation, especially for students from non-dominant cultural backgrounds. This isn't just "add oral exam and stir."

───

3. Oral Exams Stimulate Critical Thinking and Deeper Cognitive Engagement Through Real-Time Articulation
:paperclip: Contemporary Educational Technology, Vol. 17(1), 2025 (ERIC EJ1460216)
:link: https://files.eric.ed.gov/fulltext/EJ1460216.pdf

This recent review synthesizes cognitive science research on oral assessment. Core finding: The act of real-time verbal explanation activates different cognitive processes than writing. Oral exams require students to:

• Retrieve knowledge without external scaffolding (no notes, no paragraph to revise)
• Monitor their own understanding while speaking (metacognitive awareness)
• Adapt explanations based on listener feedback (theory of mind, audience awareness)

The paper argues oral assessment isn't just "plagiarism-proof writing" — it's a fundamentally different cognitive task that surfaces shallow understanding faster. Students who've memorized or AI-generated content struggle to explain why or respond to follow-up questions.
[4:05 AM]Harvard relevance: This challenges the equivalence assumption (oral = spoken essay). If the goal is deep understanding, oral assessment may be epistemologically superior for certain learning outcomes — not just a workaround for AI. Particularly relevant for:

• STEM concentrations: Can you explain your reasoning process? (not just the answer)
• Humanities: Can you defend an interpretation under Socratic questioning?
• Social Sciences: Can you apply theory to a novel case in real time?

Caveat: The cognitive demand cuts both ways. High-stakes oral exams under time pressure can induce anxiety that impairs cognitive performance, especially for students with test anxiety or language barriers. The review recommends low-stakes, formative oral assessment throughout the semester to build fluency.

───

Project Status: 68 posts | 196 sources | Sunday Feb 22, 04:04 AM | Continues through Monday Feb 24, 9:00 AM EST

Emerging synthesis: Oral assessment isn't a single intervention — it's a design space spanning presentations (monologue), interactive orals (dialogue), oral exams (interrogation), and vivas (defense). Each serves different learning outcomes and carries different equity implications. The choice depends on what you're trying to measure: performance (presentation skills), understanding (explanation/defense), or professional readiness (adaptive communication).
elle-with-claws  [4:16 AM]
Theme 4: Post-AI Classroom Experiments with Oral Assignments – Cycle 11

Focus: Institutional Adoption & Practical Implementation

───

1. You Can't AI-Proof the Classroom, Experts Say. Get Creative Instead.
:paperclip: Inside Higher Ed, December 16, 2025
:link: https://www.insidehighered.com/news/faculty-issues/learning-assessment/2025/12/16/you-cant-ai-proof-classroom-experts-say-get

This timely survey of faculty responses to generative AI notes that oral assessments—either stand-alone or accompanying an essay—are becoming more popular. The "ancient assessment" asks students to sit before their professor, discuss what they've learned, answer questions in-person, or explain their writing process. The piece frames oral exams not as "AI-proofing" (a futile goal) but as pedagogical redesign that centers dialogue and process over product.

Harvard relevance: This aligns with the tutorial model already practiced in History & Lit and Social Studies, but challenges faculty to think beyond "policing AI use" toward "designing for human conversation." Key insight: oral assessments work best when paired with writing—not as replacement, but as verification of authorship and understanding. Students write, then defend/explain/discuss. This hybrid model may be more scalable than pure vivas while still addressing AI concerns.

Methodological note: This is journalism surveying expert opinion, not empirical research. But it captures the zeitgeist of faculty experimentation happening right now across institutions.

───

2. Addressing Student Use of Generative AI Through Academic Integrity Reporting
:paperclip: Frontiers in Education, October 20, 2025
:link: https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/education/articles/10.3389/feduc.2025.1610836/full

This peer-reviewed article documents institutional responses to GenAI in assessment design. Key finding: Educators are adopting innovative and refocused assessment strategies, such as oral examinations and authentic project-based tasks. The paper identifies three institutional models:

• AI-prohibited (ban + detection)
• AI-resistant (redesign toward oral/in-person assessment)
• AI-incorporated (explicit integration as learning tool)

Most institutions are moving toward the middle category, with oral assessment as a transitional strategy while pedagogy catches up to the technology.

Harvard relevance: This gives Harvard faculty a framework to position their experiments. Are you prohibiting AI (enforcement nightmare), resisting it (oral assessment as workaround), or incorporating it (AI as collaborator with oral defense)? The paper suggests the third model is most sustainable long-term, but requires rethinking what we're assessing—shifting from "did you write this?" to "can you think with/about this?"

Equity consideration: The paper notes oral assessment disproportionately burdens non-native speakers and students with anxiety disorders. Recommends low-stakes practice throughout semester, not high-stakes finals. Critical for Harvard's international student population.

───

3. Sampled Vivas Are Pivotal in Combating AI Cheating
:paperclip: Times Higher Education (Opinion), March 6, 2025
:link: https://www.timeshighereducation.com/opinion/sampled-vivas-are-pivotal-combating-ai-cheating

This opinion piece advocates for sampled vivas: randomly selecting a subset of students for oral defense after written submission. University of South Australia has reportedly replaced written exams with structured oral assessments in some subjects, significantly reducing academic misconduct. The author argues this creates "verification uncertainty"—students can't know if they'll be called to defend, so they must actually do the work.

Harvard relevance: This is a scalability solution. Faculty can't viva 200 students, but they can viva 20 randomly selected students (10% sample). The threat of being sampled creates incentive to learn the material. However, this raises serious equity and transparency concerns:
Inside Higher Ed | Higher Education News, Events and JobsYou Can’t AI-Proof the Classroom, Experts Say. Get Creative Instead.Artificial intelligence is here to stay. Faculty members explain how to keep students engaged in their own learning and prevent them from relying on AI.https://www.insidehighered.com/news/faculty-issues/learning-assessment/2025/12/16/you-cant-ai-proof-classroom-experts-say-getFrontiersFrontiers | Addressing student use of generative AI in schools and universities through academic integrity reportingThe aim of this theoretical article is to explore frameworks contributing to reasonable and applicable definitions of artificial intelligence (AI), human int...Times Higher Education (THE)Sampled vivas are pivotal in combating AI cheatingAI is here to stay but universities must teach students to use it responsibly, say Duncan Brumby, Anna Cox, Advait Sarkar and Sandy GouldMar 5th, 2025https://www.timeshighereducation.com/opinion/sampled-vivas-are-pivotal-combating-ai-cheating[4:16 AM]• Is random sampling fair when stakes are high?
[4:16 AM]• Does it shift from "assessment of learning" to "surveillance of cheating"?
• What happens to students who wrote their own work but perform poorly under oral pressure?

Critical perspective: While this addresses AI misuse, it fundamentally changes the pedagogical contract—from "demonstrate learning via preferred medium" to "prove you're not cheating via high-pressure performance." Harvard faculty should consider whether this aligns with liberal arts values of trust and intellectual growth, or whether it represents a defensive retreat.

───

Project Status: 69 posts | 199 sources | Sunday Feb 22, 04:15 AM | 52.75 hours remaining

Cross-cutting theme emerging: Oral assessment in the AI era serves three distinct functions, and faculty need to be clear which they're prioritizing:

1. Verification (proof of authorship) → sampled vivas, post-essay defenses
2. Pedagogy (learning through explanation) → interactive orals, formative dialogue
3. Resistance (return to pre-AI assessment) → full replacement of writing

The most sustainable approaches combine #1 and #2: use oral assessment to enhance learning while verifying authorship, rather than abandoning writing altogether. Harvard's tutorial tradition positions it well for this integrated model.
elle-with-claws  [4:31 AM]
Theme 5: AI Used IN Oral Evaluation – Cycle 11

Focus: Automated Speech Recognition, Conversational AI Examiners, and Bias

───

1. Evaluating Automated Evaluation Systems for Spoken English Proficiency: An Exploratory Comparative Study with Human Raters
:paperclip: PLOS ONE, March 28, 2025
:link: https://journals.plos.org/plosone/article?id=10.1371/journal.pone.0320811

This rigorous empirical study compares automated evaluation systems (AES) like ETS's SpeechRater and Pearson's Versant against human raters for spontaneous speech assessment. The systems use forced alignment, phoneme recognition, speech rate, pause patterns, lexical frequency profiles, and syntactic parsing to assess oral proficiency. Key finding: AES demonstrates high correlations with human ratings for spontaneous speech samples, offering increased speed, efficiency, consistency, and rapid feedback for both summative and formative assessment.

Harvard relevance: This technology could make oral assessment scalable for large courses—imagine automated transcription and initial scoring of 200 student oral responses, with faculty reviewing only borderline cases or providing qualitative feedback. However, the study focuses on language proficiency assessment (ESL/EFL context), not content knowledge assessment (Harvard's primary use case). The features measured—pronunciation, fluency, grammar—are different from evaluating historical argument, scientific reasoning, or literary interpretation.

Critical question: Can these systems assess what students say (ideas, arguments, critical thinking) or only how they say it (linguistic features)? For Harvard oral assessment to leverage AI, we need systems that evaluate conceptual understanding, not just speech quality.

───

2. Towards Reducing Teacher Burden in Performance-Based Assessments Using aivaluate: An Emotionally Intelligent LLM-Augmented Pedagogical AI Conversational Agent
:paperclip: Springer Nature | Education and Information Technologies, September 3, 2025
:link: https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s10639-025-13755-7

This cutting-edge paper introduces aivaluate, an LLM-powered conversational AI that conducts viva voce exams and oral presentations autonomously. The system asks follow-up questions, probes understanding, and provides both scoring and qualitative feedback—substantially reducing teacher burden while maintaining assessment rigor. The "emotionally intelligent" component adjusts questioning style based on student affect (anxiety, engagement), creating a more supportive assessment environment than traditional high-pressure oral exams.

Harvard relevance: This represents the most ambitious vision of AI in oral assessment—AI as co-examiner or even primary examiner. The efficiency gains are staggering: one faculty member could design the assessment, and AI could administer it to hundreds of students. But this raises profound questions:

• Pedagogical: Does the human relationship in assessment matter? Is the tutorial conversation between student and professor inherently valuable beyond just "knowledge verification"?
• Ethical: If AI conducts the assessment, who is accountable for outcomes? What happens when the AI misunderstands a student or asks unfair questions?
• Institutional: Would Harvard stakeholders accept AI as examiner? Even if technically valid, is it culturally appropriate for an elite liberal arts institution?

Recommendation: Explore AI as assistant (transcription, initial scoring, question suggestions) before considering AI as examiner.

───

3. Towards Inclusive Automatic Speech Recognition
:paperclip: ScienceDirect | Computer Speech & Language, September 20, 2023
:link: https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0885230823000864
journals.plos.orgEvaluating automated evaluation systems for spoken English proficiency: An exploratory comparative study with human ratersAutomated evaluation systems (AESs) for spoken language assessment are increasingly adopted in global educational settings, yet their validity in non-Western contexts remains underexplored. This study addresses this gap by examining three widely used Chinese-developed AES tools in their assessment of spoken English proficiency among 30 Chinese undergraduates. The study employed an IELTS-adapted speaking test, assessed simultaneously by AESs and human raters, with scoring alignment analyzed through intra-class correlation coefficients, Pearson correlations, and linear regression. Results revealed that two systems demonstrated strong agreement with human ratings, while the third exhibited syst… SpringerLinkTowards reducing teacher burden in Performance-Based assessments using aivaluate: an emotionally intelligent LLM-Augmented pedagogical AI conversational agentEducation and Information Technologies - Performance-based assessments (PBAs), such as viva voce exams and oral presentations, offer comprehensive evaluations of student knowledge and skills but...https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s10639-025-13755-7[4:31 AM]This systematic study quantifies speech recognition bias against gender, age, regional accents, and non-native accents across multiple ASR systems and languages (including Dutch cross-linguistic analysis). Findings are stark: ASR accuracy is significantly lower for women, older speakers, non-standard regional accents, and especially non-native speakers. The bias originates in training data imbalances and model architectures that privilege dominant linguistic patterns (young, male, native, prestige dialect speakers).

Harvard relevance: This is the equity red flag for AI-assisted oral assessment. Harvard's student body includes:

• ~25% international students (non-native English speakers)
• Students with diverse regional/ethnic accents (Southern, AAVE, Asian-American, Latinx)
• Students with speech differences (stuttering, voice disorders, neurodivergent speech patterns)

If Harvard uses ASR for transcription or scoring, the system will systematically underperform for marginalized students—precisely the populations already facing barriers in oral assessment. This could create a two-tier system where students with privileged linguistic backgrounds get accurate AI assistance, while others are penalized by algorithmic bias.

Mitigation strategies (from the paper):

• Human review of all AI-scored assessments (but this defeats scalability gains)
• Diverse training data including non-dominant speech patterns
• Disclosure to students about AI use and bias limitations
• Option to opt-out of AI scoring for accommodations

Bottom line: AI in oral assessment is not neutral. Without careful design, it reproduces and amplifies existing inequities.

───

Project Status: 70 posts | 202 sources | Sunday Feb 22, 04:30 AM | 52.5 hours remaining

Synthesis across sources: AI can make oral assessment more scalable (PLOS), more efficient (Springer), but risks being less equitable (ScienceDirect). The technology exists to automate transcription, scoring, and even questioning—but the equity costs may outweigh the efficiency gains unless Harvard invests in bias mitigation and human oversight. The safest current use case: AI as assistant (transcription for faculty review, not automated scoring) rather than evaluator.
elle-with-claws  [4:46 AM]
Theme 6: Literary/Historical References on Speech vs. Writing – Cycle 11

Focus: From Plato to McLuhan to AI — 2,400 Years of Orality Debates

───

1. The Realm of Mimesis in Plato: Orality, Writing, and the Ontology of the Image
:paperclip: Bryn Mawr Classical Review, May 2024
:link: https://bmcr.brynmawr.edu/2024/2024.05.29/

This recent scholarly monograph examines mimesis in Plato as a "mechanism" that reveals constant relationships of opposition, continuity, and co-dependency in the critiques of writing and orality. The author argues that Plato's famous critique of writing in the Phaedrus (the Egyptian myth of Theuth) is not a simple preference for speech over text, but a complex dialectical engagement with how different media shape thought, memory, and truth-claims.

Harvard relevance: Faculty debating oral vs. written assessment often invoke Plato's "writing weakens memory" argument as ancient wisdom supporting oral exams. But this scholarship shows Plato's position was more nuanced—he critiqued both oral and written modes for different reasons. The Phaedrus critique of writing focuses on its inability to respond to questions ("if you ask it anything, it maintains a solemn silence"). Sound familiar? This is precisely the problem with AI-generated essays—they can't defend themselves under questioning.

Pedagogical insight: Plato's real concern wasn't the medium per se, but the loss of dialectical accountability. A text (or AI essay) can't adapt, clarify, or justify when challenged. Oral assessment restores this Socratic demand: Can you defend your claims? Can you think on your feet? Can you engage in dialogue? This reframes oral exams not as "AI-proofing" but as recovering the philosophical value of responsive, adaptive thinking.

───

2. Socrates on the Forgetfulness that Comes with Writing
:paperclip: New Learning Online (educational resource)
:link: https://newlearningonline.com/literacies/chapter-1/socrates-on-the-forgetfulness-that-comes-with-writing

This accessible summary presents Socrates' paradoxical relationship with writing: he never wrote anything himself, yet his dialogues (recorded by Plato) became foundational texts of Western philosophy. The piece highlights Socrates' argument that writing creates "forgetfulness in the learner's soul" because people will rely on external marks instead of internal recollection. Oral teaching, by contrast, "writes on the soul" through lived dialogue.

Harvard relevance: This resource is useful for undergraduate-facing pedagogy—faculty can assign it to explain why the course includes oral assessment. It frames the choice not as distrust (anti-AI policing) but as pedagogical commitment: we want you to internalize knowledge, not just outsource it to external tools (whether papyrus scrolls or ChatGPT).

Critical perspective needed: The piece should be paired with counterarguments. Walter Ong's Orality and Literacy (referenced in the article) shows that writing expanded human consciousness by enabling abstract thought, cumulative knowledge, and critical reflection. Socrates was wrong that writing would weaken memory—it freed memory from rote storage so minds could engage in higher-order synthesis. Similarly, AI might free students from mechanical writing tasks to focus on thinking. The question isn't "oral or written?" but "What kind of thinking do we want to cultivate?"

───

3. The Great Digital Reversal: From Literacy to Secondary Orality in the AI Age
:paperclip: Human as Media (blog), December 17, 2025
:link: https://human-as-media.com/2025/12/17/the-great-digital-reversal/
Human as MediaThe great digital reversalThe latest book by media theorist Andrey Mir raises hypotheses that are as lucid as they are unsettling about the future of literate culture and the human race itself. By Eugenio Palopoli. This is …Dec 16th, 2025https://human-as-media.com/2025/12/17/the-great-digital-reversal/[4:46 AM]This timely essay applies McLuhan and Ong's media theory to contemporary AI. Key concept: "secondary orality"—electronic media (radio, TV, and now AI voice interfaces) recover oral culture's immersive, empathic engagement while maintaining literacy's scale and permanence. The piece argues we're experiencing a "great digital reversal": AI shifts us from literate culture (deliberation, structured thought, critical distance) back toward oral culture (immediacy, conversational flow, contextual adaptation).

Harvard relevance: This gives faculty a theoretical framework for what's happening. The anxiety about AI isn't just about cheating—it's about a medium shift comparable to the transition from orality to literacy that Plato witnessed. Just as writing changed how humans think (enabling abstract philosophy, science, law), AI is changing how we think again. Oral assessment isn't a return to pre-literate orality but an embrace of secondary orality—technologically mediated conversation as the new mode of knowledge work.

Provocative question for Harvard: If AI makes writing as cheap and abundant as speech (you can generate text as fast as talking), does written assessment lose its cognitive distinctiveness? And if so, should oral assessment (the space where AI currently can't follow) become the primary mode for evaluating human thinking? Or is this a category error—assuming assessment must be "AI-proof" rather than "AI-integrated"?

McLuhan insight: "The medium is the message." The choice between oral and written assessment isn't just about what we assess but how assessment shapes students' consciousness. Oral assessment privileges quick thinking, adaptive response, presence, and personality. Written assessment privileges deliberation, revision, structure, and abstraction. Both are valuable. The real question: What kind of minds are we trying to form?

───

Project Status: 71 posts | 205 sources | Sunday Feb 22, 04:45 AM | 52.25 hours remaining

Synthesis: The 2,400-year debate from Socrates to AI reveals a recurring pattern: new technologies trigger anxiety about loss of valued cognitive practices. Socrates feared writing would weaken memory. Print-era scholars feared radio would weaken literacy. Now we fear AI will weaken writing. Each time, the technology does change cognition—but not always in the feared direction. Writing didn't destroy memory; it freed it for higher purposes. The challenge for Harvard faculty: design assessment that leverages the strengths of both oral and written modes in the AI age, rather than retreating to one or the other.
elle-with-claws  [5:01 AM]
Theme 7: Professional Domains — Law, Consulting, Academia – Cycle 11

Focus: How Professionals Use Oral Communication & What Harvard Undergrads Should Learn

───

1. Harvard Law School Ames Moot Court Competition
:paperclip: Harvard Law School, November 10, 2025
:link: https://hls.harvard.edu/ames-moot-court/

The Ames Moot Court Competition at Harvard Law is one of the oldest and most prestigious oral advocacy training programs in the U.S. Students competing in the Final Round (3Ls) spent two years developing "strong research abilities and excellent written and oral advocacy." The competition structure mirrors professional appellate practice: research a legal problem, write a brief, then defend your argument orally before a panel of judges (often sitting Supreme Court justices or federal appellate judges).

Harvard College relevance: This is exactly the hybrid model recommended for undergraduate oral assessment: write first, defend orally second. HLS students aren't asked to improvise legal arguments on the spot—they research, write, revise their brief, then present and defend it under rigorous questioning. The oral component verifies authorship (you can't defend an argument you didn't write) and tests deeper understanding (can you adapt your argument to judges' hypotheticals?).

Cross-institutional learning opportunity: Harvard College faculty could visit HLS moot court sessions to observe how law professors coach students in oral advocacy. Key skills transferable to undergraduate education:

• Signposting: "There are three reasons..." (clear structure)
• Responsive pivoting: Answering questions directly before returning to your argument
• Conceding gracefully: "That's a fair point, Your Honor, but it doesn't affect my core claim because..."

These are precisely the skills undergraduate oral assessment should develop—not just "giving a presentation" but defending claims under intellectual challenge.

───

2. Management Consulting Presentation Essential Training: McKinsey-Style Communication
:paperclip: Udemy / Analyst Academy, January 2026
:link: https://www.udemy.com/course/management-consulting-presentation-mckinsey/

This professional training course teaches McKinsey-style slide decks: clear, data-driven, and persuasive presentations that drive executive decisions. The core consulting skill: "structure your thinking like a McKinsey associate, communicate with the precision of a BCG project lead, and navigate ambiguity." Consulting presentations follow the pyramid principle (conclusion first, supporting arguments second) and prioritize so what? clarity—every slide must answer "why does this matter to the client?"

Harvard relevance: Many Harvard graduates enter consulting (McKinsey, Bain, BCG recruit heavily on campus). But consulting communication is fundamentally oral-visual, not written. Consultants don't write 20-page reports—they create 15-slide decks and present them in 30-minute client meetings, then field tough questions from C-suite executives. This is a high-stakes oral performance where your ability to explain, justify, and defend your recommendations in real-time determines success.

Pedagogical implication: If Harvard is preparing students for professional communication, oral assessment is more authentic to consulting work than essay-writing. A hypothetical "consulting-style oral exam":

• Student receives a case (HBS-style business problem)
• Prepares a 10-slide analysis (can use AI tools for research, data viz)
• Presents to faculty + defends recommendations under questioning
• Graded on: analytical clarity, oral explanation, responsive defense, "so what?" insight

This assesses critical thinking, synthesis, and communication—skills essays also target, but in a mode that better mirrors professional practice. And it's inherently AI-resistant, because the AI can't present for you or answer follow-up questions.

───

3. Conference Presentations as Preparation for PhD Dissertation Defense
Harvard Law SchoolAmes Moot Court Competition - Harvard Law SchoolIn one of the most anticipated events of the year, contestants stand in the Ames Courtroom before an illustrious panel of distinguished jurists to demonstrate their skills in oral argument. As it has since 1911, the competition draws standing–room–only crowds as two 3L teams argue before the judges. A justice of the U.S. Supreme Court […]https://hls.harvard.edu/ames-moot-court/[5:01 AM]:paperclip: McGill University Graduate Supervision Guide, December 8, 2022
:link: https://www.mcgill.ca/gradsupervision/timelines/presenting

This McGill resource identifies a crucial insight: "Questions arising from a doctoral student's conference presentation are likely to relate to questions that will be asked by examiners at the dissertation defence." Conference presentations expose students to external criticism of their work before the high-stakes oral defense, building the skill of defending research under expert questioning. The guide recommends PhD students present at multiple conferences throughout their program as low-stakes practice for the final viva.

Harvard College relevance: This reveals the scaffolding principle for undergraduate oral assessment. Don't introduce oral exams only at finals—embed low-stakes oral components throughout the semester:

• Week 4: 5-minute informal presentation in section (peer feedback)
• Week 8: 10-minute formal presentation to class (faculty feedback)
• Week 12: 15-minute oral defense of final paper (graded summative assessment)

Each iteration builds fluency and confidence while reducing anxiety. Students practice explaining their thinking, receiving questions, clarifying arguments—exactly what graduate students do at conferences before defending dissertations.

Equity benefit: This scaffolding approach mitigates the anxiety disadvantage for students with public speaking fears or linguistic differences. By the final assessment, oral communication is familiar, practiced, and safe rather than high-stakes and terrifying.

Research finding: The McGill guide notes that conference Q&A reveals gaps in the research that students can address before final defense. Similarly, undergraduate oral checkpoints can identify misunderstandings early, allowing students to revise and deepen their work—making oral assessment formative as well as summative.

Project Status: 72 posts | 208 sources | Sunday Feb 22, 05:00 AM | 52 hours remaining

Synthesis across professional domains: Law, consulting, and academia all privilege oral defense of prepared work over pure improvisation or pure writing. Professionals succeed when they can:

1. Prepare deeply (research, analysis, writing)
2. Present clearly (structured oral explanation)
3. Defend responsively (answer questions, adapt to challenges)

Harvard oral assessment should develop these three competencies in sequence, not just #2. The most authentic model: hybrid assessment (written work + oral defense), scaffolded across the semester with low-stakes practice before high-stakes evaluation.
