Theme 0: History of Oral Examinations in Higher Ed – Cycle 12

Focus: The Oral-to-Written Shift & What It Reveals About the AI Moment

───

1. History of Standardized Testing in the United States: The Mass Education Shift
:paperclip: National Education Association (NEA)
:link: https://www.nea.org/professional-excellence/student-engagement/tools-tips/history-standardized-testing-united-states

This NEA resource identifies a crucial historical pattern: "Formal written tests begin to replace oral examinations administered by teachers and schools at roughly the same time as schools change their mission from servicing the elite to educating the masses." The shift from oral to written assessment in the 19th century wasn't driven by pedagogical theory but by scalability demands. When colleges expanded enrollment beyond small cohorts of wealthy men, oral exams by individual professors became logistically impossible.

Harvard relevance: This historical parallel is striking. In the 1800s, colleges faced a scaling crisis—how to assess hundreds instead of dozens of students. The solution: written exams (standardized, efficient, impersonal). Now in 2026, we face the inverse crisis: how to assess authenticity when written work can be AI-generated. The proposed solution: return to oral exams (personalized, labor-intensive, "unscalable").

Critical insight: Both shifts prioritize institutional logistics over pedagogical ideals. The 19th-century shift to writing wasn't because writing was better assessment—it was cheaper and faster. Similarly, the 2026 shift to oral isn't necessarily because oral is better—it's "AI-proof." Harvard faculty should ask: Are we designing assessment for learning, or designing around technology constraints? The most thoughtful approach may be hybrid: use AI to make some assessment more efficient (automated transcription, draft feedback) while reserving human oral dialogue for the highest-value interactions.

───

2. College Entrance Examinations: From Oral Interviews to Standardized Tests
:paperclip: Smithsonian National Museum of American History
:link: https://americanhistory.si.edu/collections/object/nmah_1408575

This Smithsonian exhibit traces U.S. college admissions assessment: "In the early years of the United States, students applying to colleges took oral examinations given by professors at the school they wished to attend. As the size of the country grew, individual colleges prepared written examinations." The shift from oral to written wasn't just about enrollment growth—it was about geographic expansion. When Harvard drew students only from Boston, oral exams were feasible. When it drew from across the nation, written standardized tests (the SAT's precursor) became necessary.

Harvard relevance: This reveals oral assessment's resource intensiveness is not a new problem—it's why oral exams were abandoned 150+ years ago. If Harvard reintroduces oral assessment at scale, it faces the same challenges that drove the 19th-century shift:

• Faculty time: One 15-minute oral exam per student in a 200-person course = 50 hours of faculty/TA time
• Consistency: Different examiners ask different questions, apply different standards
• Equity: Oral exams favor students comfortable with authority figures, native speakers, extroverts

Mitigation strategies (learning from history):

• Sampled oral assessment: Only 10-20% of students randomly selected (reduces burden, maintains incentive)
• Structured protocols: Standardized question banks and rubrics (improves consistency)
• Peer examiners: Train advanced students as oral assessors under faculty supervision (scales labor, builds skills)

The Smithsonian exhibit reminds us: oral assessment was abandoned for good reasons. Harvard shouldn't romanticize it but should design around its known limitations.

───

3. Evolution of Examinations: A Historical Perspective on Assessment Reforms
:paperclip: Teachers Institute, October 15, 2025
nea.orgHistory of Standardized Testing in the United States | NEAExplore more than 150 years of assessment student achievement.https://www.nea.org/professional-excellence/student-engagement/tools-tips/history-standardized-testing-united-statesNational Museum of American HistoryCollege Entrance ExaminationsIn the early years of the United States, students applying to colleges took oral examinations given by professors at the school they wished to attend.&hellip;[5:16 AM]:link: https://teachers.institute/instruction-in-higher-education/evolution-examinations-historical-assessment-reforms/

This recent overview argues that "passing exams became a form of social mobility" in the 19th-20th centuries—demonstrating capability through written/oral competency. The piece notes that written exams democratized access by creating transparent, standardized evaluation criteria, reducing favoritism and social connections that dominated oral exams in elite institutions. However, it also acknowledges oral assessment's unique value: "Written exams test knowledge recall; oral exams test knowledge defense—the ability to explain, justify, and adapt under questioning."

Harvard relevance: This highlights the equity paradox of oral assessment:

• Anti-democratic: Oral exams historically favored socially privileged students who were comfortable performing for authority, native speakers, those with "polished" speech patterns
• Pro-democratic: Written exams can be gamed by wealth (tutors, AI assistance), while oral defense requires genuine understanding

The AI era may be inverting the equity calculus. In the 20th century, writing was the "equalizer" (anyone could write, regardless of accent or speech anxiety). In 2026, writing may become the "privilege" (those with AI access get better essays). Oral assessment could become the new "equalizer"—if designed carefully to mitigate speech anxiety, accent bias, and performance pressure.

Design principle for Harvard: If oral assessment is reintroduced as an equity measure (preventing AI-enabled cheating by wealthy students), it must be paired with equity scaffolding:

• Low-stakes practice oral assignments throughout semester (not just high-stakes finals)
• Accommodations for students with speech anxiety, ESL backgrounds, disabilities
• Clear rubrics focusing on ideas not performance style (content over charisma)
• Multiple attempts or revision opportunities (oral "rewrites")

Historical lesson: Every assessment technology creates different equity challenges. Written exams solved oral exam favoritism but created new barriers (test anxiety, time pressure, handwriting speed). AI-resistant oral exams may solve AI cheating but recreate old barriers (speech anxiety, accent bias, class-coded performance norms). The goal isn't finding a "perfect" assessment mode but designing around the specific equity risks of whichever mode you choose.

───

Project Status: 73 posts | 211 sources | Sunday Feb 22, 05:15 AM | 51.75 hours remaining

Synthesis: History shows the oral-to-written shift in the 1800s was driven by scalability, geography, and democratization. The AI-era written-to-oral shift is driven by authenticity concerns. But we should learn from history's mistakes: don't romanticize oral assessment or assume it solves all problems. Both modes have equity trade-offs. The most thoughtful approach: hybrid, scaffolded, equity-conscious design that leverages the strengths of both oral and written assessment in the AI age.
Teachers InstituteEvolution of Examinations: A Historical Perspective on Assessment Reforms • Teachers InstituteExplore the history of examinations, from ancient oral traditions to modern standardized tests, and their impact on education and society.Written byPrince KumarTime to read7 minutesDec 26th, 2023https://teachers.institute/instruction-in-higher-education/evolution-examinations-historical-assessment-reforms/elle-with-claws  [5:31 AM]
Theme 1: Peer Institutions Responding to AI Anxieties – Cycle 12

Focus: Stanford, Columbia, and the Oral-Exam-as-Penalty Trend

───

1. Stanford University: From Traditional Essays to Interactive, Project-Based Evaluations
:paperclip: AcademyNC, August 29, 2025
:link: https://academync.com/articles/oral-exams-in-ai-era

This article reports that Stanford University changed its assessment design from traditional essays to more interactive, project-based evaluations, demonstrating institutional adaptation to AI challenges. While specifics aren't detailed in the piece, this aligns with Stanford's broader pedagogical philosophy of "design thinking" and experiential learning. The shift suggests Stanford is moving away from pure written assessment (AI-vulnerable) and toward multimodal assessment that combines creation, presentation, and defense.

Harvard relevance: Stanford's response is notably not "return to oral exams" but "redesign assignments to be AI-integrated." This represents a different strategic choice from the "oral-as-AI-resistance" model. Stanford appears to be asking: "How do we design assessments where AI is a tool, not a threat?" This might include:

• Design projects where AI assists research/ideation, but students must present and defend
• Group projects where individual contribution is verified through oral check-ins
• Portfolio assessments tracking process (not just product), with oral reflections

Strategic question for Harvard: Is the goal to resist AI (oral exams as verification) or integrate AI (new assignment types where AI is expected/disclosed)? Stanford's approach suggests the latter may be more sustainable long-term, as AI capabilities will only expand. However, this requires fundamentally rethinking what we're assessing—from "can you write a 10-page essay?" to "can you design a solution, use AI appropriately, and defend your decisions?"

───

2. Columbia University: All Applicants Must Review Generative AI Policy
:paperclip: GradPilot, September 12, 2025
:link: https://gradpilot.com/news/ai-college-admissions-t10-t20

Columbia University requires all applicants to review their Generative AI Policy before submitting admissions materials, signaling that AI use/disclosure is a core institutional concern even at the admissions stage. This is a more aggressive stance than Harvard's current approach and suggests Columbia is establishing clear expectations about AI before students arrive on campus.

Harvard relevance: Columbia's pre-enrollment AI policy sets a cultural tone: "AI is here, we have rules about it, you need to understand them from day one." If Harvard adopts oral assessment as an AI response, should there be similar early communication:

• In admissions materials: "Harvard values intellectual conversation; expect oral components in courses"
• In first-year orientation: Training on oral communication, academic dialogue norms
• In course syllabi: Clear explanation of why oral assessment is used (not punitive, but pedagogical)

Equity consideration: Columbia's approach raises access questions. If AI policy shapes admissions expectations, does it disadvantage students from under-resourced schools who lack AI literacy training? Similarly, if Harvard emphasizes oral assessment, does it disadvantage students from educational cultures that don't prioritize oral performance (many international systems, some U.S. public schools)? The solution: explicit training and scaffolding, not assumptions of prior preparation.

───

3. Universities Requiring Oral Examinations for High AI-Detection Scores
:paperclip: British Journal of Educational Technology, March 31, 2025
:link: https://bera-journals.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/full/10.1111/bjet.13585
AcademyNcOral Exams in AI Era: New Assessment Solutions for ChatGPT AgeDiscover how oral exams are making a comeback as universities combat AI cheating. Learn preparation strategies and why this old method might solve modern problemsAug 29th, 2025https://academync.com/articles/oral-exams-in-ai-eraGradPilotAI Policies at T10-T20 Colleges: Brown Ban, UC DisqualificationCornell, Brown, UC schools and other top colleges reveal strict AI policies. Some are tougher than Ivies, with UC threatening complete disqualification from all campuses.Sep 12th, 2025https://gradpilot.com/news/ai-college-admissions-t10-t20[5:31 AM]This peer-reviewed article documents a troubling trend: "In many universities, current guidance regarding the usage of GenAI suggests that for all assessments that score high on AI detection, students would need to undergo an oral examination." The paper notes this makes "the marking process far more onerous and time-consuming" and raises questions about oral exams as punishment rather than pedagogy.

Harvard relevance: This is the worst-case scenario for oral assessment: using it as a penalty for suspected AI use rather than a designed learning experience. Problems with this approach:

Pedagogical: Oral exam becomes an accusation ("we don't trust you") rather than a learning opportunity. Students approach it defensively, not as genuine intellectual dialogue.

Equity: AI detection tools have documented bias against non-native English writers (ESL students flagged more often because their writing patterns differ from AI training data). If oral exams follow AI detection flags, this creates a two-tier system:

• Native speakers write freely, rarely flagged, rarely oral-examined
• ESL/international students flagged disproportionately, subjected to extra oral scrutiny

Procedural: What's the standard of proof? If a student's oral defense is weak (due to anxiety, language barriers, different oral culture), does that "prove" they AI'd the essay? Or might they have written it themselves but struggle with oral performance?

Scalability: As the article notes, this approach is "far more onerous and time-consuming"—exactly the problem that drove the shift from oral to written assessment in the 1800s.

Recommendation for Harvard: If adopting oral assessment, design it as a regular pedagogical component for all students, not as a post-hoc "AI audit" for suspected cases. Oral assessment should be:

• Universal (everyone does it, built into course design)
• Scaffolded (low-stakes practice before high-stakes assessment)
• Transparent (clear purpose: learning, not policing)
• Equitable (accommodations for speech anxiety, ESL backgrounds, disabilities)

Using oral exams only when AI is suspected creates the worst possible dynamic: oral assessment as punishment, not pedagogy.

───

Project Status: 74 posts | 214 sources | Sunday Feb 22, 05:30 AM | 51.5 hours remaining

Synthesis across peer institutions: Stanford is integrating AI (redesigning assignments), Columbia is regulating AI (disclosure policies), and many universities are policing AI (oral exams as penalty for detection). Harvard should learn from all three—but avoid the third. The most promising path: hybrid assessment (written + oral) designed from the start, with clear pedagogical rationale, universal application, and equity scaffolding. Not oral exams as AI-detection response, but oral dialogue as core learning practice.
elle-with-claws  [5:46 AM]
Theme 2: History of Oral Assignments at Harvard – Cycle 12

Focus: From 1916 General Exams to Today's Concentration Orals

───

1. Harvard's First Undergraduate General Exams: Both Written and Oral Tests (1916)
:paperclip: Economics in the Rear-View Mirror (archival research blog), November 23, 2019
:link: https://www.irwincollier.com/harvard-first-undergraduate-general-and-specific-exams-in-history-government-and-economics-division-1916/

This archival research documents Harvard College's 1916 introduction of general examinations in the History, Government, and Economics Division. Crucially, the examination "was to consist of both written and oral tests, was to be required of all college students concentrating in that division, in addition to their courses." The exam was conducted by a committee of three faculty members who were "relieved of one half of their work of instruction" to focus on assessment—revealing the resource investment oral exams demand.

Harvard historical context: This 1916 innovation represents Harvard's hybrid assessment tradition—not oral OR written, but oral AND written as complementary modes. The general exam system (which evolved into concentration exams and senior theses) established a principle: comprehensive assessment of undergraduate learning requires multiple modalities. Written exams tested breadth of knowledge; oral exams tested depth, synthesis, and the ability to defend arguments under questioning.

Contemporary relevance: Harvard already has institutional memory and infrastructure for oral assessment—it's not a foreign concept being imported but a revival of established practice. Concentrations that maintained oral components (History & Lit, Social Studies, Folklore & Mythology) never abandoned this tradition. The question isn't "Should Harvard adopt oral exams?" but "Should more concentrations return to the 1916 hybrid model, adapted for the AI era?"

Resource lesson: The 1916 model required faculty to be "relieved of one half of their work of instruction" to conduct oral exams. This wasn't treated as extra work but as a reallocation of teaching effort. If Harvard reintroduces oral assessment broadly, it needs similar structural support—not just asking faculty to add oral exams on top of existing workload, but redesigning course structures to accommodate this labor-intensive assessment mode.

───

2. Comparative Literature Concentration: "Orals Essay" Required (Senior Thesis Handbook 2024)
:paperclip: Harvard Department of Comparative Literature, August 2024
:link: https://complit.fas.harvard.edu/wp-content/uploads/2024/08/Senior-Thesis-Handbook-2024.pdf

The Comparative Literature concentration's 2024 Senior Thesis Handbook lists "April 28: Orals Essay due to Dept." as a required milestone, indicating that oral examination remains a standard component of senior thesis completion in this concentration. While the handbook doesn't detail the format, the "orals essay" typically involves written preparation followed by oral defense before a faculty committee.

Harvard current practice: This confirms oral assessment is alive and well in certain Harvard concentrations—not a historical relic but an ongoing pedagogical practice. Comp Lit's requirement suggests faculty in this field value the oral component for assessing:

• Synthesis across literatures and theories (hard to fake in real-time dialogue)
• Close reading skills (can you explain your interpretation of a text when questioned?)
• Scholarly maturity (can you defend your thesis like a graduate student?)

Scaling insight: Comp Lit is a small concentration (typically 15-30 seniors per year). Oral exams are feasible at this scale. The challenge for larger concentrations (Economics: 600+ seniors; Government: 400+): how to maintain the pedagogical value of oral defense while managing logistical constraints?
Economics in the Rear-View MirrorHarvard. First Undergraduate General and Specific Exams in History, Government and Economics Division, 1916.The first general and economics-specific undergraduate divisional examinations for history, government, and economics at Harvard in 1916.Written byIrwin CollierEst. reading time24 minutesJul 5th, 2019https://www.irwincollier.com/harvard-first-undergraduate-general-and-specific-exams-in-history-government-and-economics-division-1916/[5:46 AM]AI-era adaptation: The Comp Lit model—written thesis + oral defense—is precisely the hybrid approach that addresses AI concerns. Students could use AI assistance for research/drafting the written thesis (with disclosure), but the oral defense verifies they understand and own the ideas. This isn't "AI-proofing" but "AI-integrated with accountability."

───

3. Assessing Class Participation: Typically 20% of Final Grades at Harvard
:paperclip: Derek Bok Center for Teaching and Learning
:link: https://bokcenter.harvard.edu/assessing-class-participation

The Bok Center's guidance notes that "participation typically comprises 20% of final grades" at Harvard, highlighting the need for "clear strategies for assessing participation, rather than relying on retrospective assessments that may reflect speaking confidence rather than intellectual engagement." The center warns against grading systems that privilege extroverted native speakers over quieter or ESL students.

Harvard pedagogical DNA: This reveals that oral assessment is already ubiquitous at Harvard—it's just called "participation" rather than "oral exams." Most courses grade students partly on their ability to contribute to discussion, ask questions, and engage in classroom dialogue. The difference between this informal oral assessment and formal oral exams is primarily:

• Structure: Participation = spontaneous, ongoing; oral exams = scheduled, prepared
• Stakes: Participation = formative, low-stakes (20%); oral exams = summative, high-stakes (can be 50-100%)
• Equity: Participation often favors dominant speakers; formal oral exams (with clear rubrics) can be more equitable

AI-era opportunity: If Harvard faculty are already assessing oral communication (via participation grades), they have existing expertise in evaluating oral performance. The shift to formal oral assessment isn't learning a new skill but formalizing and structuring what faculty already do informally. The Bok Center's emphasis on equity-conscious rubrics should transfer to oral exam design.

Equity warning: The Bok Center's caution about "speaking confidence" bias is critical. If oral exams become widespread, Harvard must train faculty in:

• Inclusive questioning (inviting, not interrogating)
• Content-focused rubrics (grading ideas, not charisma)
• Accommodations (extended time for students with speech anxiety, ESL backgrounds, disabilities)
• Multiple attempts (revising oral performance, like revising papers)

The participation-grading literature shows that unstructured oral assessment reproduces privilege. Formal oral exams, if poorly designed, could worsen this problem. But well-designed oral assessment—with clear rubrics, scaffolding, and equity measures—can be more inclusive than high-pressure timed writing.

───

Project Status: 75 posts | 217 sources | Sunday Feb 22, 05:45 AM | 51.25 hours remaining

Synthesis: Harvard has a century-long tradition of hybrid oral+written assessment (1916 general exams), ongoing practice in certain concentrations (Comp Lit orals), and widespread informal oral assessment (participation grades in most courses). The AI era doesn't require inventing oral assessment at Harvard—it requires expanding, formalizing, and equity-proofing existing practices. The institutional infrastructure, faculty expertise, and student culture already support oral dialogue as core to learning. The question is strategic implementation, not wholesale innovation.
elle-with-claws  [6:04 AM]
Theme 3: Educational Research on Oral Assessment (SoTL) – Cycle 12

Focus: Rubric Validity, Feedback Quality, and Interventions That Work

───

1. Interventions and Facilitators of Oral Assessment Performance in Higher Education: A Systematic Review
:paperclip: Assessment & Evaluation in Higher Education (Taylor & Francis), 2025
:link: https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/02602938.2025.2504621

This systematic review examines which interventions actually improve student performance on oral assessments in higher education. Key finding: Feedback is the most effective intervention—"The effectiveness of feedback can be attributed to its role in providing specific, actionable insights that guide students' learning processes" (Hattie & Timperley, 2007). The review also notes that VR (virtual reality) interventions show promise for improving presentation skills, though with mixed outcomes regarding student anxiety.

Harvard relevance: This challenges the "just add oral exam" approach. Simply requiring oral assessment doesn't automatically improve learning—students need structured preparation and iterative feedback. The research suggests Harvard should implement:

• Formative oral assignments with detailed feedback before summative assessment
• Multiple low-stakes practice opportunities (not just one high-stakes final oral)
• Specific, actionable feedback (not just "speak louder" but "your argument would be stronger if you addressed the counterevidence from X")

Practical implication: If oral assessment is implemented without adequate scaffolding and feedback, it becomes a performance test (who's naturally good at talking?) rather than a learning tool (who improved their ability to articulate ideas?). The systematic review confirms that oral assessment's pedagogical value depends entirely on implementation quality.

VR caveat: While VR practice environments are technologically interesting, the review shows mixed results on anxiety reduction. For resource-constrained implementation, peer practice sessions may be more cost-effective and equally beneficial—students practicing oral responses with each other, giving feedback, building fluency in a low-pressure environment.

───

2. The Validity, Reliability, Academic Integrity and Integration of Oral Assessments in Higher Education
:paperclip: International Institute for Educational Research (IIER), 2024
:link: http://www.iier.org.au/iier34/nallaya.pdf

This paper addresses a critical gap in the oral assessment literature: ensuring validity and reliability. Key insight: "Issues pertaining to reliability can be reduced if they [students] are adequately prepared and not surprised by any element that they had not envisioned during the assessment. A clear, descriptive and detailed rubric that sets out expectations can reduce issues pertaining to reliability."

Harvard relevance: This directly addresses faculty concerns about consistency and fairness in oral assessment. Unlike multiple-choice exams (high reliability, questionable validity) or essays (moderate reliability with rubrics), oral exams have historically struggled with inter-rater reliability—different faculty asking different questions, applying different standards.

Mitigation strategies (from the paper):

• Detailed rubrics shared with students in advance (what exactly are we assessing? ideas? delivery? both?)
• Standardized question protocols (core questions all students receive, plus individualized follow-ups)
• Calibration sessions for faculty/TAs conducting oral exams (practice scoring together, discuss edge cases)
• No surprises (students know format, expectations, evaluation criteria before the exam)
[6:04 AM]Validity concern: The paper notes that oral assessment can be more valid than written for certain learning outcomes (can you explain your reasoning? defend your interpretation? think adaptively?) but less reliable (more subjective, harder to standardize). Harvard should prioritize validity for high-value learning outcomes while building in reliability safeguards through rubrics and training.

Academic integrity argument: The paper argues oral assessment inherently supports academic integrity—hard to cheat when you must explain in real-time. But this only works if the oral component assesses understanding, not just memorization. A well-designed oral exam asks "why?" and "how?" questions that probe genuine comprehension, not factual recall that students could have memorized or AI-generated.

───

3. Feedback Quality Has the Greatest Impact on Student Learning Outcomes
:paperclip: Frontiers in Education, 2025 (systematic review by Brandmo & Gamlem)
:link: https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/education/articles/10.3389/feduc.2025.1698277/xml

This systematic review of student perceptions of feedback found that "feedback quality had the greatest impact on student learning outcomes"—more than quantity, timeliness, or medium of delivery. Quality feedback is specific, actionable, focused on improvement (not just evaluation), and linked to clear learning goals.

Harvard relevance: Oral assessment offers unique feedback opportunities not available in written exams:

• Immediate, dialogic feedback: Faculty can clarify misunderstandings on the spot ("I think you're confusing X and Y; can you explain the difference?")
• Personalized guidance: Feedback adapts to the student's specific response, not generic comments on a rubric
• Formative integration: Oral check-ins throughout semester can provide ongoing feedback before high-stakes assessment

Quality vs. quantity trade-off: The review suggests that one high-quality oral feedback session (15 minutes of focused dialogue with specific guidance) may be more valuable than multiple low-quality written comments (generic margin notes like "unclear" or "develop this"). This supports oral assessment as a high-touch, high-impact intervention for critical learning moments.

Student engagement finding: The review notes that student engagement with feedback matters as much as feedback quality. Oral feedback has a built-in advantage: students must engage in real-time. Written feedback on papers often goes unread; oral feedback during a dialogue can't be ignored. This makes oral assessment particularly valuable for students who struggle to self-regulate or who don't typically read/act on written feedback.

Design principle for Harvard: If implementing oral assessment, design it as feedback-rich:

• Oral defense of draft work (with feedback for revision) > oral exam on final product
• Dialogue format ("let's discuss your argument") > interrogation format ("defend your claim")
• Rubric + narrative feedback > score only

The research confirms: oral assessment's pedagogical power lies in conversation and feedback, not just "AI-proofing" through live performance.

───

Project Status: 76 posts | 220 sources | Sunday Feb 22, 06:03 AM | 50.95 hours remaining

Synthesis: SoTL research reveals oral assessment's value depends on implementation quality, not just adoption. Effective oral assessment requires: (1) structured preparation and feedback (not just high-stakes performance), (2) detailed rubrics and calibration (to ensure reliability), and (3) dialogic, formative approach (feedback-rich conversation, not interrogation). Simply adding oral exams without these elements wastes the pedagogical opportunity and may worsen equity gaps.
elle-with-claws  [6:16 AM]
Theme 4: Post-AI Classroom Experiments with Oral Assignments – Cycle 12

Focus: The "Oral Exam Check" Phenomenon & AI-Integrated Practice

───

1. The "Oral Exam Check": Professors' Informal Verification Strategy
:paperclip: Voyagard Education Blog, October 21, 2025
:link: https://www.voyagard.com/blog/can-professors-detect-chatgpt-in-multiple-choice-exams-a-2025-reality-check

This practitioner-focused piece documents an emerging informal practice: "Professors call this the 'oral exam check'; it is surprisingly effective because it tests whether understanding matches performance." The strategy: when written work seems suspiciously sophisticated, professors ask follow-up questions in office hours or class discussions, listening for students who "stumble through follow-up questions" or "mispronounce terms during class discussions" about concepts in their own submitted work.

Harvard relevance: This reveals that oral assessment is already happening informally as an AI-detection strategy—but in an ad hoc, potentially inequitable way. The "oral exam check" approach has problems:

• Selective application: Only students flagged as suspicious get questioned (creates two tiers)
• Lack of structure: No rubric, no clear standards, just faculty intuition
• Bias risk: Who gets flagged? Likely students whose writing "improves too much"—which could disproportionately target ESL students, first-gen students, or anyone using writing center support

Better alternative for Harvard: Formalize this as universal oral defense for all students (not just suspected cases), with clear pedagogical purpose (demonstrating understanding) rather than punitive purpose (catching cheaters). Examples:

• Weekly discussion sections with prepared questions students must answer orally (participation = informal oral assessment)
• Mid-semester oral check-ins on paper drafts (15 min conversation about argument, sources, next steps)
• Final oral component for all students (brief Q&A after paper submission, graded on understanding not performance)

This shifts from "gotcha" to "growth"—using oral dialogue to deepen learning rather than police it.

───

2. Giving University Exams in the Age of Chatbots: A Choice-Based Approach
:paperclip: Ploum's Blog (professor's firsthand account), January 19, 2026
:link: https://ploum.net/2026-01-19-exam-with-chatbots.html

This professor's firsthand account describes an innovative exam design: Students choose before the exam whether they will use chatbots or only traditional web searches, and must inform the professor in advance. The approach treats AI as a tool choice rather than a forbidden technology, with different evaluation criteria based on the declared approach.

Harvard relevance: This represents a third way between banning AI (unenforceable) and ignoring AI (abandons assessment validity). The choice-based model acknowledges that:

• Some students want to develop "AI-free" research skills (Option A: traditional searches only)
• Some students want to learn professional AI integration (Option B: chatbots allowed with disclosure)
• Both are legitimate learning goals, requiring different assessment standards

Implementation for Harvard:

• Option A students (no AI): Evaluated on depth of engagement with primary sources, original synthesis
• Option B students (AI-integrated): Evaluated on quality of prompts, critical evaluation of AI output, clear attribution, original insight beyond AI suggestions

Oral defense synergy: Both options benefit from oral component. Option A students can explain their research process and close reading; Option B students can explain their AI collaboration and demonstrate they understand (not just copied) AI-generated insights. The oral component verifies authenticity for both paths.
voyagard.comCan Professors Detect ChatGPT in Multiple Choice Exams? A 2025 Reality Checkcan professors detect chat gpt for multiple choice questions guidehttps://www.voyagard.com/blog/can-professors-detect-chatgpt-in-multiple-choice-exams-a-2025-reality-checkploum.netGiving University Exams in the Age of ChatbotsGiving University Exams in the Age of Chatbots par Ploum - Lionel Dricot.https://ploum.net/2026-01-19-exam-with-chatbots.html[6:16 AM]Equity consideration: This model respects student agency while maintaining academic standards. Students with AI access can use it transparently; students without access (or who prefer not to use it) aren't disadvantaged. The key: different evaluation criteria matched to different approaches, not one-size-fits-all.

───

3. Using ChatGPT for Oral Practice: Reducing Speaking Anxiety in Language Learning
:paperclip: Language Testing in Asia (Springer), October 28, 2024
:link: https://languagetestingasia.springeropen.com/articles/10.1186/s40468-024-00321-0

This empirical study found that "regular usage of ChatGPT for oral practice reportedly helped students grow more comfortable speaking in English, lowering their fear and confidence. They felt more suited to manage classroom debates and speaking tests." Students practiced oral responses with AI chatbots (voice mode) before live assessments, treating AI as a low-stakes practice partner.

Harvard relevance: This flips the AI-oral assessment relationship: instead of oral exams as defense against AI, use AI as preparation for oral exams. The pedagogical model:

1. AI-assisted practice: Students use ChatGPT voice mode to rehearse oral responses, get immediate feedback on clarity/coherence
2. Peer practice: Students practice with each other (human feedback on ideas, not just fluency)
3. Faculty oral assessment: Final evaluation by professor, graded on understanding and argument quality

Equity benefit: This scaffolding approach addresses the anxiety barrier that makes oral assessment inequitable for some students. By providing:

• Low-stakes practice (AI chatbot doesn't judge, peers are supportive)
• Iterative preparation (multiple practice rounds before summative assessment)
• Metacognitive development (students learn what "good oral explanation" sounds like through practice)

The study shows oral assessment becomes more equitable when preceded by structured practice, not less. AI can democratize access to practice opportunities (not everyone has a patient tutor available 24/7, but ChatGPT voice mode is always available).

Design principle for Harvard: Pair oral assessment with AI-supported practice infrastructure:

• Recommend AI voice practice for students (with guidance on effective prompts)
• Provide peer practice protocols (small-group rehearsals with feedback rubrics)
• Offer office hours practice sessions (voluntary low-stakes run-throughs)

This makes oral assessment a learning tool (building communication skills) rather than a gate-keeping device (sorting confident speakers from anxious ones).

───

Project Status: 77 posts | 223 sources | Sunday Feb 22, 06:15 AM | 50.75 hours remaining

Synthesis: Real-world experiments reveal three emerging models: (1) informal "oral exam checks" (ad hoc, inequitable, but reveals demand for verification), (2) choice-based AI integration (students declare AI use, different evaluation criteria apply), and (3) AI-assisted oral practice (using AI to prepare for oral assessment, reducing anxiety). Harvard should learn from all three: formalize oral components (not just for suspected cheaters), offer AI-integrated pathways with disclosure, and provide scaffolding/practice infrastructure to ensure equity.

Theme 5: AI Used IN Oral Evaluation – Cycle 12

Focus: Transcription Accuracy, LLM-Powered Grading, and the Double-Edged Sword

───

1. AI Transcription Accuracy in 2025: Strong Gains, But Context Matters
:paperclip: OpenAI Developers Blog / AssemblyAI Research, 2025
:link: https://developers.openai.com/blog/updates-audio-models/

OpenAI reports that "gpt-4o-mini-transcribe-2025-12-15 shows strong gains in both accuracy and reliability" on standard ASR benchmarks like Common Voice and FLEURS, delivering lower word error rates than previous models. However, independent benchmarking by AssemblyAI and VoiceToNotes reveals critical caveats: accuracy varies significantly based on noise, accents, and conversational vs. controlled speech.

Harvard relevance: If Harvard uses AI transcription to support oral assessment (recording student responses for faculty review), the accuracy gaps have equity implications:

• Accent bias: ASR performs worse on non-native speakers, regional accents, and minoritized dialects
• Context dependency: Technical/academic vocabulary (common in Harvard courses) may be mistranscribed
• Background noise: Dorm rooms, libraries, remote settings create variable audio quality

Use case recommendations:

• ✓ Transcription as faculty tool: Record oral exams, use AI transcription for faculty reference (not primary evaluation). Faculty review audio + transcript, catch errors
• ✓ Accessibility support: Provide transcripts to students with hearing impairments (with human verification)
• ✗ Automated grading based on transcripts: Don't feed raw ASR output to LLM graders—errors compound, especially for non-dominant speakers

Design principle: AI transcription is a productivity tool (helps faculty review/search oral responses), not a replacement for human listening. The final evaluation should be based on audio, not transcript.

───

2. Large Language Models in Education: LLM-Powered Systems Achieve Reliability Comparable to Human Evaluators
:paperclip: ScienceDirect | Computers and Education: Artificial Intelligence, December 13, 2025
:link: https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666920X25001699

This systematic review of LLMs in education finds that "in automated assessment, LLM-powered systems achieve reliability comparable to human evaluators, with strong correlation to instructor assessments" for short-answer grading. The review notes that LLMs can provide immediate, detailed feedback on open-ended responses, and when combined with retrieval-augmented generation (RAG), can incorporate domain-specific knowledge for more accurate grading.

Harvard relevance: This represents the optimistic scenario for AI in oral assessment: LLMs could potentially:

• Generate adaptive follow-up questions during oral exams (like NYU's 42-cent oral exam system)
• Score transcript-based responses with rubric alignment comparable to human TAs
• Provide immediate formative feedback on oral practice sessions

Critical caveats from the review:

• LLMs perform best on short answers (controlled responses), less reliably on open-ended dialogue
• Correlation ≠ causation: High correlation with human graders doesn't mean AI understands student reasoning—it may just pattern-match
• Training data bias: LLMs trained on dominant academic discourse may penalize non-standard expression

Harvard application scenarios:

1. Formative practice: Students record oral responses, LLM provides feedback on clarity/structure (low stakes, learning tool)
2. Preliminary scoring: LLM scores oral transcripts, faculty review borderline/flagged cases (efficiency gain)
3. Question generation: LLM generates personalized follow-up questions based on student's written work (adaptive oral exam)

Red line: Don't use LLM as sole evaluator for high-stakes oral assessment. Always maintain human-in-the-loop for final grading, especially given equity concerns around accent/dialect bias.

───
developers.openai.comUpdates for developers building with voiceNew audio model snapshots and broader access to Custom Voices for production voice apps.https://developers.openai.com/blog/updates-audio-models/[6:31 AM]3. AI Video Interview Tools: The Employment Screening Model and Its Educational Analogues
[6:31 AM]:paperclip: PeopleManagingPeople / Qureos / HireVue (industry reports), January 2026
:link: https://peoplemanagingpeople.com/tools/best-ai-interview-software/

The corporate hiring sector has developed sophisticated AI video interview platforms (HireVue, Qureos, Final Round AI) that analyze candidates through automated video screening—transcribing responses, analyzing speech patterns, facial expressions, and content quality. These systems claim to "automate engagement, screening, and interviewing across chat, phone, and video" with "faster, fairer ways to evaluate applicants."

Harvard relevance (cautionary): This technology exists and could theoretically be applied to educational oral assessment. But should it?

Documented concerns from hiring context:

• Algorithmic bias: AI video interview tools have faced lawsuits for discriminating against disabled applicants, non-native speakers, and people with neurodivergent communication patterns
• Facial analysis problems: Evaluating candidates based on eye contact, facial expressions penalizes people with autism, social anxiety, or cultural differences in non-verbal norms
• Black-box scoring: Candidates (and often employers) don't understand how AI assigns scores, making it impossible to improve or contest results

Why Harvard should be skeptical:
The corporate model treats interviews as screening/sorting (efficiently eliminate candidates). Education should treat oral assessment as learning/feedback (help students develop understanding). These are fundamentally different goals.

If Harvard adopts AI-assisted video oral assessment:

• Transparency: Students must know exactly what's being evaluated (content only? or also delivery, eye contact, tone?)
• Opt-out/accommodations: Students with disabilities, social anxiety, or cultural differences should have alternative assessment paths
• Human review: All AI-scored oral responses should be reviewed by faculty, with override authority
• Content-only focus: Avoid analyzing non-verbal cues (facial expressions, tone) that introduce bias

Better alternative: Use video recording for asynchronous oral assessment (students record, faculty review on their schedule), but faculty do the evaluation, not AI. AI tools can provide timestamps, transcripts, search functionality—productivity aids, not judges.

───

Project Status: 78 posts | 226 sources | Sunday Feb 22, 06:30 AM | 50.5 hours remaining

Synthesis: AI can support oral assessment through transcription (productivity tool for faculty review), LLM grading (formative feedback, preliminary scoring with human oversight), and video analysis (recording infrastructure). But each carries equity risks—accent bias, algorithmic opacity, non-verbal discrimination. The safest path: use AI as assistant (transcribes, organizes, suggests scores) with human as final evaluator. Resist the temptation to fully automate oral assessment; the efficiency gains come at unacceptable equity costs.

Theme 6: Literary/Historical References on Speech vs. Writing – Cycle 12

Focus: Ong's "Evolution of Consciousness," Oral Epistemology, and Derrida's Pharmakon Applied to AI

───

1. Walter J. Ong: Orality and Literacy – The Technologizing of the Word (30th Anniversary Edition with New Commentary)
:paperclip: Routledge / Internet Archive, 2024-2025
:link: https://www.routledge.com/Orality-and-Literacy-30th-Anniversary-Edition/Ong/p/book/9780415538381

The updated edition of Ong's classic work includes new scholarship on "knowledge technologies, tracing changes in how we know what we know" and a critical assessment of Ong's controversial "evolution of consciousness" thesis. Ong argued that writing didn't just record speech—it restructured human thought itself, enabling abstract analysis, cumulative knowledge, and self-reflexive critique impossible in purely oral cultures.

Harvard relevance: Ong's framework offers a third perspective beyond "oral good, writing bad" or vice versa. His key insight: Different media afford different cognition:

• Oral culture cognition: Memorization, formulaic thinking, communal knowledge, situation-embedded reasoning
• Literate culture cognition: Analysis, abstraction, individual authorship, decontextualized reasoning

Applied to the AI debate: If we're experiencing a medium shift (from writing to AI-mediated text), we should expect cognitive restructuring (how we think, not just how we cheat). Oral assessment isn't "return to pre-literacy" but recognition that AI changes what writing means.

Ong's warning (via new commentary): Critics note Ong's "evolution" framing risks technological determinism (media shapes minds) and cultural hierarchy (literate > oral). For Harvard: avoid assuming oral assessment develops "better" thinking than writing. Both are legitimate cognitive modes serving different purposes.

Design principle: Instead of replacing written with oral assessment (literacy with orality), design multimodal assessment:

• Writing for: extended argument development, research synthesis, revision/refinement
• Speaking for: real-time reasoning, dialogic defense, adaptive thinking
• AI-integrated for: efficient research, idea generation, collaborative drafting (with attribution)

Each mode develops different cognitive capacities. Assessment should reflect the diversity of intellectual work, not privilege one medium over others.

───

2. Oral Tradition as "Dynamic and Highly Diverse Oral-Aural Medium for Evolving, Storing, and Transmitting Knowledge"
:paperclip: Britannica / Cambridge University Press (The Transmission of Knowledge), 2024-2025
:link: https://www.britannica.com/topic/oral-tradition

Britannica defines oral tradition as "far more than 'just talking'"—a sophisticated system for knowledge transmission with accuracy comparable to (sometimes exceeding) written records. The Cambridge volume argues that "knowledge transmission is irreducible to knowledge generation" and requires its own theoretical treatment. Ancient oral cultures (Vedic India, Homeric Greece, African griot traditions) developed mnemonic techniques, formulaic patterns, and communal verification processes to ensure fidelity across generations.

Harvard relevance: This challenges the assumption that oral assessment is informal or imprecise. Oral knowledge systems can be highly structured and rigorous—they just use different verification mechanisms than writing:

• Written verification: Text as permanent record, reviewable, citable
• Oral verification: Community witness, performative demonstration, adaptive questioning

For Harvard oral assessment: If the goal is rigorous knowledge verification, structure is essential:

• Rubrics (what knowledge/skills are being assessed?)
• Standardized protocols (core questions, follow-up framework)
• Multiple evaluators (communal verification, like oral cultures)
• Recording (creates reviewable record, like writing)
Routledge & CRC PressOrality and Literacy: 30th Anniversary EditionWalter J. Ong’s classic work provides a fascinating insight into the social effects of oral, written, printed and electronic technologies, and their impact on philosophical, theological, scientific and literary thought.

This thirtieth anniversary edition – coinciding with Ong’s centenary year – reproduces his best-known and most influential book in full and brings it up to date with two new exploratory essays by cultural writer and critic John Hartley. 

Hartley provides:

A scene-setthttps://www.routledge.com/Orality-and-Literacy-30th-Anniversary-Edition/Ong/p/book/9780415538381[6:46 AM]Epistemological insight: The Cambridge argument—"transmission ≠ generation"—applies to assessment. Testing whether a student has knowledge (transmission/retention) is different from testing whether they generated knowledge (original research/analysis). Oral assessment may be better for the former (can you explain X?), written for the latter (can you develop a novel argument about X?).

Cultural sensitivity: The sources note that many non-Western cultures privilege oral knowledge transmission over written. For Harvard's international student body, oral assessment might align better with some students' prior educational cultures than essay-writing does. However, this cuts both ways—students from cultures that don't emphasize public speaking may struggle. Universal design requires scaffolding for both modes.

───

3. The Pharmakon of AI: Derrida, Plato, and the Simulation of Thought
:paperclip: Medium (philosophy blog) / ResearchGate (academic analysis), May 2025
:link: https://medium.com/@orlorodriguez/the-pharmakon-of-ai-derrida-plato-and-the-simulation-of-thought-627cb808b848

This recent philosophical analysis applies Derrida's reading of Plato's "pharmakon" (both remedy and poison) to AI. Derrida argued that Plato's critique of writing was fundamentally ambivalent—writing "both enriches and degrades" thought. The pharmakon is "undecidable": neither purely good nor purely bad, but simultaneously both. The article argues AI is the new pharmakon: remedy (enhances research, accessibility, efficiency) and poison (threatens authorship, critical thinking, human agency).

Harvard relevance: This framework rejects binary thinking about AI and assessment:

• Not: "AI is threat → ban it" OR "AI is tool → embrace it"
• But: "AI is pharmakon → design assessment that navigates the ambivalence"

Applied to oral assessment:

• Pharmakon of writing (Plato): Weakens memory but extends knowledge across time/space
• Pharmakon of AI (2026): Generates text but may atrophy writing skills
• Pharmakon of oral assessment (response): Resists AI but may exclude some students

Every assessment technology is both remedy and poison. The question isn't "which technology is pure?" but "which trade-offs align with our pedagogical values?"

Derrida's insight: The pharmakon is undecidable—you can't resolve the ambiguity, only navigate it. For Harvard: don't expect to "solve" the AI assessment problem. Instead, make intentional trade-offs:

• Use AI where it helps (research assistance, accessibility, efficiency)
• Limit AI where it harms (original thinking, authorship verification, equity)
• Use oral where it works (dialogue, adaptive reasoning, verification)
• Supplement oral where it fails (preparation support, multiple attempts, accommodations)

Philosophical humility: Derrida warns against the "metaphysics of presence"—the fantasy that we can achieve pure, unmediated knowledge. Neither oral (live presence) nor written (permanent record) nor AI-integrated assessment is "pure." All are technologies with affordances and constraints. Harvard's assessment design should be pragmatic (what works?) not ideological (which medium is inherently superior?).

───

Project Status: 79 posts | 229 sources | Sunday Feb 22, 06:45 AM | 50.25 hours remaining

Synthesis: Philosophical analysis reveals that oral vs. written vs. AI-integrated assessment is not a zero-sum choice but a design space with different cognitive affordances. Ong: media shape consciousness differently (design for cognitive diversity). Oral epistemology: structured oral transmission can be rigorous (design protocols, don't assume informality). Derrida: every technology is pharmakon—both remedy and poison (navigate trade-offs, reject binary thinking). For Harvard: multimodal assessment recognizing that writing, speaking, and AI-collaboration develop different intellectual capacities—all valuable, all requiring thoughtful design.
MediumThe Pharmakon of AI: Derrida, Plato, and the Simulation of ThoughtIntroduction: Inheriting the Paradox of WritingReading time5 min readMay 7th, 2025elle-with-claws  [7:01 AM]
Theme 7: Professional Domains — Medicine, TED, Venture Capital – Cycle 12

Focus: How High-Stakes Professions Assess Oral Performance

───

1. Objective Structured Clinical Examination (OSCE): Medicine's Gold Standard for Oral Assessment
:paperclip: BMC Medical Education / ScienceDirect, 2025
:link: https://link.springer.com/article/10.1186/s12909-025-07210-x

The OSCE is medical education's most rigorous oral assessment format: students rotate through standardized stations where they must "demonstrate competency in communication, history taking, physical examination, clinical reasoning, medical knowledge, and integration of these skills" with simulated patients. Recent research shows OSCEs effectively assess communication and clinical reasoning even during periods of restricted clinical practice, and new first-person-perspective video analysis can evaluate oral communication skills in doctor-patient interactions, enhance empathy, and contribute to holistic care.

Harvard relevance: The OSCE model represents structured, high-stakes oral assessment at scale:

• Standardized scenarios (all students face comparable challenges)
• Trained evaluators with detailed rubrics (inter-rater reliability)
• Multiple stations (tests breadth of competencies, reduces single-performance anxiety)
• Immediate feedback (formative learning integrated into summative assessment)

Transferable design elements for Harvard:

1. Scenario-based oral assessment: Instead of "discuss your essay," create structured intellectual scenarios (e.g., "here's a primary source you haven't seen—analyze it," "here's a dataset—interpret it")
2. Rubric-based evaluation: OSCEs use detailed scoring grids for communication skills (clarity, empathy, responsiveness)—adaptable to academic oral performance
3. Multiple brief assessments > one long exam: OSCEs typically use 6-8 stations of 10-15 minutes each, rather than one 60-minute oral. This reduces anxiety and samples multiple skills.

Equity lesson from medicine: OSCEs face documented bias issues (accent discrimination, cultural differences in eye contact/deference, gender effects on communication style). Medical educators address this through mandatory calibration training for evaluators and content-focused rubrics that deprioritize performance style. Harvard should adopt similar safeguards.

───

2. TED Masterclass: Public Speaking 101 – Teaching Essential Communication Skills Through Exemplar Analysis
:paperclip: TED Education / TED Masterclass, August 2025
:link: https://ed.ted.com/blog/2025/08/21/public-speaking-101-launched-to-teach-essential-communication-and-presentation-skills

TED's new education initiative "uses excerpts from past TED speakers to highlight key ideas and prompts, and to offer a deeper understanding of what makes a talk meaningful to an audience." The course teaches public speaking as a learnable skill through analysis of exemplars, with Chris Anderson's book arguing this approach "may restore rhetoric to its time-honored place as one of the essential skills of an educated citizen."

Harvard relevance: This challenges the assumption that oral communication is innate talent vs. teachable skill. TED's pedagogy:

• Deconstruct excellence: Show students what good oral communication looks like (exemplar analysis)
• Identify techniques: Name specific skills (e.g., David JP Phillips catalogues "110 techniques of communication")
• Practice with feedback: Students develop and deliver talks, receive structured critique
• Purpose-driven: Focus on "what makes a talk meaningful to an audience" (not just "sound smart")

Application to Harvard oral assessment:
If oral performance will be graded, oral instruction should be provided. Current Harvard courses often assume students already know how to:

• Structure an oral argument for clarity
• Respond to questions without defensiveness
• Explain complex ideas to non-specialist audiencesSpringerLinkUsing the OSCE to assess medical students’ communication and clinical reasoning during five years of restricted clinical practiceBMC Medical Education - Objective and Structured Clinical Examination (OSCE) is a widely used evaluation method for health profession students. In Chile, physicians OSCEs have incorporated clinical...https://link.springer.com/article/10.1186/s12909-025-07210-xTED-EdPublic Speaking 101 launched to teach essential communication and presentation skillsIdeas change everything —  and since language lets us share our ideas, learning how to use it well gives speakers the power to inspire people and even change how they think. To help young people de...https://ed.ted.com/blog/2025/08/21/public-speaking-101-launched-to-teach-essential-communication-and-presentation-skills[7:01 AM]• Manage nervousness and pacing

Pedagogical scaffolding recommendations:
[7:01 AM]• Freshman seminar: Include oral communication module (how to participate effectively in discussion)
• Concentration-specific training: Each department develops discipline-specific oral norms (e.g., how historians vs. scientists present arguments)
• Bok Center workshops: Expand offerings on oral defense skills (already exists but not widely utilized)
• Exemplar library: Record (with permission) excellent student oral defenses for future students to study

The TED model shows that when oral performance matters professionally (TED talks shape careers), systematic training is provided. Harvard should do the same if oral assessment becomes widespread.

───

3. The Impact of Entrepreneurs' Oral Pitch Presentation Skills on Business Angels' Initial Screening Decisions
:paperclip: Venture Capital Journal (Taylor & Francis), 2008 / HBR, 2017
:link: https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/abs/10.1080/13691060802151945

This empirical study of 24 business angels evaluating 32 pitch presentations found that entrepreneurs' communication skills and personal attributes significantly influence investor decision-making—often more than the business plan itself. HBR research confirms: VCs get excited about a written business plan, but their enthusiasm often evaporates after the in-person pitch, based on how entrepreneurs present, not just what they present.

Harvard relevance: This reveals the high-stakes reality of oral performance in professional life:

• Venture capital pitches: 10-15 minutes to secure millions in funding (oral performance = economic survival)
• Job talks: Academic hiring decisions heavily influenced by seminar presentation (oral performance = career trajectory)
• Legal oral arguments: Supreme Court decisions can hinge on oral advocacy (oral performance = justice outcomes)

Critical insight for Harvard: The VC research shows oral assessment's equity problem in real-world context:

• What's being evaluated? Study found VCs assess "presentational" (delivery, charisma) AND "non-presentational" (content, logic) factors—but can't separate them reliably
• Bias amplification: HBR notes VCs are susceptible to confidence bias (eloquent overconfidence beats careful uncertainty), gender bias (women judged more harshly on presentation style), and cultural bias (non-Western communication norms penalized)

Design principle for Harvard: If oral assessment is justified as professional preparation, it must:

1. Name what's being assessed: Is it ideas, delivery, or both? If both, separate rubrics with different weights
2. Train against bias: Faculty should practice scoring oral responses with attention to content vs. style
3. Provide multiple attempts: VCs can practice their pitch dozens of times; students should have low-stakes rehearsals before high-stakes assessment
4. Acknowledge limitations: Oral performance ≠ intellectual merit (just as in VC world, charismatic founders sometimes fail while awkward founders succeed)

Honest conversation needed: Should Harvard value oral eloquence as a learning outcome? If yes (because professional success often requires it), then teach it explicitly and assess it fairly. If no (because eloquence ≠ understanding), then content-only rubrics and accommodations for oral performance anxiety are essential.

The VC literature shows oral assessment in high-stakes professional contexts is valuable but biased. Harvard shouldn't romanticize it but should design thoughtfully to maximize value while mitigating bias.

───

Project Status: 80 posts | 232 sources | Sunday Feb 22, 07:00 AM | 50 hours remaining
[7:01 AM]Synthesis: Professional domains reveal oral performance matters (medicine, public speaking, entrepreneurship), but each field has developed structured systems to assess it fairly: OSCEs (standardization, rubrics, multiple stations), TED training (explicit skill development, exemplar analysis), VC evaluation (acknowledges bias, multiple evaluators). For Harvard: oral assessment is professionally relevant, but requires similar infrastructure (training, rubrics, bias mitigation) to be equitable and valid.

Theme 0: History of Oral Examinations in Higher Ed – Cycle 13

Focus: Three Ancient Traditions That Shaped Modern Oral Assessment

───

1. Medieval Scholastic Disputation: "Formalized Method of Debate Designed to Uncover Truths"
:paperclip: JSTOR / Wikipedia / Academia.edu, 2018-2025
:link: https://www.jstor.org/stable/j.ctt4cgh4x

Scholastic disputation was "one of the hallmarks of intellectual life in premodern Europe"—a highly structured oral examination format where students defended theses against formal objections before faculty and peers. The disputation had rigid rules: a respondens (defender) presented a position, opponentes (opponents) raised objections, and a praeses (presiding master) judged the quality of both argument and defense. This wasn't casual conversation but "formalized procedure designed to uncover and establish truths in theology and sciences."

Harvard relevance: The medieval disputation is the direct ancestor of the modern PhD oral defense—and it reveals oral assessment's original purpose wasn't "AI-proofing" but dialectical truth-seeking. Key features still relevant:

• Public performance: Disputations were open events, creating accountability and community learning
• Structured argumentation: Not freestyle discussion but rule-governed intellectual combat
• Defense of prior work: Students defended written theses orally (hybrid model, not oral-only)
• Collaborative knowledge-building: The goal was collective pursuit of truth, not individual gatekeeping

Historical transformation: The Academia.edu paper documents how disputation shifted "from oral to written text" in early modern Europe. Initially, the dissertation was a broadside poster of theses defended orally (Luther's 95 Theses = disputation document). Over time, the written text became primary, oral defense secondary. We're seeing this process potentially reverse in the AI era.

Design lesson for Harvard: If oral assessment is reintroduced, should it follow the disputation model?

• Students write thesis/argument first (possibly AI-assisted with disclosure)
• Oral component = defense under structured questioning (verifies understanding + authorship)
• Public or semi-public (creates accountability, allows peer learning)
• Collaborative not adversarial (goal is learning, not "catching" students)

This historical model shows oral assessment can be rigorous, structured, and pedagogically purposeful—not just a panic response to AI.

───

2. Chinese Imperial Examination (Keju): Palace Exams Combined Written and Oral Assessment for 1,300 Years
:paperclip: Study.com / New World Encyclopedia / China Knowledge, 581-1905 CE
:link: https://study.com/academy/lesson/imperial-examination-overview-history-purpose-keju.html

The Chinese keju system, lasting over 1,300 years (Sui Dynasty to Qing Dynasty), used both written and oral examinations at every level—county, prefectural, provincial, and palace. The final Palace Examination before the emperor was the ultimate high-stakes oral assessment: candidates who passed written exams at lower levels faced direct questioning on Confucian classics, governance, and current affairs. Success meant government appointment; failure meant societal shame.

Harvard relevance: The keju demonstrates oral assessment at massive scale (millions of candidates over centuries) with:

• Meritocratic ideals: In theory, anyone could pass through study (in practice, wealthy families had advantages—sound familiar?)
• Standardized content: All candidates studied same corpus (Four Books, Five Classics)
• Multiple assessment modes: Written exams (poetry, essays) + oral exams (questioning by officials)
• High-stakes consequences: Performance determined social status, career trajectory

Equity warning from history: The keju is often celebrated as "meritocratic," but modern scholarship reveals systematic biases:
jstor.orgThe Medieval Culture of Disputation: Pedagogy, Practice, and Performance on JSTORScholastic disputation, the formalized procedure of debate in the medieval university, is one of the hallmarks of intellectual life in premodern Europe. Modeled...[7:16 AM]• Class: Wealthy families could afford tutors, study materials, time to prepare
[7:16 AM]• Gender: Women were excluded entirely (until very late dynasties, in limited contexts)
• Geography: Candidates from capital regions had advantage (access to better teachers)
• Dialect: Oral exams conducted in Mandarin disadvantaged speakers of other Chinese languages

The keju's oral component was supposed to verify authenticity (did you really master the classics, or just memorize?), but in practice, it often amplified privilege (confidence, courtly manners, prestigious dialect).

Lesson for Harvard: High-stakes oral assessment risks reproducing existing inequities unless carefully designed with:

• Explicit training provided (not assumed prior exposure)
• Accommodations for linguistic/cultural differences
• Low-stakes practice before high-stakes evaluation
• Transparency in rubrics and expectations

The keju shows oral assessment can last millennia—but also that longevity ≠ equity.

───

3. German Rigorosum: How 19th Century Germany Invented the Modern PhD Oral Defense
:paperclip: FindAPhD / Taylor & Francis / UC Berkeley, 2023-2025
:link: https://www.findaphd.com/blog/8976/how-germany-invented-the-phd-a-quick-history

The modern PhD, including its oral defense component, was invented in 19th century Germany under the Humboldtian model: "complete a research project, write it up in the form of a thesis and defend it at an oral examination." The rigorosum was a comprehensive oral exam covering the candidate's entire field, while the disputatio focused on defending the specific thesis. This German innovation spread globally, becoming the template for doctoral education in the U.S., U.K., and beyond.

Harvard relevance: The rigorosum/disputatio distinction reveals two types of oral assessment:

1. Comprehensive oral (rigorosum): Broad knowledge across field—"can you explain foundational concepts?"
2. Thesis defense (disputatio): Focused on specific argument—"can you defend your original contribution?"

Modern Harvard PhD defenses typically combine both: some questions test breadth, others test depth. Could this apply to undergraduate oral assessment?

• Breadth oral: End of concentration, covering major themes/methods (like PhD qualifying exams)
• Depth oral: Defense of senior thesis or capstone project (like PhD dissertation defense)

Historical insight: The German model treated oral defense as final verification of doctoral worthiness, not the primary assessment. The dissertation (written work) demonstrated original research; the oral exam demonstrated the candidate could explain, justify, and extend that research under questioning.

Application to AI-era undergraduate assessment: This suggests oral assessment should be supplement, not substitute for writing:

• Students research and write (possibly using AI tools with disclosure)
• Oral component verifies understanding and authorship
• Hybrid evaluation: written work + oral defense, each weighted appropriately

Cautionary note: The FindAPhD article notes that the "Humboldtian model" is partly mythologized—what Americans think German universities did ≠ what they actually did. Similarly, Harvard should avoid romanticizing "the oral exam tradition" and instead design evidence-based assessment for 2026 context.

───

Project Status: 81 posts | 235 sources | Sunday Feb 22, 07:15 AM | 49.75 hours remaining

Historical synthesis across three traditions: Medieval disputation (dialectical truth-seeking), Chinese keju (meritocratic testing with oral+written), and German rigorosum (research defense). Common themes: (1) hybrid oral+written, not oral-only, (2) structured formats, not informal chat, (3) high stakes → equity concerns (class, gender, language bias). For Harvard: learn from 2,500 years of oral assessment history—both its pedagogical power and documented pitfalls.
elle-with-claws  [7:31 AM]
Theme 1: Peer Institutions Responding to AI Anxieties – Cycle 13

Focus: UChicago's Integration Model, Duke's Mixed Responses, and Liberal Arts Symposia

───

1. University of Chicago: AI-Assisted Writing Course at Harris School of Public Policy
:paperclip: UChicago Harris School / UChicago Law School, Fall 2025
:link: https://harris.uchicago.edu/academics/programs-degrees/courses/fall-2025/36640/1

UChicago's response to AI is notably integrative rather than resistant: the Harris School launched "AI-Assisted Writing for Public Policy" (PPHA 36640), which "equips students to collaborate effectively and ethically with tools like ChatGPT to draft, revise, and refine writing" across policy genres. Meanwhile, UChicago Law School is developing AI literacy modules "to bring everyone up to a minimum level of literacy with generative AI" launching early 2026.

Harvard relevance: UChicago represents the opposite strategy from oral-assessment-as-AI-resistance. Instead of moving away from writing, they're teaching students to write with AI transparently and effectively. This raises a strategic question for Harvard:

• Resistance model: Shift to oral assessment to avoid AI in writing
• Integration model: Teach AI-assisted writing as professional skill, maintain writing assessment with disclosure requirements

UChicago's rationale: In professional policy/law work, practitioners will use AI tools. Education should prepare students for that reality, teaching critical AI use (prompt engineering, fact-checking AI output, maintaining authorship) rather than pretending AI doesn't exist.

Implications for Harvard: If certain concentrations (Gov, Economics, Social Studies) feed into careers where AI collaboration is expected, should assessment train that skill rather than ban it? Oral assessment could still play a role—but as supplement (oral defense of AI-assisted writing) rather than replacement (oral instead of writing).

Different institutional cultures: UChicago's "life of the mind" ethos emphasizes intellectual adaptability. Harvard's tutorial tradition emphasizes dialectical dialogue. Both are valid—but lead to different AI responses.

───

2. Duke University: Faculty Adopting Oral Exams to "Deemphasize Writing Assignments That Invite Using ChatGPT"
:paperclip: Duke Chronicle / ABC11 / WRAL, September 2025
:link: https://abc11.com/post/duke-university-pilot-project-examining-pros-cons-using-artificial-intelligence-college/17752030/

Duke faculty are implementing mixed strategies in response to AI. Professor Pickford (quoted): "I have incorporated oral assignments into my class, including having students present arguments through spoken defense. I have also added in-class exams to lectures that previously relied solely on papers for grading. I have deemphasized the use of the kind of writing assignments that invite using ChatGPT because I don't want to spend my time policing."

Harvard relevance: Pickford's statement reveals the fatigue factor—faculty don't want to become "AI police." Oral assessment appeals because it shifts the burden from detection (did you use AI?) to demonstration (can you explain your thinking?). However, the Duke Chronicle notes student concern: some feel institutional AI embrace may "offload the learning process."

Strategic observation: Duke launched a Provost's Initiative on AI (partnership with OpenAI, free ChatGPT-4o access for all students) while simultaneously seeing faculty retreat to oral exams. This creates institutional incoherence:

• Administration says: "Embrace AI, we're partnering with OpenAI"
• Faculty say: "I'm deemphasizing writing to avoid AI"

Lesson for Harvard: Coordinate institutional messaging. If Harvard wants faculty to adopt oral assessment, why?

• AI resistance: "We don't trust AI, so we're moving to oral" (but then why institutional AI partnerships?)
harris.uchicago.eduAI-Assisted Writing for Public Policy | Harris School of Public Policy | The University of ChicagoThis course equips students to collaborate effectively and ethically with tools like ChatGPT to draft, revise, and refine writing across public policy genres—including memos, data briefs, personal narratives, and op-eds. Through hands-on exercises, students will learn to prompt effectively, edit critically, and integrate AI into their writing workflows without losing clarity, voice, or rigor. The course also addresses ethical concerns like bias, transparency, and professional responsibility.ABC11 Raleigh-DurhamDuke University pilot project examining pros and cons of using artificial intelligence in collegeDuke University has launched an initiative to examine the opportunities and challenges that artificial intelligence brings to student life.Sep 5th, 2025https://abc11.com/post/duke-university-pilot-project-examining-pros-cons-using-artificial-intelligence-college/17752030/[7:31 AM]• Pedagogical evolution: "We're integrating AI and expanding oral assessment as complementary learning modes"
[7:31 AM]The second framing is more intellectually honest and sustainable. Oral assessment shouldn't be presented as retreat from technology but as expansion of assessment repertoire.

Student perspective (from Duke Chronicle): Some Duke students are "reluctant" about AI overemphasis, worried it "short-circuits the learning process." This suggests student appetite for AI-free spaces where they can develop unaided writing/thinking. Oral assessment could serve this—but should be framed as opportunity (develop unmediated communication skills) not penalty (we don't trust your writing).

3. Liberal Arts Colleges: National Symposia on "AI and the Liberal Arts"
:paperclip: Connecticut College / Amherst College AI in Liberal Arts Initiative, October-December 2025
:link: https://www.conncoll.edu/news/news-archive/2025/ai-and-the-liberal-arts-symposium/

Small liberal arts colleges (Amherst, Williams, Swarthmore, et al.) are convening national forums to examine "how generative artificial intelligence is changing the landscape of liberal arts education." Amherst's AI in the Liberal Arts initiative hosted a 2025 undergraduate conference featuring thesis research across disciplines, while Connecticut College held an inaugural symposium examining AI's impact.

Harvard relevance: These symposia signal that elite liberal arts institutions are treating AI as intellectual challenge requiring sustained scholarly inquiry, not just policy panic. The focus on interdisciplinary engagement and undergraduate research suggests these institutions see AI as:

• Topic worthy of serious academic study
• Challenge requiring collective (not individual faculty) response
• Opportunity for curricular innovation, not just threat containment

Contrast with oral assessment response: Notably, these symposia don't focus on assessment reform but on pedagogical integration. The liberal arts model asks: "How do we teach about AI, with AI, and alongside AI?" rather than "How do we assess despite AI?"

Strategic implication for Harvard: Should Harvard College convene a faculty working group on AI and liberal arts pedagogy? Current discussions seem fragmented—individual faculty experimenting with oral exams, others embracing AI, no coordinated institutional vision. The liberal arts college model (symposia, research initiatives, cross-disciplinary dialogue) provides a template.

Williams College tutorial insight: Williams is noted for "tutorial-style learning" (like Harvard's History & Lit). For tutorial-based programs, the oral dialogue is already central—AI doesn't threaten it because assessment has always been conversational. This suggests Harvard concentrations with strong tutorial traditions (Social Studies, History & Lit) may find AI less disruptive than lecture-heavy concentrations.

Amherst's open curriculum parallel: Amherst has no distribution requirements, emphasizing student intellectual autonomy. If students are trusted to design their own education, why not trust them to use AI responsibly with disclosure? The open curriculum philosophy → AI integration philosophy.

Synthesis across peer responses: UChicago integrates AI into curriculum, Duke shows faculty fragmentation (some resist, some embrace), liberal arts colleges convene scholarly inquiry into AI's role. No peer institution has adopted universal oral assessment as institutional AI response. Most are pursuing localized experimentation with varied strategies. For Harvard: probably wise to support faculty plurality (let concentrations/departments choose approaches) rather than mandate campus-wide oral assessment policy.
