Theme 2: History of Oral Assignments at Harvard – Cycle 13

Focus: Folklore & Mythology's Oral Research Tradition, GSAS Qualifying Exams, and Tutorial Pedagogy

───

1. Folklore & Mythology: "Concentrators Conduct Independent Research on the Material, Oral, Written, or Performed Forms"
:paperclip: Harvard Folklore & Mythology Program, founded 1967
:link: https://folkmyth.fas.harvard.edu/

Harvard's oldest interdisciplinary concentration (founded 1967) explicitly centers oral traditions as primary research material—not just oral assessment but oral culture as object of study. The program description states: "Concentrators conduct independent research on the material, oral, written, or performed forms of folklore and mythology in a variety of cultures." Requirements include FM 98a: Oral Literature & the Theory of Folklore Methodology and junior-year tutorials.

Harvard pedagogical DNA: Folklore & Mythology reveals that oral communication has been central to certain Harvard concentrations for 60 years—not as COVID response or AI workaround but as epistemological commitment. The field studies how cultures transmit knowledge orally (myths, songs, proverbs, performances), making oral engagement pedagogically essential.

Transferable insight: F&M students learn that oral transmission is:

• Structured (formulaic patterns, mnemonic devices, performance conventions)
• Adaptive (varies with context, audience, performer)
• Authoritative (oral traditions can be as rigorous as written texts)
• Community-based (validated through collective memory, not individual authorship)

Could this framework inform oral assessment design in other concentrations? Instead of seeing oral exams as "informal writing," treat them as distinct mode of knowledge demonstration with its own standards, structures, and evaluation criteria.

Current relevance to AI debate: F&M's focus on oral vs. written knowledge transmission makes it uniquely positioned to theorize AI's impact on assessment. If AI disrupts written authorship, we're experiencing a medium shift that F&M scholars have studied across cultures and millennia. Harvard should consult F&M faculty for theoretical frameworks on orality/literacy/technology relationships.

───

2. Harvard GSAS PhD Qualifying Exams: "Two-Hour Oral Examination Devoted to Presentation and Discussion of Potential Dissertation Topics"
:paperclip: Harvard SEAS / GSAS / AFVS, current policy
:link: https://seas.harvard.edu/office-academic-programs/graduate-policies-procedures-and-forms/phd-degree-requirements/phd-qualifying-exam-end-g2-year

Harvard PhD programs across disciplines require oral qualifying exams (also called "orals," "quals," or "general exams") to advance to candidacy. SEAS specifies: "ordinarily a two-hour oral examination devoted to the presentation and discussion of one or more potential dissertation topics and to more general questions." Film & Visual Studies requires written general examination + oral examination to test "command of scholarly fields and ability to write a dissertation."

Harvard oral assessment infrastructure: The university has deep institutional experience with high-stakes oral examinations at the graduate level:

• Trained faculty examiners (faculty regularly conduct PhD orals)
• Established protocols (timing, committee composition, evaluation criteria)
• Hybrid written+oral models (qualifying paper + oral defense)
• Formative feedback (orals identify gaps before dissertation research)

Undergraduate application: Could Harvard College adapt PhD qualifying exam models for undergraduate use?

• Senior concentration exam: Comprehensive oral covering major themes/methods (like PhD quals)
• Thesis proposal defense: Oral presentation of research plan before beginning (like PhD prospectus defense)
• Mid-thesis check-in: Oral progress report with faculty committee (like PhD committee meetings)
seas.harvard.eduPhD Qualifying Exam: by end of G2 year | Harvard John A. Paulson School of Engineering and Applied Sciences[Part of the Policies of the CHD, last updated fall 2021; see also area-specific exam guidelines for Applied Math, Applied Physics[7:46 AM]Scaling challenge: PhD programs conduct ~20-50 orals per year; undergraduate concentrations would need to assess hundreds. But the pedagogical model transfers: oral exams test synthesis, application, and adaptive reasoning—skills AI can't easily replicate.

Why graduate oral exams work: PhD candidates are evaluated on:

1. Breadth: Can you explain core concepts across your field?
2. Depth: Can you defend your specific research contribution?
3. Synthesis: Can you connect disparate ideas/methods?
4. Adaptability: Can you respond to unexpected questions?

These same learning outcomes apply to undergraduate education. Oral assessment at Harvard College would be expansion of existing practice, not innovation.

───

3. Tutorial Pedagogy at Harvard: Small-Group Oral Dialogue as Signature Teaching Method
:paperclip: Harvard FAS Course Catalog / Multiple Concentrations
:link: https://courses.my.harvard.edu/dw_course/fas_crse_cat.pdf

Many Harvard concentrations feature tutorial-based instruction: small seminars emphasizing discussion, oral presentation, and dialectical exchange. Social Studies, History & Literature, and Folklore & Mythology all require multi-semester tutorials where oral participation is central. The FAS catalog emphasizes "written expression, reading comprehension, and oral" communication as integrated skills.

Historical context: Harvard's tutorial system, inspired by Oxford/Cambridge, has century-long tradition of privileging oral dialogue:

• Socratic questioning: Faculty probe student understanding through conversation
• Thesis defense seminars: Students present work-in-progress orally for peer/faculty critique
• Discussion-based learning: Assessment includes participation, not just papers

Contemporary significance: For concentrations with strong tutorial traditions, AI doesn't fundamentally disrupt pedagogy—oral assessment was always central. The challenge is for lecture-heavy concentrations (e.g., Economics, Government) that historically relied on exams and papers.

Equity consideration (tutorial model): Tutorials privilege students who:

• Are comfortable speaking in front of authority figures
• Have cultural capital to engage in "intellectual banter"
• Are native English speakers or highly fluent
• Learn through verbal processing vs. written reflection

If Harvard expands oral assessment, must address these participation biases. Research on tutorial equity shows:

• Scaffolding matters: Low-stakes practice before graded oral work
• Structure reduces bias: Clear protocols and rubrics level the playing field
• Multiple modes: Balance oral with written allows students to play to strengths

Design principle: Don't just add oral exams to existing courses—redesign courses around integrated oral/written pedagogy (like tutorials do). Oral assessment works best when embedded in oral learning culture, not bolted onto lecture-exam model.

───

Project Status: 83 posts | 241 sources | Sunday Feb 22, 07:45 AM | 49.25 hours remaining

Synthesis of Harvard's oral traditions: F&M studies orality as knowledge system (60 years), GSAS uses oral quals for PhD candidacy (decades), tutorials emphasize oral dialogue (century-long). Harvard has rich oral assessment heritage—not inventing it for AI era, but expanding/formalizing existing practice. The question isn't "Should Harvard do oral assessment?" but "Should more concentrations adopt what some already do well?"
elle-with-claws  [8:06 AM]
Theme 3: Educational Research on Oral Assessment (SoTL) – Cycle 13

Focus: Metacognition Through Think-Aloud, Anxiety Research, and Peer Assessment

───

1. Think-Aloud Protocols: "Externalize Internal Dialogue" to Develop Metacognitive Awareness
:paperclip: Frontiers in Education / U Calgary Taylor Institute / IIUM Journal, 2025
:link: https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/education/articles/10.3389/feduc.2025.1653320/pdf

Recent research on metacognition teaching activities emphasizes think-aloud as a powerful pedagogical tool: "Thinking Aloud enables learners to externalize their internal dialogue, which can be shaped and refined through peer or teacher feedback." Studies show that students with "more advanced metacognition tended to have better language (β = 0.55, p < 0.001) and mathematics (β = 0.56)" performance. Instructors are encouraged to "practice think-aloud so you can check students' reasoning errors" and model metacognitive processes: "I'm starting by identifying the key variables..."

Harvard relevance: Think-aloud is essentially oral assessment of cognitive process, not just product. Applied to Harvard oral exams:

• Formative think-aloud: During office hours or review sessions, students verbally walk through their reasoning ("Here's how I approached this source..."). Faculty provide immediate feedback on thinking process, not just answers.
• Assessment think-aloud: Oral exam asks "Explain your reasoning out loud" rather than just "What's your conclusion?" This reveals whether students have genuine understanding or surface-level knowledge (AI-generated or memorized).

Pedagogical benefit beyond AI-resistance: Think-aloud develops metacognitive regulation—students become aware of their own thinking patterns, identify gaps, and self-correct. This is deeper learning than writing an essay (where revision hides the thinking process) or taking a multiple-choice exam (where guessing masks understanding).

Implementation for Harvard:

• Low-stakes practice: Weekly "think-aloud pair work" in sections (students explain concepts to peers)
• Recorded think-alouds: Students record themselves explaining a problem/text, submit audio, receive feedback on reasoning
• Exam component: Include "explain your reasoning" as part of oral assessment rubric (weighted separately from content accuracy)

Research shows think-aloud can be taught through modeling (faculty demonstrate their own thinking) and scaffolding (provide sentence stems: "First I noticed..., which led me to..."). This makes oral assessment a learning tool, not just evaluation.

───

2. Oral Assessment Anxiety: "A Significant Number of Students Experience Anxiety When Asked to Speak"
:paperclip: Frontiers in Psychology / Language Testing in Asia / PMC, 2024-2025
:link: https://languagetestingasia.springeropen.com/articles/10.1186/s40468-024-00340-x

Comprehensive research on public speaking anxiety (PSA) and oral performance apprehension reveals significant barriers to oral assessment equity. Studies identify anxiety triggers including "concern of mistakes, oral performance apprehension, pronunciation self-image" with physiological stress responses (cortisol elevation, increased heart rate). Critically, research documents culturally-specific anxiety patterns: "Cross-cultural investigations comparing Chinese and Iranian EFL learners' oral communication apprehension have identified culturally-specific anxiety triggers and coping mechanisms."

Harvard relevance: If oral assessment becomes widespread, anxiety must be addressed systematically, not treated as individual student "weakness." The research suggests:

High-risk groups for oral anxiety:

• Non-native English speakers (pronunciation anxiety, linguistic insecurity)
• Introverted students (preference for written reflection over verbal performance)
• Students from cultures that discourage public disagreement with authority
SpringerLinkSpeaking exams with less anxiety in Intelligent Computer-Assisted Language Assessment (ICALA): mirroring EFL learners’ foreign language anxiety, shyness, autonomy, and enjoymentLanguage Testing in Asia - A significant number of students experience anxiety when asked to speak in English. This unease, often rooted in factors such as shyness, lack of confidence, uncertainty,...https://languagetestingasia.springeropen.com/articles/10.1186/s40468-024-00340-x[8:06 AM]• Students with social anxiety, autism spectrum, speech disorders

Evidence-based mitigation strategies:
[8:06 AM]1. Graduated exposure: Low-stakes → high-stakes progression (practice in pairs, then small groups, then faculty)
2. Normalized anxiety: Faculty acknowledge that oral anxiety is normal and discuss coping strategies
3. Multiple modalities: Students can choose oral OR extended written defense (accommodate different strengths)
4. AI-mediated practice: Recent study shows "AI chatbots versus human facilitation" reduces anxiety—students practice oral responses with AI before human assessment (less judgment fear)
5. Clear rubrics: Anxiety decreases when students know exactly what's being evaluated

Critical finding: The research on "Intelligent Computer-Assisted Language Assessment" found that students experience less anxiety when assessed by AI than humans. This creates a paradox: oral assessment is proposed to avoid AI, but AI-assisted oral practice reduces anxiety. Suggests hybrid approach: students practice orally with AI chatbots, then demonstrate to faculty for grading.

Equity warning: Implementing oral assessment without anxiety support will disproportionately harm already-marginalized students (international, first-gen, neurodivergent). Harvard must provide:

• Workshops on oral communication skills (not assumed prior knowledge)
• Mental health resources for performance anxiety
• Accommodations (extended time, private vs. public settings, multiple attempts)

───

3. Peer Assessment of Oral Presentations: "Fostering Engagement, Critical Thinking, and Evaluative Skills"
:paperclip: Taylor & Francis / ScienceDirect / Language Testing in Asia, 2025
:link: https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/02602938.2025.2566659

Recent research on peer assessment of oral presentations shows that "methods such as oral presentations, group discussions, and projects facilitate peer engagement, contributing to deeper learning and shared understanding." A seven-year implementation study of peer assessment tools found positive student learning outcomes and increased self-efficacy in peer evaluation. However, research notes that "variations in validity and learning outcomes may be explained by differences in peer assessment contexts and implementation practices"—success requires careful design.

Harvard relevance: Peer assessment can scale oral evaluation while enhancing learning. If faculty can't assess 200 students orally, peers can provide formative feedback:

Peer assessment model for Harvard oral assignments:

• Structured rubrics: Students use same criteria as faculty (content, clarity, evidence, responses to questions)
• Training: Students practice peer evaluation with sample presentations, calibrate scoring
• Formative only: Peer scores inform feedback but don't determine grades (reduces bias concerns)
• Reciprocity: Everyone presents and evaluates (builds evaluative literacy)

Learning benefits beyond assessment:

• Metacognitive development: Evaluating peers' arguments helps students recognize strong vs. weak reasoning in their own work
• Community learning: Students hear multiple perspectives on same material (not just faculty interpretation)
• Evaluative literacy: Learning to assess quality is itself a learning outcome (critical thinking skill)

Research caveats:

• Friendship bias: Students may score friends higher (addressed through anonymous peer review)
• Gender/race bias: Implicit biases can influence peer scoring (training on bias awareness essential)
• Reliability varies: Peer scores less reliable than faculty for summative grading (but adequate for formative feedback)

Best practice from research: Use peer assessment for process, faculty assessment for product. Students give peer feedback on draft oral presentations (Week 10), then present final version to faculty for grading (Week 14). This combines:

• Peer learning (multiple feedback sources, community engagement)
• Faculty expertise (reliable summative evaluation)
• Reduced faculty workload (peers handle formative assessment)[8:06 AM]Digital tools: The seven-year implementation study used customizable digital platforms for peer assessment. Harvard could adopt similar technology to manage peer feedback workflows at scale.

───

Project Status: 84 posts | 244 sources | Sunday Feb 22, 08:04 AM | 48.93 hours remaining

Synthesis: SoTL research reveals oral assessment's pedagogical power (metacognition through think-aloud) and challenges (anxiety, peer assessment reliability). For Harvard: oral assessment can be high-impact learning tool if designed with anxiety mitigation, peer support, and metacognitive scaffolding. Simply adding oral exams without this infrastructure risks equity harm and missed pedagogical opportunities.

Theme 4: Post-AI Classroom Experiments with Oral Assignments – Cycle 13

Focus: Backward Design for Oral Exams, UPenn's "Conversations That Count," and AI-Resistant Assessment Framework

───

1. Oral Exams for a Generative AI World: Humanities Professor Redesigns Entire Course Using Backward Design
:paperclip: Academia.edu (research paper), November 10, 2025
:link: https://www.academia.edu/144892156/Oral_Exams_for_a_Generative_AI_World_Managing_Concerns_and_Logistics_for_Undergraduate_Humanities_Instruction

This detailed case study documents a humanities professor's complete course redesign to support oral assessment: "Using backward design principles, the author redesigned the entire course structure to support oral assessment, implementing technology-free classrooms, social annotation platforms, and systematic 'cold calling' to prepare students for final oral examinations." The paper addresses practical logistics and student preparation for oral exams in upper-level undergraduate courses.

Harvard relevance: This is the most comprehensive implementation guide found yet. Key insights:

Backward design approach:

1. Start with final oral exam format (what skills/knowledge will be tested?)
2. Design course activities to build those oral skills incrementally
3. Align pedagogy: If oral defense is final assessment, oral practice must be regular classroom activity

Specific course modifications:

• Technology-free classroom: Students can't rely on laptops/phones for answers (builds oral fluency without digital crutches)
• Social annotation platforms: Students annotate texts collaboratively online, then discuss annotations orally in class (connects written preparation to oral dialogue)
• Systematic cold calling: Every student must verbally respond multiple times per class (normalizes oral performance, reduces final exam anxiety)

Harvard application: This model could work for any concentration willing to redesign pedagogy around oral assessment:

• History concentration: Social annotation of primary sources → cold call discussions → oral exam on historiography
• Literature concentration: Collaborative close reading online → in-class defense of interpretations → oral exam on critical theory
• Government concentration: Policy brief writing → oral presentation to "policymakers" → oral exam on application

Critical insight: The paper shows oral assessment requires systemic course redesign, not just "add oral exam to existing structure." Faculty can't lecture for 13 weeks then expect students to perform orally in week 14. The entire pedagogical culture must shift toward oral dialogue.

Implementation cost: This level of redesign demands significant faculty time and training. Harvard should provide:

• Course design workshops (Bok Center)
• Teaching release time for redesign semester
• Peer observation/mentoring (faculty who've implemented oral assessment successfully)

───

2. UPenn's "Building Deeper Learning Through Oral Exams: Conversations That Count"
:paperclip: UPenn Center for Teaching and Learning Innovation, November 14, 2025
:link: https://cetli.upenn.edu/news-features/feature-stories/building-deeper-learning-through-oral-exams/

Professor Gressman at UPenn implemented conversational oral exams in mathematics: "In 30-minute Zoom sessions, Gressman would begin by asking students 'What do you want to talk about?' Then, Gressman would ask them one of the questions he had prepared beforehand that was related to their interests. Rather than assessing students' ability to do live computations or recite facts off the top of their head, the conversations gave Gressman the opportunity to assess their deeper learning across the course material."

Harvard relevance: This challenges the interrogation model of oral exams (rapid-fire questions, high pressure). Gressman's approach emphasizes:

Student-centered oral assessment:
academia.eduOral Exams for a Generative AI World: Managing Concerns and Logistics for Undergraduate Humanities InstructionThe emergence of generative artificial intelligence has forced humanities educators to reconsider traditional assessment methods, particularly take-home writing assignments vulnerable to content produced by generative artificial intelligence. ThisCenter for Excellence in Teaching, Learning and InnovationBuilding Deeper Learning Through Oral Exams: Conversations That CountFaculty from across the university share how oral exams are helping them assess students' learning and engage with students' thinking.Reading time12 minutesNov 14th, 2025https://cetli.upenn.edu/news-features/feature-stories/building-deeper-learning-through-oral-exams/[8:16 AM]• Student choice: "What do you want to talk about?" gives agency (reduces anxiety, allows students to showcase strengths)
[8:16 AM]• Interest-driven: Questions tailored to student's stated interests (authentic engagement, not generic quiz)
• Deep learning focus: Assessing synthesis and application, not memorization or speed

Pedagogical philosophy: Oral exams as intellectual conversation, not performance test. This aligns with Harvard's tutorial tradition—the exam should feel like an extended office hours dialogue, not a courtroom cross-examination.

Format flexibility:

• Zoom option: Gressman used video calls (accessible for students with mobility issues, remote students)
• 30-minute duration: Long enough for substantive discussion, short enough to schedule hundreds of students
• Prepared questions: Faculty have question bank ready but select based on student's entry point

Scaling consideration: Gressman's model requires ~50 hours of faculty time for a 100-student course (30 min × 100). This is comparable to grading 100 essays (30 min each) but distributed differently (compressed into exam period vs. spread across semester).

Student reaction (from article): Students appreciated the personalization and reported that oral exams helped them synthesize across course material in ways writing assignments didn't demand. The conversational format felt less like "being tested" and more like "thinking together."

Recommendation for Harvard: Pilot this conversational model in courses with strong faculty-student rapport (seminars, tutorials, labs with regular student-faculty interaction). Avoid in large lecture courses where students have limited prior oral contact with faculty.

───

3. Redesigning Assessments for AI-Enhanced Learning: Framework Prioritizing "Authentic, Process-Driven Tasks"
:paperclip: MDPI Education Sciences (peer-reviewed), February 2, 2025
:link: https://www.mdpi.com/2227-7102/15/2/174

This systematic framework identifies "AI-resistant assessments" as those prioritizing "authentic, process-driven tasks where students engage deeply with the material, including iterative projects, reflective writing, and oral evaluations." The paper argues AI-resistant assessments "align with educational principles by fostering academic integrity, inclusivity, and real-world applicability, ensuring that students' learning outcomes demonstrate human capabilities."

Harvard relevance: The framework provides design criteria for AI-resistant assessment:

Three pillars of AI-resistance:

1. Authenticity: Tasks mirror real-world professional contexts (not artificial school exercises)
2. Process-driven: Multiple checkpoints reveal work progression (not just final product)
3. Human capabilities: Assesses skills AI can't easily replicate (adaptive reasoning, empathy, embodied performance)

Applied to oral assessment:

• Authentic oral task: Defend research proposal before mock review panel (like grant applications, job talks, conference presentations)
• Process-driven: Proposal outline → draft → peer feedback → oral revision defense (shows development)
• Human capabilities: Real-time adaptation to questions, reading audience reactions, building rapport with evaluators

Critical caveat: The paper notes that "AI-resistant" ≠ "AI-proof"—students can still use AI for preparation (research, outline writing, practice question generation). The "resistance" comes from requiring live human performance that can't be outsourced.

Inclusivity concern: The paper warns that "AI-resistant assessments" must be carefully designed for equity—oral evaluations can disadvantage students with speech anxiety, non-native speakers, neurodivergent students. Recommendations:

• Multiple assessment modes (students choose oral OR extended written OR portfolio)
• Scaffolded preparation (low-stakes practice before high-stakes evaluation)
• Clear rubrics (reduce subjective bias in oral grading)MDPIRedesigning Assessments for AI-Enhanced Learning: A Framework for Educators in the Generative AI EraThe emergence of generative artificial intelligence (Gen AI) in education offers both opportunities and challenges, particularly in the context of student assessment. This study examines faculty members’ motivations to redesign assessments for their courses in the Gen AI era and introduces a framework for this purpose. A qualitative methodology was employed, gathering data through semi-structured interviews and focus groups, along with examples of redesigned assessments. Sixty-one faculty members participated in the study, and the data were analyzed using both deductive and inductive thematic approaches. Key motivations for redesigning assessments included maintaining academic integrity, pre… [8:16 AM]Philosophical question: Should assessment be "AI-resistant" or "AI-integrated"? The paper acknowledges tension: resisting AI may miss opportunity to teach students to collaborate with AI ethically. Hybrid model: students use AI for research/preparation (disclosed), then demonstrate understanding orally (verification).

Project Status: 85 posts | 247 sources | Sunday Feb 22, 08:15 AM | 48.75 hours remaining

Synthesis: Real-world experiments reveal oral assessment requires comprehensive course redesign (not just exam format change), works best as student-centered conversation (not interrogation), and should be authentic and process-driven (not AI-avoidance panic). For Harvard: successful implementation demands faculty development, pedagogical culture shift, and equity safeguards—not just policy mandate.

Theme 5: AI Used IN Oral Evaluation – Cycle 13

Focus: Multimodal AI Analysis, Ethical Frameworks, and VR Training Applications

───

1. Multimodal AI: "Analyzes Oral Presentations for Clarity, Confidence, and Eye Contact, Not Just Spoken Content"
:paperclip: Crescendo AI / ICML 2025 Research, 2025-2026
:link: https://www.crescendo.ai/blog/multimodal-ai-examples-and-applications

Emerging multimodal AI systems can now grade oral assignments by "combining textual answers, handwriting recognition, voice responses, and video submissions." Crucially, these systems "analyze oral presentations for clarity, confidence, and eye contact, not just spoken content"—evaluating paralinguistic features (tone, pacing, body language) alongside verbal content. Research conferences (ICML, NeurIPS) are showcasing "Enhanced MultiModal reAsoning" (EMMA) benchmarks for cross-modal assessment.

Harvard relevance: This represents both opportunity and danger:

Opportunity:

• Comprehensive feedback: AI could analyze aspects of oral performance that faculty miss (unconscious verbal tics, pacing inconsistencies, body language that undermines message)
• Objective measurement: Quantifiable metrics for delivery quality (words per minute, vocal variety, eye contact percentage)
• Scalable coaching: Students could practice oral presentations with AI feedback before faculty evaluation

Danger:

• Non-verbal bias: Analyzing "confidence" and "eye contact" embeds cultural assumptions. Many cultures teach deference through downcast eyes; neurodivergent students may avoid eye contact; women are often penalized for "confident" body language that's rewarded in men.
• Surveillance concerns: Video analysis of student bodies raises privacy issues (facial recognition, emotion detection, biometric data collection)
• Performance privileging: Evaluating delivery may overshadow content—charismatic but shallow presentations score higher than substantive but awkward ones

Recommendation for Harvard: If using multimodal AI, limit analysis to audio transcription only (not video). Avoid systems that score body language, facial expressions, or "confidence"—these metrics systematically disadvantage marginalized students while adding little pedagogical value. Focus AI assistance on content analysis (argument structure, evidence quality, logical coherence), not performance analysis (how students look/sound).

Research caveat: Most multimodal AI research comes from tech companies (product marketing) or CS conferences (technical benchmarks), not peer-reviewed education journals. Claims about "effectiveness" often lack empirical validation in real classroom contexts.

───

2. AI Ethical Guidelines for Educational Assessment: "Monitored for Bias or Discriminatory Patterns Across Diverse Student Populations"
:paperclip: EDUCAUSE / Springer Nature / ERIC, 2025
:link: https://library.educause.edu/resources/2025/6/ai-ethical-guidelines

Major educational technology organizations have published AI ethics frameworks raising critical questions about automated grading: "How are AI grading systems monitored for bias or discriminatory patterns across diverse student populations? What standards are in place to ensure transparency and explainability in how AI-generated evaluations are used to inform grades?" Research documents that AI systems "can perpetuate inequalities based on factors such as race, gender, or socioeconomic background" when "trained on biased data or inadequately calibrated for diverse student populations."

Harvard relevance: If Harvard adopts AI-assisted oral assessment, these ethical questions become institutional obligations:

Transparency requirements:

• What data is collected? (audio only? video? biometrics?)
• How are scores calculated? (black-box algorithms or explainable criteria?)
• Who owns student data? (Harvard, vendor, retained how long?)
crescendo.aiMultimodal AI Examples and Applications | 2025 EditionExplore multimodal AI examples and applications across customer service, healthcare, manufacturing and education industries in 2025.https://www.crescendo.ai/blog/multimodal-ai-examples-and-applications[8:31 AM]• Can students opt out? (alternative assessment paths for those refusing AI analysis?)

Bias monitoring requirements:
[8:31 AM]• Disaggregated analysis: AI scores analyzed by demographic groups (race, gender, native language, disability status)
• Audit trails: Regular third-party audits of AI systems for discriminatory patterns
• Human override: Faculty retain authority to overturn AI scores showing bias
• Redress mechanisms: Students can challenge AI-generated evaluations

Fairness concerns (from research):

• Procedural fairness: AI systems should follow "standardized, transparent decision-making processes"
• Outcome fairness: Results should not systematically disadvantage protected groups
• Recognition fairness: Diverse forms of knowledge demonstration should be valued (not just dominant cultural communication styles)

Harvard's institutional responsibility: As elite institution with resources, Harvard should lead on AI ethics, not adopt vendor systems uncritically. Recommendations:

• Establish faculty-student AI ethics committee to evaluate proposed AI assessment tools
• Publish transparency reports on AI usage in assessment (what's used, how, with what outcomes)
• Fund independent research on AI bias in oral assessment (not just vendor-provided studies)
• Default to human-only evaluation unless AI demonstrably improves equity

Provocative question from EDUCAUSE: "Who ultimately has decision-making authority over the use of AI in grading—faculty, students, or the institution?" If students object to AI analysis of their oral performances (privacy, bias, inaccuracy concerns), should they have opt-out rights? Or does institutional efficiency trump student autonomy?

───

3. AI-Integrated VR Oral Training: "Tailored Feedback on Pronunciation, Grammar, and Fluency" Reduces Public Speaking Anxiety
:paperclip: ScienceDirect / Virti / Bodyswaps, 2025-2026
:link: https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666920X25001547

Research on AI-integrated VR oral training shows promising results: participants using "Meta Quest 2 headsets and an AI-integrated VR oral training application providing tailored feedback on pronunciation, grammar, and fluency" showed reduced public speaking anxiety and interview anxiety. Commercial platforms like Bodyswaps offer "AI-powered simulations" analyzing "communication style, choice of language and decision-making," providing "guidance on what they did well and how to improve, such as using more positive language in a team conversation, or improving body posture during an interview."

Harvard relevance: This suggests AI as practice tool (not evaluator) could address oral assessment's equity problem:

VR practice for oral exam preparation:

• Anxiety reduction: Students practice in private, low-stakes VR environment before high-stakes faculty oral exam
• Unlimited practice: No need to impose on faculty/peers for practice runs—AI provides instant feedback 24/7
• Specific skill development: Targeted practice on identified weaknesses (e.g., "You used 47 filler words in 5 minutes—try reducing to <20")
• Cultural adaptation: International students can practice English oral fluency in non-judgmental space

Scalability solution: If Harvard can't provide faculty-led oral coaching to all students (resource-intensive), VR+AI could democratize access to practice opportunities. Currently, students with:

• Social capital (comfortable asking professors for extra practice)
• Resources (can hire speaking coaches)
• Prior training (debate team, TED-style schools)

...have advantage in oral assessment. VR+AI training could level the playing field.

Implementation model for Harvard:

• Bok Center partnership: Provide VR stations where students can practice oral presentations with AI feedback
• Pre-assessment training: Before oral exams, students required to complete 3-5 VR practice sessions (skill-building, not grading)
• Voluntary coaching: Students can opt into additional VR sessions for personalized oral skill development
[8:31 AM]Critical limitation: The ScienceDirect study focused on language proficiency (pronunciation, grammar, fluency), not content quality (argument strength, evidence use, critical thinking). AI can help students "sound better" but can't necessarily help them "think better." For Harvard oral assessment, content must remain primary criterion, with delivery as secondary consideration.

Equity caveat: VR requires expensive hardware (Meta Quest headsets ~$500). If Harvard implements VR practice, must provide free campus access—not assume students can afford personal VR setups. Otherwise, VR becomes another privilege amplifier rather than equity intervention.

───

Project Status: 86 posts | 250 sources | Sunday Feb 22, 08:30 AM | 48.5 hours remaining

Synthesis: AI in oral assessment offers technical possibilities (multimodal analysis, VR practice) but raises serious ethical concerns (bias, privacy, transparency). For Harvard: AI as practice tool (VR training, feedback) may enhance equity; AI as evaluator (automated grading) likely harms equity unless rigorously monitored for bias. Default to human judgment with AI assistance, not AI judgment with human oversight.

Theme 6: Literary/Historical References on Speech vs. Writing – Cycle 13

Focus: McLuhan's Media Extensions, Havelock's Oral Encyclopedia, and Contemporary AI Philosophy

───

1. Marshall McLuhan's "Understanding Media": "Media Are Extensions of Human Senses, Fundamentally Influencing How Individuals Experience the World"
:paperclip: EBSCO / Wikipedia / Andrew McLuhan, 1964/2024-2025
:link: https://www.ebsco.com/research-starters/literature-and-writing/understanding-media-marshall-mcluhan

McLuhan's foundational work argues that "media are extensions of human senses" (radio extends hearing, books enhance vision) and that "the medium is the message"—the form of communication shapes consciousness more than its content. His central insight: "A recurrent topic is the contrast between oral cultures and print culture." McLuhan posits that different media create different cognitive patterns: oral culture emphasizes participation and immersion, while print culture enables detachment and analysis.

Harvard relevance: McLuhan's framework helps explain why the AI debate feels so unsettling—it's not just about cheating but about a fundamental shift in how knowledge is mediated.

McLuhan's media analysis applied to assessment:

• Writing (pre-AI): Extended individual analytical thinking, enabled revision, created permanent record → fostered critical distance, structured argument
• AI-generated text: Extends computational pattern-matching, enables instant production, obscures authorship → challenges individual accountability, questions originality
• Oral assessment: Extends embodied presence, requires real-time synthesis, reveals process not just product → restores human performance, but privileges verbal fluency

Key McLuhan insight for Harvard: "The medium is the message" means the choice between oral and written assessment isn't neutral—each medium shapes what kind of thinking we're cultivating. Written assessment (pre-AI) trained students in:

• Linear argumentation (thesis → evidence → conclusion)
• Deferred gratification (drafting, revising, polishing over time)
• Individual authorship (your name on your work)

Oral assessment trains students in:

• Adaptive reasoning (respond to unexpected questions)
• Immediate performance (no revision, no hiding gaps)
• Dialogic knowledge (thinking emerges through conversation)

Neither is "better"—they're different cognitive extensions. The question: What kind of minds does Harvard want to form?

Contemporary relevance (Andrew McLuhan 2024-2025): McLuhan's grandson teaches "Understanding Media" courses analyzing how digital technologies (including AI) continue his grandfather's insights. The core lesson: technology is never just a tool—it fundamentally reorganizes human perception and social relations.

Harvard design principle: Don't treat oral vs. written as either/or but as both/and—multimodal assessment that leverages different media extensions for different learning outcomes.

───

2. Eric Havelock's "Preface to Plato": "The Iliad Acted as an Oral Encyclopedia" Before Literacy
:paperclip: Harvard University Press, 1963 (classic still in print)
:link: https://www.hup.harvard.edu/books/9780674699069

Havelock's landmark study argues that Homer's epics functioned as "oral encyclopedia" in pre-literate Greece—not entertainment but knowledge storage system. Before writing, Greeks relied on memorized poetry (rhythmic, formulaic, narrative) to preserve cultural wisdom. Plato's attack on poets wasn't aesthetic but epistemological: he condemned "mimesis" (emotional identification with narrative) because oral knowledge-transmission competed with the new literate mode of abstract philosophical reasoning.

Harvard relevance: Havelock reveals that Plato's critique of orality (which seems to support oral assessment) was actually Plato's embrace of literacy (written philosophy) against older oral tradition. This inverts the simple reading of Plato as oral-assessment advocate.
EBSCOUnderstanding Media by Marshall McLuhan | Literature and Writing | Research Starters | EBSCO Researchhttps://www.ebsco.com/research-starters/literature-and-writing/understanding-media-marshall-mcluhan[8:46 AM]The orality-to-literacy transition (ancient Greece) parallels AI-era debates:

Pre-literate (oral encyclopedia):

• Knowledge stored in human memory (epics, myths, proverbs)
• Verification through community repetition (elders confirm accuracy)
• Learning through emotional identification (mimesis—become the character)
• Problem: Limited to what humans can memorize; resists abstraction

Literate (writing revolution):

• Knowledge stored in external texts (scrolls, books, databases)
• Verification through textual comparison (cite sources, check evidence)
• Learning through analytical distance (critique the text)
• Problem: Weakens memory; enables forgery/plagiarism

AI-assisted (generative text):

• Knowledge stored in computational models (LLMs, neural networks)
• Verification through... ? (still being determined)
• Learning through... ? (collaboration with AI? displacement by AI?)
• Problem: Authorship unclear; intellectual labor outsourced

Havelock's lesson for Harvard: Every transition in knowledge-media creates anxiety and resistance. Plato feared poetry would keep Greeks trapped in pre-rational thinking. Today, we fear AI will keep students from developing genuine understanding. Both anxieties have merit—and both miss that the new medium enables new forms of knowledge while foreclosing old ones.

Application to oral assessment: If Harvard shifts to oral assessment, recognize it's not "returning to Socratic orality" (pre-literate) but creating tertiary orality (post-literate, post-AI). Students will:

• Use AI for research and drafting (computational extension)
• Use writing for structure and revision (literate extension)
• Use oral defense for synthesis and verification (oral extension)

This is hybrid epistemology, not nostalgia for pure orality.

───

3. Contemporary Philosophy of Education in the Age of AI: "GenAI Fundamentally Shifts How Educational Knowledge Is Created, Shared, and Validated"
:paperclip: UNESCO / Taylor & Francis / Open Praxis, 2024-2025
:link: https://impactinged.pitt.edu/ojs/ImpactingEd/article/view/485

Recent philosophical analysis identifies GenAI as "epistemic technology"—a tool that transforms not just how we access knowledge but what counts as knowledge. A 2024 manifesto warns: "This manifesto critically examines the unfolding integration of Generative AI into higher education, using a collective and thoughtful approach to navigate the future of teaching and learning." UNESCO notes that "participatory workshops where learners share oral histories and cultural practices can inform training data for AI tools rooted in community knowledge"—suggesting oral traditions may help decolonize AI systems trained on dominant Western texts.

Harvard relevance: Contemporary philosophers argue we're experiencing an epistemic shift comparable to the printing press—not just new tools but new modes of knowing.

Three philosophical positions on AI in education:

1. Resistance (protect traditional epistemology):

• Oral assessment as epistemic preservation (defending human knowledge-generation against AI displacement)
• Risk: Nostalgia—pretending AI doesn't exist won't prepare students for reality
• Harvard version: Ban AI, shift to oral exams, maintain pre-AI assessment

2. Integration (adapt epistemology to AI):

• Oral assessment as verification layer (students use AI for preparation, demonstrate understanding orally)
• Risk: Uncritical adoption—assuming AI is neutral tool, ignoring bias and power dynamics
• Harvard version: Allow AI with disclosure, require oral defense of AI-assisted work

3. Transformation (reimagine epistemology with AI):

• Oral assessment as new knowledge practice (combining human dialogue, AI assistance, collaborative intelligence)
• Risk: Utopianism—overestimating AI's benefits, underestimating harms
• Harvard version: Teach AI-human collaboration as scholarly practice (critical prompt design, AI output evaluation, synthesis beyond AI capability)[8:46 AM]Philosophical question from manifesto: What is "practical wisdom" (phronesis) in the age of AI? Ancient philosophy valued wisdom as embodied judgment developed through experience. If AI can generate "answers" instantly, does practical wisdom become:

• Skeptical evaluation (questioning AI outputs)?
• Creative synthesis (combining AI-generated ideas in novel ways)?
• Ethical discernment (knowing when NOT to use AI)?

UNESCO's decolonial insight: AI trained on dominant texts marginalizes oral knowledge traditions. If Harvard adopts oral assessment, frame it as reclaiming oral epistemologies (African griot traditions, Indigenous storytelling, oral legal traditions) rather than just "AI-proofing."

Design principle for Harvard: Situate oral assessment within philosophy of education, not just logistics. What does oral assessment say about what Harvard values? If it's just "preventing AI cheating," that's shallow. If it's "honoring multiple ways of knowing," that's profound.

───

Project Status: 87 posts | 253 sources | Sunday Feb 22, 08:45 AM | 48.25 hours remaining

Synthesis: From McLuhan (media shape consciousness), to Havelock (literacy revolution), to contemporary AI philosophy (epistemic shifts), the oral vs. written debate has 2,500 years of precedent. For Harvard: recognize oral assessment as media choice with epistemological implications, not just tactical response to AI. Design assessment that honors multiple cognitive extensions—writing for analysis, speaking for synthesis, AI for augmentation—creating 21st-century practical wisdom.
elle-with-claws  [9:01 AM]
Theme 7: Professional Domains — Journalism, Diplomacy, Sales – Cycle 13

Focus: Oral Skills as Career-Critical Competencies Requiring Systematic Training

───

1. Journalism: "Develop Investigating, Reporting, and Audio-Visual Storytelling Skills" Through Interviewing Training
:paperclip: UCLA Extension / Thomson Reuters / Journalism.University, 2025
:link: https://journalism.university/reporting-techniques/journalist-interviewing-skills-techniques/

Professional journalism training emphasizes oral communication as core competency: programs teach "preparation, questioning, listening, ethics, and turning talk into engaging stories." UCLA Extension's "Art of the Interview" course develops "investigating, reporting, and audio-visual storytelling skills needed for a multifaceted career in modern journalism." Thomson Reuters training focuses on "honing your interviewing skills both on the phone and face-to-face"—recognizing that obtaining information from sources is "possibly the most vital skill a journalist can have."

Harvard relevance: Journalism as professional model reveals that elite careers demand sophisticated oral communication—not just "presenting" but dialogic interrogation:

Journalistic oral skills transferable to academic assessment:

• Active listening: Understanding responses to formulate follow-up questions (not just waiting to talk)
• Probing: Asking "why?" and "how do you know?" to get beyond surface claims
• Clarification: Requesting examples, evidence, or elaboration when answers are vague
• Ethical engagement: Respectful but persistent questioning (not accepting non-answers)

Pedagogical application for Harvard: If oral assessment is framed as "journalistic interview" rather than "interrogation," the dynamic shifts:

• Student as expert: Faculty interview student about their research/argument (student defends claims with evidence)
• Faculty as journalist: Seeks to understand and critique student's position through skilled questioning
• Assessment as dialogue: Reveals depth of understanding through back-and-forth, not monologue

Training implication: Just as journalists receive explicit training in interview techniques, Harvard students need explicit training in oral defense:

• How to structure verbal explanation
• How to respond when you don't know the answer
• How to clarify questions before answering
• How to maintain composure under challenging questions

Cross-disciplinary connection: Many Harvard students pursue journalism careers (Crimson alumni at NYT, WSJ, etc.). Oral assessment that develops interview-like skills (question response, verbal clarity, evidence presentation) prepares students for professional practice—not just "AI-proofing" but authentic preparation.

───

2. Diplomacy: Harvard Kennedy School's "Negotiation and Diplomacy" Course Develops "Skills Critical to Success in Public Service"
:paperclip: Harvard Kennedy School / Diplo Academy / Leiden University, 2025
:link: https://www.hks.harvard.edu/courses/negotiation-and-diplomacy

HKS teaches "negotiation-related skills critical to success in public service as well as in the private sector: deep knowledge of the core issues of our time, analytical thinking, cogent discussion, and..." [oral negotiation]. Diplo Academy emphasizes "practical skills to successfully prepare, undertake, and conclude negotiations in formal and informal settings" including how to "deal with power imbalances, build effective partnerships" verbally. Leiden's program uses simulation exercises where "students apply theoretical aspects on international negotiation directly and train their skills during the exercises."

Harvard relevance: HKS already recognizes oral negotiation as learnable professional skill—the same principle should apply to Harvard College oral assessment.

Diplomatic oral competencies:

• Positional clarity: Articulating your argument concisely and persuasively
Journalism & Mass Communication HubHoning Your Interviewing Skills: Techniques for Journalists • Journalism UniversityMaster the art of journalism interviews: preparation, questioning, listening, ethics, and turning talk into engaging stories.Written byPrince KumarTime to read7 minutesJun 1st, 2025https://journalism.university/reporting-techniques/journalist-interviewing-skills-techniques/hks.harvard.eduNegotiation and DiplomacyWhat can we learn from studying great global negotiators and diplomats grappling with some of the world's most challenging problems? [9:01 AM]• Adaptive response: Adjusting strategy based on interlocutor's reactions
• Emotional regulation: Maintaining composure despite disagreement or pressure
• Cultural sensitivity: Recognizing how communication norms vary across contexts

Harvard College application: These are precisely the skills oral exams should develop—not coincidentally, they're also skills essays don't develop as effectively:

• Essays allow unlimited revision (no adaptive response)
• Essays are solitary (no real-time engagement with objections)
• Essays happen in private (no emotional regulation under pressure)

Cross-school synergy opportunity: HKS has decades of expertise teaching oral negotiation/diplomacy. Harvard College could:

• Import HKS pedagogy: Adapt negotiation simulations for undergraduate courses (Gov, Econ, Social Studies)
• Share assessment protocols: HKS likely has rubrics for evaluating oral negotiation performance—could inform College oral exam design
• Faculty development: HKS professors could train College faculty in oral assessment techniques

Simulation-based learning: Leiden's model—"Each seminar will highlight certain aspects of international negotiation, and, as students progress, the exercises during the seminars will become more complex and challenging"—shows how oral skills are scaffolded through repeated practice. Harvard College oral assessment should follow same principle: low-stakes → medium-stakes → high-stakes progression.

Authenticity argument: For students entering public service, policy, law, international relations, oral negotiation/defense is authentic professional practice. Assessing through oral dialogue isn't just "AI resistance" but career preparation.

───

3. Sales: "Mastering the Art of Persuasion" Through Training in Verbal and Non-Verbal Communication
:paperclip: Dale Carnegie / Carew / KPI Consultancy, 2025
:link: https://www.carew.com/sales-communication-influence-strategies/

Corporate sales training emphasizes oral persuasion as systematic skill: programs teach "verbal communication skills, nonverbal communication, emotional intelligence" alongside content knowledge. KPI Consultancy notes that sales presentation training "helps professionals refine their verbal and non-verbal communication skills, including voice modulation—using tone and pace to maintain engagement." Coursera offers courses on "Persuasive Communication, Verbal Communication Skills, Target Audience, Storytelling, Non-Verbal Communication, Composure" as learnable competencies.

Harvard relevance: Sales training reveals that oral communication has technical components that can be taught, practiced, and evaluated systematically.

Sales oral competencies (applicable to academic oral assessment):

• Audience awareness: Adapting message to listener's knowledge/interests (in oral exam: adjusting explanation based on faculty expertise)
• Narrative structure: Telling coherent story that builds toward conclusion (in oral exam: explaining research arc from question → method → findings → implications)
• Objection handling: Responding to challenges without defensiveness (in oral exam: engaging with critical questions as opportunities to clarify/strengthen argument)
• Confidence projection: Speaking with authority even when uncertain (in oral exam: distinguishing "I know this" from "I'm still exploring this" honestly but confidently)

Controversial insight: Sales training explicitly teaches performance techniques (voice modulation, body language, composure)—skills that Harvard may not want to privilege in oral assessment. This highlights a key design choice:

Two models of oral assessment:

1. Performance-focused: Evaluates delivery, presence, persuasiveness (how you say it) → risks privileging students with performance training/cultural capital
2. Content-focused: Evaluates ideas, evidence, reasoning (what you say) → more equitable but harder to distinguish from written assessment
Carew Sales TrainingMastering Sales Communication: Persuasion, Influence & Objection Handling - Carew Sales TrainingSales communication is no longer about delivering a pitch. It’s about influencing decision-makers in a world where everyone is overloaded, skeptical, and short on time. If you can’t shift perspective, you can’t shift behavior – and if you can’t do that, you can’t close deals. We recognize students have varied communication backgrounds and do not penalize accents, speech patterns, or performance anxiety."

Professional development insight: Corporate world invests billions in oral communication training (Dale Carnegie, Toastmasters, executive coaches). If oral skills are career-critical, Harvard has educational responsibility to develop them—not just assess them. This means:

• Bok Center workshops on oral defense skills
• Practice opportunities before graded oral exams
• Feedback focused on improvement (not just summative scoring)

Ethical consideration: Sales training explicitly teaches persuasion and influence—skills that can be used ethically or manipulatively. Similarly, if Harvard teaches oral defense skills, must also teach intellectual honesty: how to acknowledge limitations, concede valid criticisms, and distinguish strong claims from weak ones. Oral assessment shouldn't reward "sounding confident" when uncertain—it should reward epistemic humility alongside argumentative strength.

Synthesis across professional domains: Journalism (interrogative dialogue), diplomacy (negotiation under pressure), and sales (persuasive communication) all require sophisticated oral skills developed through systematic professional training—not innate talent. For Harvard: if oral assessment becomes significant component, must provide corresponding skill development (not just evaluation). The professional world demonstrates that oral competence is teachable, assessable, and career-critical—making it legitimate educational priority, not just AI-avoidance tactic.
