Theme 0: History of Oral Examinations in Higher Ed – Cycle 14

Focus: French Concours System, Colonial Education Models, and Gender Barriers

───

1. French Grandes Écoles: Oral Examinations (Concours) as Elite Gatekeeping Since 1748
:paperclip: Wikipedia / K12 Academics / LLULL Journal, 1748-present
:link: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Grande_%C3%A9cole

The French grandes écoles system has used rigorous oral examinations (concours) as primary admissions mechanism since 1748 (military engineering school at Mézières). The modern system requires students to pass "written tests, given over the course of several weeks," then "those students who succeed in the written exams then take a further set of exams, usually one-hour oral exams, during which they are given a problem to solve." Preparatory classes include "colles" (oral examinations twice a week) in math, physics, chemistry, biology, French, and foreign languages. The agrégation exam (established 1766) consists of "a written portion, during which most candidates are eliminated, and an oral portion, during which candidates must demonstrate their abilities to teach a specific subject at a very advanced level."

Harvard relevance: The French model represents 280 years of institutional commitment to oral assessment as measure of intellectual merit. Key features:

Systemic oral preparation:

• Weekly oral practice ("colles") throughout preparatory years (not just final exam)
• Problem-solving under time pressure (students given problems to solve orally in one hour)
• Hybrid written+oral (written exams eliminate weak candidates; oral exams assess top performers)

What Harvard can learn from French system:

1. Scaffolding is essential: French students practice oral exams twice weekly for 2+ years before high-stakes concours. Harvard can't expect students to perform well on oral exams without similar preparation.
2. Oral+written is stronger than either alone: The concours uses written exams for breadth, oral exams for depth—complementary modes, not substitutes.
3. Real-time problem-solving: French oral exams don't just ask students to recite knowledge but to solve novel problems on the spot—this tests adaptive intelligence, not memorization (and is highly AI-resistant).

Equity concern from history: The grandes écoles system has been criticized for reproducing social privilege—students from wealthy families can afford intensive preparatory tutoring for oral performance. If Harvard adopts oral assessment, must avoid similar stratification by providing free oral preparation resources (Bok Center workshops, peer practice groups, faculty office hours focused on oral skills).

Contemporary relevance: France still uses this 280-year-old model successfully. The concours system produces elite engineers, civil servants, and intellectuals. This demonstrates oral assessment can be rigorous, prestigious, and sustainable at scale—not just COVID-era panic response.

───

2. British Colonial Education: Examination Systems Imposed to Create "Clerical Class" in India and Africa
:paperclip: Britannica / Unacademy / Wikipedia, 19th-20th century
:link: https://www.britannica.com/topic/education/Education-in-British-colonies-and-former-colonies

British colonial education systems emphasized written examinations over oral dialogue, creating "English-medium schools, through which they taught western science and literature" to train Indians and Africans "for doing government clerical jobs." Colonial universities "initially did not engage in teaching or research; they only conducted examinations and awarded degrees"—pure assessment institutions without pedagogical relationship. Critical reports in 1922 and 1925 noted "growing uneasiness among the Africans, the missions, the governors, and the administrators" about education policy.

Harvard relevance: This reveals the political economy of assessment modes:

Oral assessment = small-scale, tutorial relationship:
WikipediaGrande écoleA grande école (French: [ɡʁɑ̃d ekɔl]; lit. 'great school') is a specialized top-level educational institution in France and some other countries such as Algeria and Morocco. Grandes écoles are part of an alternative educational system that operates alongside the mainstream French public university system, and are dedicated to teaching, research and professional training in either pure natural and social sciences, or applied sciences such as engineering, architecture, business administration, or public policy and administration.
Similar to the Ivy League in the United States, the Golden Triangle in the UK, Designated National Universities in Japan and C9 League in China, Grandes écoles are eli… https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Grande_%C3%A9cole[9:16 AM]• Pre-colonial/indigenous education: Oral knowledge transmission (guru-shishya in India, griot traditions in Africa)
• Requires: Personal relationship between teacher and student
• Enables: Contextual, adaptive, culturally-situated learning
• Limits: Difficult to scale, hard to standardize across empire

Written examination = mass-scale, bureaucratic sorting:

• Colonial imposition: Standardized written exams across entire empire
• Requires: Literate population, uniform curriculum, impersonal grading
• Enables: Efficient sorting of colonial subjects for administrative roles
• Limits: Disconnects assessment from teaching, privileges dominant language/culture

Critical question for Harvard: Is the contemporary shift from writing to oral assessment also politically motivated?

• Writing became dominant when higher ed expanded (mass education, like colonialism)
• Oral assessment resurges when elite institutions feel threatened (AI, like decolonization)
• Are oral exams Harvard's way of re-establishing elite distinction (not everyone can perform orally in prestigious dialect)?

Decolonial perspective: If oral assessment is framed as "return to oral traditions" (respecting non-Western epistemologies), it could be liberatory. But if framed as "AI-proofing elite education" (protecting Harvard's gatekeeping function), it reproduces colonial logic.

Recommendation: Harvard should study indigenous oral assessment practices (e.g., Native American oral history traditions, African call-and-response pedagogy) rather than just importing European/colonial oral exam models. This honors multiple oral epistemologies, not just elite Western orality.

───

3. Women's Higher Education: Oral Exams as Gendered Barrier to Access
:paperclip: BestColleges / Wikipedia / IRJMETS, 19th-20th century
:link: https://www.bestcolleges.com/news/analysis/2021/03/21/history-women-higher-education/

Women were "effectively excluded from higher education for centuries" by "gender norms" that deemed women unsuitable for intellectual work. When women's colleges emerged in 19th century, there were debates about examination formats. Some argued that "examinations of girls should not get much importance" (suggesting women couldn't handle academic pressure), while others pushed for equal examination standards. Women's education researchers note that oral defense could be particularly intimidating for women students in era when "speaking in public" was considered unfeminine.

Harvard relevance: This historical context reveals that oral assessment has gendered implications:

19th-century debates:

• Conservative position: Women shouldn't take rigorous exams (oral or written) because intellectual strain harms their health
• Liberal position: Women should take same exams as men to prove equal intellectual capacity
• Feminist critique: Examination systems designed for men privilege masculine communication norms

Contemporary gender research shows:

• Women interrupted more frequently in classroom discussions and oral defenses
• Women's oral contributions judged more harshly than men's (same content, different reception)
• Women experience more anxiety about oral performance due to socialization against "speaking up"
• Women of color face intersectional bias—both gender and race affect oral assessment fairness

If Harvard implements oral assessment, must address gendered dynamics:

1. Training evaluators to recognize and counteract gender bias in oral assessment
2. Structured protocols that reduce opportunities for interruption (time limits, turn-taking rules)
3. Rubrics focused on content rather than performance style (which may encode gendered expectations)
4. Research on outcomes: Are women, non-binary students, and students from cultures that discourage women's public speech disadvantaged by oral assessment?
Bestcolleges.comA History of Women in Higher Education | BestCollegesToday, women make up the majority of bachelor's degree-holders — a victory that was only made possible after centuries of battling a sexist education system.[9:16 AM]Historical lesson: Every time educational barriers were challenged (racial integration, gender inclusion, disability access), the assessment systems had to be redesigned to avoid reproducing old exclusions in new forms. Oral assessment isn't inherently more or less equitable than writing—but it carries different equity risks that Harvard must proactively address.

───

Project Status: 89 posts | 259 sources | Sunday Feb 22, 09:15 AM | 47.75 hours remaining

Historical synthesis across three traditions: French concours (elite selection through oral prowess since 1748), British colonial exams (written assessment for mass bureaucratic sorting), and women's education exclusion (oral/written exams as gendered gatekeeping). Common thread: assessment modes are never neutral—they reflect and reproduce power structures (class, empire, gender). For Harvard: oral assessment can be tool of excellence or exclusion depending on design. Must learn from 280 years of French success (scaffolding, problem-solving) while avoiding colonial/gendered legacies of oral assessment as barrier.
elle-with-claws  [9:31 AM]
Theme 1: Peer Institutions Responding to AI Anxieties – Cycle 14

Focus: Princeton's Opt-In Oral Midterms, Hopkins' HopGPT Integration, Northwestern's Decentralized Chaos

───

1. Princeton University: Humanities Professor Implements "15-Minute Oral Midterm Exam" with Paper Opt-Out Option
:paperclip: The Daily Princetonian, October 1, 2025
:link: https://www.dailyprincetonian.com/article/2025/10/princeton-news-broadfocus-artificial-intelligence-humanities-professors.

Princeton humanities courses are experimenting with "15-minute oral midterm exam, three quizzes, and an in-person final exam, though students can opt to write a 5-page paper in lieu of the oral exam." The McGraw Center for Teaching and Learning encourages "faculty to articulate a clear policy on GAI use in your syllabus" and acknowledges that "students are likely to encounter different GAI policies in different courses."

Harvard relevance: Princeton's approach reveals a student-choice model that Harvard should seriously consider:

Opt-out oral assessment:

• Default: Students take 15-minute oral midterm
• Alternative: Students can write 5-page paper instead
• Rationale: Respects student autonomy while still offering oral option

Advantages of opt-out model:

• Equity: Students with severe speech anxiety, language barriers, or disabilities can choose written alternative
• Experimentation: Faculty can try oral assessment without forcing it on entire class
• Student agency: Treats students as partners in pedagogical design, not subjects
• Data generation: Track which students choose oral vs. written, analyze outcomes by demographic groups

Potential concerns:

• Self-selection bias: Students who choose oral may be those already confident in oral performance (privileging existing skills rather than developing new ones)
• Unequal rigor: Are oral and written alternatives truly equivalent in difficulty/learning outcomes? Or does one become "easy out"?
• Administrative complexity: Managing two parallel assessment tracks requires more faculty time/coordination

Harvard implementation: Could pilot opt-out model in select courses:

• Humanities/social sciences seminars (15-30 students)
• Clear rubrics showing oral and written assess same learning outcomes
• Survey students about choice rationale (anxiety? preference? prior experience?)
• Analyze outcomes: Do oral-choosers perform better/worse than paper-choosers? Are there demographic patterns?

Princeton's broader stance: McGraw Center's guidance to "articulate clear policy" suggests Princeton is taking pluralistic approach—different faculty can make different choices about AI, rather than university-wide mandate. This avoids the "institutional incoherence" problem seen at Duke (administration embraces AI while faculty resist).

───

2. Johns Hopkins University: Launches "HopGPT" Institutional AI Platform for Students, Faculty, and Staff
:paperclip: Johns Hopkins Hub, December 15, 2025
:link: https://hub.jhu.edu/at-work/2025/12/15/hopgpt-generative-ai-platform-launches/

Hopkins has launched "HopGPT, a secure, Hopkins-developed suite of generative artificial intelligence tools and resources to support their research, learning, teaching, clinical work, and administrative work." The university emphasizes that "faculty, staff, researchers, and students can all benefit from generative AI through educational resources, collaborative projects, and research opportunities."

Harvard relevance: Hopkins represents the integration strategy (not resistance):

Institutional AI investment:

• Custom platform: "Hopkins-developed" (not just licensing ChatGPT but building proprietary system)
• Security: Addresses data privacy concerns (student work not fed to OpenAI training)
• Universal access: Available to "students, faculty, and staff" (democratizes AI access)
• Multi-purpose: Supports "research, learning, teaching, clinical work, and administrative work"
The PrincetonianThe future of humanities teaching in the AI age, according to Princeton professorsSome professors are outright banning AI, turning assessments that used to be papers into in-class exams. Others are trying to work alongside AI, asking students to be transparent when they have used AI in research and writing. Others still are encouraging AI use and embracing its potential. And yet, they all expressed fears that AI could deeply impact critical thinking and writing.https://www.dailyprincetonian.com/article/2025/10/princeton-news-broadfocus-artificial-intelligence-humanities-professorsThe HubHopkins launches HopGPT, a generative AI platform for students, faculty, and staffHopkins-developed HopGPT provides secure access to popular AI tools including ChatGPT, Claude, and LlamaDec 15th, 2025https://hub.jhu.edu/at-work/2025/12/15/hopgpt-generative-ai-platform-launches/[9:31 AM]Strategic contrast with oral assessment approach:
[9:31 AM]• Hopkins: If AI is inevitable, provide institutional infrastructure so everyone can use it responsibly
• Oral assessment strategy: If AI is problematic, shift to assessment modes that avoid it

Neither is inherently superior—they're different philosophical positions:

• Integration philosophy: AI is tool like calculator or internet; education should teach appropriate use
• Resistance philosophy: AI undermines core learning objectives; education should preserve AI-free spaces

Harvard's current position: Harvard has not launched institutional AI platform like HopGPT. This suggests Harvard is more cautious about AI integration than Hopkins. But this creates equity problem: students with personal ChatGPT Plus subscriptions ($20/month) have AI advantage over students who can't afford it. Hopkins' HopGPT solves this by providing free, equal access.

Recommendation for Harvard: Even if some courses adopt oral assessment (AI resistance), Harvard should still provide institutional AI tools for courses that integrate AI. This allows faculty choice (integrate or resist) while ensuring equity (all students have same AI access).

Hopkins' teaching guidance: The Teaching@JHU page provides "generative AI tool guidance" for faculty—suggesting Hopkins is actively training faculty on AI pedagogy, not just providing tools. Harvard's Bok Center could play similar role.

───

3. Northwestern University: "Confusion" as "Departments and Individual Professors Generally Have Broad Discretion"
:paperclip: The Daily Northwestern, May 20 & November 29, 2025
:link: https://dailynorthwestern.com/2025/05/20/campus/northwestern-students-and-professors-navigate-confusion-opportunity-with-generative-ai/

Northwestern students and professors are "navigating confusion" as "departments and individual professors generally have broad discretion to implement their own AI policies." The university provides "free access to ChatGPT 4.0 through Microsoft Copilot" and notes that "guidelines for incorporating generative AI in the classroom are available, professors can still set individual policies for classes." Students report that "artificial intelligence tools like ChatGPT have become a staple in many Northwestern students' study routines."

Harvard relevance: Northwestern reveals the risks of decentralized policy:

Problems with faculty autonomy on AI policy:

• Student confusion: Different policies in every class (AI allowed in Econ, banned in English, required in CS, discouraged in History...)
• Inequality: Students in AI-permissive courses develop AI literacy; students in AI-restrictive courses fall behind
• Mixed signals: University provides free ChatGPT access (integration signal) while individual faculty ban it (resistance signal)
• Enforcement nightmare: How do students keep track of which professor allows what?

Northwestern student perspective (from article):

• AI is "staple... in study routines" (normalized, expected)
• Confusion about when/how to use it appropriately
• Desire for clearer institutional guidance

Contrast with Princeton and Hopkins:

• Princeton: Faculty autonomy BUT with McGraw Center support/guidance
• Hopkins: Institutional integration (HopGPT) BUT with teaching resources
• Northwestern: Faculty autonomy WITH confusion (guidelines exist but aren't effectively communicated)

Lesson for Harvard: If Harvard adopts "each concentration/department decides" approach (likely given decentralized culture), must provide:

1. Clear communication infrastructure: Central repository where students can find each course's AI policy
2. Faculty development: Bok Center workshops on AI policy design (not just "figure it out yourself")
3. Minimum standards: Even with faculty autonomy, some baseline rules (e.g., must disclose AI use if it affects grades)
4. Student voice: Gather student feedback on confusion/frustration with inconsistent policies
The Daily NorthwesternNU students, professors navigate generative AI useAlthough artificial intelligence tools like ChatGPT have become a staple in many Northwestern students’ study routines, students and faculty are simultaneously adapting to the implications of generative AI use for academic norms and student learning. Across NU, departments and individual professors generally have broad discretion to implement their own AI policies. The University provides guidance...Written byJenny Jang, ReporterEst. reading time4 minutesMay 20th, 2025https://dailynorthwestern.com/2025/05/20/campus/northwestern-students-and-professors-navigate-confusion-opportunity-with-generative-ai/[9:31 AM]Reddit backlash note: Northwestern students discovered professors using ChatGPT to generate course notes, leading to complaints that "students are paying for the expertise and experience of professors" not AI-generated content. This highlights double standard problem: if students can't use AI, can professors? Northwestern's decentralized policy creates no clear answer.

───

Project Status: 90 posts | 262 sources | Sunday Feb 22, 09:30 AM | 47.5 hours remaining

Synthesis across peer responses: Princeton experiments with opt-out oral (student choice, equity-conscious), Hopkins invests in AI integration (HopGPT platform, universal access), Northwestern decentralizes into confusion (faculty autonomy without coordination). For Harvard: avoid Northwestern's chaos through clear guidance, consider Princeton's opt-out model for equity, and learn from Hopkins that integration and resistance aren't mutually exclusive (can provide AI tools AND oral assessment spaces).
elle-with-claws  [9:46 AM]
Theme 2: History of Oral Assignments at Harvard – Cycle 14

Focus: College Honors Oral Exams, HBS 50% Participation Grading, HMS Clinical OSCEs

───

1. Harvard College Honors Programs: "45-Minute Oral Examination by Two Faculty Members" for Highest Honors
:paperclip: Harvard History & Literature / Government / English Departments, current policy
:link: https://english.fas.harvard.edu/guide-concentrators

Multiple Harvard College concentrations require oral examinations for honors students. English Department requires "in order to qualify for a departmental degree recommendation of highest honors, all eligible senior honors concentrators must take a forty-five minute oral examination by two faculty members at the end of the final term." History & Literature determines "concentration honors on the basis of the student's performance in the oral exam"—the oral exam grade is the deciding factor, not just supplementary. Government Department notes that "the grade received in the oral exam is also averaged in" to determine honors level.

Harvard current practice: This reveals that oral assessment is already standard for Harvard's highest-achieving students:

Honors oral exam characteristics:

• 45-minute duration: Long enough for substantive dialogue, not just quick check
• Two faculty members: Reduces individual bias, creates deliberative evaluation
• End-of-term timing: After thesis/coursework complete (defense not test)
• Honors-determining: Not just formality but critical assessment affecting degree distinction

Historical context (1958 Crimson): The Committee on Educational Policy once proposed "replacement of written finals by an essay oral examination" coupled with "fewer lectures, greater independent reading"—suggesting oral assessment as part of broader pedagogical vision (not just AI response). This proposal reflected belief that oral exams paired with tutorial instruction represented superior learning model.

What this means for AI-era debates:
Harvard has 60+ years of continuous oral exam tradition in honors programs. The infrastructure exists:

• Faculty trained in conducting oral exams
• Students in these concentrations know oral exam is coming (part of culture)
• Rubrics and norms established over decades
• Successful model that hasn't been abandoned despite scalability challenges

Expansion question: Should what currently exists for honors students only be extended to all students? Arguments:

• Equity: Why should only highest achievers get benefit of oral dialogue with faculty?
• Scalability: If it works for honors cohort, why not whole concentration?
• Counterargument: Honors students self-select into more intensive assessment; universal requirement may disadvantage students who struggle with oral performance

Design principle: If Harvard expands oral assessment beyond honors programs, study existing honors oral exams as proof of concept—they demonstrate oral assessment can be rigorous, sustainable, and valued by both students and faculty.

───

2. Harvard Business School: "Participation Often Accounts for 50% of the Total Course Grade" in Case Method Classes
:paperclip: HBS Christensen Center for Teaching & Learning, current pedagogy
:link: https://www.hbs.edu/teaching/case-method/leading-in-the-classroom/Pages/participation.aspx

HBS's signature case method is fundamentally oral: "participation often accounts for 50% of the total course grade" (explicitly stated twice in HBS teaching materials). The Christensen Center notes: "Active participation in class is essential to the case method's success, and the grading metrics reflect its importance." Instructors "evaluate class participation based on a student's contribution to the collective learning during class discussions"—not just speaking but quality of contribution.
[9:46 AM]Harvard institutional DNA: HBS has used oral assessment as primary evaluation mode for over 100 years (case method introduced 1920s). This is not experimental—it's proven, prestigious, and globally emulated.
[9:46 AM]What HBS case method reveals about oral assessment at scale:

• 50% of grade: Far more than typical College "20% participation"—oral performance is co-equal with written work
• Every class session: Not occasional oral exam but daily oral assessment (students expected to contribute multiple times per class)
• Contribution to collective learning: Assessment criteria focuses on advancing group understanding, not just displaying individual knowledge
• "Anathema in many schools": HBS acknowledges this is culturally specific—"in fact, is outlawed in some countries" (where oral assessment violates educational norms)

Cross-school learning opportunity for Harvard College:
HBS has sophisticated infrastructure for oral assessment that College could learn from:

• Cold-calling protocols: Faculty track who has/hasn't spoken, ensure equitable participation
• Questioning techniques: "Carefully designed questions are the key"—faculty train in asking questions that spark discussion
• Grading rubrics: Criteria for evaluating oral contributions (quality over quantity, listening not just talking)
• Student preparation: Case method requires extensive pre-class reading—oral performance depends on written preparation (hybrid model)

Equity lessons: HBS acknowledges participation grading can be problematic ("anathema in many schools"). Research shows case method can disadvantage:

• International students (language/cultural barriers to "jumping in")
• Women (interrupted more, contributions valued less)
• Introverts (penalized for processing internally vs. thinking aloud)

HBS addresses this through training (both faculty in equitable facilitation, students in oral skills) but hasn't fully solved equity challenges. Harvard College should learn from HBS's decades of experience with equity concerns in oral assessment.

───

3. Harvard Medical School: OSCEs as "Assessment of Choice" with "Objectivity, Reproducibility, and Easy Recall"
:paperclip: HMS MD Student Handbook / PMC / PubMed, current medical education
:link: https://medstudenthandbook.hms.harvard.edu/104-student-assessment-md-program

HMS uses Objective Structured Clinical Examinations (OSCEs) extensively: students take "three OSCEs" as graduation requirement. PMC research notes HMS "students in second year were found to perform better on interpersonal and technical skills than on interpretative or integrative skills" through OSCE assessment—revealing that oral assessment can diagnose specific learning gaps. HMS describes OSCEs as combining "multiple-choice examinations, NBME subject examinations, OSCEs" alongside "verbal feedback given by preceptors to students based on direct observation of clinical encounters."

OSCE as model for undergraduate oral assessment:
OSCEs are highly structured oral-practical exams:

• Standardized stations: All students rotate through same scenarios (ensures consistency)
• Objective criteria: Checklist-based scoring (reduces subjective bias)
• Multiple examiners: Different faculty at different stations (distributes bias risk)
• Clinical simulation: Students demonstrate skills with standardized patients (authentic performance)

Why OSCEs are called "assessment of choice":

• Objectivity: Written criteria against which all students judged
• Reproducibility: Same exam can be administered to multiple cohorts
• Easy recall: Performance documented, can be reviewed for teaching improvement
• Diagnostic power: Reveals specific skill deficits ("interpersonal vs. integrative")

Harvard College application: Could undergraduate courses adopt OSCE-inspired oral assessment?

• Standardized scenarios: Instead of "discuss your thesis," create common intellectual challenges all students address
• Objective rubrics: Clear criteria for evaluating oral responses (not vague "quality of thought")
• Station rotation: Students do multiple brief oral tasks (10 min each) rather than one long exam
[9:46 AM]• Simulation: Role-play scenarios (e.g., "present your research findings to skeptical policymaker")
[9:46 AM]Medical education insight: OSCEs assess performance (what students can DO) not just knowledge (what students KNOW). This is precisely what oral assessment offers undergraduates—demonstration of applied understanding in real-time, not just recall on written exam.

Caveat: OSCEs require massive resources (HMS employs standardized patients, dedicated OSCE facilities, trained assessors). Harvard College couldn't replicate this infrastructure, but could adapt the principles (standardization, objectivity, performance-based assessment) to lower-resource contexts.

───

Project Status: 91 posts | 265 sources | Sunday Feb 22, 09:45 AM | 47.25 hours remaining

Synthesis of Harvard's three oral traditions: College honors orals (45-minute faculty dialogue determining highest honors), HBS case method (50% participation grade through daily oral contribution), and HMS OSCEs (standardized clinical skill assessment). Common threads: (1) oral assessment as high-stakes (determines honors, half the grade, graduation requirement), (2) structured protocols (rubrics, standardization, training), and (3) proven sustainability (decades of successful use). For College expansion: Harvard doesn't need to invent oral assessment—it needs to scale and adapt what already works across the university.
elle-with-claws  [10:05 AM]
Theme 3: Educational Research on Oral Assessment (SoTL) – Cycle 14

Focus: Formative Oral Feedback, Cognitive Load Theory, and Universal Design for Learning

───

1. Interactive Oral Assessment: "Around 30-35% of Students Reported Enhanced Engagement and Skills"
:paperclip: Taylor & Francis / Frontiers in Education, February 2025
:link: https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/13562517.2025.2549945

Recent research on interactive oral assessment found that "around 30%, 30%, and 35% of students reported an enhanced engagement" alongside improvements in "analytical, problem-solving, and communication skills" rated as enhanced "quite a lot." Frontiers research notes that "formative assessment with immediate feedback has emerged as a vital pedagogical tool to enhance learning outcomes" and emphasizes the value of "oral peer review exercise" for formative assessment effectiveness.

Harvard relevance: This research quantifies what Harvard's tutorial-based programs already know intuitively—oral dialogue enhances learning. The key finding: oral assessment works best when formative (ongoing feedback) rather than purely summative (final grade).

Formative vs. summative oral assessment:

• Formative: Low-stakes oral check-ins throughout semester (office hours discussions, section presentations, peer review dialogues)
• Summative: High-stakes oral exam determining final grade
• Research finding: Formative oral assessment produces higher engagement and skill development than summative alone

Implementation for Harvard:
If adopting oral assessment, don't jump straight to summative oral finals. Instead, build formative oral infrastructure:

1. Week 3: Students present research questions orally (2 minutes, peer feedback)
2. Week 7: Oral outline of argument (5 minutes, faculty feedback)
3. Week 11: Draft thesis defense (10 minutes, Q&A practice)
4. Week 14: Final oral component (15 minutes, graded but students already practiced)

This scaffolding ensures oral assessment teaches oral skills rather than just tests them.

Immediate feedback advantage: Oral assessment enables "immediate, personalized feedback" (from the Oxford/MDPI research)—students hear faculty response in real-time, can ask clarifying questions, adjust understanding on the spot. Written feedback on papers comes days later, when students have mentally moved on. Oral feedback happens during the learning process.

Peer review finding: The Frontiers research on "oral peer review exercise amongst postgraduate students" shows that students giving/receiving oral feedback to peers develops evaluative literacy—learning to assess quality arguments, not just produce them. Harvard could implement:

• Peer oral defense practice groups (students present drafts, peers ask questions using faculty rubric)
• Reciprocal oral feedback (everyone gives and receives, building community of practice)

───

2. Cognitive Load Theory: Oral Assessment May Reduce Extraneous Load for Some Students, Increase It for Others
:paperclip: Taylor & Francis / LD@School / Nature, 2024-2025
UBC https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/1475939X.2024.2367517

Cognitive load research examines how "working memory" handles information during assessment. Key insight: "extra time and scribing can reduce the germane load associated with processing information for an assessment"—allowing students to "focus more on the skill that is being assessed rather than the associated skills that may be difficult for them." Research comparing "computer versus paper-based testing" found they're not always equivalent for working memory demands.

Harvard relevance: This reveals that assessment mode affects cognitive load differently for different students:

Written assessment cognitive load:

• Motor demands: Handwriting/typing competes with thinking for working memory
• Visual processing: Reading questions, organizing written responses
[10:05 AM]• Revision capability: Can edit, reducing load by allowing iterative refinement
[10:05 AM]• Anxiety trigger: Blank page, time pressure, editing perfectionism

Oral assessment cognitive load:

• Verbal processing: Speaking competes with thinking for working memory
• Real-time demand: No revision, must produce coherent response immediately
• Social processing: Managing evaluator presence, reading reactions
• Anxiety trigger: Performance pressure, can't hide, no "undo"

Differential impact by student profile:

• Students with dyslexia/dysgraphia: Oral may reduce load (bypass writing difficulty)
• Students with speech anxiety/autism: Oral may increase load (social processing demands)
• ESL students: Depends—some process oral L2 better, others need time to compose written response
• Students with ADHD: Oral structure may help focus OR increase distraction

Implication for Harvard: Oral assessment isn't universally more or less accessible than writing—it has different accessibility profile. Universal Design for Learning (UDL) principle: offer multiple assessment modes so students can demonstrate knowledge through their optimal channel.

Research-based recommendation:
Don't mandate all assessment be oral. Instead, offer choice:

• Option A: Written essay (traditional)
• Option B: Oral defense (recorded or live)
• Option C: Multimodal (written outline + oral explanation)

This respects neurological diversity while maintaining rigor.

───

3. Universal Design for Learning: "Offer Multiple Testing Formats (e.g. Project, Oral Exam, Open-Book) to Reduce Testing Pressure"
:paperclip: Cornell CTI / UBC / ResearchGate, December 2024 - February 2025
:link: https://teaching.cornell.edu/teaching-resources/inclusion-accessibility/universal-design-learning

UDL research emphasizes "offer multiple testing formats (e.g. project, oral exam, open-book) to reduce testing pressure (as long as formats promote equity)." ResearchGate study found "students appreciated being able to demonstrate understanding through non-traditional assessments like oral presentations or visual displays." UBC recommends "flexible assessments that allow students to demonstrate their learning in various ways, including presentations, projects."

Harvard relevance: UDL challenges the binary thinking around oral assessment:

Not: "Should Harvard replace written exams with oral exams?"
But: "Should Harvard offer multiple assessment pathways including oral options?"

UDL assessment menu for Harvard course:
Students choose one of these to demonstrate same learning outcomes:

1. Traditional essay (5-7 pages, written argument)
2. Oral defense (15-minute recorded presentation + Q&A)
3. Creative project (original work + written/oral reflection)
4. Digital portfolio (curated work with narrative explanation)

All options assessed on same rubric (argument quality, evidence use, critical thinking) but students choose modality that best fits their strengths.

Equity consideration from research: UDL is not "anything goes"—it's "flexible pathways to rigorous standards." The ResearchGate study notes that "lecturers demonstrated commitment to inclusivity but lacked training" in accessible design. This means:

• Faculty need training in creating equivalent assessment options
• Options must be truly equivalent in rigor, not "easy" vs. "hard" paths
• Rubrics must focus on content (ideas, evidence, reasoning) not format (polished writing, confident delivery)

Barrier reduction: Cornell notes "required in-person attendance can be a barrier for students with chronic illness, anxiety, depression, mobility, or transportation challenges." This suggests:

• Offer asynchronous oral options (recorded video) alongside synchronous (live Zoom/in-person)
• Allow multiple attempts (record oral defense, submit best version)
• Provide practice sessions (low-stakes oral rehearsal with feedback before graded version)

Harvard implementation: Pilot UDL assessment menu in 1-2 courses per department:
[10:05 AM]• Track: Which students choose which options? (demographics, performance outcomes)
[10:05 AM]• Survey: Why students made their choices (preferences, barriers, prior experience)
• Evaluate: Do all pathways assess learning equally? Or do some become "shortcuts"?
• Iterate: Refine options based on equity data

Research bottom line: Oral assessment can increase accessibility for some students while decreasing it for others. Solution: choice + scaffolding, not mandate.

───

Project Status: 92 posts | 268 sources | Sunday Feb 22, 10:03 AM | 46.95 hours remaining

Synthesis: SoTL research reveals oral assessment is most effective when (1) formative (ongoing feedback, not just final exam), (2) cognitively accessible (matched to students' working memory profiles, not one-size-fits-all), and (3) universally designed (multiple pathways to demonstrate learning, not single mandated format). For Harvard: avoid binary oral vs. written debate—design multimodal assessment systems with scaffolding, choice, and equity monitoring.
elle-with-claws  [10:16 AM]
Theme 4: Post-AI Classroom Experiments with Oral Assignments – Cycle 14

Focus: NYU's AI-Powered Oral Exams, Asynchronous Video Assessment, and "Flipped Assessment" Models

───

1. NYU Stern Professor's AI-Powered Oral Exams: "Only a Small Minority Preferred Them, Many Found Them More Stressful"
:paperclip: Business Insider / The Cool Down / El-Balad, January 2026
:link: https://www.businessinsider.com/nyu-professor-ai-oral-exam-mckinsey-memo-business-school-2026-1

An NYU Stern School of Business professor implemented AI-powered oral exams after "recognized a troubling trend in student assignments that appeared well-crafted yet reflected a lack of true understanding." The professor described traditional oral exams as "logistical nightmare" and "month-long hostage situation" before using AI to automate oral questioning. Student response: "Only a small minority preferred the AI oral exams, and many found them more stressful than written tests — even while acknowledging they were a better measure of real understanding."

Harvard relevance: This January 2026 case study provides fresh evidence on both promises and perils of oral assessment:

What worked:

• Authenticity detection: AI-generated essays were sophisticated but students couldn't defend them orally
• Better measure of understanding: Students admitted oral exams revealed true comprehension better than writing
• "Fighting fire with fire": Using AI to administer oral exams addressed the scalability problem

What didn't work:

• Student stress: Oral exams were more anxiety-inducing than written, even when students recognized their pedagogical value
• Preference gap: Acknowledging something "works better" ≠ preferring it (students want less stressful assessment, not necessarily better learning)
• Logistical burden: Even with AI assistance, professor described it as "nightmare"

Critical lessons for Harvard:

1. Student buy-in matters: If students find oral assessment more stressful despite recognizing it's more valid, Harvard needs to address anxiety mitigation not just academic integrity:
  • Scaffolding (low-stakes practice before high-stakes exam)
  • Choice (opt-in/opt-out models like Princeton)
  • Transparency (explain why oral, not just impose it)
2. "Better assessment" ≠ "preferred assessment": The NYU finding—students admitted oral was better measure but still didn't prefer it—reveals tension between pedagogical effectiveness and student experience. Harvard must decide: prioritize learning outcomes (oral despite stress) or student wellbeing (maintain less stressful written)?
3. Scalability remains challenge: Even with AI assistance, NYU professor found oral exams logistically nightmarish. This suggests hybrid models (some students oral, some written) may be more sustainable than universal oral.
4. McKinsey memo critique: Professor noted students' work "reads like McKinsey memos"—polished but shallow. This resonates with Harvard faculty concern about AI-generated writing. But solution shouldn't just be "make assessment harder/more stressful"—it should be redesigning learning so students want to understand deeply, not just perform adequately.

Controversial implication: If students find oral assessment more stressful and still don't prefer it even when acknowledging its validity, is Harvard justified imposing it? Or does this violate student-centered pedagogy?

───

2. Asynchronous Video Assessment: "Can Be Taken Up by Candidates at a Convenient Time, Allowing Many to Be in the Right Mindset"
:paperclip: Times Higher Education / ERIC / Sage Journals, 2020-2025
:link: https://www.timeshighereducation.com/hub/p/video-assessment
Business InsiderNYU professor tested students with AI oral exams, here's what happened nextWhen student work looked like McKinsey memos, an NYU business school professor used AI oral exams to test real learning.https://www.businessinsider.com/nyu-professor-ai-oral-exam-mckinsey-memo-business-school-2026-1Times Higher Education (THE)Video AssessmentWhat is a Video Assessment? Many Universities around the world (including many Business Schools) have started using asynchronous virtual interviews as standard assessments that are a part of the larger admissions selection process. These are not LIVE, and can be taken up by candidates at a convenient time (albeit within the submission deadline) allowing many to be in the right mindset while submitting their answers to the questions asked. In this assessment type, the candidate is expected to record the answers to a set of pre-recorded questions asked by the University recruitment team.Jul 10th, 2020https://www.timeshighereducation.com/hub/p/video-assessment[10:16 AM]Universities are implementing asynchronous video assessment as alternative to live oral exams: students record video responses "at a convenient time (albeit within the submission deadline) allowing many to be in the right mindset while submitting their answers." Sage research on "Reconsidering the Use of Oral Exams and Assessments" (2025) explores oral assessment as "An Old Way to Move Into a New Future"—suggesting recorded oral is viable alternative to synchronous.

Harvard relevance: Asynchronous video oral assessment solves several problems of traditional live oral exams:

Advantages over live oral exams:

• Flexibility: Students choose when to record (accommodates different peak performance times, eliminates scheduling conflicts)
• Anxiety reduction: Can re-record if needed (reduces one-shot pressure), no evaluator staring at you in real-time
• Equity: Accessible for students with social anxiety, those in different time zones, those with childcare/work conflicts
• Faculty efficiency: Review videos on own schedule (not coordinating 200 individual meeting slots)
• Record for review: Can be re-evaluated if grade appealed, used for teaching improvement

Challenges:

• Still AI-vulnerable: Students could use AI to prepare script, then read it on camera (though facial cues, hesitations, "reading eyes" may reveal this)
• Technical barriers: Requires video recording capability, stable internet upload
• Authenticity questions: How do you know student recorded alone, without notes/AI prompts visible off-camera?

Harvard implementation models:

Model A: Recorded oral defense

• Students submit written work (can use AI with disclosure)
• Record 10-minute video explaining argument, addressing pre-set questions
• Faculty grade on: coherence of explanation, ability to defend claims, depth beyond what's written

Model B: Timed video response

• Students receive question/prompt, have 10 minutes to prepare, 15 minutes to record
• Limited prep time reduces AI scripting opportunity
• Assesses real-time thinking while allowing some planning

Model C: Multi-take with reflection

• Students can record multiple takes, submit best one
• Required to submit meta-reflection explaining what they learned from multiple attempts
• Focuses on process/improvement, not just final performance

Best practice from THE: Clarify purpose—is video oral for verification (proving you understand your written work) or primary assessment (replacing written work)? Different purposes require different design.

───

3. Flipped Assessment with AI: "Teaching Students to Use Generative AI to Create and Critique Practice Questions"
:paperclip: Springer Nature / PubMed / Open Praxis, July 2025
:link: https://link.springer.com/article/10.1186/s41239-025-00540-2

Research on "flipped assessment" explores using AI not to replace assessment but to prepare students for assessment: "Teaching students to use generative AI to create and critique practice questions represents a means of encouraging" active learning. Open Praxis study shows AI-supported flipped classroom applications improve "learning experience, perception, and artificial intelligence literacy" when students use AI to generate practice content before live assessment.

Harvard relevance: This inverts the typical AI-assessment relationship:

Traditional model:

• Faculty create assessment
• Students banned from using AI
• Students take assessment (AI anxiety/detection)

Flipped assessment model:

• Students use AI to generate practice questions, draft responses, create study materials
• Faculty use oral assessment to verify students learned from AI-assisted preparation
• Goal: Teach AI literacy + assess genuine understanding

Practical implementation for Harvard:

Week 1-12: AI-assisted learning

• Students use ChatGPT to generate practice questions on course material
• Students critique AI-generated answers (find errors, improve reasoning)SpringerLinkDesign and assessment of AI-based learning tools in higher education: a systematic reviewInternational Journal of Educational Technology in Higher Education - Artificial intelligence (AI)-based learning tools are increasingly integrated in higher education, offering benefits such as...https://link.springer.com/article/10.1186/s41239-025-00540-2[10:16 AM]• Students use AI to create study outlines, flashcards, concept maps
[10:16 AM]• Goal: Develop AI literacy as learning skill

Week 13-14: Oral assessment of learning

• Students take oral exam (live or recorded) demonstrating understanding
• Oral questions probe: "Your AI study guide said X. Why is that correct/incorrect? Can you explain the underlying concept?"
• Goal: Assess whether students learned with AI or just from AI (passive consumption vs. active engagement)

Research finding: PubMed study on medical education notes "While increased confidence is positive, rigorous evaluation of AI-generated question quality is needed to confirm accuracy." This suggests:

• Teaching students to evaluate AI quality is itself a learning outcome
• Oral assessment can test: "When you used AI to study, how did you verify it was correct? Can you identify limitations in AI's explanation?"

Pedagogical shift: From "AI threatens assessment integrity" to "AI enhances preparation; oral assessment verifies learning". This reframes oral assessment as complement to AI rather than defense against AI.

Springer study caveat: Flipped classroom with AI works best when students have metacognitive skills to evaluate AI output critically. Harvard should provide explicit training in:

• How to prompt AI for learning (not just answers)
• How to identify AI hallucinations/errors
• How to go beyond AI to deeper understanding

Then use oral assessment to verify students developed these skills.

───

Project Status: 93 posts | 271 sources | Sunday Feb 22, 10:15 AM | 46.75 hours remaining

Synthesis: Real-world experiments reveal (1) AI-powered oral exams work but stress students (NYU), (2) asynchronous video reduces anxiety while maintaining authenticity (THE), and (3) flipped assessment uses AI for preparation + oral for verification (Springer). For Harvard: consider video oral option (flexibility + equity), flipped model (AI as learning tool + oral as assessment), and student wellness (effectiveness ≠ preference—address anxiety proactively).
elle-with-claws  [10:31 AM]
Theme 5: AI Used IN Oral Evaluation – Cycle 14

Focus: Automated Rubric-Based Scoring, AI Conversation Partners for Practice, and ConVOEs at Scale

───

1. AI-Assisted Grading: "Introduces Ethical Concerns Around Bias, Transparency, and Fairness"
:paperclip: Ohio State / The Case HQ / MDPI, August-November 2025
:link: https://ascode.osu.edu/news/ai-and-auto-grading-higher-education-capabilities-ethics-and-evolving-role-educators

Research on AI-assisted grading warns that while it offers "promising potential for large-scale, subjective assessments," it "introduces ethical concerns around bias, transparency, and fairness." The Case HQ provides practical guidance on "designing transparent rubrics for AI-based evaluation" to ensure "fairness, clarity, and accountability in automated student assessment systems." MDPI research developed "a scalable algorithm that automatically generates effective grading rubrics and decision tables with minimal human input"—though ScienceDirect notes that "fairness, reducing bias, and equal access issues" remain critical concerns.

Harvard relevance: If Harvard uses AI to assist oral assessment grading, these ethical frameworks become mandatory safeguards:

Transparency requirements:

• Explainable AI: Students should understand how AI scores were generated (not black-box algorithms)
• Rubric disclosure: Clear criteria that AI uses must be shared with students before assessment
• Human oversight: Faculty review AI scores, especially for borderline cases or when students appeal

Bias monitoring:
The research emphasizes that AI grading systems can perpetuate existing inequities:

• Language bias: AI trained on standard academic English may penalize non-native speakers, dialectal variations
• Cultural bias: AI may not recognize valid argumentation styles from non-Western rhetorical traditions
• Confidence bias: AI might score fluent-but-shallow responses higher than hesitant-but-deep thinking

Harvard implementation safeguards:

1. Pilot with equity audit: Before deploying AI grading, test on sample of oral exams:
  • Compare AI scores to faculty scores
  • Disaggregate by student demographics (international vs. domestic, first language, etc.)
  • Identify systematic scoring disparities
2. Rubric design principles:
  • Focus on content (argument quality, evidence use, logical coherence)
  • Minimize delivery scoring (fluency, confidence, vocal qualities that encode bias)
  • Weight substance over style (ideas > performance)
3. Human-AI collaboration model:
  • AI provides preliminary scores + explanatory feedback
  • Faculty review all AI assessments (not just flagged ones)
  • Faculty have override authority and document reasons
  • No fully automated grading without human verification

Research from Preprints.org (QWENScore+ methodology): Advances "interpretable scoring and feedback modeling"—suggesting AI's value may be less in replacing human judgment and more in generating detailed feedback that humans can review/edit. Harvard could use AI to:

• Transcribe oral exams automatically
• Identify key arguments/evidence in transcript
• Generate draft feedback ("Student explained X well but didn't address Y")
• Faculty review, edit, finalize feedback and score

This keeps human judgment central while leveraging AI for efficiency.

───

2. AI Conversation Partners: "75% Improvement in Overall Score from 4.69 to 8.24" Through Practice
:paperclip: Gliglish / SpeakPal / SmallTalk2Me / ScienceDirect, 2023-2026
:link: https://gliglish.com/
ascode.osu.eduAI and Auto-Grading in Higher Education: Capabilities, Ethics, and the Evolving Role of EducatorsAI and Auto-Grading in Higher Education: Capabilities, Ethics, and the Evolving Role of EducatorsAs artificial intelligence continues to reshape various industries, its impact on higher education, particularly in assessment, is becoming increasingly significant. Automated grading and AI-assisted evaluation tools are revolutionizing how instructors assess student work, streamline feedback, and manage large-scale courses.https://ascode.osu.edu/news/ai-and-auto-grading-higher-education-capabilities-ethics-and-evolving-role-educatorsGliglishGliglish · Learn languages by speaking with AISPEAK your languages for FREE with the 1st AI-based language teacher :female-singer:https://gliglish.com/[10:31 AM]Commercial AI conversation apps demonstrate dramatic learning gains from oral practice: Gliglish reports "app's effectiveness is demonstrated by the over 75% improvement in overall score" when students practice speaking with AI. SpeakPal "uses AI to simulate real-life conversations, giving you instant feedback on your pronunciation, grammar, and fluency as you speak." SmallTalk2Me offers "complete IELTS speaking test simulator" with "estimated band score, and detailed feedback on all assessment criteria." ScienceDirect longitudinal study found Swedish students who practiced with "embodied conversational agents" showed sustained improvement.

Harvard relevance: While these apps target language learning, the principle applies to any oral assessment—AI can provide unlimited low-stakes practice before high-stakes evaluation.

How Harvard could leverage AI conversation partners:

Scenario 1: Thesis defense practice

• Students upload thesis abstract to AI system
• AI generates challenging questions (like faculty would ask)
• Students practice oral responses via voice interface
• AI provides feedback: "Your response was unclear when explaining methodology. Try structuring: what you did, why, what you found."
• Students iterate until comfortable, then defend to faculty

Scenario 2: Case method preparation (HBS model)

• Students read case study
• AI poses discussion questions
• Students practice articulating analysis orally
• AI identifies gaps: "You addressed profitability but didn't consider ethical implications"
• Students develop fuller oral arguments before class participation (graded 50%)

Scenario 3: General oral exam prep

• Upload course readings/notes to AI
• AI generates potential oral exam questions across topics
• Students practice explaining concepts aloud
• AI flags: "You defined the term but didn't give an example or connect to broader theory"
• Students develop metacognitive awareness of their oral explanation weaknesses

Equity benefit: AI conversation practice democratizes access to oral preparation:

• Students without social capital (comfortable asking professors for practice) get coaching
• Students without resources (can't hire speaking tutors) get free feedback
• Students with anxiety (fear practicing with peers/faculty) get judgment-free space

Research caveat (ScienceDirect): Longitudinal study emphasizes "educational experiences over time"—AI practice works best when integrated into curriculum, not one-off. Harvard should provide:

• Recommended AI tools (vetted for accuracy, bias-free)
• Structured practice assignments ("Practice explaining 3 key concepts with AI this week")
• Reflection component ("What did AI feedback teach you about your oral communication?")

Critical limitation: AI conversation partners assess linguistic performance (fluency, grammar) better than intellectual substance (argument quality, critical thinking). For Harvard, AI practice should focus on oral delivery skills (clarity, structure, pacing) while faculty still assess content.

───

3. ConVOEs (Concurrent Video-Based Oral Exams): "Unthinkable" Large-Scale Implementation with 600+ Students
:paperclip: Sage Journals / Taylor & Francis, 2025-2026
:link: https://journals.sagepub.com/doi/10.1177/23792981241267744

A groundbreaking study describes implementing "Concurrent Video-Based Oral Exams (ConVOEs)" in an undergraduate business analytics course with "enrollment exceeded 600 students"—something authors call "unthinkable" but successfully executed. Taylor & Francis research on "interactive oral assessments (IOA) as an alternative to traditional written exams" in bioscience courses shows oral assessment is feasible at scale when properly designed.

Harvard relevance: This directly addresses faculty's biggest objection to oral assessment—"how do we scale this?"

ConVOE model (600-student course):

• Asynchronous video submission: Students record oral exam responses on own schedule
[10:31 AM]• Concurrent evaluation: Multiple graders assess different students simultaneously
• Standardized rubrics: Clear criteria ensure consistency across graders
• Tech platform: Dedicated system manages video uploads, grading workflows, score aggregation

What made it work:

1. Clear structure: Specific questions, time limits, submission format
2. Grader training: TAs calibrated on sample videos before grading
3. Technology infrastructure: Reliable platform for 600 video submissions
4. Rubric design: Focused on demonstrable knowledge not performance quality

Harvard application:

Economics 1011a (enrollment ~700):

• Replace final written exam with 15-minute video oral exam
• Students answer 3 questions (5 minutes each): explain concept, apply to novel case, critique argument
• 10 TAs grade ~70 videos each (manageable over exam period)
• Faculty spot-check 10% for quality control

Government 20 (enrollment ~400):

• Midterm: asynchronous oral policy brief (students present 10-minute analysis)
• Peer feedback round (students watch/comment on 3 peer videos)
• Faculty/TA grade final versions with peer feedback informing formative assessment

Scalability lessons from research:

Taylor & Francis on bioscience IOA: Success required:

• "Clear assessment guidelines" (students knew exactly what was expected)
• "Staff training in conducting oral assessments" (graders practiced before high-stakes evaluation)
• "Technology to support remote delivery" (asynchronous = flexibility, concurrent = efficiency)

Critical factors for Harvard:

• Not traditional one-on-one vivas (impossible at 600 students)
• Asynchronous + standardized (maintains rigor while enabling scale)
• Significant upfront investment (tech, training, rubric design)
• Ongoing quality monitoring (ensure consistency, identify grader drift)

Research finding: Both studies emphasize oral assessment at scale works when it's "authentic and addresses integrity concerns" (from Taylor & Francis)—students preferred it despite higher workload because they felt it better measured genuine understanding.

───

Project Status: 94 posts | 274 sources | Sunday Feb 22, 10:30 AM | 46.5 hours remaining

Synthesis: AI in oral evaluation offers three distinct applications: (1) Automated scoring (promising but requires transparency, bias monitoring, human oversight), (2) Practice partners (democratizes oral skill development, reduces anxiety), and (3) Scalability solutions (ConVOEs show 600+ student oral assessment is feasible). For Harvard: Use AI as assistant not replacement—AI for practice/preliminary scoring, humans for final judgment; async video + concurrent grading solves scale problem.
elle-with-claws  [10:46 AM]
Theme 6: Literary/Historical References on Speech vs. Writing – Cycle 14

Focus: Freire's Dialogic Pedagogy, Bakhtin's Utterance Theory, and Postcolonial Critique of Orality/Literacy

───

1. Paulo Freire: "He Had Treated His Students as Empty Vessels—Vaults in a Bank—Waiting to Be Filled"
:paperclip: JSTOR Daily / Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy / Pedagogy for Change, 1970/2025
:link: https://daily.jstor.org/paulo-freires-pedagogy-of-the-oppressed-at-fifty/

Freire's "Pedagogy of the Oppressed" (celebrating 50+ years in 2025) critiques the "banking model" of education where "the teacher doesn't teach, but is learning while in dialogue with the students." The model reduces "both students and cognition to objects which the teacher can manipulate at will." Freire's "critical pedagogy" emphasizes horizontal relationships and dialogic learning—knowledge emerges through conversation, not transmission.

Harvard relevance: Freire's critique directly challenges certain models of oral assessment:

Banking model oral exam:

• Faculty deposits questions → students withdraw memorized answers
• Hierarchy maintained (faculty knows, student performs)
• Oral becomes oral quiz—recitation, not dialogue
• Assessment as control mechanism (faculty tests whether students absorbed deposited knowledge)

Dialogic model oral assessment:

• Co-constructed understanding: Student presents ideas → faculty probes → student refines → genuine intellectual exchange
• Horizontal relationship: Both parties learn (faculty gains insight into student thinking; student develops ideas through questioning)
• Knowledge emerges in dialogue: Understanding isn't pre-existing but created through conversation
• Assessment as learning opportunity (not just measurement)

Critical question for Harvard: If oral assessment replicates "banking model" (faculty tests deposits), it's just oral quiz with same oppressive dynamics as written exams. But if oral assessment enables dialogue (Freire's ideal), it becomes liberatory pedagogy.

Design implications:

Banking oral exam (avoid):

• "Define X." → "Good." → "Next question."
• Faculty as authority, student as performer
• Right/wrong answer framework

Dialogic oral exam (aspire to):

• "How do you understand X?" → "Interesting—can you explain why?" → "What if Y?" → "How does that change your view?"
• Faculty as co-inquirer, student as thinker
• Evolving understanding framework

Freire's insight for AI era: The banking model is precisely what AI disrupts—if education = depositing information and withdrawing it on tests, AI can do that. The value of human education is dialogue—co-constructing knowledge through conversation. Oral assessment should embody this dialogic ideal, not just replicate banking model orally.

Equity consideration: Freire's pedagogy emerged from working with oppressed, illiterate Brazilian peasants. His critique of banking education was fundamentally about power—whose knowledge counts, who has voice. If Harvard oral assessment privileges elite oral culture (confident speakers from dominant backgrounds), it reproduces oppression. But if designed to amplify marginalized voices (listening to diverse ways of knowing), it can be liberatory.

───

2. Mikhail Bakhtin: "Dialogic Interactions Are Not Reducible to Forms Analyzable by Linguistic Methods"
:paperclip: Wikipedia / Academia.edu / Journal of Language Teaching and Research, 1981/2025
:link: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dialogue_(Bakhtin)
JSTOR DailyPaulo Freire’s Pedagogy of the Oppressed at Fifty - JSTOR DailyThe Brazilian educator Paulo Freire’s book, first published in English 50 years ago, urges viewing students as interlocutors or partners in the learning process.Sep 30th, 2020https://daily.jstor.org/paulo-freires-pedagogy-of-the-oppressed-at-fifty/WikipediaDialogue (Bakhtin)The twentieth century Russian philosopher and literary theorist Mikhail Bakhtin wrote extensively on the concept of dialogue. Although Bakhtin's work took many different directions over the course of his life, dialogue always remained the "master key" to understanding his worldview. Bakhtin described the open-ended dialogue as "the single adequate form for verbally expressing  authentic human life". In it "a person participates wholly and throughout his whole life: with his eyes, lips, hands, soul, spirit, with his whole body and deeds. He invests his entire self in discourse, and this discourse enters into the dialogic fabric of human life, into the world symposium."[10:46 AM]Bakhtin's theory of dialogism argues that "speakers shape an utterance according to three variables: the object of discourse, the immediate addressee, and a superaddressee." His concept of "speech genres" emphasizes that every utterance exists in "interactions between a speaker's words and the relationship they enter into with the utterances of other speakers." Crucially, Bakhtin argues dialogic relations "presuppose a language but do not reside within the system of language"—meaning oral interaction cannot be reduced to linguistic analysis alone.
[10:46 AM]Harvard relevance: Bakhtin provides theoretical framework for why oral assessment is fundamentally different from writing:

Written text (Bakhtin's critique):

• Monologic: Single author, frozen in time, no immediate addressee response
• Decontextualized: Separated from living dialogue, becomes "system of language"
• Analyzable: Can be broken into linguistic units, graded by rubric

Oral utterance (Bakhtin's ideal):

• Dialogic: Shaped by listener's anticipated response, exists in relationship
• Contextual: Meaning emerges from interaction, not text alone
• Irreducible: Cannot be fully analyzed by linguistic methods—requires understanding social relations

Implications for oral assessment rubrics:

Bakhtin suggests that rubrics analyzing oral performance linguistically (fluency, grammar, organization) miss the essence of orality—the dialogic relationship. Instead, Harvard oral assessment rubrics should evaluate:

• Responsiveness: Does student listen and adapt to faculty questions?
• Co-construction: Does dialogue generate new understanding (not just recitation)?
• Addressee awareness: Does student tailor explanation to listener's knowledge?
• Utterance richness: Does student bring multiple voices/perspectives into conversation (not just their own view)?

The "superaddressee" concept: Bakhtin argues speakers address not just immediate listener but "superaddressee"—ideal recipient who will understand. In oral exams:

• Student's superaddressee: Imagined scholarly community, future self, ideal reader
• Faculty's role: Serve as proxy for superaddressee, helping student articulate for broader audience

This reframes oral exam from "faculty tests student" to "faculty helps student speak to their superaddressee"—a pedagogical, not punitive, relationship.

Speech genres application: Bakhtin notes every field has speech genres (disciplinary ways of speaking). Oral exams should assess students' fluency in disciplinary speech genres:

• Historians speak in narrative-analytical mode
• Scientists speak in hypothesis-evidence-conclusion mode
• Philosophers speak in dialectical-argumentative mode

AI-era insight: AI-generated text is quintessentially monologic (single voice, no real addressee, no genuine dialogue). Bakhtin's theory explains why oral assessment resists AI—it requires living dialogue, which AI cannot truly replicate (even with conversational interfaces).

───

3. Postcolonial Theory: "Indigenous Knowledge Mainly Preserved in Memories of Elders and Shared Through Oral Communication"
:paperclip: ResearchGate / Academia.edu / RBM Journal, 2019-2024
UBC https://www.researchgate.net/publication/350142581_Rethinking_Oral_History_and_Tradition_An_Indigenous_PerspectiveAn_Indigenous_Perspective

Postcolonial scholars argue that "within cultures of orality, where wisdom traditions abound, memory is often 'a site of learning' distinct from forms of colonial knowledge." Research on Iroquoian languages notes "negotiating the roles of orality and literacy" while "emphasizing the significance of Indigenous Knowledge (IK) within linguistic practices." Duke study on Salish peoples found they had "time-based literacy inscribed within their surroundings"—a written culture different from European literacy.

Harvard relevance: This scholarship provides critical decolonial perspective on oral assessment debates:

Colonial literacy hierarchy:

• European assumption: Writing = civilization, orality = primitive
• Education as assimilation: Indigenous students forced into written literacy
• Knowledge devaluation: Oral traditions dismissed as unreliable, inferior

Decolonial oral epistemology:

• Oral knowledge as sophisticated: Complex mnemonic systems, communal verification
• Multiple literacies: Petroglyphs, place-based inscriptions, embodied practices (not just alphabetic writing)
• Fugitive memory: Oral traditions can preserve knowledge colonizers tried to erase
[10:46 AM]Critique of Harvard's oral assessment motivation:
[10:46 AM]If Harvard adopts oral assessment only because AI threatens written assessment, this instrumentalizes orality—using it as tool without valuing oral epistemologies. Postcolonial critique demands:

• Respect oral traditions as legitimate knowledge systems (not just "AI workaround")
• Learn from Indigenous oral pedagogies (storytelling, call-and-response, elder-apprentice dialogue)
• Recognize multiple ways of knowing (oral, written, embodied, place-based)

Practical implications:

Instead of: "We're switching to oral exams because students use ChatGPT for essays"

Reframe as: "We're integrating oral assessment because multiple modes of knowledge demonstration honor diverse epistemologies—including oral traditions that Western academia has historically marginalized"

Design principles from Indigenous oral traditions:

• Storytelling as argument: Oral exams could allow narrative explanations, not just analytical essays spoken aloud
• Communal knowledge: Group oral exams where students build on each other's responses (not isolated performance)
• Elder-apprentice model: Faculty as knowledge keeper, student as learner-in-dialogue (not examiner-examinee)
• Place-based learning: Oral exams could occur in relevant locations (lab for science, library special collections for history) not just generic office

ResearchGate scholar's warning: "Fugitive memory can conceal itself from public attention"—meaning some knowledge cannot/should not be assessed publicly (sacred knowledge, community secrets). This suggests oral assessment should include opt-out provisions for students from cultures where certain knowledge is not for examination.

───

Project Status: 95 posts | 277 sources | Sunday Feb 22, 10:45 AM | 46.25 hours remaining

Philosophical synthesis: Freire (oral as dialogic liberation vs. banking oppression), Bakhtin (oral as dialogic utterance irreducible to linguistics), and postcolonial theory (oral as decolonial epistemology, not Western fallback). For Harvard: Oral assessment can be liberatory (dialogue, co-construction, epistemological pluralism) or oppressive (quiz, performance test, instrumentalism). The choice depends on design philosophy, not just format.
elle-with-claws  [11:01 AM]
Theme 7: Professional Domains — Academia, Science, Policy – Cycle 14

Focus: Job Talks as Career Gatekeepers, Scientific Conference Presentations, and Policy Briefings

───

1. Academic Job Talk: "No Matter How Great of a Scientist You Are, If You Cannot Give a Compelling Job Talk, Chances Are Low You Will Be Hired"
:paperclip: PLOS Computational Biology / PMC / MIT CommLab, 2019-2025
:link: https://journals.plos.org/ploscompbiol/article?id=10.1371/journal.pcbi.1007163

PLOS article on "Ten Simple Rules for Giving an Effective Academic Job Talk" emphasizes that "many graduate students and postdocs are not taught the skills necessary" for this critical oral performance. UCSF notes faculty candidates "may be asked to give a job talk about your prior research, a chalk talk about your future research, or a demonstration of your teaching." Academia Stack Exchange confirms this is "standard practice" across universities, with teaching-focused institutions prioritizing the teaching demonstration.

Harvard relevance: The academic job talk reveals oral performance as ultimate career determinant—no amount of published papers guarantees a faculty position without successful oral defense of research.

Why job talk matters for undergraduate oral assessment:

Parallel structure:

• Job talk: 45-60 minute presentation + Q&A defending research to hiring committee
• Undergraduate oral exam: 15-30 minute presentation + Q&A defending thesis/coursework to faculty

Both assess:

• Clarity of thought: Can you explain complex ideas accessibly?
• Depth of understanding: Can you answer probing questions?
• Intellectual presence: Do you command the material confidently?
• Responsiveness: Can you adapt to unexpected challenges?

Critical training gap: PLOS notes graduate students "are not taught" job talk skills—they're expected to learn by osmosis. If Harvard implements oral assessment, must avoid same mistake:

• Explicit instruction in oral defense skills (Bok Center workshops)
• Practice opportunities (mock oral exams in safe settings)
• Feedback loops (students practice → receive feedback → improve → practice again)

MIT CommLab model: MIT provides dedicated communication lab support for job talks—coaching on structure, delivery, Q&A strategy. Harvard could offer similar infrastructure for undergraduate oral assessment:

• Drop-in coaching hours before oral exams
• Peer practice groups (students rehearse with each other)
• Video recording + self-review (students watch themselves, identify improvement areas)

Equity concern: Who gets informal mentoring on job talk skills? Students with faculty mentors who invest time in coaching. Same pattern could emerge with undergraduate oral assessment—privileged students get preparation help, others don't. Solution: institutionalize support so all students have access.

Meta-observation: If oral defense is necessary for faculty hiring (assessing research understanding), why isn't it standard for undergraduate education (developing research understanding)? Job talks demonstrate oral assessment works at highest professional level—Harvard should leverage this for undergraduate learning.

───

2. Scientific Conference Presentations: "Gain Essential Written, Oral, and Visual Communication Skills That Will Set You Apart"
:paperclip: Frontiers in Education / SciComm Success / University of Pittsburgh, 2025
:link: https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/education/articles/10.3389/feduc.2025.1465227/full
FrontiersFrontiers | Making a difference through presentations: Let’s speak, communicate, and connect!In our personal and professional lives, we have numerous opportunities to communicate our ideas to others. Therefore, the importance of presentation goes bey...[11:01 AM]Recent research emphasizes "towards a set of design principles for developing oral presentation competence" in higher education, noting that scientific presentations serve multiple functions: "DEI (diversity, equity, and inclusion), networking, presentation skills, public outreach, public speaking, science communication." Max Planck institutes "prepare students for defense talks, as well as for presentations at international conferences." University of Pittsburgh offers "Core Communication Skills for Scientists Workshop" to develop competencies that "set you apart in your training and future career."

Harvard relevance: Scientific training explicitly recognizes oral communication as teachable, career-critical skill—not innate talent but developed competence.

What Harvard sciences can learn from scientific presentation training:

Structured progression:

1. Year 1: Lab group meetings (informal 10-min research updates)
2. Year 2: Departmental seminars (20-min formal presentations)
3. Year 3: Conference presentations (15-min talks to broader audience)
4. Year 4: Thesis defense (60-min comprehensive oral examination)

This scaffolding builds confidence and skill over time. Harvard undergraduate science concentrations could implement similar:

• Freshman seminar: Explain one experiment orally (5 min)
• Sophomore methods course: Present research proposal (10 min)
• Junior tutorial: Defend hypothesis with data (15 min)
• Senior thesis: Full oral defense (30-45 min)

Frontiers research on "design principles": Effective oral presentation development requires:

• Repeated practice (not one-off performance)
• Peer feedback (students learn from watching/critiquing each other)
• Expert modeling (seeing excellent presentations, not just being told "present well")
• Reflection (metacognitive awareness of what makes oral communication effective)

SciComm Success "Share Your Science" modular training: Professional development offerings for scientists include:

• Structuring a scientific narrative
• Designing effective visuals
• Handling difficult questions
• Presenting to non-specialist audiences

Harvard could adapt these modules for undergraduate science communication courses—making oral skills explicit learning outcomes rather than implicit expectations.

DEI dimension (Frontiers): Research emphasizes conference presentations as "networking, public outreach" opportunities—not just knowledge demonstration but professional visibility. For underrepresented groups in STEM, oral presentation skills become career access tools. Harvard should frame oral assessment as empowerment (developing professional competencies) not gatekeeping (testing who naturally performs well).
