3. Policy Briefing Skills: "Structuring and Preparing for Oral Briefings to Ensure Key Messages Are Communicated"
:paperclip: Institute on Governance / UChicago Harris / Canada School of Public Service, 2025
UBC https://iog.ca/learning-and-leadership/courses/24-briefings-on-demand-part-2-oral-briefs/

Government institutions offer systematic training in "oral briefings" as distinct skill: "understanding audience needs and tailoring oral briefings accordingly" and "responding confidently to unexpected questions and challenges during briefings." UChicago Harris course combines "interactive lectures, case studies, peer workshops, and individual feedback to facilitate clarity, structure, and persuasive impact." Canadian federal government provides "briefing skills learning path" teaching how to "transform complex analysis and data into accessible and visually appealing briefing products."

Harvard relevance: Policy practitioners distinguish between written briefs (background documents) and oral briefings (face-to-face presentations to decision-makers)—each requiring different skills.

Policy briefing model for Harvard:

Written brief:

• 2-3 pages max
• Executive summary, key findings, recommendationsInstitute on GovernanceBriefings on Demand Part 2: Oral Briefs - Institute on Governance3 Hours Briefings on Demand Part 2: Oral Briefs Explore and practice skills and tips on how your oral briefs...https://iog.ca/learning-and-leadership/courses/24-briefings-on-demand-part-2-oral-briefs/[11:01 AM]• Evidence-based, neutral tone
• Graded on: clarity, evidence quality, logical flow[11:01 AM]Oral briefing (based on written brief):

• 5-10 minute presentation to "minister" (faculty role-play)
• Must answer challenging questions
• Defend recommendations under scrutiny
• Graded on: responsiveness, adaptation to audience, defending evidence

This hybrid model addresses AI concerns: Students can use AI to help draft written brief (with disclosure), but oral briefing verifies they understand the analysis enough to explain and defend it.

Canadian government training insight: Distinguishes "briefing up, briefing across, briefing out":

• Up: To senior decision-makers (concise, strategic, recommendation-focused)
• Across: To colleagues/peers (detailed, technical, collaborative)
• Out: To public/stakeholders (accessible, persuasive, responsive to concerns)

Harvard oral assessment could similarly differentiate oral formats:

• Briefing up: Present thesis to "university president" (5 min, key insights only)
• Briefing across: Discuss argument with peers (15 min, detailed engagement)
• Briefing out: Explain research to general audience (10 min, no jargon)

Each tests different audience awareness skills—central to effective oral communication.

Harris School AI integration: The policy communication course teaches "responsible use of AI as an editing tool" while emphasizing that "moral framing, behavioral insights" require human judgment. This models AI-integrated assessment: students use AI for drafting, faculty assess oral communication of ideas.

Professional preparation argument: If Harvard concentrations (Gov, Economics, Social Studies) feed students into policy careers, oral briefing skills are not optional extras but core professional competencies. Oral assessment becomes authentic preparation, not just AI workaround.

Synthesis across professional domains: Academic hiring (job talk determines career), scientific research (conference presentations build reputation), and policy work (oral briefings drive decisions) all privilege oral communication as gatekeeping skill. For Harvard: oral assessment isn't pedagogical experiment but alignment with professional reality. If graduates need oral competence for careers, undergraduates should develop it through curriculum—with explicit training, scaffolded practice, and formative feedback, not just high-stakes testing.

Theme 0: History of Oral Examinations in Higher Ed – Cycle 15

Focus: Japanese Education Reform, Soviet Standardized Orals, and ADA Accommodation History

───

1. Japanese Education Reform: "Individual Examinations to Be Abolished in Principle—Students Selected Based on Essays, Presentations, Group Discussions, Interviews"
:paperclip: Taylor & Francis / Springer / Nippon.com, 2016-2023
:link: https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/0969594X.2016.1225667

Japan's massive education reform aims to "evaluate applicants' broader competencies beyond academic ability and the mere quantity of their knowledge." The reform explicitly addresses criticism that "Japanese elite higher education... have been producing students, who may be adept at rote learning and passing academic entrance exams, but yet who lack academic proficiency, enthusiasm, motivation to learn or other broad skills." Individual university exams are being "abolished in principle" in favor of "essays written in L1 Japanese, presentations, group discussions, interviews and other forms of evaluation."

Harvard relevance: Japan's national shift from written-only to multimodal assessment (including oral) provides large-scale contemporary evidence that oral assessment addresses recognized educational problems:

Japanese critique of pure written exams:

• Rote learning: Students memorize for tests without deep understanding
• Lack of enthusiasm: Assessment doesn't measure motivation or intellectual curiosity
• Missing competencies: Graduates can pass exams but lack communication, collaboration, critical thinking skills
• Academic vs. professional disconnect: Test performance ≠ career/social success

Sound familiar? Harvard faculty express similar concerns about AI-era writing:

• Students can generate essays without understanding
• Written assessment doesn't measure genuine engagement
• Essays don't develop/assess oral communication, collaborative skills
• Academic writing ≠ professional competencies

Japan's solution: Multimodal assessment combining:

• Written essays (analytical thinking)
• Oral presentations (communication clarity)
• Group discussions (collaborative reasoning)
• Individual interviews (depth of understanding)

Critical insight for Harvard: Japan isn't just replacing written with oral—they're recognizing different modes assess different competencies. Harvard should consider similar assessment portfolio approach:

• Written work: Extended argument development, research synthesis
• Oral presentations: Public communication, structured explanation
• Group discussions: Collaborative learning, peer engagement
• Individual oral exams: Depth of understanding, adaptive thinking

Historical timing: Japan implemented these reforms 2016-2023 (pre-AI anxieties)—suggesting oral assessment addresses fundamental pedagogical concerns, not just AI-era problems. The fact that Japan's solution (more oral assessment) emerged independently validates it as educational best practice, not just Western panic response.

Equity lesson: Japan faced pushback about fairness—will reforms favor students from wealthy families with access to interview coaching? This forced attention to scaffolding (schools must teach presentation/interview skills, not assume students arrive with them). Harvard must do same.

───

2. Soviet Union Education: "National Examinations, Written and Oral, Held at Particular Times Throughout the Country"
:paperclip: The Atlantic / Reddit AskHistorians / Revolutionary Democracy, 1953/2020
UBC https://www.theatlantic.com/magazine/archive/1953/04/soviet-education/640302/
The AtlanticSoviet EducationThe Atlantic covers news, politics, culture, technology, health, and more, through its articles, podcasts, videos, and flagship magazine.[11:16 AM]Soviet education system featured "oral exams... with questions usually being shorter" that tested "not only knowledge of the student (both intra- and extracurricular), but also understanding of the topic." The Atlantic (1953) reported "From the fourth grade on there are national examinations, written and oral, held at particular times throughout the country." Soviet system emphasized that Lenin called for both "a reserve of modern learning and a precise knowledge of facts" AND "an ability for independent thought and creative work."

Harvard relevance: Soviet oral exams demonstrate that highly centralized, standardized education can incorporate oral assessment—challenging assumption that oral exams are inherently subjective/unscalable.

Soviet model features:

• Standardized nationwide: All students in same grade take oral exams on same date across USSR
• Dual format: Written exams (knowledge) + oral exams (understanding/synthesis)
• From elementary up: Oral assessment normalized from age 10, not introduced only at university
• Ideological purpose: Oral exams assessed not just facts but "independent thought and creative work"—developing good Communists meant developing critical thinkers (within acceptable bounds)

Ideological parallel to AI era: Just as Soviet education had political goals (forming Communist citizens), contemporary oral assessment debates have institutional goals (preserving academic integrity, resisting AI commodification of education). Both use assessment design to shape student formation.

Critique of Harvard's potential adoption: If Harvard implements oral assessment only to combat AI, is this:

• Defensive: Protecting institutional gatekeeping function?
• Ideological: Asserting human-centered education against technological determinism?
• Pragmatic: Developing genuinely valuable skills?

Soviet history suggests assessment modes are never neutral—they always serve institutional/ideological purposes. Harvard should be explicit about what purposes oral assessment serves (not just "because AI").

Scalability lesson: Soviet oral exams worked at massive scale (entire nation, millions of students). If USSR could administer national oral exams in 1953 (pre-digital infrastructure), Harvard with modern technology (video recording, ConVOEs) has no scalability excuse. The question isn't "can we?" but "should we?"

Warning from history: Soviet standardized oral exams also had conformity pressures—students learned to give "correct" answers to oral questions about ideology. Similarly, if Harvard oral exams have implicit right answers (faculty's preferred interpretations), they become oral conformity tests not genuine assessments of understanding. Design must allow intellectual diversity in oral responses.

───

3. ADA and Oral Exams: "Extended Exam Time, Note-Taking Assistance, Adaptive Technology" as Accommodations
:paperclip: ADA National Network / AHEAD / ADA.gov, 2011-2025
UBC https://adata.org/research_brief/higher-education-and-ada

Research on "history of disability services in higher education" notes that "accommodations and universal design: supporting access to assessments" requires consideration of how different assessment formats affect different disabilities. ADA.gov guidance on "testing accommodations" addresses oral exams specifically, though most focus remains on written tests. Madaus (2011) traces disability accommodation evolution, showing longstanding school accommodations and historical documentation can inform current practice.

Harvard relevance: If Harvard expands oral assessment, ADA compliance becomes critical legal/ethical obligation:

Oral exams may disadvantage students with:

• Speech/communication disorders: Stuttering, apraxia, selective mutism
• Social anxiety/autism: Difficulty with interpersonal interaction, eye contact expectations
[11:16 AM]• Processing disorders: Need time to formulate responses (real-time pressure problematic)
• English language learners: Oral fluency lags written proficiency
[11:16 AM]• Hearing impairments: If oral exam includes listening to faculty questions

Oral exams may advantage students with:

• Dyslexia/dysgraphia: Oral bypasses writing difficulties
• ADHD: Oral dialogue more engaging than extended writing
• Visual impairments: Speaking doesn't require seeing written text

This creates accommodation paradox: Same assessment mode that increases access for some students decreases it for others. Universal Design for Learning solution: multiple assessment pathways (students choose oral OR written OR multimodal based on strengths).

Required accommodations for oral exams (based on ADA guidance):

• Extended time: Same as written exams (1.5x, 2x standard duration)
• Separate room: Reduce social anxiety for students who need it
• Advance notice: Questions provided ahead so students can prepare (reduces real-time processing demands)
• Written option: Students can submit written responses instead of oral if speech disorder/severe anxiety
• Interpreter services: For Deaf/hard-of-hearing students
• AAC devices: Students with speech impairments can use text-to-speech or other assistive tech

Historical lesson: Disability rights advocacy transformed "separate but equal" (disability students take different, often lesser exams) to "equal access through accommodation" (same rigorous standards, different support to reach them). If Harvard implements oral assessment, must ensure accommodated oral exams are equally rigorous and valued, not "lesser alternative."

Documentation concern: Current practice requires "current clinical data" not just "longstanding school accommodations." This creates access barriers (students must get recent diagnostic testing, expensive). If oral exams become widespread, disability services must proactively reach out to potentially affected students, not wait for them to request accommodations.

───

Project Status: 97 posts | 283 sources | Sunday Feb 22, 11:15 AM | 45.75 hours remaining

Historical synthesis across three contexts: Japan (2016-2023 reforms toward oral for broader competencies), Soviet Union (1950s+ national standardized oral exams), and USA (1990+ ADA requiring oral exam accommodations). Common threads: (1) oral assessment serves institutional goals (Japanese competency development, Soviet ideological formation, American accessibility), (2) scalability is solvable (Japan national system, Soviet millions of students), (3) equity requires design (Japanese coaching concerns, Soviet conformity, American accommodations). For Harvard: learn from global history—oral assessment is feasible, but success depends on explicit goals, equity infrastructure, and legal compliance.
elle-with-claws  [11:31 AM]
Theme 1: Peer Institutions Responding to AI Anxieties – Cycle 15

Focus: Penn's Decentralized Guidance, CMU's Warning Against Oral Over-Reliance, Berkeley's Acknowledgment

───

1. University of Pennsylvania: "Faculty Across the University Have Taken Varying Approaches to AI Use in Learning"
:paperclip: Penn CETL-I / The Daily Pennsylvanian, December 2025 - February 2026
:link: https://cetli.upenn.edu/resources/generative-ai/penn-ai-guidance-and-policies/

Penn provides "guidelines for the use of AI tools with student data and educational records" while acknowledging that "faculty across the University have taken varying approaches to AI use in learning." The Daily Pennsylvanian (Feb 2026) reports that "while the policy provided a general framework for the Penn community," implementation remains decentralized. Penn CETL-I guidance addresses "privacy, intellectual property, academic integrity, and accessibility" concerns with AI, last updated December 15, 2025.

Harvard relevance: Penn represents the coordinated pluralism model—neither top-down mandate nor chaotic free-for-all, but institutional guidance + faculty autonomy.

Penn's approach:

• Central framework: University provides AI policy principles (privacy, academic integrity)
• Faculty discretion: Individual professors decide classroom AI rules
• Resource support: CETL-I offers guidance materials, not requirements
• Transparency: Policies publicly documented, regularly updated

Contrast with other peer responses:

• Hopkins (HopGPT): Institutional AI platform (integration strategy)
• Northwestern: Faculty autonomy → student confusion (coordination failure)
• Penn: Guidance + autonomy → managed diversity (middle path)

What Harvard can learn from Penn:

If Harvard adopts Penn model:

• Pros: Respects faculty expertise, allows disciplinary differences, supports experimentation
• Cons: Students face inconsistent expectations across courses, equity depends on faculty knowledge

Penn's guidance structure could inform Harvard:

1. Establish baseline principles (e.g., "students must disclose AI use when it affects graded work")
2. Provide faculty toolkit (sample syllabi language, rubric examples, assessment alternatives including oral)
3. Document publicly (students can find each course's AI policy clearly stated)
4. Update regularly (acknowledge AI landscape changes rapidly)

Critical observation: Penn's daily student newspaper reports faculty "varying approaches" in February 2026—over 3 years after ChatGPT launch. This suggests even well-resourced institutions with teaching centers haven't converged on best practices. Harvard shouldn't expect quick consensus.

Penn math department example (from Daily Penn): Some Penn math courses ban AI entirely, others integrate it explicitly. This disciplinary variation makes sense—math proofs may benefit less from AI than policy writing. Harvard should allow similar concentration-specific approaches rather than College-wide oral assessment mandate.

───

2. Carnegie Mellon: "Avoid Over-Reliance on Oral Exams and Presentations as Reaction to Generative AI Tools"
:paperclip: CMU Eberly Center for Teaching Excellence, 2025
:link: https://www.cmu.edu/teaching/technology/aitools/index.html

CMU's official teaching guidance explicitly warns against oral assessment as panic response: "Avoid over-reliance on hand-written deliverables, in-class evaluations, or oral exams and presentations: We do not recommend drastically changing your assessments to exclusively or excessively rely on the aforementioned approaches as a reaction to concerns regarding generative AI tools." Meanwhile, The Tartan (Feb 2026) reports CMU students showing "over-reliance on AI" with professors noting students "dropping difficult courses, going to fewer office hours, and receiving lower grades."
Center for Excellence in Teaching, Learning and InnovationPenn AI Guidance and PoliciesGenerative AI raises considerations related to privacy, intellectual property, academic integrity, and accessibility. Penn provides guidelines for the use of AI tools with student data and educational records. This information […]Reading time2 minutesJul 17th, 2025cmu.eduGenerative AI Tools FAQ  - Eberly Center - Carnegie Mellon UniversityGenerative AI Tools FAQ[11:31 AM]Harvard relevance: CMU—arguably the top CS/AI research university—explicitly cautions against oral assessment as AI workaround. This is strongest institutional pushback against the oral-exam-as-solution narrative.

CMU's reasoning (Eberly Center):

Why NOT to switch to oral exams:

• Reactive, not pedagogical: Changing assessment format out of "reaction to concerns" rather than educational principles
• Over-reliance danger: "Exclusive or excessive" dependence on oral/handwritten creates new problems
• Missing the opportunity: Reactively avoiding AI prevents learning to integrate AI thoughtfully

Implications for Harvard:

CMU's warning suggests that rushing to oral assessment without pedagogical justification could be:

• Defensive posture: Protecting traditional education rather than evolving it
• Missed learning: Students need to learn critical AI use for careers
• New inequities: Over-reliance on oral disadvantages students for whom oral performance is barrier

But CMU also shows AI costs (The Tartan Feb 2026):

• Students with "over-reliance on AI... almost an addiction"
• CS students dropping hard courses (using AI as crutch, not learning tool)
• Lower grades despite (because of?) AI assistance
• Fewer office hours attendance (AI replaces human help-seeking)

CMU's paradox: Warning against oral assessment while documenting serious AI-related learning problems. This suggests CMU hasn't found the solution either.

What Harvard should take from CMU:

Don't: Implement oral assessment exclusively or excessively as reaction to AI

Do:

• Consider oral assessment as one tool in multimodal repertoire
• Ensure any assessment change has pedagogical rationale beyond AI worries
• Design courses that teach critical AI use rather than just ban/avoid it
• Study CMU's findings on AI over-reliance (what are warning signs? interventions?)

CMU's research initiative: April 2025 announcement of empirical research on AI impacts on teaching/learning. Harvard should follow this research rather than react hastily. CMU will generate evidence on whether/when/how AI helps or harms student outcomes—Harvard can benefit from waiting for data.

───

3. UC Berkeley: "In-Person Assessment Types Such as Oral Examinations Are Unlikely to Be Affected by ChatGPT"
:paperclip: Berkeley Academic Senate / Berkeley Law Library, 2023-2025
UBC https://academic-senate.berkeley.edu/implications-of-llms-chatgpt-for-final-assessments

Berkeley's Academic Senate guidance notes matter-of-factly that "some in-person assessment types, such as laboratory work, musical performances, or oral examinations, are unlikely to be affected by ChatGPT and other LLMs." Berkeley Law added LLMs to formal academic integrity policy and maintains regularly updated GenAI resources (last updated October 2025). Berkeley Art of Writing program acknowledges ChatGPT's "capacity to generate essay-length responses" has "generated widespread concern among university faculty and administrators."

Harvard relevance: Berkeley treats oral assessment as obvious option (neither novelty nor panacea)—just one assessment type naturally resistant to LLM disruption.

Berkeley's matter-of-fact tone is instructive:

Not: "We must urgently shift to oral exams!"

But: "Oral exams, like lab work and performances, aren't affected by ChatGPT" [so those assessment types can continue as before]

This normalizes oral assessment rather than treating it as emergency measure or controversial innovation.

Berkeley Law's approach:

• Added LLMs to existing academic integrity policy (not creating new rules, adapting old ones)
• Sent reminders to instructors and students (reinforcing established norms)
• Provides curated resources on GenAI in legal education/practice (educational, not punitive)
[11:31 AM]Berkeley Art of Writing framing: Acknowledges faculty/administrator "widespread concern" about ChatGPT generating essay-length responses from assignment prompts. But program doesn't dictate solutions—offers resources for faculty to make informed choices.

What Harvard should take from Berkeley:

Tone: Pragmatic acknowledgment rather than panic or utopianism

Strategy: Oral assessment as continuity (we already do this in some courses; AI doesn't change that) rather than disruption (we must overhaul everything!)

Communication: Information resources rather than top-down mandates

Berkeley's model suggests:

• Concentrations already using oral assessment (History & Lit, Social Studies) → keep doing it, maybe expand
• Concentrations without oral tradition → could add oral components (but not required to)
• Central administration → provide resources (Bok Center workshops, rubric examples) but don't mandate oral assessment College-wide

Historical note: Berkeley's guidance from 2023 (early ChatGPT era) to 2025 shows evolution not revolution—institutions are adapting gradually, not transforming overnight. Harvard has time to be thoughtful.

Synthesis across peer approaches: Penn (decentralized guidance + faculty autonomy), CMU (explicit warning against oral over-reliance + documenting AI costs), and Berkeley (matter-of-fact acknowledgment of oral as AI-resistant option). For Harvard: no peer consensus on "right response"—each takes different path. Lesson: avoid mimicry (what works for CMU CS may not work for Harvard humanities); instead, synthesize insights (Penn's guidance structure, CMU's research approach, Berkeley's pragmatic tone) into Harvard-specific strategy.

Theme 2: History of Oral Assignments at Harvard – Cycle 15

Focus: Section Participation Culture, Divinity School Oral Exams, Extension School Adult Learners

───

1. Harvard Section Culture: Participation as Implicit Oral Assessment Across FAS
:paperclip: Harvard FAS Advising / Student Handbook / Bok Center, current practice
UBC https://handbook.fas.harvard.edu/book/grading-and-grades

Harvard's signature section system—small discussion groups led by TFs/faculty—makes oral assessment ubiquitous but often unexamined. Most FAS courses include sections where participation contributes to final grade (typically 10-20%). The Bok Center notes this creates ongoing oral assessment every week, though rarely formalized with rubrics. Student Handbook describes sections as "opportunity to discuss course material in depth"—essentially weekly oral examinations of understanding.

Harvard pedagogical DNA: The section system reveals Harvard has always privileged oral dialogue—it's just not called "oral assessment" but "participation."

What's actually being assessed in sections:

• Verbal comprehension: Can you explain readings/lectures orally?
• Analytical articulation: Can you formulate arguments on your feet?
• Dialogic engagement: Can you build on others' comments, respond to challenges?
• Intellectual presence: Do you contribute meaningfully to collective learning?

These are precisely the skills formal oral exams assess—Harvard sections provide 120+ years of infrastructure for oral assessment.

Critical observation: If Harvard faculty are already assessing students orally every week in sections, why the anxiety about formal oral exams? The difference is stakes and structure:

• Sections: Low-stakes, ongoing, conversational (10-20% of grade)
• Oral exams: High-stakes, scheduled, evaluative (potentially 50-100% of grade)

Scaling insight: Every Harvard course with sections already has trained teaching fellows facilitating oral assessment. This workforce could be leveraged for more formal oral evaluation—TFs could conduct oral exams under faculty supervision, using detailed rubrics.

Equity concern (unexamined): Section participation grading has documented bias—rewards students comfortable jumping in, native English speakers, those from cultures that value verbal assertiveness. If Harvard formalizes oral assessment, must address biases already present in informal oral evaluation.

Design opportunity: Learn from section pedagogy's successes (low-stakes practice, collegial dialogue) and failures (unstructured rubrics, bias toward dominant speakers) to design better formal oral assessment.

───

2. Harvard Divinity School: "Oral Examination Based on One Large Paper or Two Smaller Papers Written for Courses"
:paperclip: Harvard Divinity School ThM and MRPL Programs, current degree requirements
UBC https://www.hds.harvard.edu/academics/degree-programs/thm-program

HDS Master of Theology (ThM) program requires "oral examination based on one large paper or two smaller papers written for courses"—a hybrid written-oral model. Similarly, Master of Religion and Public Life (MRPL) candidates undergo "oral examinations [that] take place in the spring when the course requirements are nearing completion." These are not stand-alone oral exams but defense of written work—verifying students understand what they've written.

Harvard cross-school learning opportunity: HDS has decades of experience with the exact model many faculty are considering for AI-era assessment:

• Students write papers (possibly with AI assistance, though pre-ChatGPT for most HDS history)
• Faculty read written work
• Students defend work orally, answering questions about argument, sources, implications
• Grade reflects both written quality and oral defense

Why this model works at HDS:

• Authenticity: Reflects professional ministry/academic theology work (sermons require oral delivery, scholarly papers require conference defense)
[11:46 AM]• Efficiency: Faculty don't need to schedule oral exams for all students—only for those whose written work raises questions or who are advancing to higher degree
• Verification: Oral exam catches plagiarism/ghostwriting (pre-AI concern) or shallow understanding

What Harvard College could learn:

ThM hybrid model for senior theses:

• All thesis writers submit written work as current
• Faculty identify students for oral defense (could be random sample, all students, or those with questions)
• 30-45 minute oral exam covers thesis argument, methodology, sources, broader implications
• Final grade combines written thesis score + oral defense performance

MRPL timing model (spring, near completion):

• Oral exams late in semester when students have developed deep knowledge
• Not surprise "gotcha" but expected culmination of learning
• Preparation period between completing coursework and oral exam

Language competency parallel: ThM requires "intermediate reading competency in a language of scholarship"—demonstrating specialized skill beyond general knowledge. Similarly, Harvard College could view oral exams as demonstrating "intermediate oral communication competency in academic discourse"—a specialized skill worth developing and assessing.

HDS pastoral care connection: Divinity students must demonstrate oral competence (preaching, counseling, teaching)—it's not optional for their careers. Many Harvard College concentrations feed into careers requiring similar oral skills (teaching, law, policy, business). HDS shows oral assessment as professional preparation, not just academic exercise.

───

3. Harvard Extension School: Adult Learners "Prepare to Give a Presentation" Through CARC Webinars
:paperclip: Harvard Extension School Career and Academic Resource Center, current support
UBC https://extension.harvard.edu/academics/programs/take-a-course/

Extension School's CARC provides "academic webinars covering... study and research skills" including how to "prepare to give a presentation"—recognizing that adult learners returning to school may need explicit training in academic oral communication. Extension courses utilize Zoom with "webcam and microphone" requirements, normalizing video-based oral participation for remote/hybrid students.

Harvard institutional knowledge: Extension School has 115+ years of experience teaching adults who:

• Work full-time while studying
• Study remotely/hybrid (pre-COVID innovation)
• Come from diverse educational/professional backgrounds
• Need explicit skill development, not assumed prior preparation

What Extension's adult learner model teaches about oral assessment:

Assumption gap: Extension recognizes students don't automatically know how to give presentations—provides training. Harvard College often assumes traditional-age students arrive with oral skills (debate team, seminar culture) but many don't.

Explicit instruction model:

• CARC webinars on presentation skills (structure, delivery, handling questions)
• Resource library (written guides, video examples, practice tips)
• Optional but available to all (destigmatizes seeking help)

Harvard College should offer similar:

• Bok Center workshops on oral exams/defenses (how to prepare, what to expect, strategies)
• Exemplar videos (with permission, show excellent student oral defenses)
• Practice sessions (low-stakes mock orals with feedback)

Technology infrastructure: Extension already requires webcam + microphone for remote courses. If Harvard College implements asynchronous oral assessment (video submissions), Extension's tech support and best practices are institutional resources.
Harvard Extension SchoolTake a Course | Harvard Extension SchoolThere are a variety of ways to take a course at Harvard Extension School; on campus, online, in real time, or at your own pace.https://extension.harvard.edu/academics/programs/take-a-course/[11:46 AM]Equity insight: Extension serves "global adult learners" (75% hold undergraduate degrees, 25% have master's/PhD). If these accomplished professionals need presentation skills support, so do undergraduates. Explicit training reduces privilege gap—students without private school debate training can develop oral skills through institutional support.
[11:46 AM]Hybrid/remote experience: Extension has taught online synchronously and asynchronously longer than most universities. Their practices for remote oral assessment (discussion board participation, video presentations, Zoom office hours) could inform College's adaptation to students studying abroad, taking gap years, or unable to attend in-person oral exams.
