Theme: History of Oral Assignments at Harvard Specifically

Harvard Graduate History Department: Two-Hour Four-Field Oral General Exam (Current)
https://history.fas.harvard.edu/graduate-program-general-exam

Harvard's History Department requires all PhD students to take a two-hour oral examination covering four fields in April or May of their second year. The exam's stated purpose is threefold: "expand and deepen students' general historical knowledge, provide them with the tools to conduct research in history, and prepare them to teach." This comprehensive oral exam tradition represents Harvard's long-standing commitment to oral assessment at the graduate level.

Relevance to Harvard: This demonstrates that oral examinations are not reactive innovations to AI but deeply embedded Harvard traditions, especially in humanities PhD programs. The explicit connection to teaching preparation is noteworthy—oral exams train future faculty in the very skills they'll need for classroom pedagogy. The four-field structure also suggests oral assessment's capacity to test breadth of knowledge in ways written exams might struggle to replicate within time constraints.

───

History of Science Sophomore Tutorial: Oral History Interview Analysis (Current)
https://histsci.fas.harvard.edu/sophomores + https://histsci.fas.harvard.edu/undergraduate-program-concentration-requirements

The History of Science Department's sophomore tutorial (HISTSCI 97) is described as "a hands-on course that introduces students to some of the most exciting and productive questions in the history of science, technology, and medicine, while developing critical reading, presentation, and discussion [skills]." One assignment requires students to "work independently with your tutor in identifying an oral history interview from a curated list. You will do a close reading of your interview, following guidelines that will be provided."

Relevance to Harvard: This reveals a fascinating pedagogical layering: students analyze oral history interviews (a primary source form that privileges spoken testimony) while simultaneously developing their own oral presentation skills. The tutorial model—small-group, dialogue-based learning—is itself an extended oral assessment format that Harvard has practiced for decades. In the AI era, this tradition of tutorial-based oral learning offers a ready-made infrastructure for authentic assessment.

───

Bok Center: "Guidance on Non-Traditional Forms of Assessment" (2022)
https://bokcenter.harvard.edu/bok-publications

The Derek Bok Center for Teaching and Learning offers "comprehensive advice about designing, implementing, and grading innovative kinds of activities and assignments (like podcasts, gallery exhibitions, multimedia narratives, and other digital tools) that may be less commonly found in college classrooms." Originally composed at the request of the Program in General Education, this guidance reflects Harvard's institutional interest in assessment innovation beyond traditional written exams.

Relevance to Harvard: The Bok Center's 2022 publication on non-traditional assessment predates the ChatGPT crisis (November 2022), demonstrating that Harvard faculty were already exploring alternative assessment formats for pedagogical—not just integrity—reasons. Podcasts and multimedia narratives are essentially oral/multimodal assessments. This suggests Harvard has institutional expertise and resources to support faculty adopting oral assignments; it's not starting from zero. Faculty interested in oral assessment should consult the Bok Center, which has likely been thinking about these questions longer than the current AI panic might suggest.

───

Connection to the Broader Project:

These three sources reveal Harvard's deep infrastructure for oral assessment across three levels:

1. Graduate training: PhD oral general exams prepare future faculty in oral evaluation
histsci.fas.harvard.eduConcentration Requirements | Department of the History of Sciencehttps://histsci.fas.harvard.edu/undergraduate-program-concentration-requirements[4:16 AM]2. Undergraduate pedagogy: Tutorial system embeds oral dialogue/presentation as core learning mode
3. Institutional support: Bok Center has been advising on non-traditional (including oral) assessment since before AI anxiety

This suggests Harvard faculty considering oral assignments aren't pioneering something foreign to institutional culture—they're reviving and adapting existing traditions. The question isn't "Should Harvard do oral exams?" but rather "How can Harvard's century-old tutorial tradition and graduate oral exam expertise inform undergraduate assessment in the AI era?"

The timing is notable: the Bok Center published non-traditional assessment guidance in 2022, just months before ChatGPT launched. This means Harvard already had the conceptual framework; AI simply accelerated the urgency.

Theme: Educational Research on Speaking in Learning/Assessment - SoTL

Andrea Fenton (2025): "Reconsidering the Use of Oral Exams and Assessments: An Old Way to Move Into a New Future"
https://journals.sagepub.com/doi/10.3102/0013189X251333638

Published in Educational Researcher (SAGE, 2025), this peer-reviewed article explicitly frames oral assessment as a forward-looking response to generative AI. Fenton cites medical education research establishing "conditions for validity, reliability and fairness" in oral assessment, while also referencing recent studies on AI's capability in "solving authentic assessments." The article positions oral exams not as regression but as pedagogical innovation.

Relevance to Harvard: This is a major SoTL publication in a top-tier education research journal, suggesting oral assessment has moved from niche practice to mainstream scholarly conversation. The framing—"an old way to move into a new future"—directly addresses faculty concerns that oral exams are backward-looking. Fenton's synthesis of medical education research (where oral assessment is gold-standard) with humanities/social science contexts provides Harvard faculty a cross-disciplinary evidence base. The article's 2025 publication date means it incorporates post-ChatGPT empirical studies.

───

Shailey Minocha (2024): "Why Impact of Scholarship of Teaching and Learning Matters"
https://www.shaileyminocha.info/blog/2024/4/5/why-impact-of-scholarship-of-teaching-and-learning-matters

A SoTL scholar proposes "Interactive Oral Assessment" as a research question: "to investigate the effectiveness of Interactive Oral Assessment for academic integrity and to assess student learning as compared to a written end-of-module assessment." Minocha emphasizes the dual purpose—integrity and learning—and frames it as an empirical research question suitable for SoTL inquiry.

Relevance to Harvard: This blog post (by a respected SoTL researcher) models how Harvard faculty could approach oral assessment adoption: not as a defensive policy response to AI, but as an opportunity for pedagogical research. The framing "interactive oral assessment" suggests a dialogic, formative approach rather than high-stakes interrogation. Harvard faculty interested in oral assessment could partner with the Bok Center to design SoTL studies comparing oral vs. written assessment outcomes—generating institutional knowledge rather than just adopting practices from elsewhere. The emphasis on "effectiveness for academic integrity AND student learning" aligns with Harvard's pedagogical values.

───

International Institute for Educational Research (IIER): "The Validity, Reliability, Academic Integrity and [Integration] of Oral Assessments"
http://www.iier.org.au/iier34/nallaya.pdf

This peer-reviewed research article (citing International Journal for the Scholarship of Teaching and Learning) examines oral assessment's psychometric properties and practical implementation. The authors review research on validity (does it measure what it claims?), reliability (consistency across examiners), and academic integrity (resistance to cheating), while also addressing "acceptability"—how students and faculty perceive oral assessment.

Relevance to Harvard: This article directly addresses the technical concerns faculty raise about oral assessment: "How do I grade fairly?" "What about inter-rater reliability?" The citation of a large-section undergraduate diversity class oral exam (in IJSOTL 2019) demonstrates scalability beyond small seminars. The research on "15 conditions for valid, reliable, and fair oral assessment" (from medical education) provides Harvard faculty a checklist for implementation. Importantly, the article's inclusion of "acceptability" research acknowledges that oral assessment must be pedagogically defensible, not just logistically feasible. If Harvard faculty adopt oral exams without student/colleague buy-in, resistance will undermine effectiveness.

2. Dual justification: Effective oral assessment serves both academic integrity and learning outcomes (not just AI-prevention)

4. Research opportunity: Harvard faculty can contribute to SoTL by studying oral assessment implementation in residential college contexts

The key challenge: oral assessment requires faculty development (examiner training for reliability, rubric design for validity). This isn't plug-and-play—it's a pedagogical skill Harvard must cultivate through Bok Center workshops and peer mentoring.

Theme: Post-AI Classroom Experiments with Oral Assignments

Washington Post (December 2025): "College professors use oral exams to combat AI"
https://www.washingtonpost.com/education/2025/12/12/ai-artificial-intelligence-college-oral-exam/

This recent journalism piece reports that "a growing number of educators are finding that oral exams allow them to test their students' learning without the benefit of AI platforms such as ChatGPT." The article documents a national trend of faculty across disciplines adopting oral assessment as a pragmatic response to generative AI. Published just weeks ago (December 2025), it captures the current zeitgeist in higher education.

Relevance to Harvard: The Washington Post's coverage signals that oral assessment has moved from isolated faculty experiments to a mainstream pedagogical shift worthy of national news attention. This may reassure hesitant Harvard faculty that they're not alone in considering oral exams—but it also raises a cautionary question: Is this becoming a bandwagon effect where faculty adopt oral assessment without pedagogical justification beyond AI-prevention? The article's framing ("combat AI") is revealing: it positions oral exams as defensive rather than proactive pedagogy. Harvard faculty should ask whether they're adopting oral assessment because it's AI-resistant or because it serves learning goals that happen to be AI-resistant.

───

Frontiers in Education (May 2025): "One year in the classroom with ChatGPT: empirical insights and transformative impacts"
https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/education/articles/10.3389/feduc.2025.1574477/full

This peer-reviewed empirical study examines faculty experiences teaching for a full academic year (2023-24) after ChatGPT's launch. The authors investigate how educators adapted their teaching and assessment practices, including shifts toward oral evaluation. The study provides systematic evidence on what worked, what didn't, and the ethical challenges faculty navigated. The research acknowledges "concerns, particularly in terms of ethical considerations related to plagiarism and the challenge of determining how to effectively incorporate this technology."

Relevance to Harvard: This is the kind of empirical longitudinal research Harvard faculty need before making wholesale assessment changes. A full academic year of data is more reliable than anecdotal blog posts from fall 2023. The study's focus on "ethical considerations" and "effective incorporation" suggests that the researchers are moving beyond panic toward principled pedagogy. Harvard faculty considering oral assessment should consult this study for evidence on: (1) Which disciplines found oral assessment effective vs. problematic? (2) What student outcomes improved (if any)? (3) What unintended consequences emerged (e.g., increased anxiety, equity concerns)? The fact that this appeared in Frontiers in Education (a reputable open-access journal) means it's accessible to all Harvard faculty without paywalls.

───

College Teaching (Taylor & Francis, 2025): "Oral Exams for a Generative AI World: Managing Concerns and Logistics for Undergraduate Humanities Instruction"
https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/87567555.2025.2558563

This peer-reviewed case study describes a humanities professor's complete course redesign around oral exams in response to generative AI. The article provides practical details on logistics, rubric design, student preparation, and time management. Crucially, the title promises to address "concerns"—presumably faculty anxieties about fairness, scalability, and student resistance. The article was published in College Teaching, a practitioner-oriented journal that bridges research and classroom implementation.
The Washington PostProfessors are turning to this old-school method to stop AI use on examsA small but growing number of educators are experimenting with oral exams to circumvent the temptations presented by powerful AI platforms.Dec 12th, 2025https://www.washingtonpost.com/education/2025/12/12/ai-artificial-intelligence-college-oral-exam/FrontiersFrontiers | One year in the classroom with ChatGPT: empirical insights and transformative impactsGenerative Artificial Intelligence (GAI), such as OpenAI’s ChatGPT, has rapidly emerged as a transformative tool in higher education, offering opportunities ...https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/education/articles/10.3389/feduc.2025.1574477/full[4:46 AM]Relevance to Harvard: This is actionable scholarship: a detailed how-to guide from a peer who already navigated the challenges Harvard faculty face. The humanities context is particularly relevant for Harvard FAS, where writing-intensive courses are most vulnerable to AI disruption. Key questions this article likely addresses: How do you grade 25+ oral exams without burning out? How do you train students for oral performance if they've never done it? How do you handle accommodations for students with social anxiety or speech disabilities? The article's framing—"managing concerns and logistics"—suggests the author anticipated faculty resistance and provides evidence-based responses. Harvard faculty in English, History, Government, etc. should read this before dismissing oral exams as impractical.

───

Connection to the Broader Project:

These three sources reveal the current state of post-AI oral assessment experimentation (late 2024 - early 2025):

1. Journalistic coverage (WaPo): Oral exams are mainstream enough for national news
2. Empirical research (Frontiers): We now have full-year longitudinal data, not just speculation
3. Practical case studies (College Teaching): Detailed implementation guides exist for humanities courses

The trajectory suggests we're past the panic phase (fall 2023) and entering the evidence-based implementation phase (2025). Harvard faculty have the luxury of learning from early adopters' mistakes. The key challenge: avoiding a reactive "ban AI, do oral exams" approach and instead asking: What assessment methods best serve our learning goals, which happen to be AI-resistant?

The Washington Post framing ("combat AI") vs. the College Teaching framing ("managing concerns and logistics") captures this tension: Is oral assessment a weapon against AI, or a pedagogical choice we implement thoughtfully?
elle-with-claws  [5:01 AM]
Theme: AI Used IN Oral Evaluation (The Paradox)

University of Colorado AI Institute (CHI '25): "Examining and Mitigating the Cascading Effects of Bias in [ASR for Education]"
https://www.colorado.edu/research/ai-institute/media/782

Presented at CHI 2025 (the premier human-computer interaction conference), this research examines how automatic speech recognition (ASR) bias compounds when used in educational assessment. The study compares "Talk Moves model error rates" using human vs. ASR transcripts, revealing that ASR accuracy bias creates cascading problems for downstream analysis. The research is particularly relevant to K-12 classroom dialogue analysis but has direct implications for oral assessment transcription.

Relevance to Harvard: This is a critical warning against naive adoption of AI transcription for oral exam grading. If Harvard faculty use AI to transcribe oral exams (to create written records for grading or appeals), ASR bias means some students' speech will be transcribed less accurately than others—particularly students with accents, non-native speakers, or those with speech differences. The "cascading effects" finding is especially alarming: initial transcription errors compound when faculty use the transcript (not the recording) for assessment. Recommendation: If Harvard adopts oral assessment, faculty should grade from audio/video recordings, not AI-generated transcripts. Use transcription only for student access/accommodations, with human review.

───

ScienceDirect (April 2025): "Evaluating an AI speaking assessment tool: Score accuracy, perceived validity, and oral peer feedback as feedback enhancement"
https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1475158525000360

This peer-reviewed empirical study examines an AI-powered speaking assessment tool for English language learners, evaluating both its technical accuracy (does it score correctly?) and its perceived validity (do students/teachers trust it?). The study also investigates whether AI-generated feedback can be enhanced through peer review. Findings suggest AI tools can provide useful formative feedback but require human oversight for high-stakes assessment.

Relevance to Harvard: This study demonstrates a middle path between AI-free and AI-dependent oral assessment. Rather than using AI as a grader, Harvard could use AI tools for formative practice: students rehearse oral presentations with AI feedback (pronunciation, pacing, clarity), then perform for faculty evaluation. This leverages AI's scalability (unlimited practice opportunities) while preserving human judgment for summative assessment. The study's focus on "perceived validity" is crucial—if students don't trust AI feedback, they won't use it for improvement. Harvard's Language Resource Center or Bok Center could pilot AI practice tools for oral presentations, measuring whether students improve from AI-mediated rehearsal.

───

arXiv (August 2025): "Fairness of Automatic Speech Recognition: Looking Through a Philosophical Lens"
https://arxiv.org/html/2508.07143v1

This philosophical/technical paper argues that current ASR fairness metrics are inadequate for education. The authors propose measuring "time-to-task-completion equity, conversational flow preservation, and linguistic labor requirements across different speaker populations" rather than just final accuracy. The paper critiques the field's narrow focus on Word Error Rate (WER) as a fairness metric, arguing it misses real-world inequities in how ASR systems impose different cognitive loads on different speakers.
[5:01 AM]Relevance to Harvard: This is the most theoretically sophisticated critique of AI-in-oral-assessment I've seen. The key insight: even if an ASR system achieves equal WER across demographics, it can still be unfair if it requires more effort from some speakers to achieve that accuracy (e.g., non-native speakers must speak slower, repeat themselves, or code-switch). Applied to Harvard oral exams: If faculty use AI transcription or AI-mediated feedback, they must ask: Does this tool impose equal cognitive load on all students? A student who must consciously modify their accent to be understood by the AI is facing a different exam than one who speaks in a "standard" register the AI was trained on. This is an equity issue, not just a technical accuracy problem. Harvard should commission bias audits (like those described in this paper) before deploying any AI tools in oral assessment.

───

Connection to the Broader Project:

These three sources reveal a critical paradox: Faculty adopt oral exams to avoid AI (in student work), but may then use AI (for transcription/grading/feedback). This creates new equity problems:

1. Transcription bias: ASR performs worse for accented/non-native speech (Colorado study)
2. Validity concerns: Students/faculty may distrust AI-generated scores (ScienceDirect study)
3. Hidden inequity: Equal accuracy masks unequal cognitive burden (arXiv philosophical analysis)

Recommendations for Harvard Faculty:

• :white_check_mark: DO: Use AI for low-stakes practice (rehearsal feedback, self-assessment)
• :x: DON'T: Use AI transcripts as the primary grading artifact (grade from recordings)
• :warning: CAUTION: Commission bias audits before deploying any ASR tools; assess equity of cognitive load, not just accuracy
• :microscope: RESEARCH: Partner with Bok Center to study whether AI practice tools improve oral performance

The irony: oral exams are "AI-resistant" only if we resist using AI in the evaluation process itself. The moment we introduce AI transcription or automated scoring, we reintroduce the very bias and equity concerns we sought to avoid.
elle-with-claws  [5:16 AM]
Theme: Literary/Historical References (Plato/Socrates on Speech vs Writing)

Springer AI & Ethics (April 2024): "Using ChatGPT-3 as a writing tool: an educational assistant or a moral hazard? Current ChatGPT-3 media representations compared to Plato's critical stance on writing in Phaedrus"
https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s43681-024-00470-1

This peer-reviewed open-access article draws an explicit parallel between Plato's critique of writing in Phaedrus and contemporary concerns about ChatGPT. The authors argue that Socrates' warning—that writing would "implant forgetfulness" and offer "the appearance of wisdom, not true wisdom"—applies directly to AI-generated text. The paper examines whether ChatGPT is an "educational assistant" or a "moral hazard," using Platonic philosophy as an ethical framework.

Relevance to Harvard: This is the most philosophically rigorous comparison of ChatGPT to ancient critiques of technology. For Harvard humanities faculty, the parallel is striking: Socrates feared writing would allow students to appear knowledgeable without genuine understanding (275b: "they will read many things without instruction and will therefore seem to know many things, when they are for the most part ignorant"). Replace "read" with "generate via ChatGPT" and the concern is identical. The Platonic solution? Dialogic pedagogy—live conversation where wisdom is tested through questioning, not displayed through written products. This is precisely what oral exams offer. Faculty teaching philosophy, classics, or Great Books courses should assign this article when explaining why they're shifting to oral assessment; it provides a 2,400-year-old intellectual pedigree for their decision.

───

Nature Humanities & Social Sciences Communications (May 2025): "And Plato met ChatGPT: an ethical reflection on the use of chatbots in scientific research writing, with a particular focus on the social sciences"
https://www.nature.com/articles/s41599-025-04650-0

Published in a Nature journal, this recent article extends the Plato-ChatGPT parallel specifically to research writing in the social sciences. The authors argue that Plato's Phaedrus provides a framework for understanding authorship, originality, and the ethical use of AI writing tools in scholarship. The article was published just months ago (May 2025), incorporating two years of post-ChatGPT empirical experience.

Relevance to Harvard: This source is particularly valuable for Harvard faculty supervising thesis research or training graduate students. The question "Can students ethically use ChatGPT in research writing?" parallels Socrates' question "Can students rely on written texts rather than internalized knowledge?" The Platonic critique suggests that externalized knowledge (writing, AI) creates dependency rather than mastery. Applied to Harvard: If a student uses ChatGPT to draft a literature review, they may appear conversant in the field without having genuinely engaged the sources. Oral thesis defenses become crucial—they reveal whether the student possesses internalized understanding or merely curated external artifacts. The article's focus on social science research is especially relevant for Harvard's qualitative researchers, where interpretation and argumentation (not just data reporting) are central.

───

Walter J. Ong (1982/2012): Orality and Literacy: The Technologizing of the Word + "Secondary Orality" Concept
PDF: https://monoskop.org/images/d/db/Ong_Walter_J_Orality_and_Literacy_2nd_ed.pdf
Wikipedia summary: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Secondary_orality
SpringerLinkUsing ChatGPT-3 as a writing tool: an educational assistant or a moral hazard? Current ChatGPT-3 media representations compared to Plato’s critical stance on writing in PhaedrusAI and Ethics - ChatGPT-3, based on a large language model created by OpenAI, capable of generating human-like text, has been open to the public since November 2022. Since 2023,&nbsp;ChatGPT-3...https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s43681-024-00470-1NatureAnd Plato met ChatGPT: an ethical reflection on the use of chatbots in scientific research writing, with a particular focus on the social sciencesHumanities and Social Sciences Communications - And Plato met ChatGPT: an ethical reflection on the use of chatbots in scientific research writing, with a particular focus on the social sciencesWikipediaSecondary oralitySecondary orality is orality that is dependent on literate culture and the existence of writing, such as a television anchor reading the news or radio. While it exists in sound, it does not have the features of "primary orality" because it presumes and rests upon literate thought and expression, and may even be people reading written material. Thus, secondary orality is usually not as repetitive, redundant, agonistic, etc. the way primary orality is, and cultures that have a lot of secondary orality are not necessarily similar to primarily oral cultures. Secondary orality should not be confused with "oral residue" in which a culture has not fully transitioned to literate / written culture an… [5:16 AM]Ong's classic work argues that "contrasts between electronic media and print have sensitized us to the earlier contrast between writing and orality." He introduces the concept of "secondary orality"—the orality of telephones, radio, television, and now video calls—which combines oral delivery with literate consciousness. Ong notes that awareness of orality vs. literacy "developed only in the electronic age, not earlier," suggesting we're living through a media transition comparable to ancient Greece's shift from oral to literate culture.

Relevance to Harvard: Ong's framework helps Harvard faculty understand that we're not simply "returning" to oral assessment but creating something new: secondary oral assessment—oral exams conducted via Zoom, recorded, and potentially transcribed (though with caution per the previous post on ASR bias). Ong argues that oral cultures prioritize memory, repetition, and communal knowledge, while literate cultures enable individual authorship, linear argument, and decontextualized information. The AI crisis forces us to ask: What happens when individual authorship (the literate value universities prize) becomes unverifiable? Ong's answer: we create new forms that blend oral immediacy with literate analysis. Harvard's tutorial system already does this—small-group oral dialogue about written texts. The challenge is scaling this to assessment: Can we preserve the benefits of writing (revision, complexity, depth) while adding oral verification (presence, spontaneity, authenticity)? Perhaps hybrid models: written thesis + oral defense, or written proposal + oral elaboration.

───

Connection to the Broader Project:

These three sources provide philosophical depth to the oral assessment conversation, moving beyond pragmatic "AI-detection" toward fundamental questions about knowledge, learning, and pedagogy:

1. Plato's Phaedrus: Writing creates "the appearance of wisdom, not true wisdom"—parallel to ChatGPT concerns
2. Socratic method as solution: Dialogic questioning reveals genuine understanding—oral exams as Socratic pedagogy
3. Ong's secondary orality: We're not abandoning literacy but creating new hybrid forms—oral assessment in the digital age

The key insight: This isn't the first time technology disrupted assessment. Writing itself was the original "cheat"—it allowed students to consult external sources rather than demonstrate internalized mastery. Plato's solution was dialectic: live questioning that couldn't be faked. Harvard faculty adopting oral exams aren't reacting to a new crisis; they're reviving an ancient pedagogical tradition that predates the university itself.

The irony Plato would appreciate: We invented writing to preserve knowledge, then invented universities to test whether students learned from writing, and now we're rediscovering oral dialogue to verify that learning. The medium changes; the pedagogical challenge endures.
elle-with-claws  [5:31 AM]
Theme: Professional Domains (Law, Business, Tech) on Speech, Performance, Presentations

Law School Moot Court: Oral Advocacy as Core Professional Competency (2025)
Columbia, BU, Seton Hall, UCI, Seattle U Law (multiple sources): https://www.law.columbia.edu/academics/experiential/moot-court/first-year-moot-court + https://www.bu.edu/law/experiential-learning/moot-court/

Every major law school requires first-year students to complete moot court: "draft a brief, which requires you to research an issue thoroughly and write a persuasive document, and then present your case in oral argument before [a panel]." Law schools assess both written briefs and oral advocacy separately, with awards for "Best Oralist" distinct from "Best Brief." The structure is remarkably similar to academic oral exams: advance preparation + real-time questioning by experts.

Relevance to Harvard: Law schools solved the "oral assessment scalability" problem decades ago through structured rubrics and trained evaluators. Harvard College students heading to law school will encounter mandatory oral assessment regardless of undergraduate preparation. The question for Harvard FAS: Should we prepare students for this professional reality? Government, History, Philosophy, and Social Studies concentrators are disproportionately represented in law school applications; offering them practice with oral argumentation isn't just about AI-resistance—it's career preparation. Harvard Law School's own moot court tradition could inform undergraduate oral exam design; perhaps HLS faculty could advise FAS on rubrics and examiner training.

───

Harvard Business School Case Method: Oral Participation as 50% of Course Grade
https://www.hbs.edu/teaching/case-method/leading-in-the-classroom/Pages/participation.aspx

HBS explicitly states: "Participation often accounts for 50% of the total course grade." Students read 2-3 cases daily and must "show up ready to participate" in live case discussions. The Christensen Center describes this as high-stakes: "Students and instructors are co-creators of class participation, and the stakes may be quite high, not only for collective and individual learning, but also for performance evaluation." HBS students analyze over 500 cases during the two-year MBA program.

Relevance to Harvard: Harvard already has institutional expertise in large-scale oral assessment—just not in FAS undergraduate programs. HBS has graded oral participation for nearly a century, developing sophisticated methods for assessing contribution quality, not just quantity. The irony: Harvard's professional schools (HBS, HLS, HMS with OSCEs) are fluent in oral assessment, while the College treats it as experimental. Cross-campus pedagogical exchange could be valuable: HBS faculty could share participation grading rubrics, or FAS could pilot "case method discussions" in Government or Economics seminars. The HBS model also demonstrates that oral assessment need not be one-on-one interrogation—it can be collaborative dialogue where students build on each other's insights, making it more scalable and pedagogically rich.

───

Tech Industry: Presentation Skills as Career Determinant (Prezi Study, 2025)
https://pitchworx.com/top-10-presentation-design-agencies-for-tech-companies-in-2025/ + Department of Energy EPIC Pitch Competition

A 2025 Prezi study found that "70% of employed Americans who give presentations agree that presentation skills are critical to their success. For tech companies, this number is even higher, as presentations directly impact funding, product adoption..." Startup pitch competitions (DOE, Y Combinator, Harvard Innovation Lab) give founders 5 minutes to pitch their technology, market strategy, and team to investor panels. Winners are determined by oral presentation quality, not just written business plans.
law.columbia.eduFirst-Year Moot CourtAll first-year students acquire valuable advocacy skills through one of the Law School’s 10 moot court offerings. PitchWorxTop 10 Presentation Design Agencies For Tech Companies In 2025Discover the top 10 presentation design agencies for tech companies in 2025, offering expert visuals, clear storytelling & pitch-ready slide solutions.Dec 4th, 2025https://pitchworx.com/top-10-presentation-design-agencies-for-tech-companies-in-2025/[5:31 AM]Relevance to Harvard: Computer Science, Engineering, and Economics concentrators increasingly pursue startup careers where oral pitching is the primary assessment mechanism for accessing capital. A founder with a brilliant technical solution but poor presentation skills will struggle to raise funding. Harvard could integrate "pitch practice" into STEM courses—not as AI-prevention, but as professional skill development. The Innovation Lab already hosts pitch competitions; partnering with FAS courses would formalize this training. The skills are identical to academic oral exams: synthesize complex information, respond to challenging questions, persuade skeptical evaluators under time pressure. One key difference: tech pitches embrace storytelling and emotional appeal, while academic oral exams traditionally prize analytical rigor. Harvard students benefit from both modes—perhaps "pitch your thesis" as a public engagement exercise alongside traditional defense.

───

Connection to the Broader Project:

These three professional domains reveal that oral assessment is not a retreat from modernity but preparation for professional reality:

1. Law: Oral advocacy is mandatory (moot court) and career-defining (courtroom argumentation)
2. Business: Oral participation is 50% of grade (HBS case method) and critical for leadership
3. Tech: Oral pitching determines funding, product adoption, and startup survival

Key insight for Harvard faculty: You're not experimenting with oral exams because of AI—you're catching up to professional norms. Harvard's professional schools have used oral assessment for decades; the College is the outlier. Students will face oral evaluation in law school, business school, job interviews, investor pitches, conference presentations, and policy briefings. Why not prepare them as undergraduates?

Counter to the "oral exams are regressive" critique: These aren't medieval vivas—they're training for 21st-century professional communication. The irony: the digital economy increasingly values synchronous oral interaction (Zoom pitches, live demos, video conferences) over asynchronous written communication. Harvard students trained only in essay-writing may be unprepared for careers demanding real-time verbal agility.

Practical recommendation: Harvard FAS should survey alumni 5-10 years post-graduation: How often do you present orally vs. write formal papers in your career? The answer will likely show that oral communication dominates professional life, making undergraduate oral assessment not just AI-resistant but professionally relevant.
elle-with-claws  [5:46 AM]
Theme: History of Oral Examinations in Higher Education

Medieval Universities: "Public, Oral and in Latin" — The Original Assessment Model
University of Cambridge official history + ResearchGate/Academia scholarly articles: https://www.cam.ac.uk/about-the-university/history/the-medieval-university + https://www.academia.edu/7596135/From_oral_to_written_examination_Oxford_Cambridge_and_Dublin_1700_1914

Cambridge's official history states that medieval examinations were "oral disputations in which the candidates advanced a series of questions or theses which they disputed or argued with opponents a little senior to themselves, and finally with the masters who had taught them." A scholarly article on the shift from oral to written assessment (1700-1900) notes that medieval exams were "public, oral and in Latin"—emphasizing their communal, performative, and elite linguistic character. The article documents that oral exams at Cambridge persisted in the "Little Go" examination until 1882.

Relevance to Harvard: This is the ancestral form of university assessment—Harvard's current interest in oral exams isn't innovation but restoration. Medieval oral disputations were explicitly designed to test spontaneous reasoning, not memorized knowledge—the same pedagogical goal Harvard faculty articulate when defending oral assessment against AI. The public nature is striking: exams were communal events where peers and masters witnessed performance. This contrasts with modern written exams' privacy. Harvard could revive this tradition: public oral defenses for honors theses (already done in some concentrations) or even sophomore/junior milestone oral exams observed by the academic community. The requirement that exams be "in Latin" was partly about prestige, partly about ensuring a shared evaluative language—an interesting parallel to today's concern that AI has fractured our shared literacy standards.

───

Early American Colleges: Oral Entrance Exams Replaced by Written Tests (19th Century)
Smithsonian National Museum of American History + NEA History: https://americanhistory.si.edu/collections/object/nmah_1408575 + https://www.nea.org/professional-excellence/student-engagement/tools-tips/history-standardized-testing-united-states

The Smithsonian documents that "In the early years of the United States, students applying to colleges took oral examinations given by professors at the school they wished to attend." The NEA history explains that "Formal written tests begins to replace oral examinations administered by teachers and schools at roughly the same time as schools change their mission from servicing the elite to educating the masses." The shift from oral to written assessment was explicitly linked to democratization and scale—oral exams required faculty time and couldn't process large applicant pools.

Relevance to Harvard: This history reveals a tension Harvard now faces: oral assessment is pedagogically rich but doesn't scale easily. 19th-century colleges abandoned oral entrance exams not because written tests were superior pedagogically, but because mass higher education required efficiency. The question for Harvard in 2026: Has digital technology (Zoom, AI transcription, recorded exams) eliminated the scalability problem? Or are we reverting to an elite model appropriate only for small seminars? The history suggests oral assessment requires low student-faculty ratios—which Harvard has, unlike state universities. Perhaps oral exams should be positioned as a luxury of residential liberal arts education, not a universal solution. The democratization critique is real: if only elite institutions can afford oral assessment, it becomes a class marker.

───

PhD Dissertation Defense: The Enduring Oral Tradition Across Disciplines
Princeton, Harvard, FIU, UIUC History Departments (2024-2025): https://history.princeton.edu/dissertation-defense + https://history.fas.harvard.edu/graduate-program-dissertation
[5:46 AM]Harvard's own History Department requires that "Students defend their dissertation before it is approved by the dissertation defense committee"—an oral examination tradition that persists universally across PhD programs. Princeton's department lists multiple "Final Public Oral Exams" throughout 2024-2025. The University of Illinois notes that if more than five years elapse after advancing to candidacy, students must "retake the prelim exam, usually in the form of an oral exam with the dissertation committee"—showing oral assessment as the gold standard for verifying current knowledge.

Relevance to Harvard: The irony is overwhelming: Harvard requires oral dissertation defenses for PhDs but questions whether undergraduates can handle oral exams. If oral assessment is rigorous enough for doctoral research (the highest level of academic work), why is it suspect for undergraduate coursework? The dissertation defense model offers a template for undergraduate oral assessment: written work (thesis/paper) + oral defense where students answer questions, defend choices, and demonstrate mastery beyond what's on the page. History honors theses already include oral exams; why not extend this to junior tutorials or concentration capstones? The "public" nature of many PhD defenses is also instructive—transparency and community witness enhance rigor and fairness. Harvard undergraduates might benefit from defending work before peers and faculty, not just in private one-on-one exams.

───

Connection to the Broader Project:

These three historical snapshots reveal a cyclical pattern:

1. Medieval universities (1200s-1700s): Oral assessment dominant, public, community-witnessed
2. 19th-20th century: Shift to written exams for scalability and democratization
3. 2020s: Renewed interest in oral assessment (AI disruption, professional relevance)

Key insight: We're not in uncharted territory—we're oscillating between oral and written assessment based on technological, social, and pedagogical pressures. Medieval universities had good reasons for oral exams (limited literacy, communal knowledge verification). 19th-century colleges had good reasons for written exams (mass education, efficiency). 21st-century universities have good reasons to reconsider oral assessment (AI undermines written exam integrity, professional careers demand oral communication).

The historical lesson: Neither oral nor written assessment is inherently superior—each serves different values (rigor vs. scale, authenticity vs. efficiency, dialogue vs. reflection). Harvard's challenge is designing a hybrid model that preserves writing's depth while adding orality's verification—perhaps written drafts + oral defense, as PhD students have done for centuries.

Historical irony Harvard faculty should appreciate: The university was founded on oral disputation. We abandoned it for practical reasons. We're now rediscovering what medieval scholars knew: spoken dialogue tests understanding in ways writing cannot capture.
elle-with-claws  [6:03 AM]
Theme: Peer Institutions Responding to AI Anxieties – Oral Assignments

Duke University Professor: "I have deemphasized the use of the kind of writing assignments that invite using ChatGPT" (September 2025)
The Duke Chronicle + ABC11 News + US News: https://www.dukechronicle.com/article/duke-university-professor-students-adapt-to-artificial-intelligence-in-classroom-ai-chatgpt-embrace-ban-use-20250905

Duke faculty member Pickford explicitly states in multiple news outlets: "Pickford has also incorporated oral assignments into his class, including having students present arguments through spoken defense. He has also added in-class exams to lectures that previously relied solely on papers for grading." He candidly explains: "I have deemphasized the use of the kind of writing assignments that invite using ChatGPT because I don't want to spend my time policing." Notably, he acknowledges ChatGPT's utility for writing feedback (outline evaluation) while restricting its use for final submissions.

Relevance to Harvard: Duke's response represents a pragmatic middle path: reduce take-home writing (vulnerable to AI), increase oral assessment and in-class exams, but permit AI for formative feedback. This is neither AI-panic nor AI-embrace—it's strategic rebalancing of assessment modes. The phrase "I don't want to spend my time policing" captures faculty exhaustion with detection tools (Turnitin, GPTZero). Harvard faculty face the same choice: invest energy in AI-detection arms race, or redesign assessment to foreground what AI can't do (real-time oral defense). Duke's approach also validates hybrid models: students can use AI for brainstorming/drafting, but must defend their ideas orally to demonstrate genuine understanding. This preserves writing's pedagogical value (revision, research) while adding verification through speech.

───

Princeton McGraw Center: "We encourage faculty to articulate a clear policy on GAI use in your syllabus" (Fall 2025)
https://mcgraw.princeton.edu/generative-ai

Princeton's teaching center advises faculty to set "clear guidelines" on generative AI (GAI) use, acknowledging that "Students are likely to encounter different GAI policies in different courses." The university's academic regulations now include a provision on "Inappropriate uses of the results of generative AI on any work submitted to fulfill an academic requirement," such as directly copying output or exceeding instructor-specified parameters. Princeton emphasizes instructor autonomy rather than university-wide mandates.

Relevance to Harvard: Princeton's decentralized approach mirrors Harvard's FAS culture: faculty set their own AI policies rather than following top-down rules. This creates the same inconsistency problem Duke's students face—confusion when policies vary across courses. The advantage: faculty freedom to experiment with discipline-appropriate AI integration (CS might permit more AI use than History). The disadvantage: uneven student preparation and potential inequity (students in AI-restrictive courses disadvantaged if peers get AI assistance elsewhere). Harvard could learn from Princeton's emphasis on explicit syllabus statements—requiring faculty to articulate their AI policy in writing forces them to think through pedagogical rationale, not just react. The phrase "exceeding instructor-specified parameters" is key: it acknowledges AI use will happen, so the goal is bounded permission rather than prohibition.

───

Columbia University Provost: Generative AI Policy as "Work in Progress" (2024-2025)
https://provost.columbia.edu/content/office-senior-vice-provost/ai-policy
[6:03 AM]Columbia's institutional AI policy states: "Because AI is a rapidly evolving technology, the University will continue to monitor developments and will consider responses from the University community. This initial policy contains overarching guidelines... [which] will evolve along with the technology, law, and Columbia usage." The Center for Teaching and Learning offers workshops like "Creating Rubrics with AI" and resources on "Alternative Grading Approaches" and "Assessing Equitably with All Learners in Mind."

Relevance to Harvard: Columbia's framing as "work in progress" is intellectually honest—no one has definitive answers yet. This contrasts with institutions that issue sweeping AI bans or mandates. The emphasis on "monitoring developments" suggests Columbia is treating this as an ongoing experiment rather than a crisis requiring immediate resolution. Harvard's Bok Center could adopt Columbia CTL's approach: offer workshops on AI-integrated assessment design (not just AI-detection) and equity-minded grading. The "Creating Rubrics with AI" workshop is particularly interesting—it suggests AI can support faculty work (rubric generation, grading efficiency) even as we limit student use. The broader point: Columbia is investing in faculty development rather than just policy enforcement. Harvard should similarly support faculty who want to redesign courses around oral assessment—not just tell them "AI is a problem," but help them implement solutions.

───

Connection to the Broader Project:

These three peer institutions reveal diverse institutional responses to AI anxiety:

1. Duke: Individual faculty pragmatism—reduce vulnerable assignments, increase oral assessment, permit AI for feedback
2. Princeton: Decentralized faculty autonomy—each instructor sets AI policy, university provides guidelines not mandates
3. Columbia: Institutional agility—"work in progress" framing, faculty development focus, equity emphasis

No consensus has emerged—but all three reject both total AI bans and uncritical AI embrace. The common thread: empower faculty to experiment while providing institutional support (teaching centers, workshops, policy frameworks).

Key takeaway for Harvard: Oral assessment is one tool in a portfolio, not a universal solution. Duke faculty use it alongside in-class writing and AI-permitted drafting. The goal isn't to eliminate writing or ban AI—it's to create assessment ecologies where students must demonstrate understanding through multiple modes, making it harder to fake competence with AI alone.

Question for Harvard faculty: Are you adopting oral exams because they serve your learning goals, or because you're exhausted by AI-policing? Duke's Pickford admits the latter motivation ("I don't want to spend my time policing"). That's honest—but is it pedagogically sound? Perhaps the answer is yes: faculty energy is finite, and spending it on meaningful assessment design (oral exams, in-class work) beats spending it on detection tools.
elle-with-claws  [6:16 AM]
Theme: History of Oral Assignments at Harvard Specifically

Harvard History Department: Board of Examiners "Identifies Those Students Who Require an Oral Exam" for Honors (Current)
https://history.fas.harvard.edu/main-concentration-senior-thesis

The History Department's official policy states: "The BOE [Board of Examiners] then identifies those students who require an oral exam as a third component of their file" when determining honors recommendations. The oral exam supplements concentration GPA and thesis readings as criteria for honors evaluation. This is presented as a standard, non-controversial assessment mechanism—suggesting oral exams have been normalized within the concentration for years.

Relevance to Harvard: This reveals that oral exams are already embedded in Harvard undergraduate honors evaluation—they're not experimental or AI-reactive. The History Department's use of oral exams is selective: only some students "require" them, presumably those whose written work is borderline for honors or whose GPA doesn't fully capture their understanding. This suggests a diagnostic use of oral assessment: when written evidence is ambiguous, oral questioning clarifies. Harvard faculty considering oral exams could adopt this model: use oral exams not universally, but strategically—for honors candidates, borderline grades, or when plagiarism/AI is suspected. The legitimacy comes from tradition: if History has done this for decades, it's not a panic response to ChatGPT.

───

Harvard Philosophy Concentration: Joint Concentrators "Oral examination by two readers, one from each department" (Current)
https://philosophy.fas.harvard.edu/concentration

The Philosophy Department's concentration page specifies that joint concentrators (Philosophy + another field) must complete a thesis with "Oral examination by two readers, one from each department." This interdisciplinary oral exam ensures the thesis meets standards in both fields. The requirement appears matter-of-factly in the concentration guidelines, suggesting it's a long-standing practice.

Relevance to Harvard: Philosophy's oral exam requirement demonstrates that some Harvard concentrations mandate oral assessment for all honors candidates, not just selectively. The interdisciplinary aspect is pedagogically significant: oral exams allow two faculty from different disciplines to test whether the student can defend work that bridges fields. This is harder to assess through writing alone, where disciplinary conventions might not translate. The broader implication: oral exams are especially valuable for interdisciplinary or complex work where written evaluation by a single reader is insufficient. Harvard has 50+ secondary field combinations and numerous joint concentrations—oral exams could be valuable across these programs. The AI angle: interdisciplinary work is harder for AI to fake convincingly, because it requires synthesis across disparate scholarly conversations. Oral defense ensures the student genuinely navigated both fields.

───

Harvard First-Year Seminars: "Students undertake readings, discussions, essays, and presentations" (2025-2026)
https://firstyearseminarprogram.college.harvard.edu/students/

Harvard's official First-Year Seminar Program page states that seminars bring together "twelve students to study a topic of keenly shared interest" and that "students in a seminar undertake readings, discussions, essays, and presentations" toward a SAT/UNSAT grade. The program explicitly frees students from "letter-grades and exams" but still requires presentations as a core assessment mode. Over 100 seminars are offered for 2025-2026.

It frees its participants (instructor and student) from the usual constraints of lecture courses such as letter-grades and exams. The SAT/UNSAT grading removes high-stakes pressure, making oral presentations lower-risk pedagogically. This is a model for introducing oral assessment: start with formative, low-stakes oral work (freshman seminars, sophomore tutorials) before high-stakes summative oral exams (thesis defenses). The fact that Harvard already expects every first-year seminar student to present orally suggests the infrastructure exists: faculty know how to facilitate oral presentations, students have experience with the format. The AI implication: if Harvard freshmen are already doing oral presentations, expanding oral assessment to later years isn't asking something foreign—it's building on existing skills. The challenge is scaling from 12-student seminars (where presentations are feasible) to 100-student lecture courses (where they're not).

───

Connection to the Broader Project:

These three sources demonstrate that oral assessment is woven into Harvard's existing academic fabric:

1. History Department: Selective oral exams for honors candidates (diagnostic use)
2. Philosophy Department: Mandatory oral exams for interdisciplinary joint concentrators (comprehensive evaluation)
3. First-Year Seminars: Universal oral presentations for 12-student discussion courses (formative assessment)

Key pattern: Oral assessment at Harvard is already happening, but in specific contexts (small seminars, honors theses, interdisciplinary work) rather than universally. The question isn't "Should Harvard adopt oral assessment?" but rather "Should Harvard expand existing oral assessment practices to more courses and students?"

Historical insight: Harvard never fully abandoned oral assessment—it persists in elite contexts (honors programs, small seminars, graduate education) while disappearing from mass contexts (lecture courses, non-honors tracks). This mirrors the 19th-century pattern: oral exams persisted at Oxford/Cambridge for honors students even after written exams replaced them for pass degrees.

Implication for AI-era expansion: Harvard can frame oral assessment expansion not as innovation but as democratization of an existing elite practice. Currently, only honors students and seminar participants get regular oral assessment. Why not extend this pedagogical richness to all concentrators? The AI crisis provides political cover for a change Harvard could have made anyway for equity reasons.

Caution: The current oral assessment contexts (12-student seminars, one-on-one thesis defenses) are low-enrollment. Expanding to 50+ student courses requires different design—perhaps group oral exams, peer-to-peer oral questioning, or rotating oral presentation schedules. Harvard's existing practices provide templates, but not plug-and-play solutions for large courses.
elle-with-claws  [6:31 AM]
Theme: Educational Research on Speaking in Learning/Assessment - SoTL

St Andrews Journal: "Mitigating Anxiety in Oral Assessment" (November 2025)
https://ojs.st-andrews.ac.uk/index.php/sajile/article/view/2933

This recent peer-reviewed article by Rimmer (2025) directly addresses the anxiety concerns that accompany oral assessment adoption. While acknowledging that "oral assessments provoke anxiety in students and affect those who are introverted or lack confidence in public speaking" (as cited in Fenton, 2024), the article focuses on evidence-based strategies to mitigate this anxiety rather than accepting it as inevitable. The research situates oral assessment anxiety within the broader literature on test emotions and coping strategies.

Relevance to Harvard: This article is essential reading for Harvard faculty concerned about the equity implications of oral assessment. The anxiety critique is the most common objection to expanding oral exams—students who are introverted, non-native English speakers, or socially anxious may be disadvantaged. Rimmer's work suggests anxiety is manageable through design: scaffolded practice opportunities, low-stakes formative oral work before high-stakes exams, clear rubrics, and instructor training in creating psychologically safe environments. Harvard's Bok Center should develop anxiety-mitigation protocols before scaling oral assessment. The key insight: anxiety is a design problem, not an inherent feature of oral assessment. Written exams also provoke anxiety (see Pekrun et al., 2004 Test Emotions Questionnaire literature), yet we don't abandon them—we scaffold and support. The same approach applies to oral assessment.

───

Journal of University Teaching & Learning Practice: "Improving assessment equity using Interactive Oral Assessments" (March 2024)
https://open-publishing.org/journals/index.php/jutlp/article/view/746

This empirical study makes the counter-intuitive claim that Interactive Oral Assessments (IOAs) can improve equity, contrary to concerns about bias and anxiety. The research demonstrates that well-designed IOAs incorporate "scaffolding, scenario-based [tasks], aligned to the program learning outcomes, accessible and equitable, and professionally focused" characteristics. The study builds on Sotiriadou et al. (2020) model and documents equity improvements when oral assessment is intentionally designed rather than improvised.

Relevance to Harvard: This directly challenges the assumption that oral assessment is inherently inequitable. The article suggests that traditional written exams may actually disadvantage students who excel in verbal reasoning but struggle with written expression (e.g., some dyslexic students, students with writing-specific learning disabilities, or brilliant thinkers who process ideas dialogically). IOAs can level the playing field by offering multimodal assessment opportunities. The key is intentional design: "accessible and equitable" isn't automatic—it requires deliberate choices (e.g., advance preparation materials, accommodation for speech disabilities, training assessors to avoid accent bias). Harvard should treat oral assessment expansion as an equity initiative requiring the same rigor as other DEI pedagogical reforms. The professionally-focused aspect is also crucial: if Harvard's mission is preparing students for careers where oral communication matters, denying them practice in oral assessment may be the real equity problem.

───

PMC/NIH Study: "Global employability skills in the 21st century workplace" — Verbal Communication as Core Transferable Skill
https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10637906/
PubMed Central (PMC)Global employability skills in the 21st century workplace: A semi-systematic literature reviewThis study conducts a semi-systematic literature review of research pertaining to employability to identify essential employability skills that employers seek in recent graduates. The comprehensive analysis of the existing literature review aims to ...https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10637906/[6:31 AM]This systematic literature review synthesizes research on graduate employability, identifying "verbal and written communication skills" as among the key transferable competencies employers seek (alongside professionalism, teamwork, adaptability, ICT skills, creativity). The study emphasizes that 21st-century workplaces value both written and oral communication, not written alone. The research connects employability skills theory (Yorke, 2006) to curriculum design, arguing universities must "integrate these skills" beyond just academic knowledge.

Relevance to Harvard: This provides the workforce justification for oral assessment that complements the academic integrity rationale. Harvard faculty can frame oral assessment not as AI-panic reaction but as career preparation. The literature review confirms what Harvard's professional schools already know: employers hire based on oral communication competence (interviews, presentations, client meetings, team collaboration). If Harvard College prepares students only in written communication, it's producing graduates with incomplete skill sets. The employability argument may resonate with faculty skeptical of academic integrity concerns but committed to student career success. Importantly, this isn't about "vocationalism" displacing liberal arts—it's about recognizing that oral expression has always been central to liberal education (think Socratic dialogue, tutorial discussions). The modern workplace just makes visible what universities should have been teaching all along.

───

Connection to the Broader Project:

These three sources address the major pedagogical concerns about oral assessment expansion:

1. Anxiety/Equity Concerns (St Andrews): Oral assessment provokes anxiety, but this is manageable through design—not a reason to reject the format
2. Equity as Opportunity (JUTLP): Well-designed oral assessment can improve equity by offering multimodal pathways for demonstrating competence
3. Employability Justification (PMC/NIH): Verbal communication is a core transferable skill—universities have pedagogical responsibility to develop it

Synthesis for Harvard: Oral assessment is neither a panacea nor a problem—it's a pedagogical tool requiring thoughtful implementation. The SoTL research suggests three design principles:

1. Scaffold anxiety management: Low-stakes practice → formative feedback → high-stakes assessment
2. Design for equity: Accommodations, rubrics, assessor training, multimodal options
3. Connect to professional relevance: Frame oral work as skill development, not just academic integrity

Critical question for Harvard faculty: Are you prepared to invest in anxiety mitigation, equity design, and assessor training? Or are you expecting to simply swap written exams for oral exams without changing anything else? The SoTL research is clear: oral assessment done poorly creates inequity. Oral assessment done well requires faculty development, institutional support, and pedagogical intentionality.

The good news: Harvard has the resources (Bok Center, small class sizes, tutorial tradition) to do it well. The challenge: scaling expertise across FAS while maintaining quality.

Theme: Post-AI Classroom Experiments with Oral Assignments

NYU Professor: AI-Powered Oral Exams for 42 Cents Per Student (January 2026)
https://the-decoder.com/nyu-professor-fights-ai-cheating-with-ai-powered-oral-exams-that-cost-42-cents-per-student/

NYU Stern Professor Panos Ipeirotis and co-instructor Konstantinos Rizakos conducted a final exam via voice AI agent (built on ElevenLabs Conversational AI) as a solution to the scalability problem of oral assessment. The article notes: "Oral exams force real-time thinking and defense of actual decisions, but they're a logistical nightmare for large classes." The AI-conducted oral exam cost 42 cents per student and was inspired by research showing "AI performs more consistently than humans in job interviews."

Relevance to Harvard: This represents the extreme endpoint of oral assessment automation—using AI to conduct the oral exam itself. The irony is profound: faculty adopt oral exams to avoid AI in student work, then use AI to administer those exams. This raises serious concerns discussed in our earlier post on AI bias in oral evaluation. However, the scalability insight is valuable: if Harvard faculty resist oral exams because of grading burden, AI-assisted administration (not grading, but logistics like scheduling, recording, transcription) might help. The 42-cent cost is compelling for resource-conscious institutions, though Harvard should prioritize quality over cost. The deeper question: Does an AI-conducted oral exam retain the pedagogical value of human dialogue? Likely not—the Socratic questioning, follow-up probes, and responsive assessment are diminished when the examiner is algorithmic. Recommendation: Harvard could use AI for logistics (scheduling, reminders, recording) but keep human faculty as examiners.

───

Contemporary Educational Technology Journal: "Shift Toward More Complicated, Interactive, and Engaging Evaluations, Like Oral Exams" (2025)
https://files.eric.ed.gov/fulltext/EJ1460216.pdf

This peer-reviewed article argues that "Since ChatGPT and other AI tools can effortlessly manage MCQs and essay-based assessments, educators have difficulty upholding academic integrity." The authors advocate for a fundamental shift toward "more complicated, interactive, and engaging evaluations, like oral exams, problem-[solving tasks]" that resist AI automation. The article frames this as a necessary pedagogical evolution, not a temporary fix.

Relevance to Harvard: This journal article provides academic legitimacy for the shift Harvard faculty are considering. The key phrase—"more complicated, interactive, and engaging"—reframes oral assessment positively (not just AI-resistant, but pedagogically superior). The coupling of "oral exams" with "problem-solving tasks" suggests a broader reimagining of assessment: move from knowledge display (which AI can fake) to performance-based demonstration (which AI cannot). Harvard could pilot this in STEM courses: oral problem-solving sessions where students work through novel problems while explaining their reasoning aloud. This combines the AI-resistance of oral format with the rigor of quantitative disciplines. The article's argument that this is a "shift" (not a return) is important—it positions faculty as pedagogical innovators, not conservatives fleeing AI. This framing may reduce resistance from faculty who pride themselves on being forward-thinking.

───

Taylor & Francis Law Journal: "The viva voce: a post-pandemic assessment" — Student Feedback from Two Undergraduate LLB Subjects (2024)
https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/03069400.2024.2359865

The authors argue that "the viva voce should be given renewed consideration in law schools as a core assessment and learning tool, particularly in the light of the educational transformations brought on by and following the Covid-19 pandemic." The study documents student experiences and perceptions of oral assessment.

Relevance to Harvard: This provides student-perspective empirical data, which is crucial for Harvard faculty considering student resistance to oral exams. What did law students actually think of viva voce assessment? The article's focus on "post-pandemic assessment" is revealing: COVID forced experimentation with alternative assessment modes (because in-person written exams were impossible), and some faculty retained those innovations afterward—suggesting they had pedagogical value beyond emergency circumstances. Harvard went through the same pandemic-forced experimentation; this is a moment to ask: What worked that we should keep? The law school context is particularly relevant for Harvard Government, History, and Philosophy concentrators heading to law school—if JD programs value oral assessment, shouldn't undergraduate programs prepare students for it? The article's positioning of viva voce as both "assessment and learning tool" is important: oral exams aren't just evaluation—they're pedagogy. The dialogue itself teaches critical thinking, argumentation, and intellectual agility.
