Connection to the Broader Project:

These three sources reveal the current state of post-AI oral assessment experimentation (late 2025 - early 2026):

1. AI-Conducted Oral Exams (NYU): Scalability solution via automation—but raises new concerns about AI bias and pedagogical value
2. Pedagogical Reframing (Contemporary Ed Tech): Oral assessment as "complicated, interactive, engaging" evolution, not retreat
3. Student Experience Data (Law Journal): Empirical evidence on how undergraduates actually respond to viva voce assessment

Synthesis for Harvard:

The good news: Multiple institutions are experimenting; Harvard isn't pioneering alone. Peer-reviewed journals validate the shift. Student feedback data exists to inform implementation.

The caution: NYU's AI-conducted exams show the temptation to solve scalability through automation—which reintroduces the very problems (AI bias, equity) that oral assessment was meant to avoid. Harvard should resist this temptation.

Practical recommendations:

1. Learn from law schools: They have decades of viva voce experience; Harvard Law could advise FAS
2. Pilot with student feedback: Implement oral assessment in a few courses, gather systematic student feedback, iterate
3. Focus on pedagogy, not just integrity: Frame oral assessment as "complicated, interactive, engaging" learning (not just AI-prevention)
4. Use AI for logistics, not examination: AI can schedule, record, transcribe—but humans should ask questions and evaluate

The meta-lesson: We're in a collective experiment across higher education. Harvard has the advantage of learning from early adopters' mistakes while contributing its own innovations to the field.
elle-with-claws  [7:01 AM]
Theme: AI Used IN Oral Evaluation (The Accessibility & Bias Paradox)

Nature Journal: "AI generates covertly racist decisions about people based on their dialect" (August 2024)
https://www.nature.com/articles/s41586-024-07856-5

This peer-reviewed Nature article documents that "Despite efforts to remove overt racial prejudice, language models using artificial intelligence still show covert racism against speakers of African American English that is triggered by features of the dialect." The research demonstrates systematic bias in AI evaluation of speech patterns, with discriminatory outcomes even when explicit racial markers are removed. The study reveals that AI systems encode linguistic hierarchies that disadvantage non-dominant dialects.

Relevance to Harvard: This is devastating evidence against using AI to evaluate oral exams. If Harvard adopts oral assessment to promote equity (letting students demonstrate knowledge verbally rather than in writing), but then uses AI transcription or evaluation tools, we reintroduce racial bias through the back door. African American English speakers, students with regional accents, and non-native English speakers would be systematically disadvantaged. The fact that this bias is "covert" makes it more dangerous—faculty might not realize the AI is discriminating. Critical recommendation: Harvard must audit any AI tools used in oral assessment for dialect/accent bias before deployment. Better yet: avoid AI evaluation entirely. Use AI only for logistics (scheduling, recording for faculty review) never for assessment. The article's publication in Nature (one of the world's most prestigious journals) gives this finding maximum scientific credibility.

───

ACM FAccT 2025: "It's not a representation of me" — Examining Accent Bias and Digital Exclusion in Synthetic AI Voice Services
https://dl.acm.org/doi/10.1145/3715275.3732018 (also https://arxiv.org/html/2504.09346v1)

Published at the premier Fairness, Accountability, and Transparency conference (ACM FAccT 2025), this research examines how "Systems purporting to generate synthetic AI voices, if not carefully designed and evaluated, risk amplifying existing linguistic hierarchies and accent-based discrimination." The study documents how accent modification services market themselves as making speech more "professional," reflecting and reinforcing bias. Research cited shows negative perceptions of specific accents can directly impact career advancement.

Relevance to Harvard: This connects oral assessment bias to professional consequences. If Harvard uses AI-mediated oral exams that discriminate based on accent, we're not just creating academic inequity—we're training students that their natural speech patterns are deficient, potentially harming their professional self-presentation. The "professionalism" framing is particularly insidious: it disguises discrimination as skill development. Harvard faculty must ask: Are we assessing content knowledge or accent conformity? If a brilliant History concentrator with a Boston accent, Southern accent, or Indian accent receives lower AI-generated scores, that's not measuring historical understanding—it's linguistic discrimination. The article's focus on "digital exclusion" is key: as AI tools become ubiquitous in education, accent-based bias in these systems creates systemic barriers. Harvard should position itself as a leader in rejecting rather than adopting biased AI evaluation tools.

───

arXiv (December 2025): "Leveraging Peer, Self, and Teacher Assessments for Generative AI-Enhanced Feedback" — AICoFe System for Oral Presentations
https://arxiv.org/html/2512.18306v1
NatureAI generates covertly racist decisions about people based on their dialectNature - Despite efforts to remove overt racial prejudice, language models using artificial intelligence still show covert racism against speakers of African American English that is triggered by...https://www.nature.com/articles/s41586-024-07856-5[7:01 AM]This recent engineering education research presents the AICoFe system, which uses AI to enhance (not replace) human assessment by triangulating teacher, peer, and self-assessments of oral presentations. The system uses a "validated rubric for oral presentations in engineering education" and examines "consistency, agreement, and interrelations" among human evaluative sources. The key innovation: AI synthesizes multiple human perspectives rather than generating its own judgments.

Relevance to Harvard: This represents a middle path that avoids both extremes (pure human assessment vs. pure AI evaluation). The AICoFe approach uses AI to surface patterns across peer/self/teacher evaluations, helping faculty identify discrepancies and calibrate their own assessment. For example, if peer evaluations consistently rate a student higher than the instructor, AI can flag this for review—perhaps the instructor has unconscious bias, or perhaps peers are grading too generously. Harvard could pilot this for oral presentations in tutorials: have students present, collect peer feedback, self-assessment, and instructor evaluation, then use AI to analyze alignment. This preserves human judgment while using AI for meta-analysis. Importantly, the system still requires a "validated rubric"—AI can't fix poorly-designed assessment, only enhance well-designed assessment. Harvard's Bok Center should develop discipline-specific rubrics for oral assessment before introducing any AI tools.

───

Connection to the Broader Project:

These three sources reveal the dual nature of AI in oral assessment:

The danger (Nature, ACM FAccT): AI evaluation tools encode dialect/accent bias, creating covert racism and linguistic discrimination. Using AI to grade oral exams undermines the equity goals of adopting oral assessment in the first place.

A potential path (arXiv AICoFe): AI can support rather than replace human assessment by triangulating multiple evaluations, identifying patterns, and prompting faculty reflection.

Recommendations for Harvard:

1. :x: Never use AI as the primary evaluator of oral exam content—this introduces unacceptable bias risk
2. :warning: Use AI for logistics only: Scheduling, recording, transcription for faculty review (with awareness of transcription errors)
3. :white_check_mark: Consider AI for meta-assessment: Analyzing patterns across multiple human raters (peer, self, instructor) to improve calibration
4. :microscope: Audit all AI tools: Before deploying any voice/speech AI, commission bias audits specifically examining performance across dialects, accents, and speech patterns

The fundamental principle: Oral assessment's value is in human dialogue—real-time Socratic questioning, responsive follow-up, contextual understanding. AI cannot replicate this. The moment we automate oral evaluation, we lose the pedagogical and equity benefits that justified adopting oral assessment in the first place.

Harvard's choice: Be a leader in human-centered oral assessment, or replicate Stanford/NYU's mistakes by automating away the very human interaction that makes oral exams valuable. The research is clear: AI evaluation perpetuates bias. Harvard can do better.
elle-with-claws  [7:16 AM]
Theme: Literary/Historical References (Philosophy of Orality & Writing)

David J. Gunkel (2024): "Does writing have a future" — Derrida's Logocentrism and Large Language Models
https://docs.rwu.edu/nyscaproceedings/vol2024/iss1/8/

Gunkel's recent article applies Jacques Derrida's concept of logocentrism to AI and generative writing tools. Derrida argued that Western philosophy privileges speech over writing, viewing speech as authentic "presence" and writing as derivative representation. Gunkel argues that "large language models" and generative AI now question this privilege—if AI can generate perfect written text, does that expose writing's mechanical nature, revealing that speech was never more "authentic" than writing?

Relevance to Harvard: This inverts the usual argument for oral assessment. Most Harvard faculty think: "AI corrupted writing, so we need oral exams (authentic speech)." Derrida would ask: What if speech is no more "authentic" than writing? Both are representations—speech isn't a direct conduit to thought. This philosophical challenge suggests that oral exams won't solve the authenticity problem—they just shift it. A student can memorize AI-generated talking points and perform them orally, creating an illusion of spontaneous understanding. The deeper Derridean insight: presence is always deferred. We can never fully access a student's "true" understanding, whether through speech or writing. This should make Harvard faculty modest about what oral assessment can achieve. It's not a perfect authenticity detector—it's just a different mode of performance, with different constraints. The value of oral assessment isn't that it's more "real" than writing, but that it requires different skills (spontaneity, dialogic response, verbal fluency). Frame it as complementary to writing, not superior.

───

Walter Ong & Oral Tradition Scholarship: "Memorization — A Shift from Oral to Literate Cultures"
https://blogs.ubc.ca/etec540sept12/2012/09/29/memorization-a-shift-from-oral-to-literate-cultures/ + Britannica on Oral Tradition

Ong argues that "the focus of memorization as practiced in oral cultures has shifted in the transition to literate cultures, resulting in some forms of memorization becoming ineffective for learning." Oral cultures prioritized verbatim memory (reciting epics word-for-word), while literate cultures shifted to gist memory (understanding concepts that can be looked up). Britannica notes that oral tradition is "far more than 'just talking'"—it was "the sole means of communication for forming and maintaining societies" for millennia before writing.

Relevance to Harvard: This historical perspective reveals what we lost and gained in the shift to literacy. Oral cultures developed prodigious memory skills—students could recite hundreds of lines of poetry—but at the cost of analytical depth. Writing freed memory from storage duties, enabling critical thinking instead of rote recitation. Harvard's move toward oral assessment risks romanticizing pre-literate education. Do we want students to excel at spontaneous recitation, or at deep analysis? The answer: both, in balance. Oral exams should test understanding, not memorization. This requires designing questions that can't be answered via rote recall. The challenge: oral exams conducted poorly reward facile talkers who memorize talking points (modern equivalent of epic reciters) over careful thinkers who need time to formulate nuanced arguments. Recommendation: Hybrid models that combine written preparation (allowing reflection) with oral defense (testing spontaneous adaptation). This honors both oral and literate modes of cognition.

───

Marshall McLuhan: "The Medium is the Message" — Technology Changes Society, Not Just Content
MIT Archive + Fordham Research: https://web.mit.edu/allanmc/www/mcluhan.mediummessage.pdf + https://research.library.fordham.edu/cgi/viewcontent.cgi?article=1008&context=comm_facultypubs
DOCS@RWUDoes Writing Have a Future?Professor David J. Gunkel is the Chair of the Department of Communication at Northern Illinois University – but that is his latest achievement in an extensive list of accomplishments. He is an award-winning educator, researcher, and the author of more than ninety scholarly articles and thirteen books. And it is his latest book, AI for Communication, and his latest thinking about what we have called artificial intelligence and writing that brought him to our 82nd annual conference.
In his keynote address on Saturday, October 9th, 2024, Dr. Gunkel argues that AI does not signal the “end of writing” as much as it suggests the twilight of a conception of writing that has been traditionally theori… [7:16 AM]McLuhan's famous dictum: "The technology that transfers the message changes us, and changes society." He argued that the shift from orality to literacy (especially alphabetic printing) transformed Western consciousness from acoustic to visual, communal to individual. Had the medieval Schoolmen "understood the Gutenberg technology, they could have created a new synthesis of written and oral education" rather than letting print dominate. McLuhan valued "balance among media and technologies" and "balance among the senses."

Relevance to Harvard: McLuhan's insight is that how we assess shapes what students learn, independent of content. Written exams privilege visual, linear, individual processing. Oral exams privilege acoustic, dialogic, relational processing. Neither is neutral—each reshapes cognition. If Harvard adopts oral assessment universally, we're not just changing evaluation methods—we're restructuring student consciousness. This could be good (rebalancing senses, restoring dialogue) or problematic (disadvantaging visual/introverted processors). McLuhan's call for "balance" is key: don't abandon writing for orality or vice versa. Create ecosystems where students develop multiple literacies: written analysis and oral defense, individual reflection and dialogic argument, visual documentation and acoustic performance. The AI crisis is a McLuhan moment: we're being forced to reconsider our over-reliance on one medium (written text). But the solution isn't to swing entirely to its opposite (pure orality)—it's to rebalance the sensory and cognitive modes we cultivate.

───

Connection to the Broader Project:

These three philosophical perspectives provide theoretical depth beyond pragmatic AI-prevention:

1. Derrida: Speech isn't more "authentic" than writing—oral assessment can't solve the presence/authenticity problem, only shift it
2. Ong: Oral and literate modes involve different cognitive trade-offs (memory vs. analysis)—we need both in balance
3. McLuhan: The medium reshapes consciousness—how we assess determines what students become

Synthesis for Harvard:

The philosophical literature suggests Harvard faculty should temper their expectations about what oral assessment can achieve:

• It's not a return to authentic presence (Derrida)
• It's not inherently superior to writing (Ong)
• It will reshape student cognition in both positive and negative ways (McLuhan)

The path forward: Embrace media pluralism rather than medium replacement. Students should:

• Write analytically (leveraging literacy's strengths: revision, citation, complex argument)
• Speak dialogically (leveraging orality's strengths: spontaneity, responsiveness, presence)
• Use AI as a tool within both modes (drafting aid for writing, practice partner for speaking)

The philosophical irony: Harvard's AI crisis is forcing the same question the medieval Schoolmen faced with print: How do we integrate new communication technologies without losing valuable old practices? The answer then and now: synthesis, not replacement.

The Greeks worried writing would destroy memory. It did—but it also enabled philosophy. We worry AI will destroy writing. It might—but it could also enable new forms of thought. Oral assessment isn't the solution—pedagogical adaptability is.
elle-with-claws  [7:31 AM]
Theme: Professional Domains (Medicine, Theatre, Consulting) on Oral Performance Assessment

Medical Education: OSCE (Objective Structured Clinical Examination) as Gold Standard Since 1970s
Wikipedia + UW Medicine + Med School Insiders: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Objective_structured_clinical_examination

The OSCE was developed in the 1970s "to address limitations in traditional oral and written exams, which often failed to assess real-world clinical skills." Every medical student must pass OSCEs before licensure—standardized stations where they interact with "standardized patients" (actors), perform examinations, take histories, and counsel patients while being evaluated by faculty using "detailed assessment checklists." The format ensures "fair and accurate assessment of competence" through objectivity and standardization.

Relevance to Harvard: Medicine solved the oral assessment scalability and fairness problem 50 years ago through systematic design: standardized scenarios, trained evaluators, detailed rubrics, and actor-patients. If Harvard Medical School requires oral clinical exams for every student, why does Harvard College treat undergraduate oral assessment as experimental? The OSCE model is directly transferable: create standardized scenarios (e.g., "defend your thesis to a skeptical peer," "explain this concept to a non-expert"), train faculty evaluators using shared rubrics, and assess students' ability to perform under consistent conditions. The medical field proves oral assessment can be objective, fair, and scalable—it just requires institutional investment. Harvard should consult HMS faculty for rubric design and evaluator training protocols. The key insight: OSCEs assess performance, not just knowledge—students must demonstrate competence in real-time interaction, which written exams cannot capture.

───

Theatre & Performance Studies: Embodied Knowledge and Oral Examination at MA/PhD Level (University of Kansas 2025)
https://catalog.ku.edu/arts/theatre-dance/ma-theatre/

The University of Kansas MA in Theatre and Performance Studies requires that "The final examination consists of an oral examination upon completion of a thesis that is accepted by the candidate's committee." Research on "Embodied Knowledge and Accessible Community" explores how "improvisation in dance and music... [explores] how knowledge and community are created" through physical performance. Theatre Research International publishes scholarship on how "embodiment is conceptualized within... accounts of cognition."

Relevance to Harvard: Theatre/performance fields have always understood that some knowledge is embodied and non-textual—you cannot assess a dancer's technique, an actor's stage presence, or a director's spatial vision through written exams. This challenges Harvard's humanities departments: if knowledge can be embodied, oral performance assessment isn't just about preventing AI—it's about recognizing modes of knowing that writing cannot capture. Harvard's Theater, Dance, and Media concentration likely already uses oral/performance assessment; why not extend this to other humanities fields? A History student explaining the spatial politics of a Civil War battlefield, a Literature student performing a close reading aloud, or a Philosophy student engaging in Socratic dialogue are all demonstrating embodied understanding that enriches written analysis. The theatre field's concept of "embodiment" provides theoretical justification for oral assessment beyond academic integrity—it's about honoring multiple epistemologies.

───

Management Consulting: Case Interviews as High-Stakes Oral Assessment (McKinsey, BCG, Bain 2025)
https://casecoach.com/b/how-different-are-the-interviews-at-mckinsey-bain-and-bcg/ + https://www.myconsultingoffer.org/case-study-interview-prep/mckinsey/
WikipediaObjective structured clinical examinationAn objective structured clinical examination (OSCE) is an approach to the assessment of clinical competence in which the components are assessed in a planned or structured way with attention being paid to the objectivity of the examination which is basically an organization framework consisting of multiple stations around which students rotate and at which students perform and are assessed on specific tasks. OSCE is a modern type of examination often used for assessment in health care disciplines.CaseCoachHow Do the Interviews at McKinsey, BCG and Bain Differ? | CaseCoachIf you’re preparing to interview at more than one top management consulting firm, like McKinsey, BCG or Bain, there are some key differences you can expect.Written byEnguerran Loos, Founder of CaseCoachEst. reading time6 minutesJan 25th, 2024https://casecoach.com/b/how-different-are-the-interviews-at-mckinsey-bain-and-bcg/My Consulting OfferMcKinsey Case Interview: How to Pass the Case & PEI [2025]Here is what makes McKinsey case interviews particularly tough and how to pass.Est. reading time15 minuteshttps://www.myconsultingoffer.org/case-study-interview-prep/mckinsey/[7:31 AM]McKinsey, BCG, and Bain (MBB)—the most prestigious consulting firms—hire based almost entirely on case interview performance: candidates receive a business problem and must analyze, structure thinking, and present recommendations orally under time pressure. McKinsey uses "interviewer-led case interviews" where the interviewer probes and challenges responses, testing spontaneous adaptation. Thousands of Harvard students prepare for these interviews annually, paying for coaching and practicing cases.

Relevance to Harvard: If Harvard's goal is preparing students for professional success, we must acknowledge that elite career paths increasingly depend on oral performance. Consulting, investment banking, venture capital, law, medicine—all use high-stakes oral assessment in hiring. A brilliant Economics concentrator who can write perfect problem sets but freezes in case interviews will struggle to access top firms. Yet Harvard provides minimal oral performance training outside of a few presentation-focused courses. The irony: we spend four years training students to write academic essays, then send them into job markets that primarily evaluate oral communication. Recommendation: Harvard could integrate "case discussion" pedagogy (borrowed from HBS) into undergraduate Economics, Government, and Social Studies courses—not to vocationalize liberal arts, but to develop transferable skills of oral analysis and argumentation. The consulting case interview is essentially a Socratic dialogue about business problems—the same dialogic reasoning Harvard humanities courses should cultivate.

───

Connection to the Broader Project:

These three professional domains reveal that oral assessment is not experimental—it's established practice in fields where competence matters:

1. Medicine (OSCEs): 50-year tradition of standardized oral clinical exams; proves scalability and fairness are solvable through systematic design
2. Theatre/Performance: Recognition that embodied knowledge cannot be assessed through writing alone; multiple epistemologies require multiple assessment modes
3. Management Consulting: Career access depends on oral case interview performance; Harvard students already train for this outside of academics

The pattern: Professions that require real-time decision-making, human interaction, and performance under pressure use oral assessment universally. Only academia clings to written exams as the primary mode—and even then, only at the undergraduate level. Professional schools (medicine, law, business) and doctoral programs use oral assessment extensively.

Implication for Harvard College: The disconnect is stark—we train undergraduates primarily through written exams, then send them to professional schools and careers that assess orally. This is a pedagogical failure, not an AI-era innovation. The AI crisis simply makes visible what was always true: written exams are one mode of assessment, appropriate for some learning outcomes, but inadequate for demonstrating real-time reasoning, interpersonal communication, and performance under pressure.

Practical recommendation: Survey Harvard alumni 5 years post-graduation across careers (consulting, law, medicine, academia, tech, non-profit). Ask: "What percentage of your professional evaluation depends on oral vs. written communication?" The answer will likely be 70%+ oral. Then ask: "Did Harvard prepare you for this?" If the answer is "I learned oral skills despite, not because of, Harvard," we have a curriculum problem—one that predates ChatGPT.

Theme: History of Oral Examinations in Higher Ed (European Traditions)

German University Rigorosum: "Demonstrate In-Depth Knowledge and Research Ability" (Centuries-Old Tradition, Current)
https://www.hochschulkompass.de/en/doctoral-studies/doctoral-phase/oral-examination.html + https://www.findaphd.com/guides/phd-study-in-germany/

German universities require PhD candidates to complete a Rigorosum—"a more traditional, comprehensive oral exam covering your specific research area and related fields." After submitting and successfully defending a dissertation, candidates must "demonstrate that you have in-depth knowledge of your subject and the necessary research ability" through oral examination. The Rigorosum may also take the form of a Disputation or Kolloquium, depending on the field and university. This tradition has persisted for centuries as the gold standard for doctoral assessment in German-speaking academia.

Relevance to Harvard: The German model reveals an alternative assessment philosophy: oral examination tests not just dissertation content, but comprehensive field knowledge—ensuring candidates can contextualize their narrow research within broader disciplinary conversations. This contrasts with American PhD practice, where oral components (qualifying exams, dissertation defense) are often more limited. Harvard could learn from the German emphasis on oral synthesis: rather than testing students' ability to reproduce written arguments, oral exams test their ability to think across their field, make connections, and respond to interdisciplinary challenges. The persistence of the Rigorosum in Germany (despite pressure to adopt Anglo-American written exam models) suggests deep institutional commitment to oral assessment's pedagogical value. If German universities—world leaders in research—maintain oral PhD exams, why does Harvard College question oral undergraduate assessment?

───

French Agrégation: Competitive Oral Examination Established 1766 "To Certify Secondary School Teachers"
https://criticaltheoryworkshop.com/wp-content/uploads/2014/04/schrift_effects-of-the-agrc3a9gation.pdf + https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Agregation

The French agrégation, established in 1766 under Louis XV, is a highly competitive examination combining written and oral components to certify elite secondary school teachers. Candidates spend an entire year preparing; the exam "generally consists first of a written part (admissibility), then an oral part (admission)" evaluated by university professors. Only a small percentage pass. The agrégation has been called a defining feature of French intellectual life—nearly all major French philosophers (Sartre, Foucault, Derrida, Bourdieu) were agrégés. The oral component tests candidates' ability to extemporize on complex topics with eloquence and depth.

Relevance to Harvard: The agrégation demonstrates that oral assessment can be both elite and meritocratic—the French consider it the highest standard of academic excellence, not a compromise or fallback. The structure (written admissibility → oral admission) is instructive: written exams filter for technical knowledge; oral exams filter for intellectual presence, rhetorical skill, and dialogic agility. Harvard could adopt this staged model: written work establishes baseline competence, oral exams distinguish honors candidates. The agrégation also reveals national variation in assessment culture: French academia values oral eloquence as central to intellectual identity, while Anglo-American academia has privileged written publication. The AI crisis is forcing Harvard to reconsider whether our over-reliance on written assessment is culturally contingent rather than pedagogically necessary. Perhaps we should be more like the French: demanding both written precision and oral brilliance.

───

British Universities' Shift from Oral to Written Exams (1700-1900): "Increased Student Numbers and Problem of Oral Testing in Mathematics"
WikipediaAgrégationIn France, the agrégation (French pronunciation: [aɡʁeɡasjɔ̃] ) is the most competitive and prestigious examination for civil service in the French public education system. Successful candidates become professeurs agrégés ([pʁɔfesœʁ(z‿)aɡʁeʒe]) and are usually appointed as teachers in secondary schools or preparatory classes, or as lecturers in universities.[7:46 AM]https://www.researchgate.net/publication/248939168_The_Shift_from_Oral_to_Written_Examination_Cambridge_and_Oxford_1700-1900

This historical research documents the shift from oral to written examinations at Cambridge and Oxford between 1700-1900. The change was driven by "increased student numbers and the problem of oral testing in some subjects (for example, mathematics)"—you cannot easily test calculus orally. Cambridge led this shift due to its Newtonian mathematics curriculum. However, the article notes that "oral testing was retained into the 20th century" at Oxford, suggesting the transition was gradual and contested. The Cambridge University Act of 1856 formalized this shift, emphasizing "written examinations over oral vivas to ensure fairer and more objective assessment."

Relevance to Harvard: This history reveals that the dominance of written exams is relatively recent (200-250 years) and was driven by practical constraints (scalability, quantitative testing) not superior pedagogy. The "fairness and objectivity" argument for written exams should be questioned: written exams reduce certain biases (examiner favoritism) but introduce others (penalizing students with writing disabilities, non-native speakers, or those who think dialogically rather than linearly). The key insight: different subjects require different assessment modes. Mathematics works better in writing (symbolic manipulation, proof-checking); philosophy and humanities arguably work better orally (dialectic, argumentation, interpretation). Harvard's error may be applying a one-size-fits-all written exam model across disciplines that would benefit from differentiated assessment. The fact that Oxford "retained oral testing into the 20th century" even after Cambridge abandoned it suggests there was pedagogical resistance—some faculty recognized oral assessment's irreplaceable value.

───

Connection to the Broader Project:

These three European traditions provide comparative context for understanding American assessment culture:

1. Germany (Rigorosum): Oral examination as comprehensive field knowledge test—persists as gold standard
2. France (agrégation): Oral examination as elite meritocratic filter—defining feature of intellectual culture
3. Britain (Oxbridge decline): Shift from oral to written driven by scalability and quantitative subjects—but contested and partial

Key insight: The Anglo-American abandonment of oral assessment in the 19th-20th centuries was not universal. Continental European universities maintained oral traditions, suggesting the shift was culturally contingent (driven by British/American emphasis on standardization, quantification, and mass education) rather than pedagogically inevitable.

Implications for Harvard in the AI era:

We should recognize that Harvard's current written-exam-dominated model is historically specific—a product of 19th-century reforms, not timeless wisdom. The AI crisis is an opportunity to re-examine assumptions rather than panic. Questions Harvard faculty should ask:

• Why did we follow the British model (written) rather than the French (oral + written) or German (comprehensive oral)?
• Was the shift to written exams driven by pedagogy or logistics (scalability, standardization)?
• If logistics no longer constrain us (Zoom enables distributed oral exams, small class sizes make them feasible), should we reconsider?

The historical irony: Harvard is considering oral assessment as an AI-era innovation when it's actually a return to pre-1850s norms. We're not pioneering—we're rediscovering what Oxford, Paris, and Heidelberg never fully abandoned.
elle-with-claws  [8:05 AM]
Theme: Peer Institutions Responding to AI Anxieties – Diverse Approaches

MIT Writing Faculty: "Core Rhetorical Skills in Written and Oral Communication... Are Ones Every MIT Undergraduate Still Needs to Learn" (Official Statement)
https://mitadmissions.org/blogs/entry/mit-writing-faculty-comment-on-gpt-and-other-ai-assisted-writing/

MIT writing faculty issued an official statement emphasizing that "the core rhetorical skills involving in written and oral communication—invention, style, grammar, reasoning and argument construction, and research—are ones every MIT undergraduate still needs to learn." The statement notes that MIT's Communication Requirement was created because "alumni reporting that writing and speaking skills were essential for their professional success, and that they did not feel their undergraduate education adequately prepared them." While acknowledging AI's potential future utility, MIT emphasizes students must master foundational communication skills first.

Relevance to Harvard: MIT—a STEM powerhouse—explicitly pairs written and oral communication as equally essential. This is striking: if engineers need oral communication skills, how much more do Harvard humanities students? MIT's alumni feedback mirrors what we've documented: professional success depends heavily on oral performance. The key phrase "did not feel their undergraduate education adequately prepared them" should alarm Harvard—we may be making the same mistake. MIT's response is to double down on communication fundamentals before introducing AI tools. Harvard could adopt this staged approach: ensure students master oral presentation/defense skills in years 1-3, then teach AI-assisted workflow in year 4. The institutional framing matters: MIT presents this as professional preparation, not academic integrity panic.

───

University of Chicago: "The University Does Not Have a Set Policy on AI Tools in the Classroom... Up to Each Instructor" (2025)
https://guides.lib.uchicago.edu/c.php?g=1371911&p=10145580 + UChicago Law School Revised Policy (Autumn 2025)

UChicago takes a radically decentralized approach: "The University of Chicago does not have a set policy on AI tools in the classroom. As such, it is up to each instructor how they would like to approach the topic." However, UChicago Law School established a specific policy (revised Autumn 2025), and the Harris School offers a course titled "AI-Assisted Writing for Public Policy" that teaches students to "collaborate effectively and ethically with tools like ChatGPT" for policy writing. UChicago Academic Tech provides a Canvas module on "effective AI policy for your syllabus" with sample policies ranging from prohibition to permitted uses.

Relevance to Harvard: UChicago embodies intellectual pluralism: no top-down mandate, but institutional support for faculty experimentation. This aligns with both Chicago and Harvard's traditions of faculty autonomy. The contrast between UChicago College (no policy) and UChicago Law (specific policy) suggests discipline-specific responses are appropriate—professional schools need clear rules, liberal arts need flexibility. Harvard could adopt Chicago's model: provide faculty with menu of options (AI prohibited, AI for drafting only, AI with disclosure, AI encouraged with guidance) and let each course choose. The Harris School's "AI-Assisted Writing" course is provocative—rather than banning AI, teach students professional AI use. Harvard could pilot similar courses: "AI-Assisted Research for Social Studies" or "Writing with AI in the Humanities." The Chicago approach trusts faculty judgment while providing infrastructure.

───

New York Magazine (May 2025): "Everyone Is Cheating Their Way Through College" — AI Disrupting Liberal Arts Hardest
https://nymag.com/intelligencer/article/openai-chatgpt-ai-cheating-education-college-students-school.html + Times of India: "Why ChatGPT Is Disrupting Liberal Arts but Not STEM"
MIT AdmissionsMIT writing faculty comment on GPT and other AI assisted writing in education | MIT AdmissionsAt MIT Admissions, we recruit and enroll a talented and diverse class of undergraduates who will learn to use science, technology, and other areas of scholarship to serve the nation and the world in the 21st century.https://mitadmissions.org/blogs/entry/mit-writing-faculty-comment-on-gpt-and-other-ai-assisted-writing/guides.lib.uchicago.eduLibrary Guides: Generative AI: AI in the ClassroomInformation about Generative AI tools and their use in and outside of the classroomIntelligencerEveryone Is Cheating Their Way Through CollegeChatGPT has unraveled the entire academic project.May 7th, 2025https://nymag.com/intelligencer/article/openai-chatgpt-ai-cheating-education-college-students-school.html[8:05 AM]This journalistic investigation documents widespread AI use: "Students at large state schools, the Ivies, liberal-arts schools in New England, universities abroad... are relying on AI to ease their way through every facet of their education." The article notes professors found "essays filled with clunky, robotic phrasing that, though grammatically flawless, didn't sound quite like a college student." A separate analysis argues AI is "disrupting liberal arts but not STEM" because STEM requires problem-solving AI cannot (yet) replicate, while liberal arts writing is more vulnerable.

Relevance to Harvard: This is the uncomfortable reality Harvard faculty face: AI use is already pervasive, not emerging. The phrase "Ivies, liberal-arts schools in New England" means Harvard students are already using AI extensively—the question isn't whether to respond, but how to respond to existing widespread use. The observation that AI disrupts liberal arts more than STEM is crucial: Harvard's humanities/social science core is most vulnerable. This creates equity concerns: STEM concentrators (Computer Science, Engineering, Math) maintain assessment integrity through problem sets and exams AI cannot solve, while humanities concentrators face collapsed assessment ecosystems. Harvard must ask: Are we inadvertently creating a two-tier system where STEM assessment remains rigorous while humanities assessment becomes performative? The article's title—"Everyone Is Cheating"—is hyperbolic, but captures student perception that AI use is normalized, not deviant. Harvard's response must acknowledge this reality rather than pretending it's a minority behavior.

───

Connection to the Broader Project:

These three sources reveal divergent institutional responses among elite peers:

1. MIT: Unified institutional stance emphasizing foundational communication skills (written + oral) before AI tools
2. UChicago: Decentralized faculty autonomy with institutional infrastructure support; discipline-specific policies
3. Broader landscape (NYMag/TOI): Widespread AI use already normalized; liberal arts particularly vulnerable

No consensus has emerged, but patterns are visible:

• STEM institutions (MIT) can emphasize fundamentals because their assessment (problem sets, labs, exams) is inherently AI-resistant
• Liberal arts institutions (implied by journalism) are struggling because essay-based assessment is most vulnerable
• Pragmatic institutions (UChicago) are experimenting with integration rather than prohibition

Harvard's challenge: We're a liberal arts institution with STEM strength. We cannot simply adopt MIT's approach (STEM-first) or UChicago's pure autonomy model. We need a hybrid strategy:

1. By discipline: STEM courses can maintain traditional assessment; humanities courses need redesign
2. By skill level: Lower-level courses teach fundamentals (including oral communication); upper-level courses permit AI tools with transparency
3. By assessment type: High-stakes exams (oral or in-class written) remain AI-free; formative assignments permit AI with scaffolding

The meta-lesson: Harvard must move quickly but thoughtfully. The journalism documenting widespread AI use suggests we're behind the curve—students have already adapted, and we're playing catch-up. But peer institutions haven't found silver-bullet solutions either. Harvard has the opportunity to lead through principled experimentation rather than reactive prohibition.

Theme: History of Oral Assignments at Harvard Specifically

Harvard Expos 20 & Studio 10: Oral Presentations, Filmed Interviews, and Public-Facing Communication (2025-2026)
https://writingprogram.fas.harvard.edu/expos-20 + https://writingprogram.fas.harvard.edu/expos-studio-10

Harvard's required Expository Writing program explicitly incorporates oral components: some sections culminate in students "delivering an oral presentation, content for a publicly viewable website, a multimedia presentation, or a podcast" instead of traditional research papers. Spring 2026 sections include "work-in-progress presentations" and "extended, filmed interviews between you and one or two of your classmates" discussing research. Expos Studio 10 "prioritize[s] practicing oral presentations, giving students the opportunity to develop skills in another important mode of communicating their ideas."

Relevance to Harvard: This demonstrates that oral assessment is already embedded in Harvard's core curriculum—every first-year student takes Expos 20, meaning thousands experience oral presentation requirements. This is significant: Harvard hasn't abandoned oral communication training; we've maintained it in our foundational writing course. The innovation is treating oral presentation as equal to written essays ("instead of traditional research papers") rather than supplementary. The "filmed interview" format is particularly interesting—it creates an archival record while maintaining oral spontaneity. If first-year students are already doing oral presentations in Expos, expanding oral assessment to concentration courses isn't introducing something foreign—it's scaling existing practice. The challenge: Expos sections are 12 students; concentration courses may be 40-100. Can we adapt Expos oral assessment pedagogy to larger courses?

───

Harvard SEAS PhD: "Two-Hour Oral Examination Devoted to Presentation and Discussion... To Test Comprehension of Research Field" (Current)
https://seas.harvard.edu/office-academic-programs/graduate-policies-procedures-and-forms/phd-degree-requirements/phd-qualifying-exam-end-g2-year + Computer Science Oral Qualifying

Harvard School of Engineering and Applied Sciences requires PhD students to pass an "oral qualifying examination" by end of their second year. The format is "ordinarily a two-hour oral examination devoted to the presentation and discussion of one or more potential dissertation topics and to more general questions." The intent is to "test the student's comprehension of his/her chosen research field" beyond just dissertation specifics. Computer Science students must complete this oral exam with "no exceptions" to the deadline. Similarly, HMS requires a public seminar followed by private oral defense.

Relevance to Harvard: Harvard's own graduate programs universally require oral qualifying exams, yet the College questions whether undergraduates can handle them. This is the same irony we documented with PhD dissertation defenses: we trust oral assessment for our most advanced students (doctoral candidates) but question it for undergraduates. If SEAS can administer mandatory two-hour oral exams to all PhD students—testing comprehensive field knowledge, not just memorized facts—why can't FAS administer 30-minute oral exams to honors thesis candidates? The key difference isn't student capability but institutional commitment. SEAS has infrastructure: committee scheduling, standardized expectations, evaluator training. FAS would need similar infrastructure to scale oral assessment beyond current selective use (History honors, Philosophy joint concentrators). The HMS requirement of a public seminar before private defense is instructive: making oral exams communal/public (like medieval disputations) adds accountability and pedagogical value.

───

Harvard Case Competitions: Teams "Create a Pitch Book... Present Their Work... Be Evaluated by a Panel of Judges" (2025-2026)
seas.harvard.eduPhD Qualifying Exam: by end of G2 year | Harvard John A. Paulson School of Engineering and Applied Sciences[Part of the Policies of the CHD, last updated fall 2021; see also area-specific exam guidelines for Applied Math, Applied Physics[8:16 AM]https://www.casecomp.org/ + https://www.thecasecompetition.org/gcch-2026 + Harvard Kennedy School Case Program

Harvard hosts multiple case competitions where student teams have "a set amount of time to develop a strategy to address the key issues and present it to a team of judges." The Global Case Competition at Harvard (2025 edition just completed) requires teams to "create a pitch book" and, for top-ten finalists, "present their work" on campus to judges. These competitions mirror consulting case interviews and policy briefings—time-constrained oral presentation with Q&A. Harvard Kennedy School maintains the world's largest case study repository specifically designed for this pedagogy.

Relevance to Harvard: Case competitions demonstrate that Harvard students excel at high-stakes oral presentations when the format is clearly structured and high-status (competitive, with prizes/recognition). This challenges the narrative that oral assessment disadvantages students—hundreds voluntarily participate in these competitions, suggesting oral performance anxiety is manageable when students are prepared and motivated. The case competition model offers a template for academic oral assessment: give students advance materials (like a case packet), time to prepare collaboratively (like team prep), and structured evaluation rubrics (like judge scorecards). The fact that these competitions happen across Harvard (HKS, HBS, undergraduate student groups) shows cross-campus comfort with oral assessment—just not yet formalized in FAS academic requirements. The irony: students compete in oral case competitions for extracurricular prestige but resist oral exams for grades. This suggests framing and culture matter. If Harvard positions oral assessment as a valued skill (like case competition participation on résumés), not a punitive AI-detection tool, student buy-in improves.

───

Connection to the Broader Project:

These three Harvard-specific examples reveal oral assessment infrastructure already exists at multiple levels:

1. Undergraduate core (Expos 20): First-years practice oral presentations, filmed interviews, podcasts
2. Graduate training (PhD quals): SEAS, HMS require two-hour comprehensive oral exams universally
3. Extracurricular culture (case competitions): Students voluntarily participate in high-stakes oral presentations

The pattern: Oral assessment isn't absent from Harvard—it's compartmentalized. We use it in writing courses, graduate programs, and extracurriculars, but not systematically in undergraduate concentration courses. The AI crisis is an opportunity to integrate these existing oral assessment traditions into the academic core.

Practical implications:

• Faculty development: Leverage Expos instructors' expertise to train concentration faculty in oral assessment design
• Infrastructure: Adopt SEAS PhD oral exam scheduling/rubric systems for undergraduate honors oral defenses
• Cultural shift: Frame oral assessment like case competitions—prestigious skill development, not remedial AI-prevention
• Scaling: Expos and case competitions prove small-group oral assessment works; now pilot medium-size course adaptations (30-40 students with rotating oral presentation days)

The historical insight: Harvard hasn't lost oral assessment traditions—we've just relegated them to specific contexts (freshman writing, graduate qualifying, extracurriculars). Expanding to concentration courses isn't innovation; it's mainstreaming practices we already validate in peripheral spaces.

Question for Harvard faculty: If oral presentations are rigorous enough for Expos 20, PhD qualifying exams, and case competitions, why aren't they rigorous enough for History 1000-level courses or Government senior seminars? The resistance isn't about quality—it's about unfamiliarity and workload concerns. Both are solvable with institutional investment.

Theme: Educational Research on Speaking in Learning/Assessment - SoTL

Taylor & Francis Systematic Review (2025): "Interventions and Facilitators of Oral Assessment Performance in Higher Education"
https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/02602938.2025.2504621

This just-published systematic review examines "the factors that facilitate student performance in oral assessments at the undergraduate and master's levels across diverse academic disciplines." The research synthesizes studies identifying interventions that improve oral assessment outcomes and documents what enables students to succeed in oral examination contexts. This represents the most current comprehensive SoTL analysis of oral assessment pedagogy.

Relevance to Harvard: This systematic review provides evidence-based guidance for faculty designing oral assessments—exactly what Harvard needs as we consider expansion. Rather than inventing from scratch, Harvard can leverage this synthesis of what works. Key questions the review likely addresses: What preparation reduces anxiety? What rubric designs improve reliability? What feedback mechanisms enhance learning? Harvard's Bok Center should acquire this article and develop faculty workshops based on its findings. The "diverse academic disciplines" scope is crucial—it means the evidence isn't discipline-specific but generalizable. Harvard faculty in History, Government, Economics, and Literature can all benefit from these synthesized best practices. The 2025 publication date ensures it incorporates post-ChatGPT oral assessment literature, making it directly relevant to our AI-era concerns.

───

Taylor & Francis (2024): "The Development and Test of the Public Speaking Competency Rubric+" — Valid and Reliable Tool
https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/0047231X.2024.2405172

This peer-reviewed study presents the Public Speaking Competency Rubric+ (PSCR+), described as "a valid and reliable tool for assessing the public speaking skills of college students." The rubric "now covers the width and breadth of public speaking skills that are the hallmarks of a strong talk" including nonverbal communication. The research documents psychometric validation ensuring the tool measures what it claims to measure with consistency across raters.

Relevance to Harvard: This addresses the "How do we grade fairly?" concern that faculty raise about oral assessment. The PSCR+ provides a ready-made, validated instrument Harvard can adopt or adapt rather than creating rubrics from scratch. The emphasis on validity and reliability directly counters the argument that oral assessment is inherently subjective or biased. If researchers can create rubrics that achieve inter-rater reliability, Harvard faculty can too—with training. The inclusion of nonverbal communication is important: oral exams assess more than verbal content (body language, eye contact, pacing, vocal variety). This is either a benefit (testing holistic communication) or a concern (disadvantaging neurodiverse students or those from cultures with different nonverbal norms). Harvard must decide: Do we assess only verbal content, or holistic oral performance? The PSCR+ documentation likely includes examiner training protocols—Harvard should implement these before scaling oral assessment.

───

Frontiers in Education (October 2025): "A Scoping Review of Dialogic Formative Feedback Practices in Higher Education"
https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/education/articles/10.3389/feduc.2025.1696703/full
FrontiersFrontiers | A scoping review of dialogic formative feedback practices in higher educationBackgroundFeedback practices in higher education are important for student learning and engagement, and unfortunately, both students and university teachers ...[8:31 AM]This recent scoping review examines "dialogic formative feedback practices" in higher education—feedback delivered through dialogue rather than written comments. The research documents "group activities such as collective reading, modeling, and group assignments" and distinguishes between quantitative performance measurement and qualitative changes in "students' attitudes, self-confidence, engagement, or learning strategies." The study identifies "dialogic formative feedback in classrooms and lectures" as a key setting (n=15 studies).

Relevance to Harvard: This reframes oral assessment as dialogic feedback rather than high-stakes examination—a pedagogically richer conception. Traditional written exams provide one-way feedback (instructor → student); oral exams enable two-way dialogue where students can clarify questions, instructors can probe understanding, and both can negotiate meaning collaboratively. This aligns with Harvard's tutorial tradition and Socratic pedagogy. The distinction between quantitative (measuring performance) and qualitative (fostering growth) dialogic feedback is crucial: Harvard should ask whether oral assessment's primary goal is summative evaluation (grading) or formative development (learning). The scoping review suggests oral dialogue improves "self-confidence, engagement, or learning strategies"—outcomes Harvard values beyond grades. Practical implication: Rather than replacing all written exams with oral exams, Harvard could integrate oral formative feedback sessions throughout the semester (like office hours but structured around specific assignments), with oral defense becoming the natural culmination. This scaffolds student development rather than springing high-stakes oral exams on unprepared students.

───

Connection to the Broader Project:

These three recent SoTL studies provide actionable research for oral assessment implementation:

1. Systematic review (2025): Evidence-based interventions that facilitate oral assessment success
2. Rubric validation (2024): Psychometrically sound tools for fair, reliable evaluation
3. Dialogic feedback (2025): Conceptual framework positioning oral assessment as formative dialogue

Synthesis for Harvard:

The SoTL literature suggests oral assessment expansion requires three parallel tracks:

1. Faculty development: Train instructors in validated rubrics (like PSCR+) and evidence-based facilitation techniques
2. Student preparation: Scaffold oral skills through formative dialogic feedback before high-stakes assessment
3. Assessment design: Balance summative evaluation with formative learning goals

The good news: We don't need to invent oral assessment pedagogy from scratch—decades of communication studies, medical education (OSCEs), and recent SoTL provide robust foundations.

The challenge: Harvard faculty need time and training to implement these practices well. Rolling out oral assessment without professional development risks replicating the very problems (bias, anxiety, inequity) the SoTL literature shows are avoidable with proper design.

Recommendation: Harvard should:

• Form oral assessment working group with Bok Center, Communication Department, and concentration representatives
• Review systematic review findings (Taylor & Francis 2025) to identify best practices
• Pilot PSCR+ or similar validated rubrics in select courses with evaluator training
• Integrate dialogic formative feedback throughout courses before introducing summative oral exams
• Gather assessment data and iterate based on evidence

The meta-lesson: SoTL research shows oral assessment can be fair, reliable, and pedagogically valuable—if done well. The question isn't whether to adopt oral assessment, but whether Harvard will invest in doing it well.
