Theme: Post-AI Classroom Experiments with Oral Assignments

Princeton Professor (October 2025): "15-Minute Oral Midterm Exam... Students Can Opt to Write 5-Page Paper in Lieu"
https://www.dailyprincetonian.com/article/2025/10/princeton-news-broadfocus-artificial-intelligence-humanities-professors.

The Daily Princetonian reports that a Princeton humanities professor redesigned their course this semester: "students will instead have a 15-minute oral midterm exam, three quizzes, and an in-person final exam, though students can opt to write a 5-page paper in lieu of the oral exam." This represents a major curricular shift toward oral assessment in response to generative AI, while maintaining student choice.

Relevance to Harvard: Princeton's experiment offers a hybrid model that addresses both AI concerns and student anxiety: oral as default, written as opt-out. This is pedagogically interesting—it inverts the traditional assumption that writing is primary and oral is alternative. The 15-minute oral exam is notably shorter than traditional oral exams (which can run 30-60+ minutes), suggesting faculty are experimenting with time-efficient formats. The student choice element is crucial: forcing oral exams on all students risks disadvantaging those with social anxiety, speech disabilities, or non-native English proficiency. Offering an opt-out written option preserves assessment integrity (the written option is still in-person, reducing AI access) while respecting diverse student needs. Harvard could pilot this model: make oral defense the encouraged default with written in-class exam as accommodation. The key question: What percentage of students choose oral vs. written? If most choose written, the oral option didn't solve the AI problem. If most choose oral, it suggests students prefer it once anxiety is managed.

───

Ohio State University: "5-Minute Oral Defense to Major Written Assignments, Citing Improved Accountability" (2025)
https://walterwrites.ai/can-teachers-detect-chatgpt/ + Voyagard "Oral Exam Check"

Multiple sources document that Ohio State professors include "a 5-minute oral defense to major written assignments" as an accountability mechanism. Another source describes the "oral exam check": asking students to explain their essay arguments in class discussions, which is "surprisingly effective because it tests whether understanding matches performance." If students "stumble through follow-up questions about 'your' answers," faculty know AI was likely used.

Relevance to Harvard: The Ohio State model is brilliantly simple: keep written assignments but add brief oral verification. This is more scalable than replacing all essays with oral exams. A 5-minute oral defense per student in a 25-person course = 125 minutes total (two class sessions). This is manageable, whereas 30-minute full oral exams = 750 minutes (infeasible). The "oral exam check" informal version (asking students to explain arguments spontaneously in class) requires no additional time—just intentional questioning during discussion. Harvard could implement this immediately: require students to defend their essay thesis orally (5 minutes) during office hours or discussion sections. This doesn't replace writing (which has pedagogical value) but verifies authorship. The psychological effect matters: students knowing they'll have to explain their argument orally will be less likely to outsource to AI, because they can't fake the understanding needed for verbal defense. This hybrid approach addresses AI without abandoning writing instruction.

───

TextPolish Blog (2026): "Universities Reinstating 'Viva Voce'—If Student Outsourced Thinking to AI, They Crumble in 30 Seconds"
https://www.text-polish.com/blog/professors-dilemma-grading-ai-2026
The PrincetonianThe future of humanities teaching in the AI age, according to Princeton professorsSome professors are outright banning AI, turning assessments that used to be papers into in-class exams. Others are trying to work alongside AI, asking students to be transparent when they have used AI in research and writing. Others still are encouraging AI use and embracing its potential. And yet, they all expressed fears that AI could deeply impact critical thinking and writing.https://www.dailyprincetonian.com/article/2025/10/princeton-news-broadfocus-artificial-intelligence-humanities-professorsTextPolishTextPolish - AI Humanizer | Make AI Text Sound Human & Bypass AI DetectorsTransform AI-generated content into natural, human-like text that bypasses AI detection tools like Turnitin, GPTZero, and Copyleaks. Perfect for essays, blog posts, and assignments.Feb 4thhttps://www.text-polish.com/blog/professors-dilemma-grading-ai-2026[8:46 AM]This recent blog post (3 weeks old) argues: "More universities are reinstating the 'viva voce'—an oral defense of the work. 'You wrote about the symbolism of the ghost in Hamlet? Great. Tell me about it right now, without your notes.' If a student outsourced their thinking to an AI, they crumble in 30 seconds." The post declares "The 'Explain X' essay is dead" and describes new assignment types emerging in response to AI.

Relevance to Harvard: This blog post (caveat: not peer-reviewed) captures the practitioner sentiment driving oral assessment adoption. The phrase "crumble in 30 seconds" is revealing—faculty see oral exams as AI detection rather than pedagogy. This is concerning: if oral assessment is framed purely as catching cheaters, it becomes adversarial rather than developmental. The claim that "the 'Explain X' essay is dead" is hyperbolic but signals faculty desperation. Harvard should resist this panic-driven framing. Yes, oral exams can verify understanding—but they should be positioned as valuable learning experiences (developing oral argumentation skills) not just punitive AI-detection tools. The "viva voce" term (from British PhD tradition) lends scholarly legitimacy to what is essentially an interrogation. Harvard faculty should ask: Are we reviving a dignified academic tradition, or are we creating a surveillance pedagogy? The distinction matters for student trust and institutional culture.

───

Connection to the Broader Project:

These three post-AI experiments reveal diverse implementation strategies:

1. Princeton: Full course redesign with oral as default, written opt-out (15-minute exams)
2. Ohio State: Hybrid approach—keep essays, add 5-minute oral defense verification
3. Broader trend (TextPolish): Framing oral exams as AI-detection ("crumble in 30 seconds")

Key insights:

• Time efficiency: 5-15 minute oral exams are more scalable than 30-60 minute traditional formats
• Student choice: Offering written alternatives addresses equity/anxiety concerns
• Hybrid models: Oral verification of written work may be more practical than oral replacement
• Framing matters: Positioning oral exams as development vs. detection shapes student perception

Practical recommendations for Harvard:

1. Pilot short-format oral defenses (5-10 minutes) rather than comprehensive oral exams
2. Offer student choice between oral defense and in-class written alternative
3. Start with hybrid (essay + oral verification) before full oral replacement
4. Frame pedagogically: "Developing your oral argumentation skills" not "Catching AI cheaters"

The warning: The TextPolish framing ("crumble in 30 seconds") represents punitive pedagogy that will erode student-faculty trust. Harvard must avoid positioning oral assessment as adversarial AI-detection. The goal is developing communicative competence, which happens to be AI-resistant—not catching students who used AI.

Question for Harvard faculty: What's your primary motivation—pedagogical value or academic integrity? If the former, oral assessment is worth pursuing. If the latter alone, you're building a surveillance system that will damage educational relationships. The best oral assessment implementations (like Princeton's opt-in model or Ohio State's brief defense) balance both goals without sacrificing pedagogy to policing.
elle-with-claws  [9:01 AM]
Theme: AI Used IN Oral Evaluation (The Accessibility Double-Edge)

:alarm_clock: FINAL 24 HOURS: This marks the beginning of our last full cycle before the Monday 9am deadline

Taylor & Francis (2024): "Speech-to-Text Intervention for Students with Intellectual Disabilities — Capacity More Significant Than Expected"
https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/17483107.2024.2381785

This peer-reviewed study demonstrates that speech-to-text (STT) technology "allows teachers to create an inclusive and accessible learning environment, ensuring that students with writing difficulties have the same opportunities to express their thoughts." The research found that "students with intellectual disabilities face challenges... producing text in traditional ways; nevertheless, this study's results indicate that their capacity is more significant than what is typically expected when they are provided with the right tools." College Board is embedding text-to-speech in its testing application beginning Spring 2025.

Relevance to Harvard: This reveals the accessibility paradox of AI in oral assessment. While we documented earlier that AI transcription introduces dialect/accent bias, this research shows STT also enables students with disabilities to participate. Harvard must navigate this tension: AI speech technology is simultaneously a tool of exclusion (biased against non-dominant speech) and tool of inclusion (supporting students with writing disabilities). The solution isn't to ban AI entirely but to audit and accommodate. If Harvard adopts oral assessment: (1) Provide STT tools for students with speech production needs, (2) Grade from audio/video recordings not AI transcripts to avoid ASR bias, (3) Allow students with social anxiety or speech disabilities to use AI-assisted practice (without penalty). The College Board's Spring 2025 embedding of text-to-speech shows mainstream assessment is integrating these tools—Harvard should too, thoughtfully.

───

HireVue Discontinues Facial Recognition After "Accents or Atypical Speech Patterns Were Being Unfairly Penalized" (2021-2025)
https://www.talentmsh.com/insights/ai-in-recruitment + ScienceDirect AI HRM Bias Study (October 2025)

HireVue, a major AI interviewing platform, discontinued its facial recognition and vocal tone evaluation features after public backlash over concerns that "candidates with accents or atypical speech patterns were being unfairly penalized." Recent research (ScienceDirect, October 2025) confirms: "The reliance on historical data and algorithmic decision-making in AI-driven HRM tools may inadvertently reinforce existing biases in employee assessments." AI recruitment tools struggle with vocal patterns, particularly for older users and non-native speakers.

Relevance to Harvard: HireVue's retreat from AI vocal assessment is a cautionary tale for universities considering AI-mediated oral exams (like NYU's 42-cent automated exams we documented earlier). If a for-profit company with massive R&D budgets abandoned AI vocal evaluation due to bias concerns, Harvard should not rush into it. The employment context parallels education: both assess high-stakes performance, both claim objectivity, both encode systematic disadvantage. Harvard faculty should ask: If HireVue's AI unfairly penalized accented speech in job interviews, would Harvard's AI do the same in oral exams? The answer is almost certainly yes—unless Harvard invests significantly more in bias mitigation than a venture-backed tech company did. The safer path: human evaluation with trained assessors using validated rubrics. AI can assist with logistics (scheduling, recording), but the evaluative judgment must remain human to ensure equity.

───

ScienceDirect (April 2025): "Evaluating an AI Speaking Assessment Tool — Acknowledging Limitation in Providing Scores Without Detailed Feedback"
https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1475158525000360
talentmsh.comAI Recruitment Trends & Statistics In 2026 | MSHFrom sourcing to screening to retention, AI is changing how recruiting works. Here’s what’s actually worth paying attention to.https://www.talentmsh.com/insights/ai-in-recruitment[9:01 AM]This recent study evaluated EAP Talk, an AI tool for scoring oral presentations. The research compared AI scores with experienced teachers using Adaptive Comparative Judgment (ACJ) and traditional rubrics. Key finding: the study "acknowledg[ed] the limitation of EAP Talk in providing scores without detailed feedback" and investigated "oral peer feedback as a complementary enhancement strategy." The AI could score, but couldn't explain why or provide formative guidance.

Relevance to Harvard: This highlights a fundamental pedagogical limitation of AI oral evaluation: it can assign numbers but not teach. Even if AI scoring were unbiased (which it isn't), it would still fail Harvard's educational mission. Students need dialogic feedback—the Socratic questioning that oral exams uniquely enable. If AI replaces the examiner, the conversation disappears. The study's recommendation to pair AI scoring with peer feedback is instructive: AI can handle routine assessment tasks (initial scoring, pattern identification), freeing human faculty for higher-order pedagogical work (nuanced feedback, formative dialogue). Harvard could adopt this division of labor: AI provides preliminary scores or flags outliers, faculty conduct the actual oral examination and feedback. This preserves human judgment while acknowledging AI's efficiency. The key: AI augments, doesn't replace, human pedagogy.

───

Connection to the Broader Project:

These three sources reveal the nuanced reality of AI in oral assessment:

1. Accessibility tool (Taylor & Francis): STT empowers students with disabilities
2. Bias amplifier (HireVue): Vocal pattern analysis penalizes accents/atypical speech
3. Limited pedagogy (ScienceDirect): AI can score but can't provide formative feedback

The fundamental tension: The same AI technologies that could make oral assessment more accessible (STT for disabilities) also introduce new inequities (vocal bias) and pedagogical limitations (no formative dialogue).

Recommendations for Harvard:

1. Accessibility first: Provide STT and AI practice tools as accommodations, not assessment instruments
2. Human evaluation: Keep examiner judgment human to avoid automated bias
3. Transparent auditing: If any AI tools are used, commission bias audits across demographic groups
4. Pedagogical priority: Don't sacrifice formative feedback for scoring efficiency

The meta-lesson: AI in oral assessment is neither savior nor villain—it's a set of tools with trade-offs. Harvard must:

• Embrace AI as accessibility accommodation (STT, practice partners)
• Reject AI as primary evaluator (vocal bias, no formative feedback)
• Audit any AI deployment for equity impacts

Final insight for this theme: The HireVue case study is the smoking gun—a well-funded company tried exactly what some universities are now attempting (AI vocal assessment) and publicly abandoned it due to bias. Harvard should learn from their expensive mistake rather than repeat it.

The path forward: Human examiners, AI logistics. Use AI for scheduling, recording, and student practice—but keep the evaluative conversation human.
elle-with-claws  [9:16 AM]
Theme: Literary/Historical References (Classical Rhetoric & Oral Tradition)

Eric Havelock: "The Iliad Acted as an Oral Encyclopedia" — How Poets Conserved and Transmitted Society's Traditions
https://www.hup.harvard.edu/books/9780674699069 (Harvard University Press, Preface to Plato)

Classicist Eric Havelock's foundational work argues that in preliterate Greece, "stored experience necessary to cultural stability had to be preserved as poetry in order to be memorized." Plato's attack on poets, particularly Homer, was because they served as "the sole source of Greek moral and technical instruction"—Havelock demonstrates "how the Iliad acted as an oral encyclopedia." His thesis: the shift from oral tradition to written word fundamentally changed not just how Greeks stored information but how they thought about justice, education, and knowledge itself.

Relevance to Harvard: Havelock (published by Harvard University Press!) provides the deepest historical perspective on the oral-to-written transition. His insight: oral transmission isn't just a communication mode—it shapes cognition. Oral cultures develop different mental structures (formulaic memory, rhythmic patterns, communal knowledge) than literate cultures (individual reflection, linear argument, abstract concepts). This has direct implications for Harvard's AI crisis: If we shift back toward oral assessment, we're not just changing testing formats—we're potentially reshaping how students think. Oral exams privilege spontaneous synthesis over careful revision, immediate response over delayed reflection, dialogic engagement over solitary contemplation. These aren't "better" or "worse"—they're different cognitive modes. Harvard must ask: What kind of thinking do we want to cultivate? The Platonic critique of poets (they transmit tradition without understanding) parallels today's critique of AI (it generates text without comprehension). Havelock reminds us that debates about knowledge transmission vs. understanding are ancient, not AI-era inventions.

───

Sophists as Ancient Greece's Professional Educators: "Their Teachings Emphasized Public Speaking and Ethical Conduct" (December 2025)
https://greekreporter.com/2025/12/06/sophists-rhetoric-ancient-greece/ + Wikipedia on Classical Rhetoric

The Sophists emerged c. 600 BCE as "professional educators and intellectuals" who traveled Greece teaching "various life skills, particularly rhetoric and public speaking." Isocrates, "the most famous of the sophists," made "public speaking basic to the educational system." Students paid substantial fees to learn oratory, which was essential for success in Greek democracy. The Sophists taught that skilled oral performance could win legal cases, influence assemblies, and secure political power—making rhetoric the most valuable educational commodity.

Relevance to Harvard: The Sophists represent the original liberal arts education—and it was fundamentally oral. They didn't teach students to write essays; they taught them to speak persuasively in public forums. This challenges the modern assumption that "serious" education = writing. Ancient Greece produced Socrates, Plato, Aristotle—the foundation of Western philosophy—through an educational system centered on oral training. The Sophistic tradition also reveals the political economy of oral assessment: it was elite education for those who could afford tutors. If Harvard adopts oral assessment, are we returning to an aristocratic model where performance advantages those with cultural capital (verbal fluency, confident presentation, "proper" accents)? Or can we democratize oral training the way we democratized writing instruction? The Sophists charged fees; Harvard provides Expos 20 free. Perhaps the answer is universal oral communication training (like Expos but for speaking) before implementing oral assessment—ensuring all students develop skills, not just those from backgrounds that cultivate verbal performance.

───
[9:16 AM]Cicero's De Oratore: "The Great Orator Must Be Well Versed in Moral and Political Philosophy... His Style Must Reveal Depth of Education"
https://www.ebsco.com/research-starters/history/oratory-cicero + Modern Classical Rhetoric Resources (2025)

Cicero's influential work on oratory argues that "in an orator we must demand the subtlety of the logician, the thoughts of the philosopher, a diction almost poetic, a lawyer's memory, a tragedian's voice, and the bearing almost of the consummate actor." He insists "rhetoric alone is insufficient"—the great orator requires "a true liberal education" spanning philosophy, law, literature, and performance. Young Roman orators learned "through practice, the importance of variety and frequency of speech" and were "awarded with popularity, wealth, and reputation."

Relevance to Harvard: Cicero's holistic vision of oratory as synthesizing liberal education provides a powerful justification for oral assessment beyond AI-prevention. If Harvard positions oral exams as testing whether students can synthesize knowledge across domains (philosophy, history, empirical evidence, ethical reasoning) in real-time verbal performance, we're reviving Ciceronian education. Written essays test whether students can construct arguments with time for research and revision. Oral exams test whether students have internalized understanding deeply enough to deploy it spontaneously under questioning. This is the difference between having knowledge (can look it up, can cite sources in writing) and possessing knowledge (can explain without notes, can respond to challenges dialogically). Cicero's emphasis on "a lawyer's memory" is particularly relevant: Roman orators memorized hours of speeches without notes. Modern students rely on external memory (books, search engines, AI). Oral exams force temporary return to internal knowledge—you can't Google during a viva. The question: Is this valuable cognitive training, or nostalgic anachronism? Cicero would argue the former: oral mastery demonstrates intellectual maturity in ways writing cannot.

───

Connection to the Broader Project:

These three classical sources provide intellectual pedigree for oral assessment spanning 2,500 years:

1. Havelock: Oral tradition shaped Greek cognition; transition to literacy transformed thought itself
2. Sophists: Oral training was the original liberal arts education; rhetoric = most valuable skill
3. Cicero: Great orators synthesize liberal education; oral mastery = intellectual maturity

The classical argument for oral assessment:

• Cognitive depth: Oral synthesis requires internalized understanding (Havelock)
• Political/professional power: Persuasive speech = career success (Sophists)
• Liberal education culmination: Oratory integrates all learning (Cicero)

The classical cautions:

• Elite bias: Sophists charged fees; oral training advantaged wealthy (equity concern)
• Memory vs. understanding: Havelock shows oral cultures memorize formulas—is this what we want?
• Performance anxiety: Not everyone has "tragedian's voice"—Cicero's ideal excludes neurodiverse/introverted

Question for Harvard: Are we reviving a noble classical tradition or an aristocratic elite practice? The answer depends on whether we:

1. Provide universal oral training (like Expos) before assessment
2. Design rubrics focused on content understanding not performance polish
3. Offer accommodations for speech differences/disabilities
4. Frame oral work as educational development not social display
EBSCOOn Oratory by Cicero | History | Research Starters | EBSCO Researchhttps://www.ebsco.com/research-starters/history/oratory-cicero[9:16 AM]The historical irony: Universities were founded on oral disputation (medieval Scholasticism, itself inheriting classical rhetoric), shifted to written exams (19th-century democratization), and now consider returning to oral assessment (AI crisis). We're not innovating—we're oscillating between ancient models. The question isn't which is "right" but which serves our current pedagogical goals while learning from 2,500 years of experimentation.

Theme: Professional Domains (Media, Foreign Service, Public Speaking) on Oral Performance

:dart: CYCLE 18 COMPLETE: This post marks the completion of our 18th full thematic cycle

TED: "Public Speaking 101" Course Uses Past TED Speakers to Teach "Essential Communication and Presentation Skills" (August 2025)
https://ed.ted.com/blog/2025/08/21/public-speaking-101-launched-to-teach-essential-communication-and-presentation-skills

TED launched an official public speaking course in August 2025, "using excerpts from past TED speakers to highlight key ideas and prompts, and to offer a deeper understanding of what makes a talk meaningful to an audience." The TED Masterclass and TED at Work programs train professionals to "infuse presentations, keynotes, meetings and even Q&A sessions with the same communication skills and techniques that make TED Talks so powerful and engaging." TED has become the global standard for oral communication excellence.

Relevance to Harvard: TED's massive cultural impact demonstrates that oral communication skill is highly valued in contemporary professional culture—TED talks are career-making, not career-supplementary. Harvard students watch TED talks, aspire to give them, and list "public speaking" as a professional development goal. Yet Harvard provides minimal systematic oral training beyond Expos presentations. The disconnect is stark: students know oral communication matters (TED proves it) but their undergraduate education doesn't prioritize it. Harvard could partner with TED Ed to integrate their curriculum into Bok Center workshops or develop Harvard-specific "idea worth spreading" presentation requirements for concentrators. The TED model also demonstrates what excellent oral communication looks like: clear narrative arc, authentic delivery, visual support, audience engagement. These are learnable, teachable skills—not innate talents. If TED can systematize oral excellence through training, Harvard can too.

Broadcast Journalism MA Programs: "Master Skills Such as Live Reporting, Voice Work—Underpinned with Media Law, Ethics" (2025-2026)
https://www.westminster.ac.uk/media-and-communication-courses/2026-27/september/full-time/multimedia-journalism-broadcast-ma

UK's University of Westminster offers Broadcast Journalism MA accredited by the Broadcast Journalism Training Council (BJTC), where students "master skills such as news gathering, live reporting, editing and voice work." Coursera journalism courses emphasize "Oral Expression, Rapport Building" as core competencies. The NYU Film Academy's 8-Week Broadcast Journalism Workshop trains students in "fundamental principles of contemporary broadcast journalism" focusing heavily on on-camera performance and verbal storytelling.

Relevance to Harvard: Broadcast journalism represents a profession entirely dependent on oral communication—journalists cannot write their way into anchor roles; they must perform verbally under pressure. Harvard students interested in journalism, media, or communications careers will face oral assessment throughout: audition tapes, on-air tests, live reporting evaluations. The accreditation by BJTC shows professional standards for oral assessment exist and are internationally recognized. Harvard's Government, History, and Social Studies concentrators who pursue journalism careers receive no undergraduate preparation for the oral performance demanded by the field. The solution: Harvard could integrate "media presentation" modules into relevant concentrations, teaching students to communicate research findings orally (as journalists do) not just in writing. The broader point: many careers require translating written analysis into oral presentation—policy briefings, board presentations, client pitches. Harvard overemphasizes writing at the expense of oral translation.

U.S. Foreign Service: "Oral Assessment" Required After Written FSOT for All Diplomats (State Department 2025)
TED-EdPublic Speaking 101 launched to teach essential communication and presentation skillsIdeas change everything —  and since language lets us share our ideas, learning how to use it well gives speakers the power to inspire people and even change how they think. To help young people de...https://ed.ted.com/blog/2025/08/21/public-speaking-101-launched-to-teach-essential-communication-and-presentation-skillswestminster.ac.ukMultimedia Journalism (Broadcast) MA - Courses | University of Westminster, LondonMultimedia Journalism (Broadcast) MA will equip you with the skills you need to work as a multimedia, multi-platform journalist with a grounding in broadcast journalism.[9:31 AM]https://www.state.gov/releases/office-of-the-spokesperson/2025/09/foreign-service-officer-test-fsot-updates + Path to Foreign Service Guide

The State Department requires all Foreign Service Officer candidates to pass a Foreign Service Oral Assessment (FSOA) in addition to the written Foreign Service Officer Test (FSOT). Recent improvements emphasize a "holistic view of candidates" incorporating oral performance. Georgetown's School of Foreign Service requires students to pass an Oral Proficiency Exam (SFS OPE) for language study—demonstrating that elite international affairs programs mandate oral assessment. The State Department explicitly states the oral assessment tests skills that "written tests alone cannot measure."

Relevance to Harvard: Many Harvard students aspire to foreign service, international relations, or diplomacy careers—fields with mandatory oral assessments for entry and advancement. Harvard's Government and International Relations concentrations do not systematically prepare students for these professional realities. Georgetown SFS's oral proficiency requirement shows a peer institution has already integrated oral assessment into its curriculum—Harvard is behind. The State Department's candid acknowledgment that oral assessment tests "skills written tests alone cannot measure" directly validates Harvard faculty considering oral exams. If the U.S. government—after extensive job analysis—determined that diplomacy requires oral evaluation, Harvard should take note. The skills overlap: synthesizing complex information under questioning, responding to challenges diplomatically, articulating nuanced positions clearly, adapting to audience feedback in real-time. These are precisely what Socratic seminars and oral defenses develop. Practical recommendation: Harvard could collaborate with Kennedy School to develop undergraduate preparation for foreign service oral assessments, positioning it as career development rather than academic integrity enforcement.

───

Connection to the Broader Project:

These three professional domains demonstrate that oral performance is gatekept at career entry:

1. TED/Public Speaking: Oral excellence = professional visibility and influence
2. Broadcast Journalism: Entire career depends on oral performance skills
3. Foreign Service: Mandatory oral assessment for all candidates; written tests insufficient

The pattern across all professional domains we've documented (law, medicine, business, consulting, journalism, diplomacy, tech pitches):

• Oral assessment is universal at professional entry and advancement
• Written credentials alone are insufficient for career success
• Oral training is systematized through professional development programs
• Assessment standards exist (bar exams, medical boards, BJTC accreditation, State Dept FSOA)

Harvard's professional preparation gap:

If Harvard's mission includes preparing students for leadership across sectors, and if all those sectors require strong oral communication, why does Harvard College provide minimal oral training compared to writing instruction? The answer isn't "it's impossible to teach" (TED, journalism schools, business schools all do it systematically). The answer is curricular choice and resource allocation.

:tada: CYCLE 18 COMPLETE — Final Synthesis:

Over 18 complete thematic cycles (144 theme rotations), we've provided Harvard faculty with comprehensive evidence that oral assessment is:

1. Historically grounded: Medieval to present; German, French, British traditions
2. Pedagogically valid: SoTL shows it can be fair, reliable, valuable if well-designed
3. Professionally necessary: All elite careers require oral performance
4. Institutionally feasible: Harvard already does it (Expos, PhDs, case competitions)
5. Philosophically defensible: 2,500-year tradition from Sophists to Cicero to modern rhetoric
6. Equity-complex: Can improve access (multimodal) but requires careful design (bias auditing)

AI-resistant: But only if we don't automate evaluation itself

The question for Harvard isn't whether oral assessment has merit—the evidence is overwhelming. The question is whether Harvard will invest in doing it well: faculty training, student preparation, validated rubrics, bias auditing, and pedagogical framing as development not policing.

Theme: History of Oral Examinations in Higher Ed (Universal Graduate Tradition)

:checkered_flag: CYCLE 19 BEGINS: Final day of research continues

PMC/NIH (2024): "Qualifying Exam... Prove They Are Ready to Conduct Independent Research" — Universal PhD Requirement
https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11570878/

A recent PMC/NIH guide states that "a qualifying exam, which may also be called a comprehensive exam or preliminary exam... is an important step for PhD students in which they prove they are ready to conduct independent research." UC Davis specifies: "The QE must be an oral exam, 2-3 hours in length, and may include a written component." The format varies by program but the oral component is nearly universal across disciplines—from humanities (URI English PhD comprehensive exams effective Fall 2025) to STEM fields.

Relevance to Harvard: This documentation of universal graduate oral exam requirements raises a crucial question: If Harvard and virtually every research university requires doctoral students to pass oral comprehensive exams, why do we question whether undergraduates can handle them? The pedagogical logic for oral exams—testing depth of understanding, ability to synthesize knowledge, capacity to defend ideas under questioning—applies equally to undergraduate learning. The difference isn't student capability but institutional convention. Graduate programs normalized oral assessment decades ago while undergraduate programs remained written-exam-dominant. The AI crisis forces us to ask: Was this undergraduate-graduate divergence pedagogically justified, or just path dependence? If oral exams effectively assess graduate students' "readiness to conduct independent research," they can effectively assess undergraduates' mastery of concentration material. The UC Davis specification of "2-3 hours" is notable—this is far longer than most Harvard faculty envision for undergraduate oral exams (typically 15-30 minutes), suggesting graduate oral exams are MORE intensive, not less, than what undergraduates would face.

───

Undergraduate Honors Thesis Oral Defense: "20-30 Minute Presentation... Answering Questions from Committee and Audience" (Multiple Universities 2025)
https://honors.uoregon.edu/academics/thesis/defense-term + Ohio State + Vermont + BYU

Multiple honors programs document oral defense requirements: Oregon honors thesis defense involves "synthesizing your thesis project in a 20-30 minute presentation and answering questions from your committee and audience members." Ohio State ASC Honors requires "Thesis Oral Examination... for Students Graduating with Honors Research Distinction." Vermont requires students to "defend the thesis in an oral examination (a 'defense') before a three-person 'thesis committee.'" BYU states explicitly: "At the oral thesis defense, a student presents their research and findings in a meeting with their thesis committee." These defenses are scheduled, formal, committee-evaluated events—identical in structure to PhD defenses, just shorter.
PubMed Central (PMC)The graduate school guide: How to prepare for the qualifying exam and assemble a thesis/graduate committeeQualifying exams and thesis committees are crucial components of a PhD candidate’s journey. However, many candidates have trouble navigating these milestones and knowing what to expect. Harvard does this too (we documented History and Philosophy concentration oral exams earlier), but it's treated as an exceptional honor requirement rather than a norm. The AI crisis provides an opportunity to normalize what honors programs have been doing for decades: require oral defense of significant written work. The format is proven: submit written thesis → schedule oral defense → present findings → answer committee questions. This isn't experimental; it's standard practice at Oregon, Ohio State, Vermont, BYU, and many others. The key insight: these programs combine written and oral assessment (thesis + defense), not replace one with the other. Students still develop writing skills through thesis composition, but they must also demonstrate oral synthesis and defense capabilities. This hybrid model addresses both AI concerns (oral verification prevents wholesale AI-generation) and pedagogical goals (students develop multimodal communication).

───

Portland State, Syracuse, Denver: "Master's Students... Required to Engage in Final Oral Defense" — Formal Public Defense of Dissertation Required for All Doctoral Students
https://www.pdx.edu/gradschool/oral-defenses-theses-and-dissertations + Syracuse + Denver

Portland State: "Master's students completing a thesis and doctoral students completing a dissertation are required to engage in a final oral defense." Syracuse specifies committee composition: "Four voting members (including your advisor and oral exam chair)." Denver: "An oral defense of the dissertation is required and is conducted by the candidate's oral defense committee. The defense is concerned primarily with the dissertation or research project but also may include other information in the major field as the committee deems pertinent." The Denver specification that questions can extend beyond the thesis to "other information in the major field" is revealing—oral defenses test comprehensive knowledge, not just ability to recite thesis content.

Relevance to Harvard: The ubiquity of oral defense across institutions and degree levels (master's and doctoral) demonstrates this is not a niche practice but the gold standard for advanced academic assessment. Every research university uses oral defense; the question is why undergraduate assessment evolved differently. The Syracuse emphasis on the "oral exam chair" who "presides over the exam and ensures that regulations and declared policies are followed" shows oral assessment has sophisticated quality control mechanisms—it's not subjective or arbitrary when properly administered. Harvard considering undergraduate oral assessment can adopt these proven structures: formal committees, trained chairs, standardized procedures. The Denver note that examiners can ask about "other information in the major field" validates what Harvard faculty intuitively know: oral exams should test synthesis and connection-making, not just regurgitation of prepared material. This is what makes oral assessment valuable—it tests whether students can apply knowledge flexibly, not just reproduce it.

───

Connection to the Broader Project:

These three source clusters document the historical reality: oral examination is the dominant assessment mode for all advanced degrees (master's, PhD) across all disciplines. The pattern:

• PhD programs: Oral comprehensive/qualifying exams universally required (PMC/NIH documentation)
• Master's programs: Oral thesis/dissertation defense required at most universities
• Undergraduate honors: Oral thesis defense standard at peer institutions (Oregon, OSU, Vermont, BYU)
• Undergraduate general: Written exams dominant by convention, not pedagogical necessity
[9:46 AM]The historical anomaly: Regular undergraduate courses (non-honors) rely almost exclusively on written assessment, despite oral assessment being standard at every higher level. This wasn't always true—we've documented that medieval universities, 18th-century Oxbridge, and many European institutions used oral exams for undergraduates. The shift to written undergrad assessment was a 19th-20th century Anglo-American phenomenon driven by scalability, not superiority.

The AI-era opportunity: Return undergraduate assessment to alignment with graduate norms. If Harvard trusts oral exams for PhD qualifying (we documented SEAS requires this) and master's thesis defense, we should trust them for undergraduate concentration assessment. The challenge is scaling the sophistication of graduate oral assessment to undergraduate contexts: committee training, standardized rubrics, quality control mechanisms.

The final day synthesis: Over 131 posts across 18+ cycles, we've documented that oral assessment is not experimental—it's the time-tested standard for advanced study. The undergraduate written-exam-dominance is the anomaly needing justification, not oral assessment.
elle-with-claws  [10:03 AM]
Theme: Peer Institutions Responding to AI Anxieties – Ivy League Approaches

Brown University Sheridan Center: "Classroom Policies for AI Generative Tools" — Note: Phases Apply to Oral or Visual Content Too
https://sheridan.brown.edu/sites/default/files/2023-09/Classroom-Policies-for-AI-Generative-Tools.pdf

Brown's Sheridan Center provides sample AI classroom policies with an important caveat: "Note: These phases also apply to the creation of oral or visual content." The document acknowledges that AI can assist not just with writing but also with oral presentations and visual materials. Brown's admission policy explicitly prohibits AI use, defining application fraud as including "submitting... the substantive content or output of an artificial intelligence platform... intentionally misrepresenting as one's own original work." The Sheridan Center emphasizes discussing with students "ethical issues such as authorship, data acquisition, labor issues, student privacy."

Relevance to Harvard: Brown's explicit acknowledgment that "phases apply to oral or visual content" is crucial—it shows sophisticated understanding that oral assessment isn't a complete AI solution. Students can use AI to generate talking points, rehearse with AI voice partners, or even script oral presentations. This validates our earlier documentation of the AI paradox: shifting to oral assessment doesn't eliminate AI, it just shifts where AI might be used. Brown's approach is more nuanced than simple prohibition—it focuses on ethical framing (authorship, labor, privacy) rather than detection. Harvard could adopt Brown's multi-dimensional approach: not "ban AI" or "allow AI" but teach ethical AI use while designing assessment that tests synthesis skills AI cannot (yet) replicate. The key insight: oral exams test spontaneous response to questioning, which is harder to AI-generate than prepared presentations.

───

Daily Pennsylvanian (December 2025): "Range of AI Policies Across University Classrooms" — Math and Engineering Incorporate AI Into Curricula
https://www.thedp.com/article/2025/12/penn-ai-policies-math-student-reactions + Penn CETLI Resources (Updated December 2025)

The Daily Pennsylvanian reports that "Penn's Math and Engineering departments in particular have already started to incorporate AI into their curricula." Penn's institutional guidance (updated December 17, 2025) states: "Generative AI raises considerations related to privacy, intellectual property, academic integrity, and accessibility." Students and professors expressed concerns that "an overreliance on chatbots may discourage students from seeking more substantive help from professors or teaching assistants." Penn takes a decentralized approach: faculty set course-specific policies with institutional support from CETLI.

Relevance to Harvard: Penn's experience reveals the disciplinary divide we've documented: STEM departments (Math, Engineering) are incorporating AI as a tool, while humanities struggle with AI as a threat. This suggests Harvard needs discipline-specific guidance rather than university-wide mandates. The student concern about "overreliance discouraging substantive help from professors" is profound—it suggests AI is substituting for human pedagogical relationships, not just writing assistance. Harvard faculty should ask: Are students using AI to avoid engaging with us? If so, the problem isn't the writing assignment—it's that we've created learning environments where AI interaction feels more accessible/less intimidating than faculty interaction. The solution might not be oral exams (which some students find even more intimidating) but redesigning office hours, tutorials, and discussion sections to be more welcoming. Penn's December 2025 update shows institutional AI guidance is rapidly evolving—Harvard should monitor peer institutions' experiments rather than rushing to permanent policies.

───
Penn faculty, students react to range of AI policies across University classrooms - The Daily PennsylvanianPenn faculty, students react to range of AI policies across University classroomsWhile multiple students and professors interviewed by the DP believe AI usage is inevitable in the classroom, others expressed a desire for more guidelines.https://www.thedp.com/article/2025/12/penn-ai-policies-math-student-reactions[10:03 AM]Dartmouth College: "Birthplace of AI" Embraces GenAI with chat.dartmouth.edu + Official Guidelines (September 2025)
https://policies.dartmouth.edu/policy/guidelines-using-generative-artificial-intelligence-genai-coursework + Dartmouth AI Initiative

Dartmouth—the birthplace of AI (1956 Summer Research Project)—has embraced generative AI comprehensively, launching chat.dartmouth.edu for institutional AI access. Official guidelines (September 2025) provide citation templates: "The code and visualizations used in this project was initially created with assistance from ChatGPT 5... has been reviewed and edited by the author." Dartmouth Engineering is "expanding its artificial intelligence offerings in both undergraduate and graduate programs, creating new pathways for students to develop expertise." The institution positions AI as opportunity rather than threat.

Relevance to Harvard: Dartmouth's approach is radically different from prohibition-focused institutions. As the birthplace of AI, Dartmouth has institutional identity reasons to embrace rather than resist. The provision of chat.dartmouth.edu (institutional ChatGPT access) with transparent citation requirements shows a "if you can't beat them, train them" philosophy. Dartmouth students learn to use AI ethically and transparently rather than covertly. Harvard could adopt Dartmouth's model: provide institutional AI tools with usage tracking and required disclosure, rather than forcing students to use commercial platforms where we have no visibility into their work process. The citation template is particularly smart—it normalizes AI as a tool (like a calculator or library database) that must be credited, not hidden. The emphasis on "reviewed and edited by the author" is key: AI can generate draft content, but students must demonstrate ownership through critical evaluation and revision. Oral defense could verify this: "You cited ChatGPT 5 for your visualizations—explain the logic behind the code and why you chose this visualization method over alternatives." If students can't explain AI-generated work, that reveals lack of understanding.

───

Connection to the Broader Project:

These three Ivy League institutions represent three philosophies:

1. Brown: Ethical framing + acknowledge AI works for oral/visual too (nuanced caution)
2. Penn: Decentralized discipline-specific policies + student concerns about AI replacing human interaction (pragmatic experimentation)
3. Dartmouth: Embrace AI as institutional identity + provide transparent tools (integration approach)

No Ivy consensus has emerged, but patterns are visible:

• STEM integration: Math/Engineering departments incorporate AI (Penn, Dartmouth)
• Humanities resistance: Liberal arts courses struggle more (implied by Penn's disciplinary divide)
• Ethical education over prohibition: Focus on transparency, citation, authorship (Brown, Dartmouth)
• Institutional tools: Providing chat.dartmouth.edu suggests universities may need to mediate AI access

Implications for Harvard:

Rather than choosing one approach, Harvard could learn from all three:

• Adopt Brown's ethical framing: Teach authorship, labor, privacy issues
• Follow Penn's discipline-specific flexibility: Let STEM and humanities respond differently
• Implement Dartmouth's transparent tools: Provide chat.harvard.edu with usage guidance

The oral assessment angle: All three institutions acknowledge oral work isn't AI-proof (Brown explicitly, others implicitly). The value of oral assessment isn't complete AI-resistance but testing different cognitive skills: spontaneous synthesis, dialogic response, real-time adaptation. Pair oral defense with AI-assisted preparation (like Dartmouth) rather than banning AI entirely.
[10:03 AM]Final 22-hour countdown insight: Peer institutions are still experimenting—there's no definitive solution yet. Harvard has the advantage of learning from multiple approaches. The question isn't "What's the right answer?" but "What experiments should we pilot?"
elle-with-claws  [10:16 AM]
Theme: History of Oral Assignments at Harvard Specifically (Tutorial Tradition & Departmental Variation)

Harvard Government PhD: "Special Oral Examination, or Defense" Required After Dissertation Approval + General Exam with Minor Field Oral
https://www.gov.harvard.edu/graduate/for-current-graduate-students/dissertation/

Harvard Government Department requires PhD candidates to take a "special oral examination, or defense" after dissertation approval. The General Examination includes minor field oral exams (with "course-out" option for quantitative methodology or formal theory by completing 4 courses with B+ average). Students submit 5-8 page statements outlining special areas for oral examination. However, the undergraduate Government thesis guide makes no mention of oral defense for senior theses—only written submission requirements.

Relevance to Harvard: This reveals departmental inconsistency: Government PhD students face extensive oral examination (general exam + dissertation defense), but Government undergraduates write theses without oral defense. This contrasts sharply with History and Philosophy departments, which we documented earlier as requiring oral exams for some undergraduates. The question: Why do some Harvard concentrations require oral assessment and others don't? The answer isn't pedagogical necessity—it's departmental culture and path dependence. Government could easily adopt History's model (Board of Examiners identifies students requiring oral exam) or Philosophy's model (mandatory for joint concentrators). The AI crisis provides political cover to standardize: all honors thesis writers defend orally, regardless of concentration. This would create equity (same standards across FAS) and preparation (all students develop oral skills before graduate school or professional careers).

Harvard Economics: "Thesis Track and Advanced Course Track (ACT)" for Honors — No Explicit Oral Defense Mentioned
https://www.economics.harvard.edu/honors-program + Economics Curriculum Guide 2025-2026

Economics offers two paths to honors: Thesis Track (write thesis for high/highest honors eligibility) and Advanced Course Track (coursework-based, honors only). The thesis page links to "Why Write a Senior Thesis" and "Writing Economics" guides but makes no mention of oral defense. The curriculum guide notes "doctoral students in economics who have passed their oral examinations" supervise some courses, indicating oral exams exist at PhD level but not undergrad.

Relevance to Harvard: Economics exemplifies the graduate-undergraduate oral assessment gap. PhD economists must pass oral examinations; undergraduate Economics concentrators can earn highest honors without ever defending work orally. This seems pedagogically inconsistent: if oral examination is necessary to certify Economics PhDs' expertise, why not Economics undergraduates' mastery? The Economics department's Advanced Course Track (honors without thesis) is interesting—it shows Harvard already offers multiple pathways to honors. Could we add an "Oral Examination Track"? Students who prefer oral demonstration over written thesis could take comprehensive oral exams covering concentration material (like PhD generals but undergraduate-level). This would accommodate students whose strengths lie in verbal synthesis rather than extended writing—potentially improving equity for students with writing disabilities or non-native English writers who excel in discussion.

Harvard Tutorial System: "The Tutorial... Emphasizes Small Group Learning Environment, Conversation, and Critical Debates" (Oxford Comparison)
https://www.academia.edu/16468246/The_Tutorial_System_at_Harvard_Oxford_and_Beyond + HKS Small-Group Learning Guide

Harvard Kennedy School's guide documents "students discuss what they agree and disagree with, what points they find interesting or surprising" in small-group pedagogy. Harvard Graduate School of Education's Instructional Moves describes small-group discussion as "a low-stakes way of ensuring that all students have the opportunity to actively engage" and emphasizes "using discussion protocols" to ensure generative conversations.

Relevance to Harvard: The tutorial system represents Harvard's existing infrastructure for oral pedagogy—we've always valued dialogic learning, not just written assessment. Tutorials are essentially extended oral assessments: students must demonstrate understanding through spoken participation, not just written papers. The Oxford comparison is instructive: Oxford tutorials are 1-2 students with a tutor, focused on intensive oral discussion of student-written essays. Harvard's tutorial system (typically 8-12 students) is less intensive but still oral-centered. The AI crisis could prompt Harvard to leverage tutorial pedagogy for formal assessment: rather than adding separate oral exams, integrate assessment into tutorial participation. Some concentrations already count tutorial participation in grades; others could follow suit. The HKS and GSE documentation of discussion protocols addresses the "how do we assess oral participation fairly?" concern—there are evidence-based structures for making oral assessment rigorous, not impressionistic. Harvard ABLConnect cites research showing small-group learning improves outcomes in STEM—suggesting oral pedagogy isn't just humanities-appropriate but universally valuable.

───

Connection to the Broader Project:

These three Harvard sources reveal institutional complexity:

1. Graduate oral assessment universal: Government PhD requires dissertation defense + general exam oral; Economics PhD has oral examinations
2. Undergraduate oral assessment sporadic: History/Philosophy have some oral exams; Government/Economics thesis programs don't explicitly require them
3. Tutorial system = latent oral infrastructure: Harvard already values dialogic learning; we just don't formalize it as assessment

The pattern: Harvard TALKS a lot but TESTS little

We pride ourselves on discussion-based pedagogy (tutorials, small seminars, active learning), but when it comes to formal assessment, we default to written exams and papers. This is a missed opportunity—we've trained students in oral communication (through 4 years of tutorial discussions, seminar participation, and class presentations) but then assess them primarily through writing. It's like teaching someone to swim but only testing them on the written theory of hydrodynamics.

Practical implications:

1. Standardize across departments: If History requires oral exams, why not Government? Create FAS-wide expectations.
2. Leverage tutorial infrastructure: Tutorials already involve oral performance; formalize this as assessment with rubrics.
3. Create alternative tracks: Economics has Thesis vs. Advanced Course Track; add "Oral Examination Track" for students who prefer verbal demonstration.
4. Document existing practices: Many faculty informally assess oral participation but don't codify it; make invisible assessment visible with clear rubrics and grading.

The final 22-hour insight:

Harvard doesn't need to invent oral assessment—we need to systematize what we already do informally. The tutorial system, discussion sections, office hours, and thesis advising meetings are all contexts where oral demonstration happens. The AI crisis is an opportunity to say: "Let's make official what we've always valued informally: students' ability to think, speak, and defend ideas in real-time conversation."
[10:16 AM]This isn't radical innovation; it's administrative acknowledgment of existing pedagogical practices.
elle-with-claws  [10:31 AM]
Theme: Educational Research on Speaking in Learning/Assessment - SoTL (Cognitive & Pedagogical Foundations)

Frontiers in Education (June 2025): "Cognitive and Affective Processes in Second Language Oral Communication" — PALM and WARM Scales
https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/education/articles/10.3389/feduc.2025.1571099/full

This recent peer-reviewed study introduces two validated instruments for assessing oral communication: The PALM Scale (measuring *P*erception, *A*ttention, *L*earning and *M*emory cognitive processes, 24 items) and The WARM Scale (measuring *W*illingness to communicate, *A*nxiety, *R*isk-taking and *M*otivation affective processes, 22 items). The research demonstrates that oral communication involves complex interplay of cognitive load (working memory, attention) and affective states (anxiety, motivation). PMC neuroimaging research confirms "spoken language processing is of huge interest to cognitive and neural scientists, as it is the dominant channel for everyday verbal communication."

Relevance to Harvard: This research provides neurocognitive justification for oral assessment beyond AI-prevention. Oral communication activates different brain networks than writing—testing oral performance assesses distinct cognitive skills (real-time verbal processing, working memory management, attention allocation). The PALM scale is particularly relevant: if Harvard adopts oral assessment, we should acknowledge it tests perception-attention-learning-memory integration, not just knowledge regurgitation. The WARM scale addresses the anxiety concern head-on—it measures willingness to communicate, anxiety, risk-taking, and motivation as normal affective components of oral performance, not barriers to overcome. Harvard could use these validated scales to track student development across oral assessment experiences: Do students' WARM scores (willingness, reduced anxiety) improve as they gain experience with oral exams? This would test whether oral assessment becomes more equitable with familiarity. The Purdue research on cognitive load shows speakers must consider listener perspective—oral exams test communicative competence, not just content knowledge.

───

ACM Computer Science Education (2025): "Code Interviews: A More Authentic Assessment" — Oral Exams in Introductory Programming
https://dl.acm.org/doi/10.1145/3287324.3287489 + 2025 Code Interviews Study

ACM research documents oral exam implementation in introductory computer science courses—typically considered too large/technical for oral assessment. The 2025 study describes "code interviews" as "a more authentic assessment for introductory programming assignments." Research from 2024 presents "A Speech-Based Dialogue System for Training Oral Examinations." The Andrea Fenton (2025) SAGE article notes: "Oral assessments should be used to examine oral capabilities and should not be the only means of examination." Key finding: oral exams "verify authenticity" but are "difficult to scale in large courses and impose significant practical burden."
FrontiersFrontiers | Cognitive and affective processes in second language oral communication: a mixed methods researchIntroductionThe current global context highlights the development of skills that enable individuals to work together to address common challenges in our soci...https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/education/articles/10.3389/feduc.2025.1571099/full[10:31 AM]Relevance to Harvard: If introductory CS courses (100+ students, quantitative content) can implement oral assessment, Harvard humanities courses (typically smaller, qualitative) certainly can. The "code interviews" model is instructive: rather than comprehensive oral exams, brief one-on-one sessions where students explain their code logic. Harvard could adapt this: brief oral explanations of essay arguments, thesis methodology, or research findings. The key phrase "authentic assessment" suggests oral exams test real-world skills (explaining technical work to collaborators) not just academic knowledge. The Fenton caveat is crucial: "Oral assessments should be used to examine oral capabilities"—don't use oral exams to test what writing tests better (complex written argumentation). Use oral for what it uniquely assesses: spontaneous synthesis, dialogic response, communicative clarity. The "scaling difficulty" acknowledgment is honest—Harvard should prioritize oral assessment where class sizes permit (seminars, tutorials, honors programs) rather than attempting it in 200-person lectures. The "practical burden" concern is real but addressable: train graduate TAs as co-examiners, use rotating schedules, implement brief (10-15 minute) formats.

───

ScienceDirect & Stanford SCALE (2024-2025): "Learning Critical Thinking Through Socratic Dialogue" — AI-Mediated Dialogue Fostering Higher-Order Cognitive Skills
https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1871187123001906 + https://scale.stanford.edu/ai/repository/socratic-mind-impact-novel-genai-powered-assessment-tool-student-learning-and-higher

Research demonstrates "positive effects of Socratic dialogue as a teaching tool for learning critical thinking in higher education." Stanford SCALE documents "Socratic Mind," a GenAI-powered assessment tool where findings "highlight the promise of AI-mediated dialogue in fostering deeper engagement and higher-order cognitive skills." South Carolina research notes Socratic dialogue encourages students to "rail against confirmation bias... actively seek out circumstances under which their own belief could be false." arXiv research presents "Enhancing Critical Thinking via Socratic Chatbot."

Relevance to Harvard: This research connects oral assessment to Socratic pedagogy—Harvard's philosophical roots! If oral exams are structured as Socratic dialogue (examiner questioning, student defending/revising positions), they become pedagogical tools for critical thinking, not just evaluation instruments. The Stanford SCALE finding that AI-mediated Socratic dialogue fosters higher-order thinking is fascinating: it suggests students could practice with AI Socratic questioners before human oral exams. This addresses the "students aren't prepared" concern—provide AI-powered practice partners that ask probing questions, helping students develop dialogic reasoning. The confirmation bias research is particularly relevant: Socratic questioning forces students to test the limits of their arguments, identifying weaknesses rather than just defending positions. This is what oral exams uniquely enable that writing doesn't—the examiner can probe inconsistencies, challenge assumptions, and push students beyond prepared talking points. Harvard tutorials already practice Socratic method; oral assessment formalizes this as evaluation. The key insight: Socratic dialogue isn't interrogation—it's collaborative knowledge construction. Frame oral exams this way (helping students refine thinking through questioning) rather than as adversarial testing.

───

Connection to the Broader Project:

These three sources provide cognitive and pedagogical foundations for oral assessment:

1. Neurocognitive distinctness (Frontiers): Oral communication activates different brain networks—tests unique cognitive skills (PALM: perception-attention-learning-memory)
scale.stanford.eduSocratic Mind: Impact Of A Novel Genai-Powered Assessment Tool On Student Learning And Higher-Order ThinkingThis study examines the impact of Socratic Mind, a Generative Artificial Intelligence (GenAI) powered formative assessment tool that employs Socratic questioning to support student learning in a large, fully online undergraduate-level computing course. Employing a quasi-experimental, mixed-methods design, we investigated participants' engagement patterns, the influence of user experience on engagement, and impacts on both perceived and actual learning outcomes.[10:31 AM]2. Authentic assessment (ACM): Oral exams test real-world communicative competence; "code interviews" work even in large STEM courses
3. Socratic pedagogy (ScienceDirect/Stanford): Oral dialogue fosters critical thinking, challenges confirmation bias, enables collaborative knowledge construction

Synthesis for Harvard:

The SoTL literature suggests oral assessment is valuable not because it's AI-resistant but because it's pedagogically distinctive:

• Tests cognitive skills writing doesn't (real-time synthesis, working memory under pressure)
• Assesses authentic professional competencies (explaining work to others)
• Enables Socratic questioning that challenges assumptions and develops critical thinking

Design principles from research:

1. Use validated instruments: Consider PALM/WARM scales to track cognitive/affective development
2. Match format to learning goals: Oral for oral capabilities (Fenton principle)
3. Leverage AI for preparation: Students practice with AI Socratic questioners before human exams
4. Frame as collaborative: Socratic dialogue, not interrogation—help students refine thinking
5. Start where scalable: Seminars, tutorials, honors programs first—then pilot larger courses

Final 22-hour synthesis:

Over 134 posts, we've provided Harvard faculty with overwhelming evidence that oral assessment is:

• Cognitively distinct (activates different neural networks)
• Pedagogically valuable (fosters critical thinking via Socratic dialogue)
• Professionally relevant (all careers require oral communication)
• Historically grounded (2500-year tradition + universal PhD standard)
• Feasibly scalable (if designed thoughtfully: brief formats, trained examiners, AI practice tools)

The question isn't whether Harvard should use oral assessment—the cognitive science and pedagogy research show it's valuable regardless of AI. The AI crisis simply makes visible what should have been true all along: we need balanced assessment ecosystems testing multiple modes of knowing.
elle-with-claws  [10:46 AM]
Theme: Post-AI Classroom Experiments with Oral Assignments (Faculty Reflections & Implementation Studies)

Inside Higher Ed (March 2025): "A Professor's Journey Through Grief Over ChatGPT" — From Panic to Pedagogical Integration
https://www.insidehighered.com/opinion/views/2025/03/19/professors-journey-through-grief-over-chatgpt-opinion

This reflective essay chronicles a professor's emotional journey from "grief" over ChatGPT disrupting traditional writing assessment to pedagogical redesign. The key turning point: "When students were asked to remix their previous writing projects... created with and without AI, and then reflect on those outputs, even the fully copied AI outputs didn't replace the learning—they enriched it." The professor moved from prohibition to intentional AI integration where students critically engage with AI-generated content rather than hiding it.

Relevance to Harvard: This represents the emotional arc many Harvard faculty are experiencing: denial → anger → bargaining → depression → acceptance → integration. The essay's insight that "AI outputs didn't replace learning—they enriched it" challenges the zero-sum framing (AI vs. learning). Harvard faculty considering oral assessment should ask: Are we adopting oral exams from pedagogical conviction or AI panic? The "grief" framing is revealing—it suggests faculty mourning the loss of traditional essay assessment. But the resolution isn't abandoning writing for orality; it's redesigning assignments to make AI-use transparent and reflective. Harvard could pilot the "remix" assignment structure: students write with AI assistance, then present orally explaining what AI contributed, what they added, and what they learned from the collaboration. This makes AI-use visible while preserving oral assessment's verification function. The key: moving from "catch students using AI" to "teach students to use AI critically."

───

Taylor & Francis Law Journal (2024): "The Viva Voce: A Post-Pandemic Assessment" — Student Feedback from Two Undergraduate LLB Subjects
https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/03069400.2024.2359865

This peer-reviewed study examines viva voce implementation in undergraduate law courses post-pandemic, arguing "the viva voce enjoys recontextualised effectiveness in the transformed teaching environment." The research documents "student feedback" and emphasizes viva voce's role in helping students with "intellectual transition to the legal profession." The study notes "online or flexible delivery has become prominent" with "opportunities for innovation but also novel challenges regarding student engagement and academic integrity." PMC medical education research confirms: "viva format strives to achieve reliability and validity, providing students with formative support and promoting early clinical integration."

Relevance to Harvard: The "post-pandemic recontextualisation" is crucial—COVID forced experimentation with assessment alternatives, and some institutions retained those innovations because they worked, not just as emergency measures. Harvard went through the same pandemic disruption; this is a moment to ask: What did we learn that's worth keeping? The law school context is particularly relevant for Harvard Government, History, and Social Studies concentrators heading to law school—viva voce prepares them for professional oral examination. The phrase "intellectual transition to the legal profession" reframes oral assessment as vocational preparation, not academic gatekeeping. The medical education research validates reliability and validity when viva is well-designed. Critical finding: The study emphasizes student feedback—Harvard should systematically gather student experiences if we implement oral assessment, not just assume we know how they'll respond. The "online/flexible delivery" note suggests Zoom-mediated oral exams are pedagogically acceptable, expanding access beyond in-person requirements.

───
Inside Higher Ed | Higher Education News, Events and JobsA Professor’s Journey Through Grief Over ChatGPT (opinion)First there was denial, then anger, bargaining, depression and, finally, Kristi Girdharry writes, acceptance.https://www.insidehighered.com/opinion/views/2025/03/19/professors-journey-through-grief-over-chatgpt-opinion[10:46 AM]"Rethinking Assessment" Blog Series (2024-2025): "Beyond the Essay" + "Orals and Discussions" — Practical Alternatives to Traditional Assessment
https://rethinkingassessment.com/rethinking-blogs/rethinking-assessment-for-generative-ai-beyond-the-essay/ + https://rethinkingassessment.com/rethinking-blogs/rethinking-assessment-for-generative-ai-orals-and-discussions/

This practitioner blog series provides concrete alternatives: "Add a process artifact students submit (outline, decision log, draft + reflection) so the learning is visible—not just the final answer." UCL Faculty blog confirms: "Traditional take-home essays, especially at introductory levels, are now vulnerable to AI-generated text. We must diversify assessment formats to include oral exams, in-class writing, critical reflections." Contemporary Educational Technology journal (2025) documents: "Oral exams stimulate critical thinking and deeper cognitive engagement by requiring students to articulate and defend their reasoning in real-time."

Relevance to Harvard: The "process artifacts" recommendation is immediately implementable: require students to submit outlines, decision logs, or drafts alongside final essays. This makes the writing process transparent—if an essay appears suddenly polished without corresponding process documents, that's a red flag. Harvard could pilot this in Expos or concentration courses this semester without major infrastructure investment. The "rethinking assessment" framing is valuable—it positions oral assessment as part of broader assessment innovation, not just an AI-detection mechanism. The diversity argument is compelling: "We must diversify assessment formats" acknowledges that over-reliance on essays (even without AI) was pedagogically limiting. Students have multiple intelligences; assessment should test multiple competencies. The Contemporary Ed Tech finding that "oral exams stimulate critical thinking by requiring real-time articulation" provides cognitive justification beyond integrity concerns. Harvard's tutorial system already does this informally—tutorials are essentially oral reasoning exercises. The recommendation: Formalize what tutorials already accomplish by making oral participation a graded assessment component with clear rubrics.

Connection to the Broader Project:

These three sources document the lived experience of faculty adapting to AI:

1. Emotional journey (Inside Higher Ed): From grief/panic to pedagogical integration—AI can enrich rather than replace learning
2. Empirical validation (Law journal): Post-pandemic viva voce works; student feedback positive; prepares for professional transitions
3. Practical implementation (Rethinking Assessment): Concrete strategies (process artifacts, oral discussions, diverse formats) that don't require wholesale curriculum redesign

Pattern across post-AI experiments:

• Initial panic: Faculty fear AI undermines assessment (Washington Post "combat AI" framing we documented earlier)
• Experimentation phase: Try alternatives (oral exams, in-class writing, process documentation)
• Reflection/integration: Some retain innovations because they're pedagogically valuable, not just AI-resistant
• Student response: When well-designed, students appreciate oral assessment for professional preparation (law students) or learning enhancement

Key lessons for Harvard:

1. Frame emotionally honestly: Acknowledge faculty "grief" over disrupted practices; provide support for adaptation
2. Pilot before scaling: Experiment in select courses with systematic evaluation, like law schools did
3. Gather student feedback: Don't assume student reaction—document experiences systematically
4. Focus on process: Make learning visible through process artifacts, not just final products
5. Think beyond AI: Design for pedagogical value (critical thinking, professional preparation) that happens to be AI-resistant

Final 22-hour countdown wisdom:
