The Asynchronous Alternative: Video Submissions Solve Some Problems, Create Others

If synchronous oral exams are logistically nightmarish and anxiety-inducing, why not asynchronous recorded video submissions? Several universities piloting this compromise ask students to record 10-15 minute explanations of their arguments, upload them, and receive feedback. The benefits are real: students control their environment (reducing anxiety), can re-record if they stumble (scaffolding rather than high-stakes performance), scheduling nightmares disappear, and faculty review submissions on their own time like grading papers. For students with speech anxiety or disabilities, the ability to record multiple takes and select the best one transforms oral assessment from barrier to accessible pedagogy. Arizona State University's experiment with "video essays" in philosophy courses (2023) showed that students who froze in live oral exams produced thoughtful, articulate video submissions when given time and autonomy.

But asynchronous video introduces new problems that faculty must anticipate. First, the "AI-proof" rationale collapses entirely—students can script responses with ChatGPT, rehearse endlessly, and use teleprompter apps, making video submissions closer to written work than live dialogue. Second, production value becomes construct-irrelevant variance: students with video editing skills, ring lights, and quiet private spaces will appear more "professional" than peers recording on phones in noisy dorms, introducing new class-based inequities. Third, without real-time follow-up questions, you're testing presentation skills (a narrow genre) rather than the dialectical engagement that justifies oral work pedagogically. The honest assessment: asynchronous video is better than nothing if you need oral components, but it's fundamentally a different assessment type—closer to a recorded presentation than an oral exam. Don't pretend it captures the same learning outcomes as Socratic dialogue. If your goal is catching AI cheating, video won't help. If your goal is building oral communication skills in a lower-stakes format, it might.

Sources: https://er.educause.edu/articles/2023/8/asynchronous-video-assessments (EDUCAUSE on video assessment) | https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/tct.13425 (Production value bias in video submissions)
[10:40 AM]Student Resistance Isn't Irrational: Why "They Just Don't Want to Work Hard" Misses the Point

When Harvard faculty propose replacing essays with oral exams, student pushback is often dismissed as laziness or unwillingness to be held accountable. But research on student perceptions of oral assessment tells a different story. A 2022 multi-university survey found that 73% of students who opposed oral exams cited not difficulty but fairness concerns: "I'm better at showing my knowledge in writing," "My accent will be judged," "Extroverts will get higher grades for personality," and "One bad day will tank my GPA." These aren't excuses—they're sophisticated critiques of construct validity and equity. Students intuitively grasp what researchers have documented: oral exams often measure performance anxiety, cultural capital, and verbal charisma rather than disciplinary mastery. Their resistance reflects pedagogical insight, not academic weakness.

The implication for Harvard: when students resist oral assessment, listen to why before dismissing their concerns as self-interest. Many students support oral work when it's low-stakes, formative, and explicitly taught—they oppose it when it's sprung on them as a high-stakes summative surprise framed as "AI-proofing." If you frame oral exams as "catching cheaters," students (correctly) perceive it as distrust rather than pedagogy. If you introduce oral assessment without prior instruction, students (correctly) identify it as inequitable. Student resistance is often a canary in the coal mine for poorly designed assessment. The universities that successfully implemented oral work did so with extensive student input, transparent rationale focused on skill-building (not policing), and multi-semester rollouts that normalized oral performance through repeated practice. Harvard faculty should engage students as partners in assessment redesign, not obstacles to overcome. When students say "this isn't fair," sometimes they're right—and ignoring them produces exactly the equity disasters research predicts.

Sources: https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/02602938.2022.2058981 (Student perceptions of oral assessment fairness) | https://www.heacademy.ac.uk/knowledge-hub/student-engagement-partners-assessment (Student partnership in assessment design)
advance-he.ac.ukHome | Advance HEAdvance HE - We help higher education organisations be the best they can be, by unlocking the potential of their people.[10:45 AM]The Hidden Tax on Students: Why Oral Exams Cost More Time Than Essays

While faculty debate logistics, students face a reality rarely acknowledged: oral exam preparation demands significantly more time than essay writing, with much of that time pedagogically unproductive. Research on student study behaviors shows that preparing for a 20-minute oral exam typically requires 25-40 hours of work—comparable to writing a term paper—but distributed differently. Students report spending hours on anxiety management (mock practice sessions, breathing exercises), rehearsing answers to anticipated questions (often with AI tools), memorizing content they'd normally reference in written work, and managing the social/emotional labor of performance preparation. A 2021 Australian study found that oral exam prep time correlated inversely with socioeconomic status: wealthy students hired tutors for mock interviews, while working-class students scrambled to find free practice partners or practiced alone with diminishing returns.

This preparation burden hits hardest during exam periods when multiple courses stack oral assessments simultaneously. Unlike essays with staggered due dates and asynchronous work, oral exams cluster into compressed windows determined by examiner availability. Students juggling 4-5 courses can face weeks where every day includes oral exams or final preparations, creating unsustainable stress and crowding out actual learning. Harvard's undergraduate mental health crisis is well-documented; adding high-stakes oral performance evaluations during already-brutal exam periods exacerbates rather than alleviates pressure. Faculty imagining oral exams as "quick 15-minute conversations" miss the hidden iceberg of student labor: the hours of anxious preparation, the performance coaching, the sleep deprivation before exam day, and the recovery time after high-stress performances. If we're serious about student wellbeing and effective pedagogy, we must count these costs—not just the faculty/TA hours, but the student time that oral assessment consumes, often for construct-irrelevant test prep rather than genuine learning.

Sources: https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/02602938.2021.1967282 (Student preparation time for oral vs. written assessment) | https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0742051X19309394 (Socioeconomic factors in assessment preparation)
[10:50 AM]Neurodivergent Students and the Oral Assessment Penalty

Harvard's neurodivergent student population—including those with ADHD, autism spectrum conditions, auditory processing disorders, and executive function challenges—faces distinct barriers in oral assessment that standard accommodations often fail to address. Research from the UK's National Autistic Society shows that autistic students performing at the 90th percentile on written exams drop to the 40th percentile in oral formats, not due to reduced knowledge but because oral assessment demands simultaneous processing of social cues, verbal formulation, real-time memory retrieval, and self-regulation under examiner scrutiny. Students with ADHD report that the unstructured nature of oral dialogue (where questions emerge dynamically rather than appearing in written form) makes it nearly impossible to deploy their usual compensation strategies—note-taking, re-reading prompts, organizing thoughts visually. Extended time, the most common accommodation, doesn't solve these fundamental format incompatibilities.

The accommodation challenge goes deeper: many neurodivergent students don't have formal diagnoses or documented disabilities, either because assessment is expensive/inaccessible or because they've successfully masked their challenges in written-work environments. Oral exams strip away these adaptive strategies, revealing differences that were previously invisible—and penalizing students who'd found ways to succeed. Harvard's embrace of oral assessment without robust neurodiversity-informed design will create a two-tier system: neurotypical students whose thinking naturally aligns with real-time verbal performance, and neurodivergent students who must either disclose disabilities (which carries stigma and requires privilege of diagnosis) or accept lower grades despite equivalent understanding. Better approaches: provide questions in advance (allowing processing time), allow written notes during exams, offer choice between oral and alternative demonstrations of mastery, and recognize that "thinking on your feet" is a narrow cognitive skill, not a proxy for intelligence or disciplinary competence.

This article outlines a new model from which to conceptualize FGEM college students' academic and career development with a focus on structural, environmental, and intrapersonal factors previously shown to predict their academic and career success. Social-emotional crossroads and career self-authorship are positioned as central constructs in the model and proposed as critical pathways to FGEM students' academic and career developme… [10:55 AM]The Reliability Problem: Why Two Examiners Often Disagree About the Same Oral Performance

One of the most thoroughly documented problems with oral assessment is inter-rater reliability—the degree to which different examiners assign consistent grades to the same student performance. Meta-analyses of oral exam scoring across disciplines show correlation coefficients of 0.4-0.6 (where 1.0 is perfect agreement), meaning examiners frequently disagree by entire letter grades when evaluating identical performances. A landmark 1990 study recorded medical students' oral exams and had multiple faculty members independently score the same videotaped performance: variance between examiners exceeded variance between students, meaning "who evaluates you" predicted your grade more than "how well you performed." This isn't about untrained or careless examiners—it persists even with experienced faculty using detailed rubrics. The problem: oral performance contains too many simultaneous dimensions (content accuracy, argumentation structure, verbal fluency, confidence, responsiveness) for human evaluators to weight consistently.

Harvard faculty considering oral assessment must confront this measurement validity crisis. Written essays, while imperfect, achieve inter-rater reliability of 0.7-0.85 when scored with rubrics—substantially better than oral exams. The implications are profound for fairness: if your grade depends significantly on examiner assignment rather than your actual performance, the assessment lacks basic psychometric validity. Strategies to improve reliability—multiple independent examiners per student, extensive calibration training, recorded performances for review—add enormous cost and complexity. Some universities address this by using oral assessment only for pass/fail or low-stakes formative feedback, where inconsistency matters less. But if Harvard deploys oral exams as high-stakes summative assessments (replacing final papers, determining honors), we're building grade determination on measurement quicksand. Before adopting oral assessment at scale, faculty should ask: are we willing to invest in the infrastructure (training, multiple examiners, calibration) that achieves acceptable reliability? If not, we're trading one assessment problem (AI-generated essays) for a worse one (arbitrary grading).

Sources: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC1114733/ (Inter-rater reliability in medical oral exams) | https://journals.sagepub.com/doi/10.3102/00346543066004367 (Meta-analysis of oral assessment reliability)

A Balanced Path Forward: Three Questions Before Adopting Oral Assessment

After surveying decades of research and post-AI experiments, three critical questions should guide Harvard faculty considering oral assessment. First: Is oral performance actually your learning outcome? If you're teaching public speaking, client presentation, or real-time argumentation, oral assessment has construct validity. If you're teaching sustained analytical thinking, research synthesis, or complex written argumentation—skills developed through writing's iterative, externalized cognition—then oral exams test the wrong thing. Don't mistake format for substance. Second: Can you commit to equity infrastructure? Effective oral assessment requires examiner training, rubric calibration, multiple evaluators, accommodations for neurodivergent/anxious/international students, recording systems, and explicit teaching of oral skills before assessment. Without this investment, you're creating new inequities while claiming to address old ones. Third: Are you willing to accept lower reliability? Inter-rater reliability for oral exams (0.4-0.6) is substantially worse than written work (0.7-0.85), meaning grades will reflect examiner assignment as much as student performance. If the answer to questions 2 or 3 is "no," oral assessment will cause more problems than it solves.

The evidence-based recommendation: Reserve oral assessment for specific, limited use cases where it has clear pedagogical value independent of AI concerns. Effective applications include: low-stakes formative check-ins throughout the semester (not high-stakes finals), oral defenses of written work (complementing, not replacing), practice sessions that scaffold skill development, and courses where oral communication is explicitly a learning outcome. Avoid: replacing final essays with one-shot oral exams, deploying oral assessment without prior training, framing it primarily as "AI-proofing," or implementing it without robust equity safeguards. The universities that successfully use oral work treat it as one assessment mode in a diverse ecosystem, not a silver bullet. Harvard should do the same: invest in iterative writing pedagogy, process-focused portfolios, and smaller seminar sizes before betting institutional credibility on oral assessment's contested promise.

Sources: https://www.aaup.org/article/assessing-assessment-ai-era (AAUP on AI-era assessment strategy) | https://www.insidehighered.com/advice/2024/03/15/pragmatic-approach-assessment-ai-age (Inside Higher Ed synthesis)
[11:05 AM]The Real Solution: What Harvard Should Invest In Instead

If oral assessment's costs exceed its benefits for most undergraduate courses, what should Harvard prioritize instead? The research is clear: iterative writing with visible process. Stanford's "unessay" experiments, Yale's scaffolded portfolio approach, and MIT's "staged revision" model all demonstrate that when students submit multiple drafts, receive substantive feedback between versions, and produce process documentation (annotated bibliographies, methodology memos, peer review exchanges), AI-generated work becomes pedagogically useless. You can't fake a learning trajectory—the intellectual development visible across three drafts, revised in response to specific feedback, reveals authentic engagement in ways a single polished essay (AI-generated or not) cannot. Brown University's 2024 pilot showed that staged writing assignments actually reduced student AI use voluntarily: when the process itself is where learning happens, outsourcing to AI means missing the point.

The institutional investments required: smaller seminar sizes (meaningful feedback on iterative work demands faculty time), TA training in formative assessment (coaching revision, not just grading finals), Canvas/Gradescope infrastructure for multi-stage submissions, and faculty development in designing scaffolded assignments. These are the same resources oral assessment demands—but with better pedagogical ROI, fewer equity disasters, and established evidence of effectiveness. Harvard's competitive advantage isn't adopting oral exams faster than Yale; it's providing the kind of sustained mentorship and iterative intellectual development that actually develops scholars. The AI era doesn't require abandoning writing—it requires teaching writing as the iterative, messy, revision-heavy process it always was, rather than the one-shot performance culture that made AI attractive in the first place. Fix the pedagogy, and the assessment problem solves itself.

Sources: https://www.stanforddaily.com/2024/02/09/unessay-stanford-writing/ (Stanford unessay experiments) | https://poorvucenter.yale.edu/IterativeWriting (Yale portfolio pedagogy) | https://news.brown.edu/articles/2024/05/ai-writing-study (Brown study on staged assignments reducing AI use)
